ORDERS OF THE DAY.
PUBLIC LIBRARIES BILL [S.]
Order for receiving Report of Amendment
read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I desire with the leave of the House to postpone the Report Stage of this Bill until
to-morrow. The terms of the Amendment
which I propose to put down for the Report
Stage were agreed upon too late yesterday
to enable them to be put on the Paper to-day.
I hope very much that this slight change will
not inconvenience the House.
Private Bill Procedure Bill.
Order for the Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move the Second Reading of the
Private Bill Procedure Bill. The object of
this Measure, as will be found in its title,
is to give full effect to certain enactments in
their application to Private Bill Procedure
in Northern Ireland. The powers of the
Parliament in Northern Ireland in relation
to Private Bills rest upon Section 18 of the
Government of Ireland Act, 1921, by which
it is endowed with all the powers, privileges,
and immunities of the Imperial House of
Commons. By Section 61 of the Government
of Ireland Act existing laws relating to
Ireland continue in force with the necessary
modification in their application to Northern
Ireland, unless and until repealed by the
Parliament of Northern Ireland. The present
Bill seeks to clarify the law in relation to
Private Bill Procedure by making specific
modifications by statute of Northern Ireland
to existing statutes of the Imperial Parlia
ment.
It is practically a one-clause Bill. The first
sub-section of Clause 1 provides for the application to Private Bill Procedure of a series of
enactments which may be described either as
applicable by virtue of the Government of
Ireland Act with certain necessary modifications or, as not with certainty so applicable
to our procedure here but obviously required
to be applicable. These enactments will be
found in the Schedule to the Bill. Perhaps
for the convenience of the House I may summarize them. The Act of 1837 is intended
to give effect outside Parliament to the Parliamentary Standing Orders, in so far as those
Orders require that certain documents-such
as maps, etc.-shall be deposited with local
officers for the purpose of inspection or
copying by interested parties. The Act of
1846 carries out a principle of great importance, viz., the giving of security by the
promoters of undertakings sought to be authorized by Parliamentary authority. The Act
of 1847 is the principal Act regulating the
taxing of costs on Private Bills in the House
of Commons. The Act of 1849 is the corresponding Act regulating taxation in the House
of Lords. It also extends the powers of the
Taxing Officer of the House of Commons,
chiefly so as to enable him to tax Bills comprising costs incurred in the House of Lords
or in proceedings in Courts outside the Parliament. The Act of 1879 amends the Act of
1847 by enabling the Taxing Officer to tax
costs in respect of provisional orders, confirming bills, etc.
Private Bill Procedure Bill.

1 APRIL, 1924.
Loans Guarantee Bill.
The Act of 1865 introduced the principle
of restraining vexatious litigation by empowering the Committee of a private Bill to
award costs in certain circumstances to
petitioners against a Bill or to the promoters
of a Bill. The Act of 1867 gave a similar
power to a Court of Referees on a Bill, and
the Act of 1871 gave a similar power to
Select Committees on Bills confirming provisional orders. The two Acts of 1888 relate
to the promotion and opposing in Parliament of Private Bills by certain classes of
local authority and to the charging of the
expenses upon local rates. Sub-section (2)—
This Sub-section makes, in a series of paragraphs, the particular modifications which
are required to be read into the enactments
which I have summarised when applying
them to Private Bill Procedure in the Parlia-
The following
ment of Northern Ireland.
may be specially noted:-
•
Paragraph (a)-Proviso (1) to this paragraph is required because all Private Bill
Committees in Northern Ireland are Joint
Committees of both Houses, and the Standing Orders relating to the private business
were made jointly by both Houses.
things
Paragraph (c) amongst other
provides that those Sections of the House
of Lords Costs Taxation Act, which are
merely reproductions of Sections of the
House of Commons Act, are not to apply.
Paragraph (d) makes the modifications
necessary by reason of the appointment of
a single taxing-officer, to act on behalf of
both Houses of the Parliament of Northern
Ireland.
Paragraph (e) gives to the Speakers of
both Houses jointly the power of drawing
up the list of Costs, Charges, and Expenses
in Private Bill matters.
are

The other paragraphs in this Sub-section
merely consequential. Sub-section
merely defines the general term "Private
Bill." Northern Ireland Standing Orders,
as at present in force, deal only with Local
Bills.
The second clause has been introduced in
order to provide against the possibility of a
construction being put upon the present Bill
which would in any way question, re-open,
or affect the validity of any Private Bill
proceeding already taken in the Parliament
of Northern Ireland. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for to-morrow.
Loans Guarantee Bill.
Order for Second Reading Read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move the Second Reading of the Loans
Guarantee Bill. This Measure is an extension of existing legislation for the support
and improvement of trade, and one which I
am sure will receive the assent of this
House. The first Loans Guarantee Act was
passed in 1922, and provided for a State
guarantee, not exceeding an aggregate of two
million pounds, of loans raised by "any
public authority or corporation or other
body of persons in connection with the carrying out of any capital undertaking
calculated to promote employment in
Northern Ireland." The period during
which State Guarantees could be given was
12 months after the passing of the Act in
December, 1922. The Act was so successful
that it was amended in the next Session of
Parliament, and in June, 1923, the aggregate
State guarantee was increased to three
million five hundred thousand pounds. By
Section 9 of the Finance Act, 1923, the period
during which guarantees could be given was
extended for a further period of a year. The
Bill now before the House further extends
the aggregate to a total of five million
pounds, and prolongs the period during
which these guarantees may be given to the
31st of March, 1925. I should like to remind
the House that this legislation contemplates
the guarantee only of loans, and contains no
power to make loans, or cash advances, or
grants from State Funds. Secondly, its
advantages are confined to capital undertakings, and are in no sense applicable to
current business expenditure. Thirdly,
these capital undertakings are most carefully
examined to eliminate those which involve
what may be described as speculative risk.
The prospect of any ultimate State liability
from guarantees under this legislation is,
therefore, reduced to a minimum, though it
cannot be denied that there is a certain
financial risk in giving State guarantees even
in spite of the most rigid elimination of all
taint of speculation, a risk which the
Government is bound to take into
consideration.
Finally, I would impress upon the House
that any impression that guarantees under
this legislation are confined to any
particular form of industry is quite
erroneous. Any capital undertaking caloulated
the
promote employment in
Province, and of such a nature as to satisfy
the Advisory Committee to the Ministry of
Finance in regard to the stability of the
parties involved, is qualified for State
guarantee. I beg to move.
to
71 Petrol-distributing Machines.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
THE SENATE
Moved That this Bill be now committed.-
(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the Third
time to-morrow.
Petrol Distributing Machines.
Mr. BARBOUR: I beg to ask His Majesty's
Government if they will take into consideration the question of passing legislation which
will give to local authorities the power to
grant permission for the erection and use of
petrol-distributing machines on public roads
or footpaths.
I venture to put forward this question to
the Government, not on account of the
interests of the purchasers of petrol, but on
account of the public interests which I think
would be served by the granting of this concession, in that it would enable traffic on the
roads to be cleared more quickly than at
present. I feel justified in forming that
opinion from the fact that in certain
instances public authorities believed they
had the power to grant this permission and
in a good many cases have granted it. On a
more thorough investigation, however, these
authorities have found they were acting ultra
vires, and they have been forced to withdraw
the permission or acquiescence which they
had granted. I understand there is a
possible objection to this proposal on the
ground of obstruction of the footpaths. I do
not think that objection is really as weighty
as it might first appear. I think anyone
going along the public roads will see that today there are obstructions, and I do not think
this modest addition to those obstructions
would form a ground of serious complaint to
anyone.
My main interest in the matter is that this
is a new development, and the fact that it
has been adopted and put in practice in many
other countries tells in its favour. I would
like to think that the people in the Six
Counties should be permitted to enjoy developments and improvements which have
been found beneficial in other countries.
Consequently I ask the Government if they
will take into consideration the advisability
of passing legislation necessary to empower
public authorities to grant permission for
the erection of these petrol-distributing
stations.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
As the law stands at present the hon. Member
Petrol-distributing Machines. 72
is no doubt aware that no local authority
has power to permit or even to acquiesce
in the erection of petrol pumps on public
roads. Such installations constitute an
obstruction of the highways and in consequence are illegal. No doubt they are a considerable convenience to the motoring public,
and, undoubtedly, motor traders are anxious
to be allowed to erect them, as may be seen
by the large number already in existence, as
endorsed by the insistent demand for their
extension, and by the fact that an effort is
being made in Great Britain to secure legislation to establish their legal status. They
are not altogether an unmixed blessing, since
motorists have no control over nor guarantee
as to the quality of the petrol supplied.
Furthermore, as the Weights and Measures
Act does not apply to petrol pumps their
accuracy is not ensured. In fact their
alleged inaccuracies occasioned a considerable
outcry in Great Britain during the autumn
of last year.
The claim to erect these pumps by garage
proprietors is really a claim by private
individuals to make use of the public roads
by means of an obstruction for their personal
profit. If the principle were admitted other
trades people would endeavour to use roads
for the display of their goods or otherwise
for the purposes of their business. It would
open up the question, and would give them
a right on parallel lines. Three Corporations
recently sought by Private Bills in the
Imperial Parliament to obtain powers to enable them to licence the erection of petrol
pumps in the highway. In two cases the
Clauses relating to these pumps were withdrawn, but in the remaining case the Corporation pressed for them, with the result
that the Local Legislation Committee in the
House of Commons decided that the power
sought ought not to be granted, at the same
time expressing the opinion that local authorities acquiescing in the erection of petrol
pumps on highways were neglecting their
duty.
The question was recently referred by the
Ministry of Home Affairs to the Roads
Advisory Committee for Northern Ireland,
who saw no reason for recommending any
interference with the existing law. Last
December the County Councils Association
of Great Britain announced that it is not
prepared to recommend the initiation of
legislation with regard to the erection of
roadside petrol pumps or "other actual or
potential highway obstructions." The existence of these petrol pumps on the public
highways might be a special danger in the
73 Petrol-distributing Machines.
THE SENATE

Adjournment.
event of the recurrence of trouble in
Northern Ireland.
I regret, therefore, that for these reasons
I shall not be able to give the hon. Member
the encouragement which I am sure he hoped
to find in the answer I was about to give.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that this House do now adjourn.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at twenty
minutes before Four of the
clock.
75 Dangerous Drugs and Poisons Bill
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 2nd April, 1924.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
of the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Dangerous Drugs and Poisons
(Amendment) Bill.
Order for the Second reading read,
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I beg to move the
Second reading of the Dangerous Drugs and
Poisons (Amendment) Bill. This Bill is designed to make more difficult illicit trading
in drugs and poisons. The Imperial Parliament had no power to legislate for Northern
Ireland those matters for which the Parliament of Northern Ireland had full powers to
legislate for itself. As matters stand, therefore, there is at present no legislation in
Northern Ireland amending the Dangerous
Drugs Act of 1920 parallel to the amending
legislation of the Imperial Parliament last
year. The Bill before the House, therefore,
remedies the omission, and specifically
applies to Northern Ireland the provisions
of the amending Act of the Imperial Parliament which it was not within the power of
the Imperial Parliament to apply to this
Province.
on
and
the
shop
also
Further, the Bill amends the law under
the Poisons (Ireland) Act of 1870 regarding
the sale of certain drugs to medical men.
dentists and veterinary surgeons, and makes
provision to prevent people obtaining drugs
by representing themselves as purchasers
behalf of medical practitioners,
safeguards the transit between
and the practitioners. It
amends the Act of 1870, by enabling the Governor in Council to prescribe
the particulars which are to be given on
the labels attached to medical preparations.
showing the proportion of poisonous ingredients contained therein. I am sure I shall
carry the House with me in support of a
measure designed to protect the people of
this Province adequately against the evil of
the improper use of drugs. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Thursday, 10th inst.
THE SENATE.
Explosives Bill.
Explosives Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
1 beg to move the Second Reading of the
Explosives Bill. This Measure seeks to
amend the Explosives Act, 1875, as applied to
Northern Ireland. The House is no doubt
aware that the Imperial Parliament passed
legislation amending the principal Act, the
Explosives Act of 1875, last year. The
present Bill is designed on parallel lines
with that amending legislation, because the
Government is convinced that amendment
on these lines is very desirable. I do not
anticipate that there will be any disposition
to criticize the principle of this measure.
It is very simple and contains three clauses.
The first clause gives the Minister of Home
Affairs power by order to direct what
special precautions are to be taken in the
event of circumstances requiring such
special treatment, and, in case of failure by
the occupier of the premises to carry out
the order, the Minister of Home Affairs is
empowered to secure that the precautionary
measures are carried out and to recover the
expenses of such action together with any
penalty for default under the principal Act.
The second clause gives increased protection to young persons employed upon explosives.
The third clause increases penalties for
keeping explosives in unauthorised places.
The present penalty is two shillings a pound
for every pound of explosive improperly
so kept and this clause raises the maximum
fine to £100 or the fine which can be inposed at the rate of two shillings a pound
under the existing law whichever is the
larger. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Thursday, 10th inst.
Public Libraries Bill [S.]

read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that this Report be now received.

council to adopt or carry into execution
the Public Libraries Acts shall cease; and,
where any Rural District Council was,

carrying the Public Libraries Acts into

those Acts into execution
carry

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move, in Clause 1, page 2, at end of
Section (2) to add,
"(3) For the purpose of carrying the
Public Libraries Acts into execution a
County Council shall appoint a County
Library Committee constituted in accordance with the provisions contained in the
Schedule to this Act; but the acts of the
County Library Committee shall be submitted to the County Council for their
Approval."
The Amendment which I have the honour
to propose was foreshadowed when the Bill
was in Committee, and I have every reason
to believe that it completely satisfies the
desire expressed on behalf of the District
Councils by the hon. Senator (Senator
Greer) that they should have adequate representation in the operation of the Libraries
Acts by the County Councils. Provision is
also made for the representation of Urban
Authorities and Town Commissioners, if and
when they elect to transfer their powers
under the Libraries Acts to the County Councils. I would like to draw the attention of
the House to an important point, namely
that the Amendment secures that the County
Council shall always have a majority representation upon these County Libraries Committees, as is only just, seeing that the responsibility for the proper administration of
the Public Libraries Acts is placed in their
hands under this Bill. I hope the Amendment, as it appears on the Paper, bears out
what I have said, and that it will
be satisfactory to hon. Members.
Mr. GREER: I would like to mention to
the House that I have taken steps to ascertain
the views of the persons in the county who
objected to the Bill as originally drawn, and
I understand that the Amendment as now
brought forward will be quite satisfactory
to all parties. I think that when the Bill
is passed into an Act of Parliament it will
be a very valuable one and in the interests
of all the counties.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to
say in
supporting the Amendment that it seems to
meet not only the point alluded to by my
hon. Friend who has just sat down, and
also alluded to by the noble
Marquess, but I think it meets very
fully the proposal I myself made, although
on somewhat different lines, for giving Rural
District Councils a chance of contracting out.
I am, therefore, glad it has been tound
possible to give representation to any Urban
District or town which may elect to come
into the scheme, and I think it will be found
to be of great assistance in working any
public library in any of those counties in
Northern Ireland which have not yet adopted
the Carnegie scheme. I think the Amendment meets every point, and I also think
it is particularly advantageous that power
has been given to add to the County Library
Committee members who are not included
in the membership of any of the Councils.
It has been found in the case of other County
Committees that the assistance of people
who are not members of either the County
Council
District Council was very
or any
valuable, and I am glad an opportunity has
been afforded of gaining that assistance.
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move
That the following be the Schedule to the
Bill:-
Schedule
Constitution of County Library
Committee..
The County Library Committee shall consist of the following persons:-
(a) The chairman and vice-chairman
of the County Council:
(b) Two members of the Council (of
whom the chairman shall be one) of each
Rural District, as respects which the
County Council are carrying the Public
Libraries Acts into execution;
(c) One member of the Council of each
Urban District, and one commissioner
the
of each town
Council or Commissioners of which have relinquished
their powers and duties under the Public
Libraries Acts in favour of the County
Council:
(d) An additional number of members
of the County Council equal to the
number of Rural and Urban Districts
and towns as respects which the County
Council are carrying the Public Libraries
Acts into execution.
The County Library Committee may
choose in addition to the aforesaid members
further members (not being members of
any Local Authority in the County), not
exceeding in number one for each Rural
and Urban District and town as respects
which the County Council is carrying the
Public Libraries Acts into execution.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered That the said Bill be read the
Third time to-morrow.
79 Private Bill Procedure Bill.

THE SENATE
Adjournment.
Private Bill Procedure Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I propose, with the leave of the House,
that the Committee Stage on the Private Bill
Procedure Bill should be postponed. We took
the Second Reading yesterday, and owing to
the fact that no speech was made on that
occasion, except my own, I was under the
impression that it was a Bill, the Committee
Stage of which we could put down for to-day.
But it has been represented to me by more
than one hon. Member that this is a Bill of
so complicated a character that hon. Members
would like to have a little more time to
consider it, and as I am given to understand
that there is no particular need for the Bill
to be passed at once I would ask the House
if consent could be given to postpone the
Committee Stage until some later meeting
of the House.
Ordered: That the House be put into Committee on the first sitting day after the
Recess at Easter.
Loans Guarantee Bill.
Order for the Third Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that this Bill be now read the
Third time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that this Bill do now pass.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate have agreed
to the said Bill without any Amendment.
Adjournment.

I beg to move that this House do now adjourn.

Examination of Luggage.
Examination of Luggage.
3 APRIL. 1924.
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 3rd April, 1924.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three of
the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Examination of Passengers' Luggage.
The following stood on the Paper in the
name of Mr. Glendinning:
:-
"To draw attention to the existing arrangements for the examination of the luggage
of passengers arriving at the Port of Belfast,
and to move for Papers."
Mr. GLENDINNING: I should like to ask
the noble Marquess, the Leader of the House,
if the Government would consider the desirability of making more suitable arrangements for
the examination of the luggage of passengers
entering the Port of Belfast. What happens
at the present time is this. As the passengers
come off the steamers they are intercepted by
policemen who ask them if they have any
firearms. The only facilities the police have
for examining the luggage is an empty crate
or deal box upon which the luggage is opened
and the contents examined. To my mind this
method is open to two obvious objections.
One has to do with the question of cleanliness.
A goods shed is not a particularly clean place
and any of the contents of one's bag are liable
to be more or less contaminated. Then there
is the question of undue publicity. There is
generally a crowd of people awaiting the
arrival of the steamers and when being
examined a person's luggage is unduly exposed
to the public gaze.
If it is still necessary that luggage should
be examined it ought to be done under proper
conditions and with some regard for the
feelings of travellers, and particularly lady
travellers. It must be remembered that almost
without exception the passengers are respectable residents in the Six Counties or visitors
coming here with no unfriendly intentions
With regard to the latter class they are liable
to receive an exceedingly unpleasant impression
of the country the moment they put their foot
on shore. One gentleman I know has already
had his luggage examined seven times this
year, and I quite agree with him that it is not
particularly creditable to Northern Ireland
It would not cost much to erect a light shed
or even a small canvas enclosure into which the
luggage could be taken and examined. I hope
I have only to direct the attention of the
Government to the present state of affairs to
have some better method adopted without any
delay.
Mr. GREER: I am glad the hon. Member
has called attention to this matter because my
experience has been the very same as his. I
do not in the least object to the examination
of passengers' luggage on arrival in Belfast if
the authorities think it is necessary. In that
case I should be very sorry if it was discontinued, but I think the steamboat companies or
the Harbour Commissi uers might be able to
make some arrangement by which the luggage
could be examined in some separate place apart
from the ordinary traffic and where the
articles in the luggage cases could be put on
a table. I think that would be an improvement. I have no doubt that when attention
is called to the matter something will be done
in that direction.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): No one can possibly
complain either of the tone or the substance
of the criticism which is contained in the
question my hon. Friend has addressed to
the Government, but I am not quite sure
whether, besides objecting to the manner in
which the examination of passengers' luggage
is carried out, he objects to the examination as
well. I think he is well aware that the
examination of the luggage of passengers
arriving at the Port of Belfast is carried out
with the object of preventing firearms from
being improperly brought into Northern Ireland
but it would not be desirable in the public
interest for me to indicate in detail the
arrangements which have been made in this
respect. It may be stated, however, that
prior to 27th March, 1923, the removal of
firearms from Great Britain to Ireland was
prohibited under Orders made by the Secretary
of State under Section 9 of the Firearms Act,
1920, and the examination of luggage which
was necessary to ensure that these Orders
were not infringed was carried out by Customs
Officers at the ports of departure in Great
Britain. Examination at this side of the
Channel was therefore unnecessary. By an
Order of the Secretary of State dated 27th
March, 1923, these Orders were revoked and
examination by Customs Officials at ports
in Great Britain was discontinued.
The provisions of Regulation 31 of the
Restoration of Order in Ireland Regulations
were then and are still in force and under
these the removal from or to Great Britain to
or from Ireland of any firearms, etc., except
under permit from the competent military
authority is prohibited. When the Customs
Examination of Luggage.
Public Libraries Bill.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
examination at the Ports in Great Britain
ceased it became necessary to provide for the
examination of the luggage of passengers at
the ports of arrival in order to enforce
compliance with the Regulations. This
is, as we know, carried out by the
police who, I think we can all
agree, carry out their duties with as little
inconvenience and delay to the public as
possible. However, I think it would be
premature to contemplate at this moment any
relaxation of the precautions that are taken
in this respect. So much for the reasons why
this examination is continued. We do not feel
in a position yet to relax a Regulation which
may be infringed by passengers carrying
firearms to and from Great Britain.
The other point to which the hon. Member
devoted his attention is the manner in which
this examination is carried out at the ports
of arrival. I am bound to say that to a
certain extent I share his feelings on this
subject, but I think he should remember that
there are many points of disembarkation, and
if any elaborate sheds were to be erected
by the Government it would entail a
certain amount of expense. On the
other hand, if there was one point to
which all luggage was taken it would entail
considerable delay for the passengers who, the
moment they disembark from the ship, are
desirous of taking a conveyance and going
to their destination. I can assure the hon.
Member that I shall be very happy to take
the matter up and to mention it in the proper
quarter to see whether some steps cannot be
taken to further ensure the convenience of
passengers and at the same time to carry
out the search which the competent authorities feel it is necessary should be carried out.
If the hon. Senator will leave the matter to
me I may be able to take some steps which
will be in accordance with the desire he has
evinced, and perhaps in a few days' time I
shall be able to tell him what actual steps have
been taken.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I wish to thank the
noble Marquess-
Mr. SPEAKER: Does the hon. Member
wish to move for Papers? If so he is entitled
to speak again.
I am
Mr. GLENDINNING: I only wish to say
that none of the complaints that have been
made to me have been on the point of the
examination of the luggage itself.
thoroughly aware of the necessity for the
examination of luggage and I do not object
to it. I only object to the conditions under
which the examination is carried out, and I
am sure that is the view of Senator Greer.
Mr. SPEAKER: Does the hon. Member
therefore withdraw his Motion!
Mr. GLENDINNING: Yes, my Lord.
Motion, by leave, withdrawn.
Public Libraries Bill (S).
Order for the Third reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I beg to move that this Bill be read the Third
time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
CLAUSE 3. (Expenses of county councils
and limit on rate)-(1) The expenses of a
County Council under the Public Libraries
Acts shall be defrayed out of the county
fund, and the amount required shall be
raised by means of the Poor rate equally
over so much of the county as comprises the
county districts and towns as respects which
the County Council is for the time being
carrying the Public Libraries Acts into
execution.
(2) The amount to be raised as aforesaid
for defraying any such expenses shall not
exceed in any one local financial year a sum
equal to a rate of one penny in the pound
on the rateable value, at the beginning of
that year, of the county districts and towns
as respects which the County Council is for
the time being carrying the Public Libraries
Acts into execution :
Provided that in an urban district or town
as respects which the Public Libraries Acts
are being carried into execution by a County
Council, the said limit may, with the
consent of the Ministry, be exceeded to an
extent not exceeding two pence in the pound.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
In accordance with the rules of this House
I beg to move an Amendment to omit Clause
3 from the Bill. Hon. Members will be aware
that this is a privilege Amendment, and I move
it because this House is not competent to
initiate legislation which entails the levying of
a tax or rate upon the people. The Clause
which I now move to omit will be reinserted in
the Bill by way of a Government Amendment
during the consideration of the Bill in Committee in another place, and the Bill so
amended, with any further Amendments that
may be made there, will be referred back to
this House for approval. It is merely a formal
Amendment. I beg to move.
Public Libraries Bill.

3 APRIL. 1924.
Adjournment.
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I beg to move that this Bill do now pass.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to carry down the said Bill and desire their
concurrence thereto.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I beg to move that this House do now adjourn.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twentyfive Minutes before Four of the
Clock, until Thursday, 10th
April.
Business of the House.
Business of the House.
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 10th April, 1924.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three of
the clock, Mr SPEAKER in the Chair.
Private Business.
LONDONDERRY CORPORATION
BILL.
Mr. SPEAKER: I beg to move that this
Bill be read the Second time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That a message be sent to the
Commons to acquaint them therewith.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That they have passed the Unemployment
Insurance Bill and the Local Government
(Financial Provisions) Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
UNEMPLOYMENT INSURANCE BILL.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this Bill be read
a First time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
LOCAL GOVERNMENT (FINANCIAL
PROVISIONS) BILL.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this Bill be read
a First time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be read a Second
time on the first Sitting day after the Recess
at Easter.
Business of the House.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In the absence of my noble Friend,
the Leader of the House, I beg to move:
That Standing Order No. XXV be considered in order to its being dispensed with for
the purpose of passing the Unemployment
Insurance Bill through all its stages.
Mr. LESLIE: Before dealing directly with
the Motion I should like to take the opportunity of welcoming back the noble Viscount
to the House, for his presence has been
greatly missed during the past few weeks.
As regards the proposal that we should dispense with one of our Standing Orders in
order to pass through all its stages to-day a
Bill which none of us saw until we entered
the House this afternoon, I can only say it
is a proposal which, in my judgment, ought
not to be brought before us. It is obviously
impossible to consider a Bill in such time as
may elapse between ten minutes to Three
and twenty minutes past Three. I am sure
the noble Viscount himself must recognize
that the House is being placed in an impossible position. It is unfair to the Members of this House to ask them to stand up
in apparent opposition to a Measure which
they have not read, and with which, when
they have read it, they are very likely to
be in complete sympathy. I presume this is
the Measure the production of which was
foreshadowed in the Gracious Speech from
the Throne, but the House has been in Session for a month and the Bill has not yet
been laid before us. It seems fair to point
out that however desirable this Measure may
be in itself—I cannot speak for its provisions
until I have read them our Six Counties and
our cities are entitled to have upon that Bill,
as upon any other Bill, the mind of both
Chambers of Parliament. This House can
express its approval of a Measure either verbally or silently. It does not follow that it
is necessary for the real consideration of a
Bill that anything should be said about it
in this House or that any amendment should
be introduced, but We are responsible
to those we represent for the due
consideration of every Bill that comes
before us, and I say we cannot consider a Bill unless it is laid before us
in due time. As I say, I cannot go into the
question of the merits of the Bill, but one
will naturally want to know where the
money is to come from and what the Bill
will cost. We shall also want to know why
there should be this urgency about it. It is
very difficult to support the proposal of the
noble Viscount, and I wonder whether there
is not some middle course between finally
objecting to the proposal and accepting it as
it stands. It seems to me that if we limited
the interference with the Standing Order so
as in the first instance to allow us to read
the Bill a Second time, and then after we
have had the advantage of hearing the speech
with which the Bill is introduced, which
will probably tell us something about the
Bill, we might, it seems to me, not unsuit-
Business of the House.
Business of the House.
10 APRIL, 1924.
ably adjourn for a short time to enable those
of us who wish to look into the matter to repair to the Library and compare the Billif it is possible to get sufficient copies to go
round-with other Bills to which it may
refer.
It seems to me that the better course at
the moment would be to ask the noble Viscount to accept as an amendment to his Motion a proposal to the effect that Standing
Order No. XXV be considered in order to its
being dispensed with for the purpose of passing the Second Reading of the Bill, and in
the event of the Bill being read a Second
time that the said Standing Order be again
considered in order to its being dispensed
with for the purpose of passing the Bill
through its remaining stages. If that is in
order it may give us sufficient time to consider the Bill and enable us to pass an
intelligent opinion upon it. I expect that
when we have heard the Bill introduced I
shall not desire to oppose it, but for the
moment I am not able to agree to take the
course which has been suggested.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In the first place I would like to
thank the hon. Senator (Mr Leslie) for the
kind words with which he welcomed me
back to the House after my period of sickness. I do not quarrel with what the hon.
Senator has said regarding the course which,
with great reluctance, I am asking the
House to adopt. This House has a perfect
right to insist on having due time to study
Bills that come before it, but in this case I
must frankly tell the House that the Bill is
one of great urgency indeed. The reason it
was not possible to introduce the Bill before
was that we are dependent, as hon. Members
know, on the Bill coming to us from another
place. The Bill was introduced and read a
First time in another place on Thursday,
3rd inst., and it could not have been introduced until that time because it is similar to
a Bill passed in the Imperial House at
Westminster, and as hon. Members are
aware, this Government is anxious, and naturally anxious, to know the general principles of any Bill introduced at Westminster
before it introduces a similar Bill in this
Parliament. They felt they could not introduce a Bill of this nature in the Northern
Parliament without first seeing what the
Bill in the Imperial House was going to be.
The reason it is so urgent that this Bill
should be passed at once is that by the
middle of this month the twenty-six weeks
covenanted benefit will come to an end, and
therefore it is essential that we should pass
some legislation before that time in order
that these benefits may be continued. The
notice that whoever was leading the House
would move that this Standing Order should
be considered in order to its being dispensed
with for the purpose of passing the Bill
through all its stages appeared on the Notice
Paper a week ago, and although it is unusual
I may tell the hon. Senator that if he had
desired to have a copy of this Bill he could
have obtained it. The Bill as introduced in
another place has passed through all its
stages and has not been altered in any way.
As regards the middle course suggested by
the hon. Senator, to give this Bill a Second
Reading to-day and then adjourn, I do not
know how long he proposes to adjourn.
Does he mean until to-morrow?
Mr. LESLIE: During pleasure.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am not very conversant with the
rules of the House, and I am not sure
whether we can do that or not, but I am in
the hands of the House in that respect. I
desire to give every facility to hon. Members
Mr. SPEAKER: If the Leader of the House
would like to give only a Second Reading to
the Bill to-day we could meet again to-morrow to further discuss the Measure.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am obliged to you for the suggestion. If hon. Members desire that that
course should be adopted-that we should
give the Bill a Second Reading to-day and
then adjourn and put the Measure through
its remaining stages on Friday-I am in the
hands of the House and I am prepared to
accede to that request. At the same time I
do submit that this Bill is one of great urgency, and we must pass it so that it may
receive the Royal Assent before 15th April.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I can
quite understand the hon. Member objecting
to the hasty manner in which this Bill is
being rushed through, but at the same time
I hope he will on this occasion waive his
objection in view of the urgency of the matter. This is a very important Bill from the
workers' point of view, and it is one that
affects them very much. If it is not passed
it means that Unemployment Benefit ceases
in the middle of this month. The lot of
these poor people is bad enough without their
being deprived of benefit which they would
be legitimately entitled to if this Bill is
passed. Therefore, I hope the hon. Senator
will waive his objection on this occasion, and
with the help of the Leader of the House
steps may be taken to ensure that in future
Business of the House.
Business of the House.

THE SENATE
[Mr. Cunningham]
Bills will be received in sufficient time for
them to be properly considered.
Colonel MAXWELL: With the provisions
of the Bill I do not propose to deal, because,
like the hon. Member, Senator Leslie, I do
not know anything about them except from
odd paragraphs I have seen in the newspaper,
but I do sympathize with him in the position
he has taken up about rushing Bills through
and asking us to suspend Standing Orders
in order to consider Bills which we do not
know anything about. The reply of the
noble Viscount has raised another question.
His excuse for rushing this Bill through
was that it could not be introduced in another place because we had to wait until the
Imperial Parliament had passed their legislation. I think that is a principle that can
be carried a great deal too far. How are we
to know where we are if any Bill has to wait
on the action of the Imperial Parliament
before it can be introduced in the Northern
Parliament? After passing through the
other House it is brought here and we are
told we have to pass it at once without considering it. I do not think that is a position
that the representatives of the people of the
Six Counties ought to be placed in, because
it is one that might lead to very great inconvenience and to passing very hasty legislation. I will agree to what my hon. Friend
(Mr. Leslie) thinks it is best to do under the
circumstances. Owing to the urgency of the
matter perhaps we might pass the Bill
through this time, but I think it is a thing
that this House ought to guard itself against
in the future.
Mr. GREER: I cordially agree with everything Senator Leslie has said in regard to
the improprietry of rushing Bills of this
character through the House at one sitting,
but I recognize that this is a very important
Bill in the eyes of a number of persons who
will be receiving benefit under it. Before I
can make up my mind what attitude to take
on the present Motion I should like to know
whether, if the Bill is passed through all its
stages to-morrow, there would be any doubt
as to its receiving the Royal Assent in time?
If there would be no doubt as to its not being
kept too late I think we should certainly delay the Measure a day, if only to mark our
sense of the manner in which we have been
treated.
Mr. WOODS: I would like to sympathize
with hon. Senators who have spoken in regard to the rushing of Bills through this
House. I have expressed the same opinion
on several occasions. I think we ought to be
given reasonable time in which to consider
Bills that come before us. Unfortunately this
is a Bill in which our sympathies must go in
another direction. We must sympathise with
those people who have been endeavouring to
exist for a number of years on the benefits
of Unemployment Insurance. The last Act
expires on the 15th of the present month,
and from the tenth to the fifteenth does not
give us very much time in which to get the
Royal Assent in order to ensure that these
people shall continue to receive these benefits.
Therefore I think we should waive our objections on the present occasion and pass the Bill
through all its stages to-day. I do not think
there is anything objectionable in the Bill.
It is only a matter of raising the donation a
little, and 1 think we all realize that the
donation is very low at present. I would
rather see the people employed than receiving a donation, but as we have to help them
to exist by giving this donation I think we
should waive any objection we may have to
the manner in which this Bill has been placed
before us and allow it to pass through all
its stages to-day.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: With regard to the point raised by
Senator Greer, it would not be too late for
the Bill to receive the Royal Assent if the
Second Reading only was taken to-day and
the remaining stages to-morrow. At the
same time I would prefer that we should take
the Bill through all its stages to-day. As regards the point raised by the hon. and gallant
Member (Colonel Maxwell), perhaps I did
not explain myself clearly. Before we introduce a Bill here we wish to know what the
general policy of the Imperial Parliament is
as regards Unemployment Benefit and so on,
because we desire that the workman on this
side should get exactly the same terms as
his fellow workman across the water.
do not want to introduce a Bill that may
give benefits that are not so good as are
given in some subsequent Bill which may be
produced a few weeks afterwards in the
Imperial Parliament, and then to have to introduce in this Parliament what I call a
stop-gap Bill. That is the reason we wished
to know what the policy of His Majesty's
Government at Westminster was before we
brought in our Unemployment Insurance
Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
We

be dispensed with in respect of the said Bill.

Unemployment Insurance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Second Reading of
the Unemployment Insurance Bill I desire
to lay before the House the object of the
Bill, which is a very simple one. At the
same time it is one of immense importance.
The object of the Measure is to extend the
powers of the Ministry of Labour under the
existing Unemployment Insurance legislation in order that by the exercise of those
powers the Minister of Labour may continue
the policy in regard to unemployment to
which this House has already given its
approval, namely, the policy that provides
that the workman on this side of the Irish
Sea should be in the same position in regard
to State assistance during periods of unemployment as his brother workman in other
parts of the United Kingdom on the other
side of the water.
To effect this the Bill under discussion in
the first place empowers my right hon. Friend,
the Minister of Labour, to extend the period
during which benefit, whether covenanted
or uncovenanted, is payable during the first
benefit year, that is to say, during the
twelve months ending the 15th October of the
present year, beyond the maximum aggregate
of twenty-six weeks as laid down in the
Act of 1923. My right hon. Friend with the
powers provided him under Sub-section (1)
of Clause 1 will be able to keep pace with
whatever steps are taken by the Imperial
Authorities in regard to the extension of the
95 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard].
number of weeks during which benefit may be
drawn in the current first benefit year.
In the second place, this Bill authorizes
the Minister of Labour to extend the provisions of existing legislation in regard to
the payment of benefit during the second
benefit year, that is to say, the twelve months
subsequent to October 15, 1924. to cover uncovenanted persons up to the same aggregate
of 26 weeks as covenanted persons
The Third Sub-section simply provides that
the benefits under any special Insurance
Scheme authorized under the Act of 1920
shall be increased to keep pace with benefits
under the National Scheme. The Fourth
Sub-section abolishes "gaps," and I think hon.
Members will agree with me that in the
present condition of trade the imposition of
these gaps was an undoubted hardship on
those persons who were secking to obtain
work, and they will also agree that the time
has now arrived when these gaps should be
abolished. It is true, and I am glad to be
able to say so, that the Unemployment figures
are slightly more encouraging than they were
a year ago, but it still remains a fact, and 1
would like the House to weigh it well, that
40 per cent of the persons drawing benefit
to-day are uncovenanted. That is a serious
matter, and I confidently appeal to this
House to pass this Bill. It is urgently needed
because the period of uncovenanted benefit
ends on the 15th of this month and if the
Bill is not law by that date these persons
may find themselves totally without any
means of subsistence. I therefore earnestly
ask the House to pass the Bill for the benefit
of those who through no fault of their own.
are unable to find work and have to live on
what is erroneously called the dole.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I would
like to say how pleased I am to find our
Government keeping pace so faithfully with
the Imperial Government in connection with
this Unemployment benefit. While we regard
the extension of the benefits as necessary
we look forward month after month to
seeing an improvement in trade. It will not
come rapidly and we have got to wait
patiently for it. It cannot be brought
about by the waving of a wand. We have
been looking forward to an improvement, but
in the shipbuilding trade particularly there
is no improvement, and that is the trade in
which there is a considerable amount of unemployment. While that trade remains bad
it is necessary that we should provide an
existence for those who are unemployed. The
assistance they do get is poor, but it nevertheless keeps them from starvation. I am
satisfied there is not one of them who would
not work in preference to receiving Unemployment benefit. I have had the experience on
more than one occasion of being out of work.
I have had to look for work and it is a
very disagreeable duty to have to perform.
Unfortunately you do not get much sympathy
when you go round looking for employment
and sometimes you do not even receive civility from those whom you ask for work. Those
who are unemployed would rather be working
than looking for work.
Mr. DICKSON: What about Southampton?
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I think
there are a few cases where men are quite
content to receive the dole. At the present
time there is undoubtedly a considerable
amount of unrest attributable to the unequal
wages that are being paid in different industries. That of course does not come within the scope of this Bill. I am very pleased
to see that our Government is keeping pacs
with the Imperial Government in these matters. There is one thing I would like to ask
the Leader of the House, and that is if there
is any extension or improvement of these
benefits in the Imperial House will he give
us an undertaking that there will be a
similar improvement or extension of the
benefits in the Northern area? I notice that
it was stated in the Imperial Parliament by
the Minister of Labour that he would not
be content with 26 weeks benefit and that he
intended to extend it to 41 weeks in one year.
If that is the case I hope the same thing will
apply to Northern Ireland. Perhaps the
noble Viscount will be able to give us some
information on that point. It is very necessary that our workers here should be treated
exactly the same as the workers on the
other side of the water; otherwise there will
be considerable discontent which would not
be in the interests of the people of this
Province.
Mr. LESLIE: I listened with great attention to the speech of the Noble Viscount
in introducing this Measure. As I expected,
I find that the Bill is one with which I am
to a great extent in sympathy. But leaving
that aside for a moment I am afraid I am
not in the least convinced of the necessity
for pushing it through to-day at the railroad speed that is suggested. We are
to-day at the tenth day of the month
and apparently-
Mr. SPEAKER: I think the hon. Member
must now adhere to the discussion of the
Bill itself. He has already discussed the
other question, and it is unnecessary to deal
with that again.
Mr. LESLIE: Passing to the Bill I should
like to ask the noble Viscount what this Bill
is going to cost, where the money is going
to come from, and the extent to which the
Unemployment Fund is already overdrawn?
One is interested in those questions because
there are other classes of persons in Northern
Unemployment Insurance Bill.
Adjournment.
10 APRIL, 1924.
Ireland besides those who are unemployed,
and besides those who are subject to Unemployment Insurance, and if it is the case
that the passing of this Bill will impose
any further liability upon National Funds
it occurs to me that those who have to contribute as taxpayers to those funds are not
likely to view the matter with any great
feeling of satisfaction. If, however, the
Noble Viscount is able to assure me that no
liability will be incurred in this matter
which is beyond the resources of the Province
nobody will welcome the assertion more
cordially than I shall myself. Indeed I cannot but remark that if we can face the
additional liability-and we remember for
the moment the figures in the Consolidated
Fund Bill which we passed the other day,
and the amount by which we were able to
extend our guarantees, which in itself was
a Measure to assist employment-if we take
the three Measures together it seems to show
that the financial position of this Province
is extremely satisfactory and one which reflects great credit on the Government and
particularly on the right hon. Gentleman,
the Minister of Finance. I should like to be
assured on those two matters-the cost, and
the extent of the overdraft. I did not quite
follow the Noble Viscount in his reference
to what I think must be the second Subsection of the First Clause, as regards the
receipt of benefit during the benefit year
next subsequent to the first benefit year.
Do I understand that the benefit year next
subsequent to the first benefit year terminates on the 15th October. 1924?
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD indicated assent
Mr. LESLIE: If that be so it would seem
to explain the appearance of the provision
dealing with it in this Measure, because I
did not make out that that provision had
been included in the English Act. There is
some doubt as to whether this is meant to
be our last Unemployment Insurance Bill of
this Session or whether, irrespective of the
possibility my hon. Friend (Senator
Joseph Cunningham) alluded to of a further
extension of the Act in England, we may
expect yet another Measure. I quite agree
with what the Noble Viscount said, and I
have said more than once that so far as our
resources extend we ought to do as well for
our
own unemployed here as is done for
the unemployed in Great Britain. I
have always held that opinion and I hope I
will be able to continue to hold it, but it
is not possible to put out of one's mind the
fact that certain proposals have recently
been made in England, the adoption of which
it would be impossible to support here.
They have not yet been placed on the Statute
Book and they are not to be found in this
Bill. I do not think there is anything
further on which I ought to detain the
House. I have done what I could to go
through the Bill in the time at my disposal,
and although I confess I am not fully conversant with the full effect of the last
section of the first Clause, as we are not to
have further time to consider the matter we
will have to leave those things in doubt. I
hope they will effect the improvement desired. On the whole I support the Second
Reading of the Bill.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
ARD: In reply to Senator Joseph
Cunningham, who I think raised the
question of the 41 weeks benefit, the
Bill provides for that. Senator Leslie
asked what was the extra cost of this
Bill. The additional cost involved under this
Bill, assuming 13 per cent unemployment,
which is the percentage we have got down to
in the current financial year, is estimated
to be £35,000. As regards the liability on the
Fund, the present deficit is about £1,700,000.
As trade gets better we hope to reduce this
very considerably. There is at the present
moment another big Act before the Imperial
Parliament which 1 understand is highly
controversial. How that Bill will emerge on
the Statute Book I am unable to say, and
therefore I cannot bind myself or the Government to say what we will do or what further
proposals we may have to make without know
ing in what form the Bill will pass through
the Imperial House.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill be read
a Second time," agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this Bill be now
committed.
On Question, Motion negatived.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this Bill be now
read the Third time.
On Question, Motion negatived.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this Bill do now
pass.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate have agreed
to the said Bill without any Amendment.
Adjournment.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this House do
now adjourn. Perhaps I may inform the
House that there will be a formal sitting on
Saturday at 11 o'clock, to receive the Royal
Assent to certain Bills.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at

the Clock, until 11 o'clock
on Saturday next.

THE SENATE.

Saturday, 12th April, 1924.
The Senate met at Eleven of the Clock, the
Deputy Speaker (Right Hon. Samuel Cunningham) in the Chair.
LONDONDERRY CORPORATION BILL.
Moved: That the Senators following be
named of the Joint Opposed Committee on
the Bill:-
Mr. H. A. Milne Barbour,
Mr. J. Cunningham,
Mr. J. G. Leslie.
(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Royal Assent.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I am authorised by
the Prime Minister on his behalf to inform
the Senate that his Grace the Governor of
Northern Ireland, in the name and on behalf
of His Majesty the King, has been pleased
to give his Assent to the following Bills:-
Loans Guarantee,
Unemployment Insurance.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That they have passed the Char-a-banc
Regulation Bill, to which they desire the
concurrence of this House.
CHAR-A-BANC REGULATION BILL.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that this Bill be read a First
time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
BELFAST CORPORATION BILL.
Moved: That the Senators following be
named of the Joint Opposed Committee on
the Bill:-
Sir E. C. Herdman,
Mr. J. A. Long,
Mr. J. A. Woods
(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I beg to move that this House do now adjourn.
The date on which we should be called
the
together again I propose to leave to
Speaker to determine. I think that will
probably be the wish of the House.
Mr. BARBOUR: May I ask if the noble
Leader of the House can give us any indication
as to the probable date of the re-assembling
of the House?
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I think I can give the hon. Senator an
indication as to the date. I should say it will
be somewhere about the middle of May. The
House of Commons, I believe, proposes to meet
a little earlier than that, and the time when
business will come down to this House for
discussion will be a few days after the
meeting of the House of Commons. In view
of members' engagements it will perhaps meet
their convenience if I say that the date which
Mr. Speaker will probably appoint when we
shall meet again will be about the middle of
May.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Fifteen
Minutes after Eleven of the
Clock until such date as the
Speaker shall appoint.

THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 13th May, 1924.
The Senate-after the adjournment on 12th
April for the Easter Recess-met at a Quarter
after Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
PAPERS PRESENTED.
Ministry of Labour--(1) Night Employment
of Young Persons (Reverberatory or Regenerative Furnaces) (Northern Ireland) Order,

Force) Amendment Regulations, 1924.

Ireland), 1924.

Electricity Special Order, 1924.

ordered to lie on the Table.

Corporation Bill in the place of Sir Emerson
Herdmar.
ELECTION OF SENATOR.
Mr. SPEAKER I have to inform the House
that I have received the certificate of the
Clerk of the Crown that Sir Thomas James
Dixon, Baronet, of Cairndhu, Laine, County
Antrim, was on the 24th April duly elected
Senator to serve in the Parliament of Northern Ireland in room of the late Mr. Adam
Duffin.
NEW MEMBER SWORN.
Sir Thomas James Dixon, Baronet, sworn,
and took his seat in the room of the late Mr.
Adam Duffin.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That they have passed the Illegitimate
Children (Affiliation Orders) Bill and the
Commissions of the Peace Bill, to which they
desire the concurrence of this House.
ILLEGITIMATE CHILDREN (AFFILIA-
TION ORDERS) BILL.

time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)

Moved: That this Bill be now read the
First time.-(Viscount Massereene and

(ii) the expression "Act of Parliament,"
where it occurs in sections two and three
of the Borough Funds (Ireland) Act. 1888,
shall be construed as including any Act of

(b) References in the Parliamentary Deposits
Act, 1846, to the House of Lords and to

of that House shall be construed as a reference to a certificate of the Speaker of the

*

1920, have been transferred as respects

ferences to the department or authority to

as rea
103 Private Bill Procedure Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House) : The Amendment
which I desire to move is in Clause 1, on page
2, line 17, at end ("Parliament of the United
Kingdom") to add "(b) So much of the
Parliamentary Documents Deposit Act, 1837,
as relates to postmasters shall not apply."
This is quite a simple Amendment directed
to remove an objection that has been made on
behalf of the Imperial Government that, as
the Bill stands it seeks to impose duties and
liabilities on postmasters.
The Parliamentary Documents Deposits
Act,. 1837, relates to documents which may be
required by Parliamentary Standing Orders
to be deposited with postmasters amongst
other officials. It directs that these persons
are to receive the deposited documents; also
to issue receipts for them and to permit all
persons interested to inspect and take copies
of the documents deposited on payment of
fees. Furthermore, it imposes penalties upon
these officials for failing to comply with the
provisions of the Act.
hon.
This difficulty could hardly arise in
practice, even were the Bill left unamended,
because no requirement exists in the Standing
Orders of the Northern Ireland Parliament
requiring documents to be deposited with
postmasters. But I feel sure that
Members will realize in view of the objection
which has been made by the Imperial Government, that the Government desires the insertion of the words in the Amendment, which
specifically exclude postmasters from the
operation of the Bill. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The next Amendment which I desire to move
occurs in Clause 1, page 4, line 8, after "State"
("a Secretary of State shall be") to insert "if
they occur in relation to such functions."
This Amendment is introduced to meet a
possible difficulty which, at the same time, in
the opinion of the Government, would be most
unlikely to arise.
It is desired to insert the words of this
Amendment in order, to make it clear that
the Clause does not refer to functions of the
Secretary of State in relation to an "excepted"
or "reserved" service. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Moved: That the Clause, as amended, stand
part of the Bill.
Mr. GREER: I desire to thank the noble
Lord for postponing the Committee stage of
this Bill until to-day in order to give Members
an opportunity of going into the various provisions of the Bill. I feel specially interested
in this Bill because I have had personal
experience of the difficulty of carrying
through a private Bill. I have now gone
through the Clause and I am satisfied that
it will clear up these difficulties admirably
But I should like to say a word about subsection (b). The Government of Ireland Act
contains a general Clause applying English
Acts in regard to private Bill procedure in
Northern Ireland with certain modifications.
and one of the provisions in the English Act
was that a certificate had to be given by a
certain officer of the Imperial House of Parliament. We have no such officer in this
Parliament although we have another officer
who does similar work; and I had serious
misgivings as to whether I would get back a
deposit of no less a sum than £25,000. I
fortunately did get it back, but I had considerable uneasiness in the matter. I am very
glad, therefore, that this has been made
quite clear by sub-section (b) and the Bill in
my opinion admirably clears up all the
difficulties.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
CLAUSES 2 (Saving for proceedings taken
before commencement of the Act), and 3
(Short title) ordered to stand part of the Bill
Schedule agreed to.
Title and preamble agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported with
amendment to the House,
Ordered That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with amendment to the House.

105 Local Government Bill-

13 MAY 1924
