Local Government (Financial
Provisions) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
desire to move the Second Reading of the
Local Government (Financial Provisions)
Bill.
This Bill, as hon. Members will see, deals
with Local Government finance.
a
permanent and
Act
non-reby
The first Clause provides that loans which
are made by a County Council for permanent
work on public roads shall not be reckoned
as part of the total debt of the County Council for the purposes of Sub-section (2) of
Article 22 of the Schedule to the Local Government (Application of Enactments) Order,
1898, which, as hon. Members are aware.
limits the borrowing powers of a County
Council to one-tenth of its rateable value.
In
of the Roads
consequence
of last session, considerable expenditure of
current character has to be faced
the county councils, expenditure which will
ultimately result in a considerable saving
annually upon road repairs. Modern means
of locomotion and of traction necessitate
modernized methods of road construction, and
these roads, which were originally made for
horse traffic, now require reconstruction in
order that they may become fitted to bear the
stress which is occasioned by modern transport. Properly reconstructed roads will mean
far lighter charges for their annual maintenance. Though the expenditure may be
heavy now, it will certainly mean a repayment later on.
I may mention that the proposed exemption
of loans for roads from the Order under the
Local Government Act of 1898 involves no
new principle, because the Criminal Injuries
Act of 1920 already provides for a like exemption for loans to meet criminal injuries.
Clause 2 provides for the amendment of
Section 10 of the Local Government Act,
which we passed last session. That Section
provided that grants to local authorities
would be paid upon a basis of the average of
the payments made in three specified years.
In the course of administration it has been
found that this operates somewhat unfairly
upon the interests of local authorities, as the
years 1918, 1919, and 1920 were not normal
years, seeing that owing to a variety of circumstances, which it is not necessary for me
to recapitulate, many payments due to local
authorities during these years were not paid
to them within the financial years during
which they were properly payable.
The effect of the amendment we propose
will be to allow all amounts payable to a
County Council in any of these particular
years, even though not paid within that year,
to be accounted for the purpose of arriving at
the average amount of grant.
Sub-section 2 of this Clause refers to grants
to county councils or urban district councils
in respect of railway and harbour guarantees.
These grants involve a very small amount,
and it has been found that it would be inequitable to apply the basis of the three years'
average in these cases.
We propose, therefore, to return to the Act
of 1898, under which the Government contracted to pay one-half of the excess over the
produce of a sixpenny rate, with this slight
difference, that we pay nine-twentieths instead of one-half of the excess, which is onetwentieth less. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: It seems to me impossible to
pass the Second Reading of this Bill without
expressing in a word the gratification of the
County Councils, who are so much interested,
and who have been the target for the last few
years for a very great deal of abuse on the
subject both of the bad condition of the
county roads, and secondly on account of the
extreme cost of keeping up these roads in a
very faulty condition. Most of our County
Councils have been making a
very definite
effort to grapple with the problem, and most
of them, I think, have felt that their efforts
to be successful require rather more financial
powers than they possess. This Bill renders
that possible which would have been impracticable without it. I do not know whether
the House generally has considered the cost
of bringing the roads into a condition in
which they may hope to stand the strain of
modern traffic. But from certain inquiries
made in Antrim County Council I am able
to say that the cost is likely to be between
£11,000 and £12,000 a mile. It is hoped that
even that tremendous expenditure will result
in the future in a net annual saving to which
the noble Marquess alluded. We hope that
in the strip of road I have particularly in
mind the newly constructed road will be such
as to compensate the ratepayers for the heavy
initial capital outlay. But the County
Antrim could not have undertaken such a
scheme without the assistance which will be
afforded by this Bill.
Char-a-Banc Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be now
read a Second time) agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now committed.
-(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Char-a-Banc Regulation Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
THE MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
desire to move the Second Reading of the
Char-a-banc Regulation Bill. As hon. Members are
aware, this Bill is based upon
certain recommendations made to my right
hon. Friend the Minister of Home Affairs by
the Roads Advisory Committee for Northern
Ireland
That Committee recommended that Urban
District Councils and Town Commissioners
should be empowered to make by-laws determining the places where char-a-bancs and
similar vehicles shall stand for hire; also the
number of vehicles which shall be permitted
to stand on each such place; and, in addition,
the parking positions for such vehicles while
waiting to take up their positions on these
assigned stands.
Secondly, the Committee recommended that
these local authorities should be the licensing authority for all such vehicles standing
or plying for hire within their areas, the
right of appeal to the Ministry of Home
Affairs being reserved against the refusal of a
local authority to grant a licence. Each local
authority should be required to fix one day
for the issue of these licences in each of the
three months, April, May, and June.
Finally, the Committee considered that
local authorities should only have jurisdiction in regard to fares where a journey lay
entirely within the area under their charge.
That. briefly, will be found to be the effect
of the provisions of this Bill. I feel sure
hon. Members will not require that 1
should enter fully into the details of the
various clauses, because I think hon Members will find the Bill quite simple and
straightforward. I shall be glad, however,
to answer any questions as to the interpretation of any clause in which hon. Members
may find difficulties.
I think the House will agree with me that
while every encouragement should be given
to those enterprising individuals and associations who are doing so much to facilitate intercommunication between the towns and
villages of the Province, a measure of control
of the nature contained in this Bill has become absolutely necessary. These vehicles
are certainly a convenience to the public
generally, and we anticipate that nothing but
good can arise from legislation which extends
the accepted rules governing public vehicles
to cover the activities of this new form of
motor transport. I beg to move.
Mr. DICKSON: I would just like to draw the
attention of the House to the fact that in this
Bill there is no regulation for a mirror at the
driver's seat to enable him to see following
traffic. On our narrow roads the big conveyances in which the driver cannot see over the
top, and which are confined to a limited
speed of twelve miles an hour, make it impossible for an ordinary car to get past, and
the people in that car have to swallow the
dust all the way. I would ask the Leader of
this House to consider when the Bill arrives
in Committee whether a Clause should be
added compelling such vehicles to carry
mirrors on the right hand side of the driver
so that he can see following traffic.
Another matter that I see is in paragraph
4 about the number of passengers. It definitely states the number of seats, but I
would hope that the law would be strong
enough to compel owners of these carriages
not to exceed the seating capacity as is done
at present.
Mr. BARBOUR: I am in thorough sympathy with the general tenor of the Bill, but
I do see a point which, I think, is capable of
improvement. It is in Clause 2, where it is
provided that the local authority shall fix one
day in each of the months of April, May, and
June. It seems to me that it is likely that
these char-a-bancs may be bought and put
into use in other months of the year besides
these three months. One of the duties of the
County Council is to deal with people who
have failed to observe the requirements of the
law in regard to licences. It is a very laborious job that we have on occasions. It really
racks one's feelings to hear the hardships
that the law inflicts. At other times we wish
the law gave us larger powers to deal with
those who have failed to comply with the law
and sometimes have attempted to flout it. It
seems to me that confining the issue of
licences to three days is really making a law
that carries with it a very great likelihood of
the breaking of the law. I do think it would
be an improvement of the Bill if more days
could be allocated to the granting of licences.
From my own experience--I have been away
from home until a month or two ago--since I
have been away I have seen several new cars
that certainly have come on the road outside
the three months mentioned in the Bill.
Mr. LONG: The Roads Advisory Committee on whose recommendations this Bill is
based, received representations and deputations from the char-a-banc owners, also from
13 MAY 1924
Char-a-Bane Bill-

Second Reading.
order to catch the tourist traffic for that
season it will be practically necessary
that the appeals should be decided
off-hand. I think there should be words
put
into the Section that such appeal
shall be heard and determined forthwith, so that the Ministry will take it up at
once and decide in order that the man may
know whether he can carry on the traffic or
the Urban authorities, and the interests of
these two parties are not always alike. There
was very often a conflict between the char-abanc owners and the Urban authorities as to
the parking of the cars, control of the cars,
and the licensing of cars. The Roads Advisory Committee had interviews with representatives of the char-a-banc owners and
Urban authorities throughout Ulster, and the
Bill satisfies the Urban authorities and satisfies the char-a-banc owners. At least the representatives expressed themselves satisfied
at our meeting.
Colonel
SHARMAN-CRAWFORD : The
question of mirrors has been referred to. As
the noble Leader of the House knows, I am
Chairman of the Advisory Committee. A
meeting has been summoned which will take
place in a few days, and which will consider
that question of mirrors, not only upon
heavy vehicles but on many other vehicles
where it will be necessary. There is one Section in the Bill about which I am not quite
clear. It is on page 3, Clause 3, paragraph
(d). It says "for preventing within the prescribed distance the overcrowding of any
char-a-banc." Does that mean distance in
the prescribed area-in the area which affects
char-a-banc proprietors or Borough Councils?
Because I hope that full power is given for
watching the overcrowding of char-a-bancs. In
the summer time there is hardly a char-abanc-at least on our County Down roadsthat does not carry more than the prescribed
number they are allowed to carry, thereby
not only making it inconvenient and uncomfortable for the passengers, but defrauding the
County Council of a certain number of fees.
I hope full power will be given in that Section to prevent overcrowding whether it is in
the area of the municipality or in the whole
area.
Mr. GREER: I think it is absolutely
necessary to have a Bill of this
character passed, because this is a
large and increasing traffic, to some
extent of a novel kind, and anyone who
has been in any of our watering-places in the
summer time on a special excursion day will
recognize the absolute necessity of having
some regulation of this char-a-bane traffic.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I heartily approve of the provisions of the Bill, but there
are one or two little points that I must refer
to in Committee, and I should like to mention
them now in order to have them considered.
The first one arises in Clause 2, Sub-section 2,
providing for the right of appeal to the
Ministry of Home Affairs for Northern Ireland, where a licence has been refused. Now,
according to the Bill these licences can only
be granted in April, May, and June, and in
not.
Another point arises on Section 3, providing that the local authority, that is, the
local council may make by-laws for regulating the traffic. These by-laws of course
will have to be approved by the Ministry of
Home Affairs, and I think it is essential in
the interests of the char-a-banc owners that
these by-laws if possible, should be uniform
in all the various little towns, because if a
char-a-banc is going to travel from Belfast to
Portrush-which it may be doing very frequently going by the coast road and returning by the inland road-it would have to pass
through Carrickfergus and Ballycastle on the
coast road and through Ballymoney, Ballymena, and Antrim on the inland road. Now
unless these by-laws are uniform the driver
would have to get the by-laws of these five
towns, consider the large number of differences between them, and know what
different methods he is to adopt in passing
through each little town. I think it would
be a simpler thing to have modern by-laws
and have them adopted and applied to all the
towns.
I have another objection to the Clause,
but this is of a different kind. I dislike very much legislation by referring
to another Act of Parliament that has
been already passed. I think if we
particular
can
deal with it in the
Act it should be done. Now it defines “prescribed distance" thus: The expression "prescribed distance" has the same meaning as
the expression "such prescribed distance" in
the principal Act. How is the ordinary layman to find out what it means? He must
first find out what principal Act is meant.
Then he must get it and refer to it, and he
will find what distance is meant. It would
do perfectly to say the expression "prescribed
distance" shall mean a distance extending
four miles from the post office of the town
included in such urban district. I think it
would be much better than legislation by reference to another Act passed.
I do not know whether it would be possible
to have the regulations that the Ministry of
Home Affairs has power to make under the
Locomotives on Highways Act, and the Motor
Cars Act put on the Table of the House before
they become operative. If there is any means
by which that can be done I think it would
Char-a-Bane Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Greer].
be a very desirable thing. I intend to look
into that before the Committee Stage.
The question of mirrors that has been
raised brings a matter up to my mind, because it will be dealt with in all probability
by regulation of the Ministry rather than
by regulation of the local authority. Now
I thought like my hon. Friends that mirrors
would be a proper thing, in order to ensure
that the driver of the char-a-banc would see
the traffic behind him, but I have been
assured by the proprietor of a number of
these char-a-bancs that they would be little
or no use. He told me his experience was
this. He started by putting mirrors on and
he found that they were no use. He took
them off and is not using them now. He told
me that he is making experiments with a
sound-box which he places at the back of
the car. That receives the sound of the horn
that is being blown behind. The sound in
the sound-box is taken to the driver, either
by electric wire or by tube or something of
the kind.
Mr. DICKSON: Give him a wireless set.
Mr. GREER: He says that is very much
more effective, and he thinks that before long
a very valuable improvement can be made by
which the driver of a char-a-banc or motor
lorry will always know when a horn is being
blown behind him.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: The
discussion of this Bill has been of a very interesting character, but I do feel that the
majority of the points that have been raised
are suitable to the Committee Stage rather
than to the Second Reading of the Bill. J
should like to express my gratitude to hon
Members for having given some indication of
the amendments which I have no doubt they
will put down for the Committee Stage, and
which I need hardly say I shall be very
happy to go into so as to pass from this
House a satisfactory Bill. My two hon.
Friends (Mr. Dickson and Mr. Greer) have
mentioned the question of looking glasses.
It appears there is some divergence of
opinion as to the efficacy of looking-glasses
or other methods of discovering following
cars desiring to pass. I would say that the
Home Office already has ample powers to
deal with these matters, under Section 6 of
the Locomotives on Highways Act, 1896, consequently there would be no need, even
if the House had decided that a lookingglass was the method that should be
employed, to add it as a Clause to
the Bill, because if it is found necessary
or if a sounding-box which my hon. Friend
referred to is necessary, all these can be
ordered to be attached to char-a-banes by
regulation under the Locomotives on Highways Act of 1896.
My hon. Friend, Senator Milne Barbour,
referred to the days on which licences can
be taken out-one day in each of three
months. I think also that, if he reads further
into the Clause, the final words in that Clause
satisfy the suggestion that he is making to the
House that at their discretion they may fix
additional days for the purpose. I hardly
think he need have any apprehension that
there will be any hide-bound desire on the
part of the authorities, if advantage is not
taken of the statutory days laid down, that
on that account the owners will not be able
to have their needs attended to by the local
authorities.
This is a Bill which I might almost say is
breaking new ground, and even when it has
been passed into law we shall find difficulties
which will probably have to be overcome, perhaps in an amending Act. I think we can
take a certain amount of assurance that there
are regulations which can be enforced by the
Home Office, and which will cover a great
many of the difficulties and objections that.
are uppermost in the minds of hon. Members
at the present moment.
as
My hon. and gallant Friend, Colonel
Sharman- Crawford, has referred to overcrowding of char-a-bancs at the present
moment. The statutory area controlled by
urban authorities or town commissioners is
four miles from the head post office of the
urban district or town, and this probably
touches the point which was made by
the hon. Gentleman, Senator Greer,
to the desirability of uniformity of
by-laws. This is a matter which 1
will give these hon. Gentlemen an undertaking
that I will consider very carefully between
now and the Committee Stage. If they put
down amendments on these particular points
I shall certainly be very happy to consider
them. I shall probably be able to put down
amendments on my own account which will
satisfy the points which hon. Members have
raised on Second reading.
I do not think there is any other point
raised in this debate to which I can profitably
give an answer at the present moment. I
have ventured to say that the points are more
Committee points than Second reading points.
When we reach that stage I have every reason
to hope that we can return this Bill to the
House of Commons in an amended form which
will constitute an improvement of the Bill.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be now
read a Second time) agreed to.
Bill read a Second time accordingly and
committed to a Committee of the whole House,
the House to be put into Committee on
Tuesday next.
113 Humane Legislation.

13 MAY 1924
Humane Legislation.
Humane Legislation.
The following questions stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Greer :-
To ask the Leader of the House, whether,
for the purpose of preserving uniformity regarding humane legislation as far as reasonably possible between the Parliament of
Northern Ireland and the Imperial Parliament, the Government of Northern Ireland
will consider the desirability of introducing
Bills similar to the Importation of Plumage
(Prohibition) Act, 1921, the Captive Birds
Shooting (Prohibition) Act, 1921, and the
Protection of Animals Act (1911) Amendment
Act, 1921, passed by the Imperial Parliament.
To ask the Leader of the House whether
Bills were recently introduced in the Imperial Parliament (a) for the protection of
Wild Birds; (b) for the protection of Animals,
etc., and (c) the humane slaughtering of Animals, all of which have passed the Second
Reading.
Whether a deputation of the U.S.P.C.A.
waited upon the Prime Minister and other
members of the Government of Northern
Ireland on the 8th November, 1923, to urge
the Government to undertake legislation on
similar lines, and were assured that this
would be done, although for the purpose of
preserving uniformity in legislation Bills
would probably not be brought forward until
after the Imperial Bills had passed into law.
Whether, owing to circumstances which
have since arisen, and were not then foreseen,
the passage of the Bills has been indefinitely
delayed.
And whether the Government, in these
circumstances, is now prepared to introduce
similar Bills at an early date and have them
carried through with reasonable despatch,
or if such Bills are brought forward by a
private Member the Government will give
facilities for taking the opinion of Parliament
thereon.
Mr. GREER: The question which stands in
my name on the paper is a long one, but I
shall undertake not to occupy more than a
very few minutes in introducing it to the
House, as it practically explains itself. I
put this question down at the urgent request
of the Ulster Society for the Prevention of
Cruelty to Animals, and a request from a
Society of that kind deserves to be treated
with respect. When I mention that the present Patron of the Association is His Grace
the Governor of Northern Ireland, and the
President is my Lord the Speaker, I think
that will be sufficient evidence of the respectability and importance of the Association.
That Asociation operates through the whole
area of Northern Ireland, and has
some
twenty-six branches. It has been in existence
since 1836, and has done an immense amount
of useful work in the direction of preventing
cruelty. It has been assisting the police in
prosecutions, has supplied veterinary surgeons to the police when necessary, and,
really, the work that it has done in connection with humanitarianism during all those
years has been splendid. Now the question
itself is two-fold. It is in reference first to
certain humanitarian Acts that have been
passed into law by the Imperial Parliament,
and I want to know if our Government is
prepared to pass similar Acts for Northern
Ireland. These Acts do not affect Northern
Ireland at present, and the Society will be
very anxious if possible to have similar Acts
passed in this area.
The other part of the question is slightly
different. It refers to Bills that have been
brought forward-
Mr. SPEAKER: I think the hon. Member
will see that is a separate question altogether.
Mr. GREER: I beg your pardon. I need
not deal with the second part of the question,
then, until we come to it. I think I have
said all I want to say about the first partwhether the Acts of Parliament that have
been passed by the Imperial Parliament will
be brought forward and applied to Northern
Ireland.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
With reference to the question the hon.
Member has raised, he is aware that the
Importation of Plumage Prohibition Act of
1921 applies to Northern Ireland, and that
the Parliament of Northern Ireland has no
power to legislate in respect of matters which
are dealt with by this Act. The hon. Member knows that we come under the jurisdiction of the United Kingdom on the question
of importations, and that, therefore, this is
not a matter which the Parliament here can
deal with. As regards the other Acts which
are referred to, the question of introducing
similar legislation here has been very carefully considered, and it was thought desirable
that we should wait to see what progress was
made with the Bill dealing with the protection of birds and animals now before the
Imperial Parliament, so that our legislation
should be on parallel lines with the legislation which would be passed on the other side.
Should these Bills pass I can hold out a hope
to hon. Members that it may be possible to
deal with the whole set at the same time
here.
Mr. GREER: That is very satisfactory.
Now, I will deal with the second questionthat is, about these Bills that have been
introduced into the Imperial Parliament.
They have been before the Imperial Parliament now for more than a year. They are
115 Humane Legislation.
The Boundary Conference. 116
THE SENATE
[Mr. Greer
very desirable Bills. I have no intention to
go into them at this stage. It would be an
improper thing to do, but the sponsor for the
first Bill, the Protection of Wild Birds, is a
very good sportsman, himself-Viscount Grey
-and there has been a long discussion in
Committee in the Imperial Parliament over
this Bill. It has passed the Second Reading,
and I do not really know why it has been
hung up. I think it might have been passed
long ago if the Government had been serious
about it. Of course there have been great
changes in the Imperial Parliament since
the Second Reading of the Bill, and the
Association that I refer to are afraid that
these Bills possibly may now be overlooked
and may not be proceeded with any further
in the Imperial Parliament. They were
aware that we were in complete sympathy
with them, and they would very much like
that our Government would bring forward
similar Bills and pass them here. They are
quite alive to the fact that it is desirable,
if possible, that legislation on these subjects
should be the same in Northern Ireland
as in England and Scotland. They have, 1
know, postponed bringing forward these Bills
until passed in the Imperial Parliament. It
would be open to them to bring forward
exactly similar Bills for Northern Ireland.
Having regard to the length of time
that has elapsed since these Bills
were brought forward the Association
would strongly urge the Government
to bring forward the same Bills in the
Northern Parliament and pass them into
law, and the Imperial Parliament will, no
doubt, be just as glad to enact similar legislation on the subject and will therefore adopt
the Acts as passed by our Parliament. I
hope the Government may see their way to
do so.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
With reference to the question which the
hon. Member has raised I can tell him for
his information that from the Parliamentary
list of Bills which was dated for the 5th May
and for the few days previous to the same
date it would appear that the Protection of
Wild Birds Bill was read a Second time on
the 5th and was referred to Committee. The
Protection of Animals Bill was down for
Second Reading on the 6th and was deferred.
The Humane Slaughtering of Animals Bill
was referred to a Committee last year, but
it does not appear to have been reintroduced
this year. I think that the hon. Member is
no doubt aware that the Prime Minister has
received the deputation to which he refers
in his question, and that he has carefully
considered the question of introducing similar
legislation for Northern Ireland in our own
Parliament. I can assure him that should
these Bills pass through the Imperial
Parliament the question of introducing similar legislation on this side would be considered at once. The Government is prepared to carry out this undertaking in so far
as these Bills may not extend to Northern
Ireland, but it is not considered desirable to
introduce legislation on these lines until we
can see what changes may be made in the
Imperial Bills in their passage through
the Imperial Parliament, because I think
hon. Members will agree that it is most important that whatever legislation of this
character is passed in the Imperial Parliament should be passed in this Parliament on
uniform lines.
I do not think that I can add with advantage anything else to this answer to the question which the hon. Member has raised. 1
think he knows quite well that there is a
great deal of sympathy in the direction in
which his question points. We most sincerely hope that this legislation will pass
through the Imperial Parliament when we
will have the opportunity here of debating
the same legislation or legislation of a
uniform character.
Mr. GREER rose.
Mr. SPEAKER: Order, order. The hon.
Member cannot address the House twice.
Adjournment.
THE BOUNDARY CONFERENCE.
Moved: That this House do now adjourn.
(The Marquess of Londonderry.
Mr. WOODS: As this is the first meeting
we have held of this House since the breakdown of those meetings that were called the
Boundary Conference, I think it would be
out of place to pass away without saying
something at least about that Conference.
I do not wish to promote a discussion, nor
do I wish if possible to draw anything from
the noble Marquess, the Leader of this
House, regarding what took place at these
Conferences, because personally I am fully
satisfied with the conduct of our Prime
Minister and his colleagues on that
particular occasion. I am satisfied that the
Boundary question is quite rightly and quite
well dealt with by the Prime Minister and
his colleagues. I rise to-day to congratulate
this House on having a Leader like the
noble Marquess who went over to attend
that Conference just a few days after meet-
(Hon.
ing with a very serious accident.
Members: Hear, hear.) I really think it
would be out of place to-day if we pass the
adjournment without in some way
complimenting him on his brave actionan action which showed not only to us but
117 The Boundary Conference.

13 MAY 1924
Adjournment.
to every loyal man and woman in Ulster his
sincerity on the Ulster question. I am here
to-day to compliment him very highly on
his action on that particular occasion.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to take the opportunity of thanking the
hon. Senator for the very kind words he has
used in regard to myself. With reference
to the point which he made about the
Boundary Conference I do not feel that I can
usefully add anything to the statement which
the Prime Minister has made on more than
one occasion. I should most certainly agree
that if there was any doubt in the minds
of the people of the Six Counties as to the
attitude of the Government we should leave
no stone unturned to remove that doubt, but
I do not feel that in the very explicit statement which the Prime Minister has made,
as I said, on more than one occasion I can
add anything usefully to the discussion. I
would only thank the hon. Member very
kindly for what he has said of me.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at a
Quarter after Four of the
Clock.
Local Government Bill.
Business of the House.
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 14th May, 1924.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate have
agreed to the said Bill without any
amendment.
The Senate met a Quarter after Three of
the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That they have passed the Ministers (Temporary Exercise of Powers) Bill, and the
Marketing of Eggs Bill, to which they desire
the concurrence of this House.
MINISTERS (TEMPORARY EXERCISE OF
POWERS) BILL.
Moved: That this Bill be read a First
time.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
MARKETING OF EGGS BILL.
Moved: That this Bill be read a First
time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Belfast Water Bill.
Moved: That the Senators following be
named of the Joint Unopposed Committee on
the said Bill:-
Mr. Hill Dickson.
Mr. Milne Barbour.
-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Local Government (Financial
Provisions) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Moved: That this Bill be read the Third
time. (The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved That this Bill do now pass.-(The
Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that this House do now adjourn. I should like to state on this Motion
that if it will meet the convenience of the
House it is proposed that we should take
the Second Reading to-morrow of the two
Bills which have just been received from
another place, namely, the Ministers
(Temporary Exercise of Powers) Bill, and the
Marketing of Eggs Bill. It may be said on
behalf of hon. Members that the time allowed
between the First and Second Reading of
these Bills is not very long, but I am inclined
to suggest that whereas it is most important
that there should elapse a certain amount
of time between the Second Reading and the
Committee Stage, so as to enable Members
to put down any Amendments they may desire, it is not so important that there should
be a long interval between the First Reading
of a Bill-which after all is the presentation
of the Bill to this House and the Second
Reading. I thought I would take this
opportunity of expressing my own views on
this particular point, and perhaps elicit
from hon. Members some opinions they may
have as to the length of time that should
elapse between the First and Second Reading.
I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: The Noble Marquess has
suggested that we should take to-morrow the
Second Reading of the two Bills which were
read a First time to-day. The first of those
two Bills is, if one may say so, explained by
its very title, and I think it is obvious that
there can be no object in deferring the
Second Reading of a Bill of that class. The
second Bill, if I have the order correctly, is
the Marketing of Eggs Bill, which is also
an ordinary Bill in one way. It is,
I believe, a Bill brought in in persuance of
the policy outlined in the Gracious Speech,
which was to give effect to certain recommendations of the Development Commission.
It is a Bill which we have all been more
or less expecting, and about which we
must have read a good deal, and it is
not a Bill which would require any great
consideration between the First and Second
Reading. If I may venture to express an
opinion, I am entirely in accord with what
the Noble Marquess has said as regards Bills
of that class, but there are-and I have one
in my mind at the moment-Bills regarding
which it would be important that a greater
length of time should be left between the
First and Second Readings.
Business of the House.
Adjournment.
14 MAY 1924
I do not think it would be desirable to
mention any particular Measure, but I may
perhaps take it upon me to express the
opinion that looking back over the past three
years I do not remember an occasion where
in the case of a Bill of what I should describe as Second-class--that is the class that
does require consideration between the First
and Second Readings-this House has been
invited to hurry itself between the First
and Second Readings, although I admit
there have been one or two exceptions when
urgency was pleaded. My own opinion is
that we may very safely leave these matters
in the hands of those responsible for ordering
the business of the House, reserving to ourselves the right to ask for more time in the
case of any Measure that appeals to one or
other of us as one requiring further consideration.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty-five Minutes after
Three of the Clock.
Private Business.
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 15th May, 1924.
THE SENATE
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Belfast Water Bill.
The following Report was made from the
Joint Committee on the said Bill, viz. :

That they had considered the said Bill
and examined the allegations thereof, which
were found to be true, and the Committee had
gone through the Bill and made some amendments thereto."
Ordered That the Report do lie on the
Table.
Larne and District Electricity
Special Order.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I beg to move :
That the Special Order made by the
Electricity Commissioners for Northern
Ireland, and confirmed by the Ministry of
Commerce under Section 26 of the Electricity (Supply) Act, 1919, in respect of the
Urban District of Larne, and portions of certain townlands within the Rural District of
Larne, all in the County of Antrim, be
approved.
In moving that the House do approve of
this Special Order, I may be allowed to say
that it is a Special Order granted by the
Electricity Commissioners and confirmed by
the Ministry of Commerce to one of the
oldest electricity undertakings in Northern
Ireland, giving them statutory powers to
supply electricity for public and private
purposes within the Urban District and
portion of the Townlands within the Rural
District of Larne. The effect of granting
those powers would be to impose upon the
Company the usual obligation to furnish a
proper supply and to assure consumers of the
due protection of their interests. It also
gives security against wasteful or unregulated competition. The rules of procedure have
been complied with, and I therefore ask the
House to approve of the Order.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Commissions of the Peace Bill.
Private Bill Procedure Bill.

Order for receiving Report of Amendments
read.
Moved: That the Report be now received.—
(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The said Amendments were, accordingly,
reported and agreed to by the House.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the Third
time on Tuesday next.
Commissions of the Peace Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The object of this Bill is the simplification of
the procedure in Northern Ireland for making
appointments to the Commission of the Peace.
At present every time new appointments
are made it is necessary to issue a fresh Commission under the Great Seal reciting the
names of all persons already included
in the Commission. As hon. Members
will recognize, this is a very expensive
and a very cumbrous method of procedure which was abandoned in England nearly fifty years ago, when the
Crown Office Act of 1877 very considerably
simplified the method of appointment. By
the Rules made under the Act of 1877 a single
Commission is issued for each County with
a Schedule to which the names of Justices
are added from time to time. We have
decided to adopt similar methods in Northern
Ireland.
Clause 1 of this Bill, therefore, applies
the procedure of these Rules to Northern
Ireland in so far as they relate to the Commissions of the Peace. There are various
Sub-clauses.
Sub-section (1) prescribes a new form of
Commission which is found in the Schedule.
Sub-section (2) provides for the issue of
the new Commission in supersession of existing Commissions, upon the passing of the
Bill.
Sub-section (3) deals with the periodic revision of the Schedule annexed to the
Commissions.
Sub-section (4) prescribes the keeping of a
central record in Northern Ireland of all
persons appointed to the Commission.
Sub-section (5) provides for the issue of
certificates in place of Commissions under
the Great Seal.
Marketing of Eggs Bill. 126
Ministers (Powers) Bill.
15 MAY 1924
The second Clause is consequential upon
the adoption of the first, and relates to the
making of orders in regard to the fees payable by individual Justices on appointment.
The Bill is a simple and straightforward
measure, and I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Tuesday next.
Ministers (Temporary Exercise of
Powers) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The effect of this Bill is to empower the
Government to meet an emergency where a
Minister is for any reason temporarily
unable to exercise the functions of his office.
Ministers have certain powers, the exercise
of which is confined strictly to themselves,
as holders of their office, and cannot be delegated by them to any other person. Hon.
Members will be aware of, and I have no
doubt, deplore as sincerely as we do,
the present emergency which has given rise
to this Measure. My right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Home Affairs has been seriously
ill. I am happy to say he is fortunately
now on the high road to recovery, but he is
still, and will be for some time, quite unable
to perform the duties allotted to his office. It
has, therefore, become necessary to provide
a remedy in the case of his office, and it
seems fitting that opportunity should be
taken to give powers to the Governor to meet
any recurrence of such an emergency in connection with any of the Ministries.
I may remind the House that there is a
precedent for the appointment of a substitute
of this nature. The Office of Attorney-
General Act of last Session provides for the
appointment of a deputy Attorney-General
for such period as the Attorney-General may
from time to time be unable, owing to sickness or other cause, to act in his capacity as
Attorney-General. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure we all join with
the noble Marquess in expressing our regret
at the particular circumstance which has
rendered it necessary to consider this Bill
at the present time. We were all deeply
grieved to hear that the Minister of Home
Affairs was suffering from a serious ilness
and we are greatly relieved to hear that he is,
in the words of the noble Marquess, on the
high road to recovery. The Bill itself is in
one way perhaps a little interesting, as drawing attention to the differences that exist
between our Executive administration and
that in Great Britain, because in Great
Britain they have Secretaries of State, and
one of those Secretaries of State is for all
practical purposes as good as another. Here
in Northern Ireland, as the noble Marquess
pointed out, we have Ministers whose activities are more circumscribed. I do not
imagine that any question will arise on the
Bill itself. The only thing that could
possibly arise is the purely technical question
as to whether it goes exactly the required
distance, and as to that the Government will
no doubt acquaint us at a later stage if any
alteration should be desired. It is, I imagine,
a Bill that we will require to pass at an early
date-next week-and I cannot think that
we shall find any difficulty in doing so.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Marketing of Eggs Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I desire to move the Second Reading of this
Measure. It has not been in the hands of
hon. Members for a very long period, but it
passed through the other House with
debates which, I am sure, are still
fresh in the memories of hon. Members.
The rehabilitation of the agricultural
industry has, as we all know, been a pressing
question since the War. The United Kingdom is, of course, very largely dependent
on foreign countries and the colonies for food
supplies, and when importation was interfered with during hostilities the agricultural
output at home was strained to its fullest
limit. With the return to normal trade
conditions the demand was suddenly lowered with what I may call disastrous results.
Other industries were, of course, similarly
hit, but in agriculture it was perhaps more
obvious, since it is really our biggest -
dustry. It is estimated that in Northern
Ireland there are 212,000 people engaged in
agriculture, as against 206,000 in other
industries, a figure which includes 68,000
engaged in linen work and 35,000 in shipbuilding. The computed output from agriculture is £30,000,000; linen £17,000,000 and
shipbuilding £9,000,000.
The farming community has had the full
sympathy of the Government during these
trying times. In Northern Ireland some relief
was afforded by the special grant of £128,000.
Under the chairmanship of Lord Pirrie, a
Member of this House, a Special Committee
investigated the question of how best these
funds could be used to remedy the depression,
and after detailed analysis of the situation
it was recommended that distribution should
be by way of an abatement of rates. As a
result of the facts which were elicited by this
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Marquess of Londonderry]
Committee, the Government took a further
step to help the industry by certain special
development schemes. For this purpose a
sum of £54,000 was made available for such
official activities as the encouragement of
milk recording, the promotion of agricultural
education, the improvement of pastures and
the development of certain important aspects
of the poultry industry. I have mentioned
these facts to show that the interests of the
farming community have had the serious,
continuous, and sympathetic consideration
of the Government. But this is not all. The
very interesting and exhaustive examination
of the Ulster Egg Industry contained in the
recent valuable Report of the Commission
which sat under the chairmanship of Lord
Charlemont, demonstrates the steps which
are necessary for the full development of
that valuable industry. In the present
Bill are embodied the main legislative proposals of that report.
I believe very few people have even an
approximate idea of the value of the egg
industry in Northern Ireland. Not only is
Ulster self-supporting, but from Northern
ports eggs to the value of some three million
pounds are despatched annually to Great
Britain. Such, however, are the possibilities
of the industry that there is no reason whatever why this valuable source of income
should not be substantially increased. We
have many natural advantages in the Six
Counties. The climate and soil are particularly suitable, the Irish egg in point
of quality excels any supplied to the
British market by other countries, and
the farming system in Northern Ireland is
just of that type which permits the maintenance of large numbers of birds. Northern
Ireland is a country of small holdings, mixed
farming being the rule, so that poultry fit
in as a natural, and granted a little attention, a profitable adjunct to the other
farming operations. Every effort has been
made by the Ministry of Agriculture to develop
the industry on practical and scientific
lines. Schemes of instruction in poultrykeeping are in operation in each county,
and instructors give their whole time to conducting classes, and to visiting and advising
farmers in regard to the management,
housing, feeding and selection of birds.
Premiums are also provided by the County
Committees of Agriculture for egg distribution stations, with a view to grading up the
egg-yielding qualities of poultry kept in the
country.
The first egg-laying competition conducted
by the Ministry was "over-subscribed,"
many would-be competitors having been refused admission on the grounds of lack of
accommodation. The results of that competition show that there are in Northern
Ireland as good laying strains as in any
other country-in fact a world's record in
egg production was established by one particular bird. At present the average yield is
estimated at about 100 or 110 per bird, and
when regard is had to the record at these
competitions, in which from 1913 to 1923 the
number of eggs per bird rose from 120 to
189, it should not be impossible to raise the
average to 150. Granted a keener appreciation of the possibilities of poultry keeping
a considerable increase of the poultry kept
as well as in the number of eggs per bird
is a reasonable ambition. This dual advance
in numbers and yield could easily mean an
addition to the agricultural wealth of Ulster
of a million pounds per annum.
A real incentive to this end lies in the
proximity of the British market, which is
capable of absorbing as many consignments
as Ulster is able to send. To maintain and
develop this market and to popularize our
eggs, better marketing will have to be introduced. As part of its normal functions the
Ministry of Agriculture devotes very considerable attention to giving advice and instruction not only in regard to production,
but also respecting the preparation of the
consignments which are destined for the
markets. The Bill may be said to break
new ground so far as United Kingdom legislation is concerned, but it has been
designed specially to meet Ulster conditions.
It is keenly appreciated that it is dependent quite as much on efficient and
sympathetic administration, as on the
nature of the legislative provisions which
are contained in the Bill. The Ministry's
object will be the minimum of interference
with the normal flow of trade consistent
with placing the Ulster egg in a more
honoured position in the markets. I beg to
move.
Mr. BARBOUR: I am in thorough
sympathy with the Bill, and I am very glad
that our Minister of Agriculture has taken
the matter in hand and has pointed out to
us a way of bettering this very important
industry. I should like to say that I feel
sure every Member of this House would like
to extend to the Minister of Agriculture a
very full measure of sympathy in the unfortunate loss he has sustained. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear) I am glad that the
Ministry of Agriculture, in taking this step,
was fortified by the Report of the Charlemont Commission. I think it is always well
that as much public feeling as possible
should be elicited about any topics of legislation before the legislation itself is intro-
Marketing of Eggs Bill -
Second Reading.
15 MAY 1924
duced. Those of us who have had any experience of seeing how Irish produce compares
with competing continental produce on the
English Market will need no urging to
support this Bill in every way they can. I
do not think that Ulstermen would feel
flattered to be told that on the basis of
civilisation they were the equals of the
Russians, but I doubt whether, judging by
the products they put on the British
market as far as eggs are concerned, they
can claim to be the equal of the Russians.
I have seen Russian eggs come into the
British Market in bulk, of uniform quality,
and at regular intervals, and properly fulfilling the demands of the big wholesale
trade on the other side. Although the egg.
have probably travelled thousands of miles
in Russia and have had a long sea
journey, they are delivered in the London
market clean and in good order, and when
the contents are laid open to view they
certainly furnish an example that we could
afford to follow with
great deal of
advantage to ourselves.
a
It is not only Russian produce that we are
up against, but Danish and Dutch and
Colonial produce, and here, with a very
great measure of natural protection in the
British market, namely our proximity to it,
we are content to send in an article which
is superior to all others and damning it at
the point of sale by the careless way it is
put on the market. I am delighted to think
that the Ministry have taken their
courage in their hands and introduced
this legislation, which will certainly assist
in bringing our produce to the measure of
popularity and confidence it ought to enjoy.
I think we can copy every competitor we
have with advantage, and from what I have
seen myself I would say without hesitation
that Irish produce is put on the London
market in
condition than the
produce of any other country. I am
also glad to think that after the
issue of this Bill the Journal of the
Farmers' Union gave the Measure its blessing. I think it recognized the necessity
for some change, and I am glad to think
that that important body has welcomed the
Bill as a good one. I can speak for
another organization in which for a good
many years now we have been trying to
persuade the farmers to follow the example
of competitors, not with the measure of
success we would have liked to see. Still,
we have been trying to spread the light,
and I think that by the work we have done
we have made the passage of this Bill and
worse
a
its loyal acceptance in the country a little
Lit more easy than otherwise it would have
been.
own
I congratulate the Leader of the House on
reminding us manufacturers that there are
other industries in the Six Counties besides
the manufacturing industries. It is well to
realize that the prosperity of this area of
ours does not depend altogether on the
manufactories in the towns, and as a
manufacturer myself I am glad of that,
and I am glad to think that our
Government is giving this particular
attention to our most important industry.
I could only wish that the same measure of
attention could be given always and in all
countries, because I regard a civilisation
which is trying to exist without a
prosperous people living on the countryside
as being on a somewhat dangerous foundation. As a manufacturer I do not need to
be told that it is desirable to put a good
product in decent containers, and. I would
like to pass on the lesson I have learned in
business, and that is that no matter how
good the stuff may be that they produce, unless they conform generously to the demands of the market they will never get
just that last ounce of profit which makes
all the difference between prosperity and a
mere living. It is done by looking after
the details, and this Bill is a serious
attempt to cope with one of the details and
to respond to the demand of the market
that goods shall be placed on it in
attractive form. I do not think we need
attempt to say anything of the person who
willingly holds up eggs in order that he may
get a better price. We all know that in a
rising market there is that temptation, but
we hope that in a short time people who
adopt that practice will be tripped up. We
also hope that with a little bit more education our farmers will be able to see far
enough ahead to realize it is a foolish policy
to attempt to gain a halfpenny to-day and
jeopardize their whole market in the future.
It gives me great pleasure to support this
Bill in every way.
an
Mr. LESLIE: Like my hon. Friend (Mr.
Barbour) 1 welcome the Bill, and I only rise
to draw the attention to a small point which
has been omitted from the Bill, although I
rather think the Minister of Agriculture may
prefer to deal with it by Rule. I notice that
in an earlier section of the Bill proper
penalties are provided for people who deal
with a wholesale dealer who is not properly
licensed, but there is nothing to enable the
honest and well-meaning person to ascertain
that the person he is about to deal with is
E
Rent Restriction Bill.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
a
[Mr. Leslie]
properly licensed dealer. Therefore,
although I do not think it need go in the
Bill, steps ought to be taken whereby a man's
licensed premises should have a proper
board erected, and especially when he is
doing collecting in the country his cart
should be properly labelled. I think that
could be done by Rule and that there is no
necessity to insert a Clause in the Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Rent Restrictions Bill.
MOTION FOR ADJOURNMENT.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that this House do now adjou.
Mr. WOODS: In the Gracious Speecn
delivered at the opening of this Session one
of the legislative measures mentioned was a
Rent Restrictions Bill. I do not know how
far the Government have gone in the drafting of that Bill, but I would ask the noble
Marquess, the Leader of the House, to use
his influence with the Government to have
a Clause inserted in the Bill for the protec
tion of small shopkeepers. Some of them
have suffered very severely owing to high
increases in their rent. Some of the rents
have increased over 100 per cent., and one
of the things that strike me in this respect
is that there has been no increase in the
valuation of these premises, and, therefore,
I understand that the landlord has
pay any increased income tax, although he
has increased the rent of a small shop over
100 per cent. I think something should be
done for those small shopholders. They have
no redress because they cannot leave their
premises and establish a shop somewhere
else. If they did they would be in the same
boat. I hope the noble Marquess will use
his influence to have a Clause inserted in the
Rent Restrictions Bill for this purpose.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The hon. Member has given me no notice
that he was going to raise this question and
therefore I know he will not expect any
specific answer now. I can, however, assure
him that any suggestion he makes will be
most carefully considered.
Question put, and agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Ten
Minutes before Four of the
Clock, till Tuesday next.

Char--a-Banc Regulation Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill (The
Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to
In Committee accordingly,
(The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND AVA
in the Chair.)
CLAUSES 1 (Application of certain provisions of 17 and 18 Vict. c. 103 to char-a-bancs.)
2 (Licensing days, appeals and offences), and
3 (By-laws) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Application of s. 11 of 14 and 15
Vic., c. 92 as to overcrowding of carriages.)
on
Every char-a-banc, and every omnibus as
defined in sub-section (3) of section fourteen
of the Roads Act, 1920, shall, when used
any public road or street (whether within
the district of a local authority or not), be
deemed to be a public stage carriage for the
purposes of section eleven of the Summary
Jurisdiction (Ireland) Act, 1851; and, accordingly, references in the last-mentioned section
to the number of passengers for whom seats
shall be provided in or on any public stage
carriage, or which any such carriage shall be
intended to carry, shall be construed as references to the number of persons to be carried by a char-a-banc or omnibus as regulated by the by-laws of any local authority
under section eighty-eight ot the principal Act
as extended by this Act, or by any other
enactment or by-law.
The following Amendment stood on the
Paper in the name of Mr. GREER:
Clause 4, page 4, line 23, at end add" Provided, however, that Sub-Section (1) of Section eleven of the Summary Jurisdiction
(Ireland) Act, 1851 ,shall not apply to motor
omnibuses or char-a-bancs running a regular service to a time table."
Mr. GREER: I beg, by leave of the House,
to withdraw the first Amendment standing in
my name, at the end of Clause 4, page, 4, line
23.
Amendment not moved.
Clause 4 ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Char-a-Banc Bill-

CLAUSE 5 (saving for local Acts) ordered

CLAUSE 6 (construction and short title).
(1)-In this Act-
The expression "local authority" means
the council of any urban district or the commissioners of any town, but does not include
the council of a county borough:
The expression
"town":

district" includes
has

the same meaning as the expression "such

Act.
(2) This Act shall (save as otherwise expressly provided therein) apply to every district to which the principal Act applies.

(3) This Act, so far as it amends the principal Act, shall be construed as one with the
principal Act, and the expression this Act"
in the principal Act shall be construed to
mean the principal Act as amended by this
Act.
(4) References in this Act to enactments of
the Parliament of the United Kingdom shall
be construed as references to those enactments
as they apply in Northern Ireland.
(5) This Act may be cited as the Char-abanc Regulation Act (Northern Ireland), 1923.
وو
Mr. GREER: I beg to move in Clause 6,
Sub-clause 1, page 4, line 37 to leave out from
"distance ("prescribed distance" has) to
the end of line 39 ("principal Act"), and
insert means a distance extending four
miles from the principal post office of the
town included in such urban district or under
such commissioners." The reason I bring
forward this amendment is that I object very
strongly to Bills being drafted by reference
to other Bills. I think that where it is at all
possible the entire law should be contained
in the Bill brought before the House. I look
upon this particular Clause as a very bad
instance of what I object to. The framers
of the Bill want to define what the words
prescribed distance," which have been used
several times in the Bill, mean, which would
have been quite easy to do. They say the
expression "prescribed distance" has the
same meaning as the expression "such prescribed distance" has in the principal Act.
That really gives no information whatever to
any person reading this Bill. He cannot tell
what the prescribed distance is until he provides himself with a copy of the other Act
and reads it. Even then he may find difficulty
in understanding what is meant by it. I submit that the words I have put in my amendment are quite clear and distinct, and would
be much better than defining it by reference.
The Clause would then read:
The expression "prescribed distance'
means distance extending four miles from
the principal post office of the town included
in such urban district or under such
commissioners,

There can be no doubt whatever in the mind
of anybody, lawyer or layman, as to what the
meaning of prescribed distance" is if
these words are inserted. I have been speaking to the framers of the Bill about that, and
I gather that what they say is that they think
it would be inartistic, as they have been
referring in other parts of this Bill to this
Act of Parliament. They think it would not
be an artistic thing when referring to that
Act to put the meaning in plain English.
Well, I do not know that the Bill itself is
such a work of art that it would be spoilt,
and even if it were inartistic I would put the
meaning of prescribed distance" in plain
English.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
My hon. Friend has addressed himself with
great vehemence on a subject, on the principle of which I entirely agree with him. I
have always been very much opposed to legislation by reference, and while I do not feel
myself in agreement with the hon. Gentleman to-day, still, on the general principle, I
always feel myself in total agreement with
him. He goes further and accuses the Bill of
not being an artistic measure. That is a
reflection on the draftsman, which I am sure
the hon. Member had no intention of conveying. He has consulted with, I presume,
the advisor of the Home Office, who has given
him his answer to this question on the
artistic side of the Bill. I would venture to
give him another reason, and that reason is
that this Bill refers entirely to the principal
Act, the Act of 1854; and really to carry out
the desires which he and I both have at heart
it would mean that the whole of that Act
would have to be drafted in this Bill. It
seems to me that it would be wholly irregular, from a drafting point of view, to include
such a definition as is embodied in his amendment in this Bill, because this definitionand it is only a definition-is really
contained in the principal Bill. If the
hon. Gentleman's suggestions were to be
given effect to it would really be necessary to
destroy the whole Bill and draft it on those
hines to which I have referred.
The hon. Gentleman's idea, no doubt, in
putting forward this Amendment is to enlighten the public as to what the prescribed
distance is. In this respect it may be added
that the owners of char-a-bancs, etcetera,
form a very small portion of the community
at large, and, therefore, I cannot see that
there is a necessity to put these words into
this Bill when there is a definition
which appears in the original Bill.
Illegitimate Children Bill. 138
Char-a-Banc Bill.
20 MAY 1924
Owner
The Bill provides that each licensing
authority shall formulate by-laws, and in
these by-laws I can assure the hon.
Member that care will be particularly
taken to indicate either specifically or
by way of definition what the prescribed
distance is, and every char-a-banc
will, before applying for a licence, be
in a position to inform himself as to the
necessary conditions, including information
as to the prescribed distance. I am given to
understand that the Home Office will naturally be in touch with all the proceedings
that are bound to take place, and they will
make it their special business to see that this
information is forthcoming in all by-laws that
may be made as to uniformity as far
as possible, which will naturally be very desirable, and the danger which the hon. Member
seems to believe will arise, I think, will in
any case be obviated. I hope that with this
explanation the hon. Member will feel inclined to withdraw his amendment.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I would
just like to point out that as far as I am
concerned I am in agreement with the proposer of the amendment. The noble Marquess, the Leader of the House, pointed out
that the only persons concerned were char-abane owners. I take a rather different view
from that. It is more for the information of
the ordinary policeman and the ordinary civilian, and people of simple intelligence especially. The Clause I am greatly interested in
refers to overcrowding. That would give the
policeman or the ordinary civilian like myself
an opportunity of knowing what the prescribed area was. We would otherwise know
nothing about it. We would have to look up
the principal Act, and probably would not
undertake the trouble of going on with a prosecution of the char-a-banc in question.
some way could be discovered of getting over
the difficulty, I am still of opinion it would
be much better, and would simplify the reading of the Act.
If
Mr. GREER: Having regard to the assurance given by the noble Marquess that in the
by-laws that will have to be made by the
local authorities the Home Office will see that
a proper definition will be put in of these
words, I am quite satisfied, and beg leave to
withdraw the amendment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
With reference to what the hon. Member (Mr.
Greer) stated, I will certainly give the assurance that I will approach the Home Ministry
in that direction. With reference to what
the right hon. Gentleman (Colonel Sharman-
Crawford) has said, I think really it will be
the by-laws which will affect what I think he
calls the ordinary civilians, amongst which I
would never class the right hon. Gentleman,
because he has been connected with Bills, and
is not an ordinary citizen. What will affect
the ordinary citizen are the by-laws, and he
will have an opportunity of studying in these
by-laws the points to which the right hon.
Gentleman has alluded
Mr. DICKSON: I would like to know if
these regulations as to four miles distant do
not affect the counties. When they get outside the four miles distant, can they put on
as many as they like? Does it mean they are
regulated for four miles from the post office,
and after that they can crowd on as many as
they like. Is there no regulation?
Colonel
-
SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
County will safeguard us.
The
Mr. DICKSON: If the County safeguards

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
