Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Schedule agreed to.
Preamble and title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported without amendment to the House.
Ordered: That this House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without amendment to the
House.
Moved: That this report be now received
(The Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Illegitimate Children (Affiliation
Orders) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This is a Bill which I feel sure hon. Members
will agree is one of great importance. It is
intended to remove a long-standing grievance
which has existed in Ireland. In England
the putative father of an illegitimate child
has been liable for the maintenance and education of the child for many years, but in
Ireland no such liability has been recognised
except where the child received poor relief,
when the Guardians were empowered to sue
the father at Quarter Sessions. In its main
outlines this Bill follows the English legislation on the subject.
139 Illegitimate Children Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
The statement has often been made that
legislation of this nature leads to blackmail,
but the provisions in the Bill requiring the
mother's evidence to be corroborated, and
giving also an appeal to a Recorder or County
Court Judge sitting alone will, I trust, remove
this objection.
I may say that on inquiries which I have
made in reference to this matter I am told
that cases of blackmail are very few and far
between. The Bill is not difficult of interpretation, but I will take this opportunity,
with the agreement of hon. Members, of endeavouring to explain its main provisions as
I go on.
The Court, which consists of a Resident
Magistrate sitting alone, has power on the
application of the mother, or of the Guardians when the mother or child is in receipt
of poor relief, to order the father to pay a
sum not exceeding 20s a week for the maintenance and education of the child, up to
14 or 16 years.
woman
The mother is defined to be a single woman
who is with child or delivered of an illegitimate child, and the expression "single
"includes a married woman who is
with child or has already had an illegitimate
child at the time of her marriage. This will
remedy the hardship of the mother being
unable to sue the father of her illegitimate
child because she subsequently marries another man.
I should like once more to draw the attention of the House to the very important provision in Paragraph (c) of Sub-section (3) of
Section 1, whereby it is enacted that the
evidence of the mother in establishing the
parentage of her child must be corroborated
in some material particular by other evidence
to the satisfaction of the Court. This, together with the right of appeal to Quarter
Sessions, should be, as I have already said,
sufficient insurance against blackmail under
threat of proceedings under the present measure.
on
As
as
one
Proceedings for an Affiliation Order are
not to be stayed by the death either of the
mother or of the putative father. In particular, this Bill enables the mother's written
evidence n oath to be received
evidence after her death.
instance of the necessity for such a provision,
I may point out that where the father has
left Northern Ireland it may not be possible
to have the case heard against him for a considerable time, and it would be inequitable
that he should escape because he fled from our
jurisdiction and did not return until after
the mother's death. Where the father dies
after the order has been made his estate is
liable for a lump sum, the amount of which
is at the discretion of the Court within a
maximum aggregate of such aggregate sum as
the putative father would have had to pay
under the Affiliation Order had he lived.
I now come to an important provision in
Section 4, whereby power is given to the
court to vary the original order by extending
the age of the child up to which the father
will be liable from 14 to 16 years, or by altering either the sum payable or the person to
whom it is to be paid. It seems desirable that
if there is a material alteration in the
father's circumstances the amount which he
has to pay should be capable of variation.
This operates both ways. It might be capable
of being increased or of being decreased.
There is a somewhat similar provision in subsection (3) of Section 30 of the Criminal Justice Administration Act of 1914, which does
not apply to Northern Ireland. But there is
a corresponding provision in that Act which
seems to justify the inclusion in this Act of
this particular provision. This sub-section in
the Criminal Justice Administration Act
allows the Order to be varied with the safeguard contained in the words "upon cause
being shown upon fresh evidence to the satisfaction of the Court."
In order to meet the case where the father
would prefer to meet his liability at once
rather than to continue for years to pay a
weekly sum, the Court is empowered to commute the weekly payments for a lump sum,
arrangements being made for its proper administration for the benefit of the child.
In case of default in payment by the father
the Court is given power to enforce payment,
including power of imprisonment, provided
that the father is given an opportunity of
making his defence.
Section 7 is a very important section, and it
provides for an appeal to a Court of Quarter
Sessions consisting of the County Court Judge
or the Recorder sitting alone. I would call
hon. Members' attention to the fact that this
right of appeal is given not only against an
Affiliation Order by the Court of Summary
Jurisdiction, but also against the refusal of
such Court to issue an Affiliation Order.
I would venture again to call the attention
of the House to paragraph (a) of sub-section
(4) of this section which provides that the
Court shall not confirm the order appealed
against unless the evidence of the mother is
corroborated in some material particular by
other evidence to the satisfaction of the
Court. I have already pointed out what, in
our opinion, will be the effect of this important provision
The remaining Sections of the Bill are concerned with administration, but I think I
Illegitimate Children Bill-
Second Reading.
20 MAY 1924
should make a passing allusion to Section 8
which embodies an important principle of the
English Acts. A Collecting Officer will be appointed for each county borough and each
petty sessions district. His duty will be to
receive the payments ordered and to hand
them over to the mother or guardian of the
child. The Collecting Officer will be paid a
small commission for this work, but this will
come out of the petty sessions fund and the
mother will receive the full amount ordered
without any deduction for his services.
well
The procedure will be generally that
laid down in the Summary Jurisdiction
(Ireland) Acts. This procedure is
known and is the most satisfactory
code for summary proceedings. The
Lord Chief Justice of Northern Ireland and
the Minister of Home Affairs are given power
to make rules for the purposes of the Act.
Finally, we have expressly laid down that
the mother's liability to maintain her child
is not affected by the Bill. I am
sure the
House will agree with me that this is a simple
measure of justice which has been too long
delayed. I may also state that the death rate
of illegitimate children in England and Wales
in 1921 was double that of legitimate children,
and I assume the same proportion obtains in
this country.
This Bill will make the father legally responsible, and I do most sincerely hope that it
will save the lives of many of those unfortunate children who perish annually, not only
from wilful neglect but from the difficulty
which the mother has in providing proper
maintenance for her child.
Before I resume my seat I should like to
quote from a pamphlet, written by Mr.
Robert Parr, director of the National Society
for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children,
and which was published as long ago as
the year 1913. I will ask the indulgence of
the House while I quote this extract, because
it bears very forcibly on the subject before
us at the present moment:-

A short Act should be passed with as
little delay as possible to extend the operation of the Bastardy Acts to Ireland. . . .”
"This proposal for a change in the law is
commended as practicable, necessary, and
just. A great wrong is being done to the
women of Ireland, placed, as they are, at
the mercy of the reckless libertines, who not
only laugh at the discomfiture of those they
have wronged, but also escape from the
financial responsibility of their acts.
"An even greater wrong is being done to
the children born under the triple disadvantage of shame, poverty, and physical
risk. Many are doomed from birth, others
struggle through an enfeebled existence,
and a certain proportion become a national
encumbrance.
"National burdens caused by the necessity for caring for the unfit press heavily.
One of the most effective means of lightening these burdens for the next and succeed.
ing generations is, surely, to take such
precautions as may be possible to prevent
the number of the unfit from increasing in
such proportions as they have hitherto been
permitted to do.

There is no contention here that any
one proposal will provide a complete solution of a difficult problem. Many subjects
have to be considered, some necessarily of
more worth than others, but all possessing
a certain value in their respective places.
Therefore, while it is on the one hand most
desirable that the primary evil of illegitimacy should be reduced, it is a matter
of equal importance that the child born
without the legal right to a father's protection should not have its life jeopardised
because its mother's means are insufficient
to provide sustenance for it. Hence the
claim made for placing the women of Ireland on the same footing as their sisters in
Great Britain.
*
* *
*
*
* *
*
"By bringing these facts into publicity,
ignorance may no longer be pleaded as an
excuse for inaction. A short Act, which
need not be a contentious one, could pe
easily passed through both Houses of Parliament, and the object would be attained.
There is the need, this is the moment,
where is the man?"
That pamphlet reached a second edition in
1913. I think Members of this House will
agree with me when I say that we have recognised the need, that we have found the
moment, and that the right hon. Gentleman
the Minister of Home Affairs may be justly
proud to have his name associated with legislation so long called for and so beneficial to
this country. I beg to move.
Mr LONG: The Board of Guardians with
which I was connected became greatly concerned over thirty years ago about the number
of young, unmarried women admitted to the
workhouse in a condition of pregnancy. With
a view to stamping out in some measure this
great evil, they instructed the workhouse
master in every case to make inquiry, and
interview the reputed father, with the object
of getting him to contribute to the support of
the child. That has been done in all the
numerous cases admitted to the work house for
over thirty years, and in only one instance has
the paternity of the child been admitted and
a small contribution given to the support of
the child and the relief of the mother. I
143 Illegitimate Children Bill-
THE SENATE.
Second Reading.

[Mr. Long]
quite agree with the noble Marquess when
he said that legislation on this matter has
been too long delayed. It is a wonder to re
that it was not introduced long ago.
On behalf of the Boards of Guardians I welcome the Bill, and I commend it to the House.
There is no doubt that in the minds of some
people there was a fear that it would lead to
blackmail. It is quite a natural thing to
suppose that an unmarried mother would
endeavour to fasten the blame on the person
most likely, or most able, to pay. I am glad
to say that, although I shared the view that
the Bill might lead to blackmail, in view of
the provisions of the Bill that corroboration
is required, and that cases will be heard
before a Resident Magistrate sitting alone, I
am quite sure those fears are unfounded. I
heartily support the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: Like my hon. Friend who has
just sat down, I welcome the introduction of
this measure. I agree with what the Noble
Marquess said to the effect that a Bill of this
nature, and for this purpose, is very much
over-due. We in Northern Ireland are
approximately eighty years behind Great
Britain in this respect. I may say at once,
at the very commencement of a speech which,
I am afraid, will be somewhat longer than
those I am accustomed to inflict on the
House, that I have no fear as regards the
question of blackmail. The provisions in this
Bill seem to me to be quite sufficient to deal
with that matter, and the experience in England is entirely against the assumption that
there is blackmailing done to any great extent
in this connection. The difficulty is far
more likely to be the other way-the difficulty
which has always existed in Ireland of getting
the woman to say who the father is. You will
have much more trouble in that direction
than in the matter of blackmail. It seems
to me that every object of the Bill is a laudable object, and one that is in every way consistent with the general trend of our legisla
tion in Northern Ireland. I rejoice that at
last-at very long last-we are recognising
that those unfortunate children born with a
moral stigma upon them are just as much the
care of the State as if they were physically
afflicted children in another way.
The primary object of this Bill was brought
before us by the Noble Marquess, the Leader
of the House. It is primarily a Bill for
infant life protection. That is really the
effect of it. After that comes the desire to
secure a somewhat less miserable life for some
of these unwanted children; and after that
again I hope, perhaps, that we may succeed
in improving the standard of morality in this
country. The method by which we propose to proceed is the inculcation, and the
enforcement where necessary, of the doctrine
of parental responsibility; and we want to
do that on more equitable terms than ever
before. We want to do it, I think, on terms
of equity, and we desire to distribute, but not
wholly to transfer, the burden. At present
the whole burden falls practically on the
mother, and we do not wish that to continue.
We recognise that the penalty of joint transgression should be jointly borne; but I am
not one of those who appear to be prepared
to absolve the woman from all blame in this
matter, and to cast the entire opprobrium on
the man. I do not think it is any help to us in
discussing what is really a difficult subject to
hold up the man as having the monopoly of
vice, and to suggest that the woman is a
paragon of virtue. She is not. She cannot
be, unless it is a case of rape, in which the
woman would have a civil remedy. We must
remember that in other cases there has been
consent on the part of the woman. That
consent implies blame, and, incidentally.
would destroy any right she might have of
a civil action for her own benefit. This Bill
gives her no civil action whatever. The
Bill is directed to the interests of the child.
Now, let us look for a moment at the size
of the problem in Northern Ireland. Reliable
figures are very hard to get. Statistics of
this nature are never up-to-date. It so
happens that the disturbances of the last few
years have operated in many ways to the disadvantage of the community, and I am afraid
that, amongst other things, they have increased the rate of illigitimacy all over the
British Isles. So far as I can make out from
figures, which I do not propose to give to
the House
Col. SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: Hear, hea: !
Mr. LESLIE: Because they are not substantially reliable, the rate of illegitimacy in
Northern Ireland is no worse than the rate in
England and Wales. We appear to have to
consider about 1,200 to 1,300 illegitimate births
in Northern Ireland in a year. The number
is somewhat important, as I shall have to
refer to it again.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: Agreed,
agreed.
deal
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry, because I am
afraid I shall have to inflict a great many
numbers on the right hon. and gallant
Gentleman. Approaching the consideration
of this subiect, we have a great
of information to draw upon. We
have the English Act of 1845, and
the later and more important Act that
will, I suppose, be described as the principal
Act in this matter, the Act of 1872, which was
amended the next year. Then there was a
lull in English legislation until 1914. Between
those two dates, 1872 and 1914, we had the
report of a Select Committee of the House
of Commons in 1909, which is, I think, a very
145 Illegitimate Children Bill-
20 MAY 1924

Second Reading.
some
to
valuable report, and which was
extent based on the report of the Royal
Commission on the Poor Law which sat in
the same year. On that Report again was
based a Bill which Lord Bathurst brought
into the House of Lords in 1913. I think it
passed the House of Lords, but was not
brought up elsewhere. It was succeeded in
1914 by a Bill brought in as a private measure
by Captain Jessel. That Bill got on the
Statute Book, and is known as the Affiliation
Orders Act, 1914.
The Report of 1909 recommended, amongst
other things, the consolidation of the English
law dealing with this matter, and I regret,
as I am sure every member of the Senate
regrets, that the recommendation as regards
the consolidation of the English law was not
followed.
Moreover, in
addition to the wealth
of information we have to draw upon from
England, this Bill comes to us from another
place where there has been a great deal of
very earnest debate on the various points
raised. I think we may take it that our desire
in a measure of this kind is to follow the
English practice as far as may be, and it was
on the English lines that this Bill was originally drawn. It is very important for us to
keep on level terms with England-not with
Scotland, because the Scottish law is entirely
different-but it is important that we should
keep as closely as possible to England in this
matter, and were it not for that I should
have attacked the 20s allotted. The Bill is
certain of a favourable reception here, but
the details of the measure will require the
closest examination. I do not think there
will be any room for anything approaching
an acrimonious discussion on
any of the
details at issue, but there is room for very
sharp differences of opinion as to whether this
or that provision is really conducive to the
end we desire to serve, and therefore I should
like to appeal to the Government that when
they have got their second reading, as I am
sure they will, I hope that the greatest
latitude will be allowed in the remodelling
of the measure. It is not, I think, an
occasion when the activities of the noble
Viscount (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard)
should be particularly called upon.
I would also ask that ample time
should be allowed for the consideration
of the Bill. It would be absolutely
fatal for us to try to take the Committee
stage for at least a fortnight, and we ought
also to have plenty of time between the
Committee stage and the succeeding stages of
the Bill. We have to remember that if our
Bill is to be an absolute success we have to
bring under review, before the children are
actually born, the 1,200 to 1,300 cases which
we may annually, expect. If we are to do
that, and if we are to bring the mothers forward so
as to get for these children the
advantages of the Act, it follows that we must
have a procedure supported by a court, a
procedure which is-if you will allow me to
use the word-attractive, but, above all,
simple, and a court that is accessible, inexpensive, quickly moving, and, subject to
that appeal which is necessary in all cases
If these
summary jurisdiction final.
canons, if I may call them so, are accepted,
we have to inquire whether the Bill follows

of
to
The Bill contains many very admirable pro-
'visions. It breathes humanity in almost
line, and I
every
want
do not
forget that in anything I say, and
I do not want the House to forget
it either. The Bill is full of humanity,
but some of it seems to be very elementary
humanity. The charity is not sufficiently
diffused. It is too much concentrated altogether. The House, I think, must take a
very broad view of the whole question. They
must not allow commiseration with the unfortunate but erring woman to lead them to
forget either the best interests of the child or
to work any injustice to the putative father.
The Bill will have very far-reaching and very
long-enduring consequences.
I am afraid I must
say that in
my judgment the Bill is dangerously
overloaded. We ought to remember that
we in Northern Ireland have no experience worth mentioning of the administration of any law of bastardy. In England
they have at least eighty years' experience,
and they have not found it necessary to introduce some of the provisions contained in this
Bill.
I think we shall have to throw over various
things. The first great change I desire to see
made is in connection with the Resident Magistrate, and other changes I would like to see
are connected with the arrangements for the
perpetuation of testimony, the question of the
continuation of the action after the death
of the putative father, the liability of the
estate of the father if he should die, and
finally the perpetual and unrestricted power
of variation. I want to deal to-day with these
points because I do not think we ought to leave
them altogether to a later stage. Neither the
Bill nor our present circumstances would
justify us in doing anything which might be
described as springing any suggestion upon
the Government or the House. We ought to
have, as I have said before, the fullest time
for considering everything the Noble Marquess has said to-day, and for putting down
amendments in the future.
Passing now to the question of the
jurisdiction which it is proposed to
confine to Resident Magistrates, I think
we have to consider that from two
points of view, the theoretical and the prac-
147 Illegitimate Children Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
tical. I propose to take the theoretical first.
The Resident Magistrates were originally appointed for special purposes under special
conditions. The exercise of civil jurisdiction
was not one of those purposes. Your Resident Magistrate of to-day is not necessarily
a lawyer, and in any case he cannot have that
local knowledge which seems to me to be of
the greatest value in dealing not only with
the evidence in cases of this nature, but also
with the assessment of the sum that ought
to be paid, if money is to be paid, and with
the further question of the enforcement of
any Order of the Court. It seems to me that
those are matters with which the local magistrates are eminently suited to deal.
I know there are precedents for confining
jurisdiction to the Resident Magistrates.
There is the precedent we put in the
recent licensing legislation, but in that
case there were greatly complicated
matters of law to deal with, and I am
afraid there was evidence of Bench
packing in the past. There is no evidence
of that in bastardy cases, for there
has never been jurisdiction in such cases in
Northern Ireland, and it seems a bold assumption that the ordinary magistrates will
not do their duty in a matter of this kind. If
you are going to deal with the whole question
of Summary Jurisdiction in Northern Ireland
deal with it, but do not deal with it in a
piecemeal manner. The other precedent, of
Course, is in the Civil Authorities (Special
Powers) Act, and the jurisdiction there naturally follows closely on the old Crimes Acts.
Special jurisdiction under the Crimes Acts
was given to Resident Magistrates not entirely
in order to make sure of impartiality, but
partly for other reasons. I do not think those
are altogether relative precedents. Moreover, in this Bill it will be possible for a
Court of first instance to make orders which
may entail an expenditure of 850 on the part
of the man, and an expenditure of £850,
although it be spread over sixteen years, is
not a power which ought to be given to any
one man. It is not right. I know there will
be the protection of appeal, but there will
also be the protection of appeal the other
way.
I
Passing from the theoretical to the
practical, again I must mention the fact that
you may expect 1,200 to 1,300 illegitimate
births per annum in Northern Ireland.
believe you have got in Northern Ireland
fourteen Resident Magistrates. Those fourteen Resident Magistrates are expected to
attend your Petty Sessional Courts, of which
I believe there are 120, and they have jurisdiction in those licensing and Civil Authorities matters to which I have already
referred, and also in certain Labour affairs.
I do not quite see how they are going to
find time to attend to all these cases,
and that point will attain greater importance
if the intention of the Bill is that the preliminary summons issued is only to be issued
by the Resident Magistrates. On that point
the Bill is not clear.
I presume the intention of the Bill is that
the first summons shall be issued by any
magistrate. From the earlier sections of the
Bill that would appear to be the case, but
a grave doubt is introduced towards the close
of the Bill by a section which is confined to
Resident Magistrates. Let us imagine, for
instance, the case of a woman in Ballintoy,
where there is a Post Office, but no means
of railway communication. It is a place
about eight miles from Ballycastle where
there is, or was, a rather theatrical railway
to Ballymoney, which is resting at the moment, but no other means of communication.
Ballintoy is twelve miles from Portrush
where there is a Resident Magistrate.
Where on earth is this woman in Ballintoy
to go to lay her information and get her
summons? I do not think the intention of
the Bill can be that that first summons should
be issued by the Resident Magistrate. It is
unworkable.
Passing from that, if you are successful in
getting all these cases under review before
the child is born, there must be at
least two hearings in every case, and
your Resident Magistrates have got to
hear them. I do not think that upon
re-consideration it will be possible to
maintain the Resident Magistrates. I think
the first jurisdiction should properly be given
to the ordinary magistrates. As regards the
fact that jurisdiction under the Act of 1863,
which gave power to Guardians, was given to
the County Court Judge and not to the
ordinary magistrates, the reason was, as
hon. Members will remember, that in 1863 all
magistrates were ex-officio members of Boards
of Guardians and, therefore, could not plead
in their own court.
Leaving Resident Magistrates and coming to
the question which I have described as perpetuity of testimony-taking a woman's evidence on oath and putting it aside, I presume
until after the child is born-here again the
Bill is not clear as to whether that section is
limited to a case where a child has not been
born. If you do that you are making provision against the chance of the woman dying
in child-birth. I presume that is what the
section is for. Nobody can tell which
woman is going to die, and all your 1,200 or
1,300 cases have to go through this stage in
case the woman should die in child-birth. I
was able to get from the 1921 Report of the
Registrar-General for Ireland the number of
deaths from this cause. In 1919 there were
263, and in 1920 there were 298. You cannot
get down as low as a per-centage, but it
amounts to 3 deaths per 1,000 in child-birth.
If you take in other cases where the woman

over
a
if the mother of the illegitimate child dies
leaving property, that property is not avail.
able for the support of the child.
Going on then to the question of the
liability of the deceased's man's estate, I
again refer to a later paragraph of that
report of 1909 which was dealing at the
moment with the Bill which had been
introduced by Mr. Arnold Herbert, himself a member of the Committee, and with
the evidence of Sir Ernley Blackwell.
dies from other causes the number would be
about six deaths per thousand, and
the
period of ten
1910-1919, is 5.83
years,
per 1,000.
That is the number of deaths, and to meet
the possibility there it is proposed to set up
this elaborate machinery. Moreover, suppose
you do set it up, and suppose the woman does
die and you bring up her written statement
in court, what is the value of it? The putative father had an opportunity of attending
when it was taken down, and, if he was a wise
man probably he kept perfectly quiet. The
case comes on and the woman is dead. There
is nothing but her story on paper. The man
can tell any story he likes; there is nobody
there to contradict him. Moreover he can
get witnesses to assist him in perfect safety.
This question was considered and reported
upon by the Select Committee in 1909. The
report was as follows:-It is dealing with the
suggestion that the case should be allowed to
go on even if the woman had died and was not
there. I will read it, if I may, because the
report is not, perhaps, very readily accessible:
"It has been urged that in cases of the
mother's death, or insanity, or other sufficient reason, the evidence of the mother
should be dispensed with, and the guardians
or person maintaining the child should be
enabled to apply for an order. In view of
the possibility of fraud and blackmail
would inevitably ensue on such a
serious change in the law, we are unanimously of the opinion that such a proposal
should not be entertained, unless it were in
the case of admission by the male parent,
either made orally in court, or by written
document properly proved."
which
Moreover, you have to consider not only the
very small proportion of women who die in
childbirth. I take it the maximum is six per
1,000. Look at it from another point of view,
in how many cases where the mother dies in
childbirth, does the infant survive? I do not
think 10 per cent. I cannot find the figures
myself. Neither in England nor in Scotland
have they thought it necessary to introduce
this provision.
The question of the continuation of the
action comes on now, and I am afraid I see
here an apparent desire to punish the man,
not for an act of immorality but for the fact
that the act of immorality has resulted in the
birth of a child. It is not very reasonable.
There is no precedent, as far as I can find, for
continuing the man's liability after death.
What he has done is not what the lawyers
would call a tort. If it were a tort the action
would have died with the man. It would not
be one of the excepted cases. So far as I can
see there is no precedent whatever for the
proposal to attack the father in that way. It
is inequitable, because when the mother dies,
Mr. Blackwell, in his evidence on behalf
of the Home Office, pointed out that such
a process would be applicable to a very
small number of cases, since in the majority
of cases no estate is left worth administering; and that it would promote the interests
of illegitimates at the expense of lawful
children and widows.
If you are going to give illegitimate children special rights over the estate of the puta
tive father, you are giving them an advantage which the legitimate child does not
possess. You have got no
law of the
unofficious testament here. We are living in
Northern Ireland. We are not living in
ancient Rome, neither are we living in Scotland, where there are and were provisions
of that sort. We have here no law whatever
dealing in this way with estate. A man
or woman can leave property exactly to whom
they like. If you give an illegitimate child
the preference you run the risk of working
an injustice to the legitimate, who may be
orphaned just as much as the illegitimate
child. If the illegitimate child should be
rendered an orphan by accident to the male
parent, it is fair to remember that the Workmen's Compensation Act provides that for
the purposes of that Act the illegitimate child
comes in just as much, I think, as the legitimate. At all events he does come in.
Then again, in assessing the amount that is
to be paid, you propose to leave it to the
Court-a Court of Summary Jurisdiction, I
presume. Now, there is another Court in
existence for looking after the interests of
orphans and widows. It is not a Court of
Summary Jurisdiction; it is the Court of
Chancery. You have no right whatever to
entrust the interests of legitimates to a
magistrate I think that Section, perhaps,
on consideration, will be withdrawn.
I come now to what I described as the perpetual, unrestricted power of variation that
is given. The proposal is apparently-you
will find it on page 5 of the Bill-to give any
Court of Summary Jurisdiction the perpetual
right to review any order which may have
been made by that Court or by any other
Court. You keep the thing perpetually open.
Neither the man nor the woman is ever to get
any chance of living it down. The moment the
woman thinks the man has got a rise in his
Illegitimate Children Bill--
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
salary you propose to give her power to get a
bigger order, and supposing the woman gets
into a better position in life you give power
to the man to get the order reduced. It seems
to me it goes far beyond anything done in
England. When I say that I am not forgetting
what was done in the Married Woman's
Summary Jurisdiction Act of 1895. There
was power to apportion there up to a limit
of £2 in favour of the lawful wife, but it was
provided before that case could be made, that
the change in circumstances must be brought
before the court. There is no provision of
that sort in the Bill at all. And again there
is the case in the Children Act of payments in
connection with children at certified schools,
but that again is of a different character. You
have to remember here, I think, that the
status of the illegitimate child must follow
the status of the mother. Again you would
make him more fortunate than the legitimate
child, because if a workman gets more wages
this week than he did last there is nothing
fixing the amount he is bound to spend on
giving cake to his children.
We have to remember again that one of the
elements of success in this matter is finality
A man ought to know what the limit of his
liability is. If you leave this section as it is
he will not do that. There have been special
cases in those two Acts of 1918 and 1923. The
1918 Act was for increasing the maximum
of the payment under an affiliation
order when it was decided on account
of the rise in the cost of living to
make the five shillings into ten shillings,
and power was specially given as regards current cases to go in and get a revised
order. That power was given again in the
1923 Act when the limit was raised from 10s
to a sovereign. You are proposing to start with
the sovereign. We are not proposing to start
with the smaller sums. In the 1923 Act also
power was given to Boards of Guardians to go
back to the magistrates, when the order had
originally been made on their information, to
ask for an extension of the amount. That is
as far as the English Acts have gone. There is
none of this perpetual re-opening of the question. Surely the right thing to do on these
points is to wait a little for experience and
see if it is necessary to amend your Act later
They had to do it in England. The '73 Act
followed very closely on the '72 Act, and we
should incur the greatest danger of an utter
breakdown if we went so far beyond existing
legislation. It seems to me the Bill requires
the closest examination, not only on this
matter, but on others
Now I want to deal with the question of
the age up to which it is proposed to make the
payment run. It is very difficult to find the
right age. The Bill suggests 11. There seem
to be four possibilities-13, 14, 15, and 16, for
everyone of these possibilties might have been
adopted, and, I think, defended. Every one of
them has been adopted for some purpose.
There is some precedent or other for
each of them. Take the age of 13.
That is the age to which the English
Bastardy Acts run. The normal limit
there is 13, but there is a possibility of extension to 16. If you take 14, as you have done,
you are taking the age in our own Act of 1863,
and you are also taking the limit of the compulsory school age both in England and in
Ireland. If you go to 15 you follow the ge
that is imposed by the Irish Poor-law upon the
mother. Under the Poor-law the mother's
liability terminates at 15. The liability in
England similarly does not terminate until 16.
And the question is which of these ages, all of
which I say might be defended, you ought to
take. I venture to suggest that unless you
raise the age from 14 to 15 you are doing unfairly as regards the mother. The mother's
liability in Ireland being up to 15 under the
Poor-law, the putative father's liability should
be 15 also. I do not think you can go higher
than 15 in the case of a boy, because you have
to have special regard there to agricultural
work. If a boy is going to work on the land
he has got to be there at 15. I do not see any
reason for depriving him of parental support
before that age. The case of a girl is some.
what different I think, and in her case you
might properly make the age 16 at once.
you go to 16 in the case of a girl you are not
beyond the possibility of the English Act, and
you have to remember, or should remember.
that since 1872 when the English Act was
passed opinion has advanced very much.
What is called the age of consent has very
properly been very materially raised. It is
now 16. You cannot shut your eyes to the fact
that a girl who has the misfortune to be born
out of wedlock, particularly in our great
cities, requires very special protection. We
shall not be going beyond the possibility of
England if we fix the age for a girl at 16
years. If we did that-if it should be decided
to make it 15 for a boy and 16 for a girl-it
will enable us to simplify our Bill very considerably, because we shall be able to get out
of a great deal of the possibility of variation
as regards the age of 16.
If
Having dealt with the larger matters of the
Bill I want to run very quickly through the
other ones, because I want to have the chance
of drawing attention to some of the provisions
again. I take one subject as regards which I
may say the noble Marquess was unduly
modest on behalf of the Government he represents. First of all there is the title. I do not
know what Dean Alford would have said of it.
To my mind the title is very good; it is a
great improvement on anything we have seen
before.
153 Illegitimate Children Bill-

20 MAY 1924
Second Reading.
We have got rid of the word "bastard,"
and I hope it may be possible to adopt the
English precedent, and get rid of the words
"illegitimate children" also. In Clause 1 I
hope to see a slight alteration which will
make it clear that the Affiliation Order is an
Order of Affiliation, by which I mean that an
Affiliation Order is a declaration of putative
paternity rather than a definition of results
that flow from that declaration. That was
carefully kept in mind in the English Act of
1872, although it has been lost sight of since,
but it is of some importance to-day, because
of the trend of certain thought in favour of
the legitimisation of children by reason of
subsequent marriage, in which case appeal
on
that special point might be of some
importance.
As regards the 20s a week, I congratulate
the Government on having held out against
the suggestion that a minimum should be
inserted. I have ascertained that in Scotland
since 1919 the usual rate has been 4s 6d only,
and it seems to me that the way this section
comes before us has a great bearing on the
question of variation, because there is nothing
to show here that this 20s a week-
Mr. SPEAKER: I am sorry that I must
interrupt the hon. Member. I have allowed
him considerable latitude, but I think he is
now going rather too much into Committee
points The object of the Second Reading is
to discuss the Bill as a whole, and to reserve
points such as those upon which the hon.
Member is now touching for the Committee
stage.
I was
as
Mr. LESLIE: I must apologise.
anxious to give the information as early as
possible on the points I wanted to draw
attention to. I will not deal further with

Committee points, but I should like to say a
word regarding the appointment of the
Collecting Officer, and to ask for information
as to Petty Sessions Clerks. I want to know
whether the Government has obtained the
consent of the Petty Sessions Clerks to act
as Collecting Officers. I should also like to
know whether the Government is satisfied
that the Bill contains nothing repugnant to
the Imperial Acts which deal with the Mobile
Forces, in connection with which we have no
power. I do not think there is anything else
that can be described as a Second Reading
subject, but I will reserve other matters for
the Committee stage, which I trust will not
be taken too soon. I support the main
principle of the Bill.
Mr. GREER: I do not intend to occupy
very much time in the observations I propose to make on this Bill, but I would like
to congratulate the hon. member who has
just sat down on the speech he has made.
I think it is a record for this House in point
of time. It is a speech of great ability and
lucidity and bears evident traces of the close
study and attention the hon. Senator has
given to this Bill. Having given him credit
for that I would like to take a little credit to
myself in connection with this Bill, because
in March, 1923, I raised the question of a Bill
of this character on an amendment I had to
the Criminal Law Amendment Act, and I
obtained a promise from the Government
that they would bring forward an independent Bill of this nature as soon as possible.
I notice that the Bill has been received with
a chorus of approval in another place, and
every Member who has addressed the Senate
has also expressed his approval of the
measure, so naturally I wish to claim a small
It is a
portion of the credit for the Bill.
Bill which raises a great many difficult
questions. It has gone in for novel and very
drastic powers, and it is a Bill that really
requires to be very carefully considered
clause by clause. I would, therefore, appeal
to the noble Marquess to see that a sufficient
length of time elapses between the S cond
Reading and the Committee stage, and also
between the Committee stage and the Report
stage, because there is really a great deal of
work to be done in regard to making the Bill
satisfactory.
I consider its aims are absolutely satisfactory, and it will be a great boon to the unmarried mothers, because it gives them a
remedy they never had before in this country.
We all welcome the measure, because it is an
endeavour to do justice to these women. Some
of the novel powers that are put into the Bill
are very far-reaching, and will be very difficult
to carry out satisfactorily, and I hope we will
have the opportunity of carefully considering
them. For instance, a married woman, not
living apart from her husband, has power to
take proceedings for an affiliation order.
think I am right in saying that in England
no such power is given to the married woman
who is living with her husband. That section
goes a very long way, and I almost think it
is dangerous power to give.
I
In Clause 3 there is also an extraordinary
power given. The proceedings for an affiliation order have to be commenced by summons.
A summons is a document which requires the
attendance of the defendant at a certain time
and place ramed in the summons, and, as a
rule, no proceedings can be taken until the
return day arrives. But under Section 3 it
is very doubtful whether, after the summons
is served, and before the return day for
the summons arrives, # Resident Magis
trate is not entitled to hear the woman's
case behind the back of the putative father,
and have what she says taken down,
and sworn and recorded, and given in evidence
against the putative father in subsequent
proceedings, when he has had no opportunity

THE SENATE

of having the woman's evidence sifted by

cery.
I agree with the observation of my hon.
Friend (Mr. Leslie) when he says it is an
extraordinary thing to allow a completed
order that has been signed and acted upon
to be altered from time to time. It appears
to me as if the framers of this Act had confused the Petty Sessions Court with the Court
of Chancery. It appears as if an application
can be made from time to time to a Resident
Magistrate, just as if it was a suit in Chan-
I do not say it is wrong, but it should
be carefully considered before powers of that
sort are given. The Bill provides for certain
proceedings that can be taken if default is
made in the payment of the weekly sums
awarded, but it does not say how long the
default may continue. Is the father liable
to be imprisoned if he fails in making one
payment? If not, the Act should say how
many payments must be missed before proceedings may be taken for default. I do not
think it is necessary for me to refer to any
other sections specially, but I merely wish to
record my approval of the Bill
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: There is
one point in this Bill that I hope the Government will inquire into. In connection with
the liability of the putative father's estate,
Senator Leslie stated that the illegitimate
child would be in a much better position than
the legitimate child. I think it would be
against all the principles of justice that a
child of the marriage should have money
taken from it and given to the illegitimate
child. I hope the Government will carefully
consider that point, and that they will not
allow the interests of the legitimate child to
be prejudiced.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
think we have had an interesting debate on
this very important measure, and I should
like particularly to congratulate the hon.
Member (Mr. Leslie) on his very valuable
contribution. I am glad to feel that the
House is in sympathy with the principles of
the Bill, but I have been able to ascertain
very clearly that there is a great difference ct
opinion on many of the clauses which are
contained in the Bill, and which involve
matters that are more pertinent to the Committee Stage than to the stage we are dealing
with at the present moment. I think hon.
Members are aware that it is always my
object to save this House as far as I possibly
can from being hurried into the consideration of any measure, and especially into the
consideration of a Bill of the importance of
the one before us at the present moment.
Therefore I will say at once that I have.
with the consent of the Government, suggested that the Committee Stage on this Bill
should be postponed until the Autumn
Session. The time during which we could
discuss the Bill before the Recess is not very
long, but if it was possible to bring the measure into operation before 1st January next I
would feel that it would be our duty to prolong the sittings of this Session to such time
as would enable ԱՏ 10 go meticulously
through the remaining stages. I am given
to understand, however, that there is so much
to be done, and that various Orders have
to be formulated before the Bill can be put
into operation that we could not hope that
the principle with which we are all in agreement can become the law of the land until
1st January. Therefore, I feel quite justified
in suggesting to this House that we should
postpone the Committee Stage until we meet
in the autumn.
The hon. Senator Leslie has obviously devoted a great deal of attention to this Bill.
and I think we in this House may congratulate ourselves on the study which we give to
Bills of this description, and so rectify the
deficiencies, if I may use the expression, of
another place. (Laughter.)
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD:
Hear.
Hear,
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
The measure has been put before us in a
manner which requires a great deal of explanation. I am bound to say there are
many provisions which the hon. Gentleman
has mentioned in his speech which, I have no
doubt, will be the subject of amendmentsamendments which will probably be accepted
by the Government. Therefore, I feel that
the House will excuse me from going carefully into answers to all the points which the
hon. Gentleman has made. The Bill which is
before the House differs in some considerations from the English Measure. Naturally
We are bound to feel that any differences
which there are between this Bill and the
Measure which is in operation in England are
differences which we should very carefully
study, and, after weighing the pros and cons.
should either incorporate them into our Bill
on reconsideration, or else be guided by what
is in operation on the other side of the water.
Having said this, I do not know that there
are any points which I can usefully answer
which have been put forward by the hon.
Gentleman opposite.
The hon. Senator, Mr. Greer, raised the
point of a woman living with her husband
not having the opportunity of taking advantage of the provisions which are in this Bill.
I think there are many hard cases which
might appeal to him in this connection. A
woman might have concealed the fact from
157 Illegitimate Children Bill.

20 MAY 1924
Second Reading
her husband that she had got an illegitimate
child. Without some provision of this sort
there would be a charge on her husband for
the maintenance of the illegitimate child.
But these, as the hon. Member said himself,
are Committee points, and not points which I
can usefully deal with on a Second reading
debate.
The other small point which the hon.
Member (Mr. Leslie) made was with reference
to petty sessions clerks. Petty sessions clerks
are appointed with the approval of the
Governor, and hold office during his pleasure.
Therefore, they are officers of the Government, and their consent is not necessary.
I think, with the consent of the House, I
will not go into any of those points which
have been put forward, because I am bound to
say that I am in considerable sympathy
myself with the great majority of the points
which the hon. Member has raised. I will
only go the length to-day of saying 'hat they
are matters which are being considered very
carefully by the Home Office, and by the time
this Bill reaches the Committee stage in this
House I can assure him that we shall probably have amendments on behalf of the
Government, and I most cordially invite the
hon. Member to put down whatever amendments he thinks are useful for the fashioning
of a Measure which will be a credit to this
Assembly.

CLAUSE 1 (Temporary Exercise of Powers
of Minister).
The Governor of Northern Ireland may, if
at any time a Minister of Northern Ireland
is unable to act, designate any Parliamentary
Secretary of the Ministry or Department of
which such Minister is the head, or any other
Minister of Northern Ireland, to act for such
first-mentioned Minister during such period as
the said Governor may think fit; and, during
the said period, references in any enactment
to such first-mentioned Minister shall be construed as references to the Parliamentary
Secretary or Minister so designated to act.
Mr. LESLIE: I would just like to ask the
Government, as I see there is no amendment
on the paper, whether they are thoroughly
satisfied with the wording of Clause 1, or
whether they will take the opportunity of
making some slight alteration at a later stage.
As I said the other day, there will be no difficulty, I am sure, in making any alteration
that may be desired.

CLAUSE 1 (Licensing of Wholesale Dealers
in Eggs).
(2) Any person who carries on business in
Northern Ireland as a wholesale dealer in
eggs without being licensed for the purpose
under this section, and any person who deals
by way of wholesale purchase of eggs with
any wholesale dealer who is not so licensed,
shall be guilty of an offence against this
Act
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move in Clause 1, page 4, line 9, at end
("an offence against this Act") to add:-
'Provided that a person who deals with a
wholesale dealer in eggs who is not licensed
as aforesaid shall not be liable to conviction under this Sub-section, if he proves
to the satisfaction of the Court before whom
he is charged that he did not know, and
could not reasonably have known, that such
wholesale dealer was not so licensed."
This amendment explains itself. One of
the objects of the Bill is to do away with the
unlicensed dealer in egg. The Government
have been considering the case of the individual who makes wholesale purchases of eggs
from an unlicensed dealer. While there is no
desire to afford shelter for the individual who
breaks the law with his eyes open there is no
desire to penalize a person who did not know
and had a reasonable excuse for not knowing,
that he was breaking the law, because the
dealer with whom the transaction was made
was not a licensed dealer. This amendment
is drafted to meet his case. I think the
House will agree that it should be inserted
in the Bill. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause as amended ordered to stand part of
the Bill.

kept or furnished in pursuance of this subsection, he shall be guilty of an offence
against this Act

square, with the words "Preserved Eggs,"

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: 1

CLAUSE 4 (Provision of packing accommodation at markets).
(1) The market authority of every market
in which tolls are for the time being
authorised to be taken and actually are
taken in respect of eggs, shall provide and
maintain such covered sheds or other
accommodation for the packing of eggs
brought for sale at the market as the
Ministry considers suitable.
(2) If the market authority fail to comply
with any requirement of the Ministry as
to the provision of such accommodation, it
shall not be lawful for them to demand or
recover tolls in respect of eggs brought for
sale at the market, so long as such failure
continues.
(3) In this section the expression "market
authority" means the company, corporation or person by whom the tolls in the
market are authorised to be taken
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move in Clause 4, page 6, Sub-Section
2, to leave out the words "so long as," (so long
as such failure continues), and insert the
words "for any period during which." The
wording of this amendment is preferred to
that at present in the Bill as it makes it clear
beyond doubt that no recovery of tolls could
be demanded for the period of non-compliance.
even after the authority had complied with
any requirement of the Ministry. The power
to demand tolls for the period of non-compliance is specifically denied, and not merely
suspended for the period
Amendment agreed to.

CLAUSE 1 (Temporary Exercise of Powers
of Minister).
The Governor of Northern Ireland may, if
at any time a Minister of Northern Ireland
is unable to act, designate any Parliamentary
Secretary of the Ministry or Department of
which such Minister is the head, or any other
Minister of Northern Ireland, to act for such
first-mentioned Minister during such period
as the said Governor may think fit; and, during the said period, references in any enactment to such first-mentioned Minister shall
be construed as references to the Parliamentary Secretary or Minister so designated to
act.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
There are two amendments to be moved on
the Third Reading. The first Amendment is
on page 1, line 10, after " period" (such
period as the said Governor ") to insert " and
subject to such conditions." This Amendment
is, as you are no doubt aware, brought forward pursuant to an undertaking given yes
terday by my noble Friend, the Leader of the
House, to hon. Senator Leslie, when the Bill
was in Committee. The effect of this Amendment is to ensure that no difficulty will arise
under Section 8 of the Government of Ireland
Act in connection with temporary appointments under the Bill now before the House.
I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
There is another Amendment standing in my
name on the paper. Page 1, line 11, at end
("during the said period") to add “and subject as aforesaid." This Amendment is entirely consequential on the former Amendment having been accepted. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
