district or place.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move, in Clause 9,
page 9, line 29, to leave out the words
"resident magistrate" ("Where a resident
magistrate") and to insert instead thereof
the words "justice of the peace."
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This Amendment is down in the names of
Senator Greer and Senator Leslie. In the
three cases referred to in the Paper, lines
29, 32, and 35, they are consequential on the
acceptance by the Government of an Amendment which Senator Greer has already
moved. These Amendments are consequential, and I accept them.
Amendment agreed to.
Further Amendments made:
(Clause 9, page 9. line 32, leave out
"resident magistrate" and insert "justice
of the peace."-(Mr. Greer.)
Clause 9, page 9, line 35, leave out "resident magistrate" and insert "justice of the
peace." (Mr. Greer.)
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause 9,
page 10, after line 4, to insert as a new
Sub-section :--
(5) Notwithstanding anything in any
enactment a Court to which any application or appeal is made under this Act
may make an Order for the payment by
the applicant or the defendant, or both of
them, of the costs of the Court and of
such reasonable costs of either of the
parties as the Court thinks fit.
This is the redemption of a promise I
made when I was dealing with costs in
what was originally the first section of the
Bill. The wording is, as nearly as possible,
the wording of Section 31 of the Act of 1914,
to which I referred.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I accept this Amendment.
Mr. GREER: The only observation I have
to make is that I think the words "and
expenses" might be put in after "costs"
("reasonable costs and expenses").
Mr. LESLIE: I would suggest that that
stand over until Report. I should like to
look into it.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I am prepared to accept the Amendment
moved. In reference to what Senator Greer
has said, we can consider that between now
and Report.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause 9,
page 10, after line 4, to insert as a new subsection:-
"(6) It shall be lawful for the mother
to apply for a summons, or take any proceedings under this Act, before any Court,
or make any request or give any consent
in pursuance of this Act, notwithstanding
that she is under the age of twenty-one
years."
Here, again, I rise to redeem a promise
on my part. I need not detain the House
by talking about the collecting officer, because we all anticipate that we shall get the
same benefits for the woman through his
services as were obtained in England, but if
we refer to Clause 8, sub-clause (4), we see
that the collecting officer may proceed on
the request in writing of the mother or
such other person, and, if so, various liabilities of costs and so forth arise A woman
under twenty-one years of age could not do
that without statutory sanction, and I am
now asking the Committee to give that statutory sanction. I beg to move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
We accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Ordered, That Clause 9, as amended, stand
part of the Bill.

Mr. GREER: I beg to move, in Clause 10,

insert and for enabling any applications
to a court of summary jurisdiction under
this Act to be heard and determined otherwise than in open court."
These words are consequential on the
Clause that has already been agreed to, providing for the settling of cases out of court.
It is introduced in order to enable the
Ministry of Home Affairs to make rules
for bringing forward these applications. I
think it is necessary that it should be so.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I would venture to suggest to the hon.
Member that this Amendment is really not
necessary. I am aware that it is intended
to cover cases of agreement where there is
really no necessity to go into open court
at all. The Clause as it stands appears to
us to be wide enough to empower the
Ministry of Home Affairs to make rules for
this purpose.
I think the Amendment is
unnecessary, and I would venture to suggest
that the hon. Member should withdraw it.
Mr. GREER: I beg leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Ordered, That Clause 10 ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

The following Amendment stood on the

as
Page 10, line 23, at end add "And shall

the

Mr. LESLIE: I do not move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move in page 10, line 23, at end to
add as a new Sub-section :-
"(2) This Act shall come into operation
on the first day of January, nineteen
hundred and twenty-five."
The Government consider that it is
necessary that a definite date for the coming
into force of important legislation of this
nature should be laid down by statute. We
feel that it is very necessary that the public
should receive the earliest possible notice,
instead of the matter, being left vague and
undetermined in the statute, as would be
the case where an Order in Council would
be required for appointing a date for the
coming into operation of new legislation. I
find myself in agreement with my hon.
Friend (Mr. Leslie) in this matter.
Amendment agreed to.
Ordered, That Clause 12 as amended stand
part of the Bill.
Preamble agreed to.
Title agreed to.
Ordered, That the Bill be reported with
Amendments to the House.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I have not the programme of work with me

end of the Session-which we are hoping will

253 Industrial Assurance Bill--

22 OCTOBER 1924
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 22nd October, 1924.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
PAPER PRESENTED.
Ministry of Commerce.-Rules made by the
Minister of Finance for the disposal of
valueless documents in pursuance of the
Public Records Act (Northern Ireland), 1023.
Laid before the House (pursuant to Act),
and ordered to lie on the Table.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That they had passed the Expiring Laws
Continuance Bill, to which they desire the
concurrence of this House.
EXPIRING LAWS CONTINUANCE BILL.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this Bill be now
read the First time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Industrial Assurance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I desire to move the
Second Reading of the Industrial Assurance
Bill.
The object of this Bill is the protection
of the poorer classes of the community, and
also the encouragement of thrift. The
practice of insuring against the changes and
chances of life is one which is generally
agreed to be of benefit to the conímunity,
and also to add to the stability of society.
The great bulk of insurance, so far as money
value is concerned, is naturally confined to
the richer classes, who can afford to pay
large premiums, but it will be readily understood that there is a vast number of persons
who, though unable, owing to their circumstances, to pay premiums large enough to
be calculated in quarterly payments or
payments over longer periods than that,
are yet anxious to exercise the virtue of
thrift and to obtain a measure of security
and stability in their affairs by taking out
a smaller class of insurance policy. The
premiums for these policies are payable
weekly or monthly, and for their payment
the holders set apart a few pence out of
their weekly wage. The payment of these
premiums largely depends upon the continuance of the weekly employment of the
policy holder.
This type of insurance business is called
industrial assurance, and a definition of it
will be found in Clause 1 of the Bill. The
volume of this business may be gauged when
I tell the House that in 1921 the premium
income of such policies at an average payment of 2d. per policy per week was estimated at the enormous sum of thirty-seven
million pounds. This business is conducted
by incorporated assurance companies and
registered friendly societies. The difference
between these is that under the Assurance
Companies Act of 1909 a life assurance company must deposit twenty thousand pounds
before it can be registered. A friendly society,
however, can commence business without
making any deposit whatsoever. The small
premiums are collected from door to door by
collectors who work on commission, and who
are empowered and encouraged to extend the
business of the company or society for
which they act by placing new policies
wherever they can possibly do so.
There is no doubt that many abuses have
sprung up, and the Imperial Government
set up a Departmental Committee in 1919 to
enquire into the whole subject. The Industrial Assurance Act passed last year by
the British Parliament was founded upon
the unanimous Report which was issued by
this Committee in 1920
The Bill which I am venturing to put forward for the agreement of this House is a
practical adaptation of the British Act of
1923 to meet the circumstances as they affect
Northern Ireland. It is a somewhat lengthy
Bill, as hon. Members will see, inasmuch as
it consolidates previous legislation. Perhaps I may draw particular attention to
Clause 7 and Clause 12 of the Bill.
Clause 7 as hon. Members will see brings
collecting societies under the same liability
as assurance companies to make the twenty
thousand pounds deposit under Section 2
of the Assurance Companies Act, 1909.
Existing societies must make the deposit before the commencement of this Act, but a
discretionary power is vested in the
Ministry of Commerce, in consultation with
the Industrial Commissioner, to postpone
the making of the deposit by existing
societies for periods not exceeding 5 years
at a time. In the case of new societies the
deposit must be made before the society
commences business. Societies which, subsequent to the passing of this Act, take
powers in their rules to carry on industrial
assurance business shall not have these
rules registered by the Commissioner until
Industrial Assurance Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
the deposit has been made. Sub-section (1)
(f), however, makes the important proviso
that societies which make the deposit in
Great Britain shall be exempted from making a separate deposit in Northern Ireland.
The Commissioner is given power to dissolve
a society which makes default in this
matter of deposit. That comes under Subsection (3). Under Sub-section (5) where
management of a collecting society is vested
in another friendly society, the deposit may
be made by the latter society or the Commissioner may accept a guarantee in lieu of
a deposit.
Clause 12 to which I have referred makes
industrial assurance business a separate
class of assurance business under the
Assurance Companies Act, 1909, and so liable
to a deposit of £20,000 subject of course to
the same provision as in collecting societies
for exemption from making a deposit in
Northern Ireland in cases where it has
already been made in Great Britain. Subsection (3) authorises the Ministry for the
purposes of accounts, to treat Northern
Ireland business as business within the
United Kingdom. Sub-section (3) (d)
authorises the Ministry to refuse the issue
of a warrant for making a deposit in cases
where it considers it inexpedient that a
company should be authorised to carry on
industrial assurance business, but such refusal is subject to an appeal to the Court.
This is the Bill I desire to put before the
House at the present moment. As I have
said it is a lengthy Measure, and I shall be
glad to reply to any question on matters
which hon. Members may think I have left
out in the statement I have ventured to
make.
Mr. LESLIE: The general principles laid
down by the noble Marquess will, I am sure,
be agreed to by all of us, because it is absolutely unthinkable that we should have a
different system in Northern Ireland to deal
with these very important matters from that
which obtains across the water. I confess
that the only points that seem likely to
arise in the passage of the Measure through
the House are of the very smallest character
and perhaps would be best discussed in the
first instance outside. The only serious
matter that does occur to me is
whether it would be possible without conflicting with the general principles of the
English Act to introduce a little more elasticity in the way of providing for dependants.
I am thinking particularly of the question
of adoption, which is very prevalent in
our rural districts. If anything could be
done really to amplify some of the phrases
of the Bill I think it might be of advantage,
particularly, as I say, in the country districts. If that would be possible I think it
would be worth doing, but it is not a matter
which perhaps could be pressed in connection
with this Bill if it would be likely to give
rise to any inconvenience on the other side,
where, as a matter of fact, I believe all
the societies, with the possible exception of
one, do it. I need scarcely say that, taking
the Bill as a whole, I heartily support it.
Mr. GREER: Like my hon. Friend who
has just spoken I approve of the principle
of the Bill, and I understand that it has
been accepted and approved by the industrial
societies. I think it is desirable that we
should pass it. I think it is a very good
idea that all companies carrying on assurance business should make this deposit of
£20,000, and I also agree with what the hon.
Member said about the desirability of extending the objects for which assurance may
be made. It is particularly in reference to
Clause 3 of this Bill, where it says--
Amongst the purposes for which collecting societies and industrial assurance
companies may issue policies of assurance
there shall be included insuring money to
be paid for the funeral expenses of a
parent.
I think that word might be extended to
mean parent by adoption, extended also to
mean a wife's parent, and extended in
various ways.
But that is a matter which
will come at a later stage.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I am grateful to the House for the ready
acceptance of this Measure. The point
which has been raised by my two hon.
Friends is one which I should not dream
of brushing lightly aside, but I shall be
very happy to consult with my adviser as
to whether something of the kind can be
incorporated in the Bill at a future stage.
I hold myself free to make a contrary statement if such a provision should conflict
with the Bill on the other side of the water,
which we are naturally anxious to keep in
line with as much as we possibly can. I am
grateful to the hon. Members for bringing
forward this point.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for to-morrow.
Assurance Companies Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
LONDONDERRY:
The MARQUESS OF
The Bill which I desire to put before the
is
House for Second Reading
almost as
brief as the other one is lengthy, and I can
257 Assurance Companies Bill. 22 OCTOBER 1924
Adjournment.

Government in bringing this Bill forward,
but I would like to have it made clear.
move it in a very few words. This short
Measure is designed to deal with certain
administrative difficulties which have
arisen in regard to matters of assurance.
The Assurance Companies Act of 1909 compelled Assurance Companies dealing in Fire,
Life, Accident and other insurance business
of this type to deposit a sum of twenty
thousand pounds upon the warrant of the
Board of Trade. The Order in Council
adapting the Assurance Companies Act of
1909 to Northern Ireland has dealt with
those insurance companies doing business
both in Great Britain and in Northern
Ireland by relieving them of the necessity of
making a second deposit of twenty thousand
pounds in Northern Ireland if they have
already paid the deposit in Great Britain,
or in Great Britain if they have already
paid the deposit in Northern Ireland.
The present Bill deals with companies
formed or to be formed since the establishment of the Government of Northern Ireland, and places them on the same footing
as the companies dealt with by the Order in
Council to which I have alluded.
the effect of Clause 1 of the Bill.
This is
The second clause of the Bill provides
that in the case of companies doing business
in Northern Ireland as well as in Great
Britain copies of the accounts of these
companies, examined by the Government
Actuary's Department at Westminster and
accepted by the Board of Trade in Great
in Northern
Britain, shall be accepted
[reland as a sufficient compliance with the
requirements of the Assurance Companies
Act of 1909. I think that describes the Bill
that I am putting before the House and I
will not say more on it at this stage.
was
Mr. GREER: There is just one point I
would like to ask about in connection with
this Bill, although I have no doubt it is
quite correct. This Bill proposes to alter
that
made
the Privy Council Order
under the Government of Ireland Act, 1920,
and the doubt I have in my mind is whether
our Parliament can alter a Privy Council
Order or whether it should not be altered
by the body which made it. 1 have no doubt
that that point was considered by the
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I am grateful to the hon. Member for
raising this point. It is one that has not
struck me myself, but I will certainly ask
the question. I should be inclined to say
that this is a matter that has been considered before and I have no doubt that
when I have an opportunity of consulting
with the Government Draughtsman I shall
be able to furnish the hon. Member with an
answer that will satisfy him on the point
he has raised.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for to-morrow.
Motion for Adjournment.
con-
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that this House do now
adjourn. Since we adjourned yesterday
evening the programme of the business has
undergone a certain change. It is not now
proposed that we shall sit next week. As
hon. Members are aware, next week the
Province will be engaged in a very important election, and the polling day,
Wednesday, 29th October, would have fallen
on a day on which it was proposed the House
should sit. In view
these
of all
siderations I feel that I shall be expressing
the sense of the House when I say that they
agree to the suggestion that the Senate shall
meet on Monday, November 3rd, rather than
on Monday, October 27th. It is proposed that
we should adjourn after the sitting of the
House to-morrow until Monday, November
3rd, and I shall be happy to show the programme of business to any hon. Member who
desires to know what further business there
is before the House prorogues, as we hope
the Prorogation will take place on Friday,
November, 7th.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty Minutes before
Four o'clock.
Oral Answers.

THE SENATE
Oral Answers.
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 23rd October, 1924.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
PAPER PRESENTED.
Ministry of Labour.-Trade Boards: Retail
Bespoke Tailoring Trade, Northern Ireland.
Regulations dated 16th September, 1924, made
by the Ministry of Labour, under Section 17
of the Trade Boards Act (Northern Ireland),
1923.
Laid before the House (pursuant to Act),
and ordered to lie on the Table.
Oral Answers to Questions.
THROUGH TICKETS FROM ENGLAND.
The following question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
"To ask the Leader of the House, whether
the attention of the Government has been
drawn to the inconvenience experienced by
travellers from Great Britain to Northern
Ireland owing to their inability to obtain
through tickets, and through labelling of
luggage to up-country stations; and whether
any representations on the subject can be
made through the proper channels."
Mr. LESLIE: The question which stands
in my name is intended to direct attention
to a very real and, as it seems to me, wholly
unnecessary inconvenience to which the present arrangements or lack of arrangements
of certain railway companies subject those
people who desire to travel from Great
Britain to Northern Ireland. In the old
days before the war, as the House will remember, it was possible to obtain a through
ticket to practically any station in Northern
Ireland from London, and if you could not
obtain the little printed pasteboard you
could get a piece of paper with the written
name of the station on it to which you
desired to travel. Since the war this
arrangement has been altered.
The explanation usually given is that the
alterations in the fares for the Irish
and for the English railways have
been on a different scale, and that it
is therefore inconvenient to issue through
tickets, notwithstanding the fact that the
greater part of the traffic of Northern Ireland is carried over a system which belongs
to one of the companies located in Great
Britain. And as there are no through
tickets it seems to follow that you cannot
have through labelling of luggage either, for
the reason that it is inconvenient to label
a man's baggage beyond the point to which
he has taken a ticket. During the twelve
months I was at St. Pancras, in London, and
I wanted a ticket to Ballymoney on the
Northern Counties, and I wanted the luggage
labelled. I was informed I could not get
a through ticket nor could my luggage be
labelled to a point further than that to
which I got a through ticket-Larne Harbour. As a matter of fact the luggage was
labelled to Larne because the official did
not know the difference between Larne
Harbour and Larne. It is a difference of
considerable importance. Larne Harbour
and Stranraer are very pleasant places if
the weather is fine, but they are, neither of
them, places which a man who has come
off a long journey wants to hang round, and
a woman objects still more. I have, therefore, ventured to ask whether attention can
be directed to this through the proper channels. I am aware it is not a matter over
which our Government has definite authority.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon. Member has asked whether the
attention of the Government has been
directed to the inconvenience experienced by
travellers from Great Britain to Northern
Ireland. The answer to that is in the
negative, and as far as I am aware no representations have been made to the
Government on this specific point. But I
may tell the hon. Member for his information
that I have received this information from
my right hon. Friend the Minister of
Commerce-The facilities for through bookings and for through labelling between all
stations in Great Britain and all stations
in Northern Ireland were discontinued
during the war, as the hon. Member has
said, and, except in a few instances, have
not thus far been restored. The practicability
of restoring these facilities is at present
engaging the attention of the various railway companies concerned.
I think the hon. Member is aware that
matters which appertain to the railway
services are reserved to the Council of Ireland or to the Imperial Government while
the Council of Ireland is in abeyance. However, I am prepared to say to the hon.
Member, what he probably knows already,
that the Ministry of Commerce is in what
I may call informal touch with the local
representatives of the railway companies on
this and other similar matters appertaining
to the railway companies. I regret that
that is the most I can say to the hon.
Member in answer to his question. I hope
he may be able to see some ray of hope in
the remarks I have made.
Expiring Laws Bill.
Oral Answers.
23 OCTOBER 1924

THE USE OF WELSH BRICKS.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I beg to
ask the Marquess of Londonderry, whether it
is a fact that bricks from Wales have been
specified for the new buildings for the Training College of the Ministry of Education,
when an equal quality of bricks could be obtained locally at a much lower cost.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The question which the right hon. and
gallant Gentleman has aked is in reference to
bricks to the effect that bricks from Wales
have been specified for the new building at
the Training College of the Ministry of
Education. I may say that the specification
for the proposed Lecture Block at the Stranmillis Training College has not yet been
drawn up, but the matter has been under
consideration.
It is proposed to use local bricks for all
purposes except the facing of the external
walls, for which it is intended to utilise best
Welsh facing bricks. I am advised that these
bricks are the most suitable for that purpose and are specified for all high-class brickfacing work throughout the United Kingdom
for buildings of a similar character.
I think that the right hon. Gentleman is
aware that the clay from which these special
bricks are made does not exist in Ulster
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: Arising
out of that answer may I say that the noble
Lord has not answered the last part of my
question. If these bricks are employed what
will be the difference in cost? Also referring
to the matter of clay what he says is incorrect. The clay which can be obtained in
Belfast is of as high-class quality as can be
obtained anywhere for bricks used for this
which
facing. We have a class of brick
would not be suitable, but I am also informed
that there would be a difference in cost of
from £700 to £800.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I am sure the right hon. and gallant Gentleman is an expert on bricks as he is on many
other things, and on this question I am afraid
I am not prepared to compete with him. I
am informed that bricks used for facing purin Ulster. The
poses are not obtainable
bricks which possibly could be obtained in
Ulster are such as would not be suitable for
the buildings. I may say also that this is a
matter which comes under the Ministry of
Finance and if the right hon. and gallant
Gentleman would like any further information in the matter I shall be very glad to
get it for him.
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
Order for the Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move the Second Reading of the Expiring Laws Continuance Bill. This Bill
continues as hon. Members are aware for a
further period of a year certain enactments,
whether passed by this Parliament or the
Parliament of the United Kingdom, which
may be sub-divided as being still experimental in character or naturally of a
temporary nature, inasmuch as they were
devised to meet certain passing conditions
and so to be prolonged in their operation so
long as those conditions themselves operate.
The Expiring Laws Act passed by the
Imperial House of Parliament this year
applies to Northern Ireland "in so far as it
deals with any enactment relating to a subject with respect to which the Parliament of
Northern Ireland has not power and competence to make laws." The present Bill
supplements this provision so far as Imperial legislation is concerned by dealing
with enactments relating to subjects with respect to which the Northern Ireland Parliament has legislative power. At the same time
it seeks to prolong certain measures which
the Parliament of Northern Ireland has
itself enacted during the time it has been in
being. In this way this Measure and the
corresponding Measure in the Imperial
Parliament taken together deal with all
temporary laws applicable to Northern Ireland according as they come within the power
of one or other of the two Parliaments.
I hardly think there is any need for me to
go into detail on this Bill, but I shall be
glad to answer, so far as I am able to do so
any questions which hon. Members may desire to ask.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House, the House to be in Committee
on Tuesday, 4th November next.

read.

itself into Committee on the said Bill.-(The
Marquess of Londonderry.)

263 Industrial Assurance Bill-

THE SENATE
(The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.)
CLAUSES 1 (Industrial Assurance Business) and 2 (Industrial Assurance Commissioner for Northern Ireland) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Purposes for which Policies
may be issued).-Amongst the purposes for
which collecting societies and industrial
assurance companies may issue policies of
assurance there shall be included insuring
money to be paid for the funeral expenses
of a parent, child, grandparent, grandchild,
brother, or sister, and the issuing of such
policies shall be treated as part of the industrial assurance business of the society
or company.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 3,
page 3, line 42, at end (society or company)
to insert," For the purposes of this section
the expression parent and child shall be
deemed to include parents and children by
adoption or reputed adoption."
I am
I am,
I believe, of a comparatively
sanguine temperament, but I am not quite
sure that my optimism runs to the extent of
thinking it would be possible to accept the
amendment which I have the honour to submit to the Committee this afternoon.
anxious to draw attention to the necessity
for dealing in some way with the desire and
power of adoption which seems to prevail
in Northern Ireland. There is a good deal
of what I may describe as unrecognised
adoption-the taking in of a child purely
for charitable purposes and for reasons of
affection, and it might very well be that it
would be a help to all if some legal recognition could be given. Adoption as such has
never been recognised by law in England, but
the Committee will remember it was common
enough in Greece and Rome in the old days,
and the amount of ceremonial attached to it
was almost equal to that gone through on
occasions of marriage. In India for many
years it really acted as a substitute for the
power to make wills and testaments.
In England adoption has never been recognised as legal. Consequently it has not
been recognised in Northern Ireland. It
would seem possible if a beginning is to be
made it might be done in some small way,
because if it were possible to add the words
I am suggesting to this particular section
we should be limiting the privilege of adoption to the privilege of taking out a
policy of insurance to secure money to
the funeral expenses of the
parent by adoption or the child by adoption. I think, if I remember aright, we
should find in some other clauses of the Bill
that the amount so secured would be too
small-or so small as to make it almost
meet
impossible that such policy could be taken
out for any other purpose. If the Government should be able to accept my amendment I shall be pleased, but if, as I am
afraid, they give their advice against, I can
only hope that on some future occasion the
whole matter will be taken up and gone into
thoroughly.
Mr. GREER: I would be in favour of
carrying out what the hon. Member has
suggested if it could properly be done, but
I am afraid it may raise difficult questions
about insurable interest, and so on. At the
same time, if it could be done, I think it
would be an advantage to and improvemeut
of the Bill.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
confess myself in considerable sympathy
with the Amendment proposed by my hon.
Friend. He has already, I think, foreseen
the difficulties which are bound to arise if
an amendment of this description is added
to the Bill. I have looked into the question
raised by the hon. Member (Mr. Leslie) as
to whether this clause could be enlarged to
admit adopted children and adopted parents,
and I have come to the conclusion, on behalf
of the Government, that this ought not to
be done at the present time. The principal
objection to the proposal is that it would
enlarge the field of insurable interest in
respect of which policies of assurance may
be granted, and, bearing in mind the grave
abuses which have arisen in the past in this
direction, I do not think that it would be
wise to extend the scope of insurable interest
without very strong reasons and also without
very careful consideration. Another objection
which hon Members will appreciate is that
the law in this respect in Northern Ireland
would be at variance with that of Great
Britain, and bearing in mind the fact that
industrial assurance in Northern Ireland
is in the main carried on by British companies and societies, this would naturally
create some confusion and some very serious
misunderstanding. I feel that on these
grounds the Committee will agree that this
is an Amendment which we could not with
benefit to the Bill accept.
Mr. LESLIE: I quite appreciate what has
been said by the noble Marquess, and ask
leave to withdraw the amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
on
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

(2) In relation to industrial assurance

Provided that, where the company transacts other business besides that of industrial assurance business, nothing in this

I should have apologised to the Committee

which relates wholly or partly to industrial

This relates to the accounts and valuations

accounts so that the Minister who is responsible for presenting Assurance Companies
accounts to Parliament under Section 27 of
the Assurance Companies Act 1909 shall be in
a position to do so with respect to industrial
assurance accounts as well as other classes
of assurance accounts. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.

Assurance Companies Bill.

CLAUSE 1 (Provision as to making of
money deposit).
*
(2) It is hereby declared that, notwithstanding any provisions contained in
Article 12 of the Government of Ireland
Companies, Societies, &c.) Order, 1922, and
any Order of His Majesty in Council
amending that Order (whether made before or after the passing of this Act), an
assurance company is not, and never has
been, exempt by reason only of such provisions from making a deposit in Northern
Ireland in respect of any class of assurance
business if, being a registered company, its
registered office, or, not being a registered
company its head office was situate at the
appointed day in any part of Ireland other
than Northern Ireland.
*
*
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon. Member (Mr. Greer) asked me a
question yesterday and I assured him I
would seek the information he required. It
is in regard to Section 69 of the Government
of Ireland Act, 1920. As the hon. Member
is aware extensive provisions had to be
made in that section for setting in motion
the Parliament and Government of Northern
Ireland during the very difficult period of
transition through which we had to pass.
It was inevitable that these provisions should
Ideal with matters which would come within
the competence of the new Parliament and
Government as soon as they were exercising
their full functions. Instances of this are
to be found in the Order of His Majesty in
Council which is referred to in Sub-section
(2) of Clause 1 of the Bill which is now
before the House and to which the hon.
Member drew my attention. The hon.
Member will find however that such cases
were foreseen and provided for in this Order
in Council as in various similar Orders,
because the Orders expressly declare that
their provisions are subject, as respect matters within the powers of the Parliament of
Northern Ireland to repeal or alteration by
Acts of that Parliament.
Assurance Companies Bill.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
Member
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
The comment which the hon.
made is a perfectly reasonable one, and the
Bill which gave rise to that comment affords
a good illustration of the scope of an Order
in Council as compared with the scope of an
Act of Parliament of Northern Ireland. The
Order in Council modified the Assurance
Companies Act of 1909 only in so far as was
at the time of the transfer absolutely necessary to set the separate administration in
motion in Northern Ireland. As soon as
that administration was set in motion it
became competent for the Parliament of
Northern Ireland to alter that administration according to its own discretion. The
alterations which are proposed by the Bill
are not complete without a minor alteration
of the Order in Council, and that Order
was, as I have explained, actually framed
in such a manner as to permit such alteration being made by this Parliament.
I hope this explanation will satisfy the
hon. Member and give him the answer to
the question which he asked on the Second
Reading of the Bill.
Mr. GREER: The noble Marquess has explained the matter quite clearly, and I have
to thank him for the answer he has just
given. I see that he is quite right. I had
some doubts.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 2 (Provisions as to deposit and
laying before Parliament of documents), and
3 (Short Title and Construction) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.

of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.

Illegitimate Children (Affiliation
Orders) Bill.

CLAUSE 1 (Power to make Affiliation
Order upon man alleged to be father of
illegitimate child).
(5) A court of summary jurisdiction, when
hearing and determining any matter under
this Act, shall be constituted of a resident
magistrate sitting alone:
Provided that a summons under this section may be issued by any justice of the
peace having jurisdiction in the petty sessions district or place where the summons
is returnable.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): The first amendment
which I desire to move is to Clause 1, page
2, line 39, after magistrate ("resident
magistrate sitting") to insert " appointed
under the Constabulary (Ireland) Act, 1836."
This amendment supplies a definition of the
term resident magistrate. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 1,
page 2, line 40, to leave out "section"
("this section may ") and insert "Act."
If, as I expect, the amendment
standing in my name will be accepted simply as a drafting amendment
it will not be necessary to detain the
House with any further observations on
the subject. I am quite satisfied myself
that it carries out what is in the mind of
the House.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This amendment simply provides that a
justice of the peace may issue a summons
issuable under any of the sections of the
Act, instead of under Section 1 alone, provided he has jurisdiction in the Petty
Sessions district where the summons is returnable. I am willing to accept the
amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 2 (Provisions as to Summons.)
The following amendment stood on the
Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :
Clause 2, page 3, line 31.-At end insert
the following new Sub-section :-
“(5) When application for a summons
under this section is made before the
birth of the child, the justice of the
peace to whom such application is made
may issue a summons for the appearance
of the man alleged to be the father of
such child, returnable at some petty
session to be held on a day after the time
when the mother shall expect the child to
be born, provided that if on such day the
woman shall not have been delivered, or
the court shall be satisfied that she has
been delivered at so short a period before
such day that she cannot appear at the
said session, it shall be lawful for the
court to adjourn the hearing of the said
case until some other day, and so from
time to time, until the child shall have
been born and the woman shall be able
to attend at the said session."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move Clause 2, page 3, line 31, to
insert the following new Sub-section :-
"(5) Where the application for a summons
under this section is made before the birth.
of the child, the justice of the peace issuing
the same shall make it returnable at some
petty sessions to be held upon a day after
the time when the child is expected to be
born, but the court of summary jurisdiction shall have power to adjourn the hearing
of the case from time to time to such later
day as may be required in order to secure
the attendance before the court of the
mother or any other necessary witness."
The amendment which I am proposing is
instead of the amendment put forward by
my hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie). I am proposing that a new sub-section should be inserted. This amendment provides a suffi-
Illegitimate Children Bill-
Report Stage.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
ciently elastic procedure to meet every
contingency in a class of case where the
definite fixture of a date is naturally most
difficult. In view of this uncertainty, it will
be noted that this amendment extends to
every important witness in the case, as well
as to the mother, whereas the amendment in
Mr. Leslie's name did not apply to any witness beyond the mother.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the first thing to be
done is to congratulate the Government
upon having reduced a very lengthy amendment, which was based, as will be recognised,
strictly on the wording of the English Act of
1845. The Government has been able to
reduce it to more manageable proportions.
There are two remarks which I have to offer
on the proposals of the noble Marquess.
The first is a very small matter, we see in
the second line of the proposed section the
expression" issuing the same." I should
like to see that altered to "issuing the
summons." I think it will be recognised
that that will read better and carry out the
intention.
I
The second point is more important.
want to be quite clear that the Government
is satisfied that the words as they are now
before us provide for the adjournment until
the court is satisfied that the child has been
born. What we want to aim at in this new
Clause is, as the noble Marquess has indicated, that when the case does come on
everything necessary to the hearing and disposing of it shall be there. The first thing
necessary is that the child shall be born.
I confess I am not quite satisfied that that
is being carried out in the wording of the
section. The House will remember that we
cannot rely on the use of the word mother
as implying that there is a child, because
the expression "mother" has already been
defined in the early part of the Bill as including a single woman with child. I should,
therefore, like to be quite sure that the
Government is advised that they have put in
all that is required. If anything more is
necessary I would suggest that it might be
done by adding in the new sub-clause the
words, "or in order that the court may be
satisfied that the child has been born alive."
The question is whether that is really necessary and if so whether we should do it now
or perhaps postpone it until the Third
reading or leave it until later on.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
What is the proposal?
Mr. LESLIE: Instead of "issuing the
same," "issuing the summons."
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon. Member suggests that the words
should be inserted as a second line-" justice
of the peace issuing the summons shall make
it returnable to some petty session." The
Government have no objection to moving an
amendment on these lines. I should be prepared to alter the section to read accordingly,
but I think the correct procedure would be
to insert the section and amend it afterwards, but I am open to correction from
you, my Lord, as to the procedure.
The SPEAKER: I think the better course
would be to amend it later.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
As regards the other portion of my hon.
Friend's remarks, I am advised that there is
no danger and that the section covers all
the difficulties which he has enumerated. I
feel that he can be quite satisfied that the
wording of the section covers those difficulties which he thinks that he foresees at the
present moment. I will, therefore, put an
amendment in on the Third Reading.
Amendment (to insert new Sub-clause)
agreed to.
CLAUSE 3 (agreement as to payment of
weekly lump sum).
Where an agreement is entered into between the mother of an illegitimate child
and the man alleged by her to be the father
of that child, for the payment by that man
of a weekly sum of money, or of a lump sum,
for the maintenance and education of the
child, and in discharge of any liability under
this Act in respect thereof, such an agreement shall be a bar to proceedings to obtain
an Affiliation Order under this Act in respect of that child, or in case such agreement is entered into after a summons has
been issued under this Act, but before the
hearing of the case, a bar to further proceedings for that purpose, if a resident
magistrate is satisfied on an ex parte application by either party (which may be heard
and determined otherwise than in open
court):-
*
(a) that the agreement will afford to the
child benefits at least equal to those which
might be expected to have been obtained
by the making of an Affiliation Order or
the commutation of the weekly sums
payable under an Affiliation Order; and
*
*
*
*
Mr. GREER: I beg to move in Clause 3,
page 3, line 33, to leave out "
("man
by her
alleged by her to"). This is merely a
drafting amendment and I think it will probably be accepted.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I accept.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move in Clause 3,
page 3, line 37, to leave out "an" ("such
an agreement"). This is also the same kind
Illegitimate Children Bill- 3 NOVEMBER 1924
Report Stage.
of amendment. I think it will probably be
accepted.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
It is a drafting amendment and I accept it.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move in Clause 3,
page 4, line 6, to leave out "will afford"
("agreement will afford to"), and insert
"affords." This is also a drafting amendment, and I think it will probably be
accepted.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
As the hon. Member says, this is a drafting
amendment. As the amendment harmonises
the language used in paragraph (a) with
that used in paragraphs (b) and (c) below.
I accept it.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 4 (Provisions as to duration,
effect and variation of Affiliation Orders).
(1) A liability under an Affiliation Order
shall not be determined by the death of the
mother or of the putative father of the child
in respect of whom the order has been made.
In the case of the death of the putative
father the court may make an order for the
payment out of his estate of such lump sum
as the court thinks just, but in no case exceeding the aggregate of the sums for the
payment of which the putative father would,
if he had lived, have been liable under the
Affiliation Order.
*
*
*
(4) A court of summary jurisdiction may,
on the application of the mother or a board
of guardians or any person for the time
being having the custody of the child, or
upon the application of the collecting officer
or of the putative father of the child, make
an order varying an Affiliation Order-
*
*
*
*
*
*
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move in Clause 4, page 4, line 24,
after "made" ("has been made. In the") to
insert "In the case of the death of the
mother after the making of an Affiliation
Order proceedings for the enforcement of the
Order for the commutation of weekly
sums may be taken or continued by the
person having the custody of the child, or,
if the child is in receipt of relief from the
poor rates of any poor law union, by the
board of guardians of that union."
or
This amendment becomes necessary in
view of the amendment which was passed
on the Committee Stage of the Bill which
removed Sub-section (2) of Clause 3. On that
occasion I think my hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie)
will remember that I said that the Government accepted his amendment provisionally,
and reserved the right to restore or reintroduce in another form the sub-section
removed by his amendment, when the Bill
came for consideration upon Report.
This amendment provides for proceedings
for the enforcement of an affiliation order or
for the commutation of weekly sums under
such order being taken or continued by the
guardian of the child, in the event of the
death of the mother. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I quite agree that the
introduction of some words of this nature is
desirable, if not absolutely necessary, but I
am in a difficulty over the reference to
commutation of weekly sums. I quite agree
if the mother has died we must provide for
somebody acting on behalf of the child coming
to the court to obtain the enforcement of
any payments that may be due. But when
we go on to say "or for the commutation of
weekly sums" the commutation of weekly
sums, as I understand it, can only be done
after the Affiliation Order is made. The
commutation can apparently only be done
under Section 5 of the Bill, when it is done
on the application of the putative father, and
if we leave those words in to-day we are
giving those representing, so to speak, the
deceased mother, a power which she herself cannot have. I cannot see any necessity
for inserting the words, "or for the commutation of weekly sums," and I am afraid that
it is not desirable that we should insert
them. It is not necessary to give a power
to the successors of the woman in the case
of the variation of an Order because the
wording of the Clause dealing with the
variation of an Order is sufficient to provide
for the case. I can imagine that the intention
might be to allow proceedings for the continuation of the proceedings, but I do not
think it is altogether well expressed. Therefore, while supporting the amendment as a
whole, I suggest that the words, "or for the
Commutation of weekly sums' "be omitted.
I think it would be better to do that also
on the Third Reading of the Bill.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I hardly think the hon. Member is right in
his contention, because if the mother is dead
there will be representatives of the mother
for the purpose of dealing with the case.
Consequently, it is necessary that these
words, "or for the commutation of weekly
sums" should be in the Bill. I do not feel
that there is any substance in the contention
of the hon. Member. This Clause has been
very carefully considered, and it has satisfied
the advisers of the Government that it
carries out the intention of the Bill. Therefore, I am not prepared to alter it.
Amendment agreed to.

CLAUSE 4 (Provisions as to duration,
effect and variation of Affiliation Orders) (1)
A liability under an Affiliation Order shall
not be determined by the death of the
mother
or of the putative father of the
child in respect of whom the order has been
made. In the case of the death of the
putative father the court may make an order
for the payment out of his estate of such
lump sum as the court thinks just, but in
no case exceeding the aggregate of the sums
for the payment of which the putative father
would, if he had lived, have been liable
under the Affiliation Order.
*
*
*
*
*
(b) if the child is in receipt of relief
from the poor rates, such board of guardians
or relieving officer as may be designated by
the court.
(4) A court of summary jurisdiction may,
on the application of the mother or a board
of guardians or any person for the time
being having the custody of the child, or
upon the application of the collecting officer,
or of the putative father of the child, make
an order varying an Affiliation Order-
*
*
*
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move on Clause 4, page 4, line 25,
after the word "father" ("father the court")
to insert the words,
"after the making of the Affiliation
Order, his personal representative may be
made a party to any proceedings for the
enforcement of the Order, and"
This amendment is necessary in order to
secure the continuity of an Affiliation Order
after the death of the father. This provision
was originally in the Sub-section of Clause
3 which was deleted when the Bill was in
Committee, as I have already said.
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move, in Clause 4, page 4, line 29,
at the end of Section 1, to add,
"Such lump sum shall be paid to the
collecting officer or to such other person or
body of persons as may be designated by
the court and shall be invested and applied
for the maintenance and education of the
child in such manner as the court may
direct."
This amendment secures the proper appropriation of the lump sum for the benefit of
the child. This safeguard is identical with
that which will be found in Sub-section (1)
of Clause 5, which deals with the lump sum
awarded by way of commutation.
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move in Clause 4, page 5, to leave
out from the word "may" in line 7 to the
word "make" in line 11, and to insert instead thereof the words:-
"upon cause being shown
upon fresh
evidence to the satisfaction of the court."
It will be remembered that the hon.
Senator (Senator Leslie) in Committee on the
Bill sought to introduce an amendment
abolishing the power of the court to vary
the amount payable weekly under an Affiliation Order. On behalf of the Government
I then said that while I could not accept
the hon. Member's amendment, the Government would consider the advisability of
introducing an amendment upon Report,
which would limit the power of variation by
making its exercise dependent upon the
production of some material fresh evidence.
I think this amendment satisfies that point.
Mr. LESLIE: I must thank the Government very sincerely for introducing this
amendment, which I admit goes very far to
remove the objections which I had to the
paragraph referred to by the noble Marquess. Certainly the introduction of these
words will have the effect of assimilating
our legislation on this point in cases where
the power of variation has been allowed.
Such a case is the Maintenance of Deserted
Wives, which is very much on the same
lines. I feel quite satisfied that the introduction of these words will act as a great
protection against many flimsy and undesirable cases, and that it will go very far to
remove any possible objection to the paragrapt which can only have been passed on
speculative grounds with no evidence in
support of it. I am cordially in favour of
the amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
NEW CLAUSE (Provision as to unexpended portion of lump sum).
If an illegitimate child in respect of
whom any lump sum has been paid under
the provisions of sections three, four, or
five of this Act, shall die before the lump
sum paid in respect of him has been fully
expended on his behalf, the unexpended
balance of such lump sum shall be paid
to the putative father of such child.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move that this new
Clause be inserted after Clause 5. The
object of the new clause, as I am sure will
be recognised, is to prevent any possibility
of a lump sum made payable in respect of
an illegitimate child falling into possession
of the mother of that child and so providing
a possible incentive to causing the death of
the child. We all know, unfortunately,
that illegitimate children die at the rate of
Illegitimate Children Bill- 3 NOVEMBER 1924

Report Stage.
•
vision has been made in the Bill for plurality
of births, including twins and triplets. The
lump sum would be affected by the number
of children born, but as far as I can see the
Bill deals with a child in the singular. In
the event of a lump sum being payable one
child might die and another survive. Is it
not necessary to provide in the Bill for
plurality of births?
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I hardly think the hon. Member (Colonel
Sinclair) need have any fears on that point.
Plurality of births would follow on the
same lines as two illegitimate children. In
calculating the amount of money to be paid
it would be governed by that consideration.
The hon. Member (Mr. Leslie) has moved an
amendment for the purpose of returning the
unexpended balance of a lump sum to the
putative father. That is the substance of
the amendment he has moved. At the
beginning of his remarks he referred to the
danger of children being done away with
for the benefits which might accrue from
their death. That point does not actually
arise in this amendment, but I will venture
to deal with it as the hon. Member has put
it. This amendment is an attempt to provide for the disposal of the unexpended
portion of a lump sum in the event of the
death of the child.
about two to every one legitimate child, and
it is most desirable to prevent any possibility
of holding out any inducement to commit
what is practically murder. The whole
question of the lump sum has given me a
good deal of trouble in considering this Bill.
The principle of a lump sum is entirely unknown to the English law of Bastardy.
Lump sums have always been paid in respect
of illegitimate children, but it has always
been in consequence of an agreement made
outside the Court. The Courts would, of
course, enforce an agreement as an agreement, but as far as an illegitimate child is
concerned a lump sum has never been recognised. We are proposing now to admit a
lump sum under three conditions. The first
is that we introduce a new Clause which, I
am sure, with the safeguards provided, will
be found of use. Then again we have in
Clause 4 given a new power altogether to the
resident magistrate to take so much out of a
deceased man's estate and provide a lump
sum for the maintenance and education of
the child. Under Clause 5 we have provided
for the commutation of the weekly sums
payable under the Affiliation Order on the
application of the putative father, but in no
case have we said what is to happen to the
lump sum if the child should die before the
lump sum is entirely used up. In the first
case I know that what was in my hon.
Friend's mind was that the money should be
expended in purchasing a weekly payment
from an Insurance Society, in which case
no difficulty would arise on the death of the
child. In the second case we are taking
money which would otherwise go to the
man's heirs, if he had any, and holding
them up temporarily so that the illegitimate
child may be provided for if it is alive, but
if it does not survive no provision is made
for the allocation of what remains of the
lump sum. I do not see any reason why the
legitimate heirs of the putative father, having
already been fined for the protection of the
illegitimate child, should continue to be
fined after the child is dead. That money
ought surely to go back to the estate of the
putative father, and under Section 5 we
ought to define by statute that the lump sum
shall return to the man who paid it. My
proposition is to prevent anybody having
any inducement to do away with the child,
and I may perhaps digress from that point
to say that I accept the explanation which
the noble Marquess gave us on the
Clause dealt with a minute
ago that
no difficulty will arise in that connection.
But having regard, as I have said, to the
paramount necessity of infant life protection
I beg to move the amendment standing in
my name.
Colonel SINCLAIR: I should like to ask
the Leader of the House whether any pro-
I am not prepared on behalf of the
Government to accept this amendment, and
I would venture to urge against its acceptance, first, that in all probability the lump
sum will have been invested by order of the
Court under some insurance scheme whereby the periodical sums are calculated and
fixed at a figure which includes the risk of
the child dying before the lump sum runs
out.
In the second place, if the child survives
for nine or ten years after the father's
death, does the hon. Member suggest that
the administration of the father's estate
should be re-opened after such an interval?
But the main foundation of the argument
I would venture to put forward for not
accepting this amendment is that the child
being illegitimate, and the hon. Member
referred to this point himself, in the event
of its death, its property passes to the
Crown, and it is obvious the Crown would
then make a fair disposal of it, after a very
full consideration of all claims which must
necessarily be brought before them. I feel,
therefore in the case the hon. Member has
in his mind a sum of money, substantial or
otherwise, would be most meticulously dealt
with by the Crown, and that either the
putative father or the representatives of
his estate would be the first to bring their
case before the Crown in the hope of
recovering such sum of money as they deemed
283 Illegitimate Children Bill-

THE SENATE
Report Stage.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
they would be entitled to. I hope I have
convinced the hon. Member that I am not
able to accept his amendment.
the
Mr. LESLIE: Remembering that
Crown acts on the advice of its responsible
Ministers, I think I may ask the House to
permit me to withdraw the amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
CLAUSE 7 (Appeals to Court of Quarter
Sessions and Recorder).
(1) An appeal shall lie to the court of
an Affiliation
quarter sessions, against
Order, or an Order varying the same, and also
against the refusal by a court of summary
jurisdiction to make an Affiliation Order, in
like manner as an appeal lies in cases of a
civil nature to which the provisions of section twenty-four of the Petty Sessions
(Ireland) Act, 1851, apply.
*
*
*

*
#
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move in Clause 7, page 7, line 8, to
leave out from "against" ("sessions against
an") to "in" in line 10 ("Affiliation Order
in like ") and insert any order (other than
an order for the adjournment of a hearing)
made by a court of summary jurisdiction
under this Act, and also against any refusal
by such court to make such an order."
My hon. Friend (Senator Leslie) on the
Committee Stage called the attention of the
Government to the fact that no provision
was made for an appeal against the decision of the court which made an award of
a lump sum to an illegitimate child out of
the estate of the deceased father, or refused
to make such award.
I promised to look into this matter, and
the present amendment will remove all
doubt upon this head, making as it does
provision for an appeal against any and
every order of a court of summary jurisdiction under this Act, excepting an order
for the adjournment of a hearing, to the
court of quarter sessions. I think that this
amendment gives the appeal which the hon.
Senator thought would be advisable to have
in the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: Again I have nothing to do
but to thank the noble Marquess for inserting these words which will bring our
law into absolute accordance with the law
in England in this matter.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 8 (Appointment and duties of
collecting officer in respect of Affiliation
Order).
(1) There shall be appointed for each
county borough and each petty sessions district an officer of the court who shall carry
out the duties of the collecting officer under
this section. The collecting officer shall be---
(a) in a county borough, such person as
may be appointed by the justices with the
approval of the Ministry of Home Affairs
for Northern Ireland;
(b) in a place outside a county borough,
the petty sessions clerk or such other person as may be appointed by the justices
with the approval of the said Ministry.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move to leave out
paragraph (a) and (b) and insert "such
person as may be appointed by the Ministry
of Home Affairs for Northern Ireland."
My object in putting down this amendment
was to provide for the appointment of a
collecting officer who would be an important
officer of the court, being made not by the
justices but by the Ministry of Home Affairs.
It will be remembered by hon. Members
that the jurisdiction of dealing with these
cases is now taken from the ordinary justices
and transferred to the resident magistrate,
and I do not see why in these circumstances
the justices should have power to appoint
an important officer to deal with these cases.
I presume that there may possibly be some
difficulty in getting them to make any
appointment, I can conceive that the ordinary justices may think they have been overlooked in this Bill, and that they will not
lend themselves to any procedure that would
make the Bill work, and I do not see how
that difficulty can be overcome, unless some
words are inserted in the Clause. I think
it would be better to leave the justices out
altogether and let the appointment be made
by the Ministry of Home Affairs. But I
will not press the point, and if the Government consider that it is better that the Bill
should stand as it is I am prepared to
withdraw the amendment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I regret I am unable to accept the amendment which has been moved by my hon.
Friend. I think the House will recognise
that it has been the aim of the Government
throughout to make the petty sessions clerk,
who is elected by the justices of the peace in
the first instance, the collecting officer in
country districts. The Government accepted
in Committee an amendment from my hon.
Friend by which a departure was made from
our original intention, inasmuch as it provided that the justices of the peace might
appoint some other person than the petty
sessions clerk, with the approval of the
Ministry of Home Affairs. That is as far
as the Government can go in this direction.
To give the appointment of the collecting
officer to the Ministry of Home Affairs,
which this amendment is designed to carry
out would be to take away one of the few
rights of the ordinary bench of justices of
285 Illegitimate Children Bill-

3 NOVEMBER 1924
Report Stage.
the peace under the Bill, and I feel that in
view of the great and overwhelming vote
against Socialism that we
have recently
seen-(Hon. Members: Hear, hear)-this
amendment would undoubtedly be felt and
resented as an unwarrantable intrusion of
the bureaucracy of the Government. I
would, therefore, suggest to the hon. Member
that we should leave this appointment to
the discretion and power of the magistrates.
Mr. GREER: I have to thank the Noble
Marquess for the explanation he has given
me of the reason why he cannot accept my
amendment. I am quite satisfied with it,
and ask the leave of the House to withdraw
the amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 9 (Application of Provision of
Summary Jurisdiction Acts for purposes
of Act.)
*
*
*
*
(5) Notwithstanding anything in any
enactment a Court to which any application or appeal is made under this Act may
make an Order for the payment by the
applicant or the defendant, or both of them
of the costs of the Court and of such reasonable costs of either of the parties as the
Court thinks fit.
*
*
*
*
Mr. GREER: I beg to move in Clause 9,
page 10, line 22, after "costs" ("reasonable
costs of either") to insert "and expenses."
This amendment is to add the words "and
expenses" where it provides for the reasonable costs being given either of the parties.
It will be remembered that in this Act we
provide that the defendant is to be brought
from where he happens to reside to the
court where the mother of this child
resides, which may be a long distance away.
The class of persons who have these illegitimate children do shift about from place to
place, and therefore a woman may have an
illegitimate child here in Belfast or County
Antrim, whereas the putative father lives
in Donegal or Fermanagh or some distant
county. Considerable expense will be incurred by the defendant, and if the complainant should fail in making her case, I
think that the expenses coming that long
distance should be provided for.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am willing to accept the amendment of my
hon. Friend. In fact it certainly is the
Government's intention that the clause
should cover travelling expenses and other
such charges, as well as the costs of the
court. This amendment will explicitly
ensure this, as there may be some doubt
whether costs in County Court practice
would cover expenses.
Amendment agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time.-(The Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I have two amendments to move. The first
one is in Clause 2, in the new Sub-section
(5) as set out on the Paper, in the second
line to leave out "same" ("issuing the
same shall") and insert "summons." This
amendment is in accordance with what my
hon. Friend (Senator Leslie) moved and I
think it satisfies his wishes on that point.
I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move a new amendment in
page 4, Clause 4, line 24, on the Paper, to
leave out "or for the commutation of weekly
and at the end after the word
"union" (" of that union") to add "and
such person or board may be made a party
to any proceedings taken for the commutation of weekly sums." I beg to move.
