YD C8451
NON-CIRCULATING BOOK

LOED

Tuesday, 11th March, 1924.
the good order which had recently been
established in the Province might continue.
I am glad to be able to state that this
expectation has been most happily realized
by the experience of the past twelve
months.
The Fourth Session of the First Parliament
of Northern Ireland, appointed to meet 11th
March, 1924, in the fourteenth year in the
Reign of King George V, was opened by the
Governor (His Grace the Duke of Abercorn,
K.P.) in person.
The House met at a Quarter before Twelve
of the Clock. Mr. SPEAKER (Right Hon.
the Marquess of Dufferin and Ava, D.S.O.,
D.L.) in the Chair.
KING'S SPEECH.
His Grace, the Governor, being seated on
the Throne, commanded the Gentleman
Usher of the Black Rod to let the Commons
know "It is His Grace's pleasure that they
attend him immediately in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar with their
Specker, His Grace delivered the following
Speech from the Throne, and then retired:-
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
When a year ago I first had the privilege
of addressing this House as Governor of
Northern Ireland I expressed the hope that
respect for the law would prevail and that
Shortly after their assumption of office
I received and transmitted a suggestion
from the new Imperial Government that
there should be a Conference to consider
the Boundary question and certain other
matters outstanding between Northern
Ireland and the Irish Free State. This
suggestion was accepted and the Government was represented at a Conference
which took place with three Members of the
Imperial Government and three Members
of the Free State Government by the Prime
Minister of Northern Ireland, the Minister
of Education and the Attorney-General.
After two preliminary meetings the Conference was postponed for a month, but
owing to the ill-health of the Prime
Minister, which I am sure we all greatly
deplore, a further postponement has been
requested.
There are indications of a revival in
trade, and in the Linen Industry an im-
A
3 King's Speech.

THE SENATE.
King's Speech.
a
provement has already occurred which has
exceeded general expectation. This is likely
to have
salutary effect on other
Industries. In other directions, however,
I fear that the position is still abnormal
and exceptional measures are still required
for relieving the graver cases of distress
among our people. I am happy to note that
the Local Authorities have realized the
continued seriousness of the situation and
are doing their utmost, with the assistance
of the Government, to promote most useful
work to meet the difficulties created by
unemployment. The success of the Loans
Guarantee Act has been of such a character
as to render it very desirable that further
steps should be taken in this direction.
Accordingly, your assent will be invited to
proposals seeking for authority to issue
guarantees over and above the existing
limit in respect of loans to be applied
towards the carrying out of capital undertakings calculated to create employment in
Northern Ireland. Legislation dealing with
Unemployment Insurance will be introduced to make the necessary provision as
regards unemployment benefit for the latter
part of the current year.
I take this opportunity of endorsing the
appeal which has recently been made by
the Government on behalf of unemployed
disabled ex-Service men, and I trust that
every employer who has not yet enrolled
on the King's National Roll of Honour will
take immediate steps to qualify for enrolment by employing the requisite number of
ex-Service men.
I would also appeal to every firm to do
all it can to provide work for the large
number of persons, both men and women,
whose return to their normal employment
is so essential.
I am glad that the relations of employers
and employed in Northern Ireland con.
tinue on such harmonious lines, and it is
my earnest hope that this satisfactory state
of affairs may be maintained in order that
there may be no injury to the reviving
industry of the Province.
The Commission on Development, to
which I referred last year, has been actively at work and has already presented
Reports on which the Government intends
to take action during the present session.
The reports of the Reformatory and Industrial Schools Committee, and of the
Committee which investigated the Jury
system have been received and are
receiving careful consideration.
I am glad to learn that it is intended
to appoint very shortly a Commission to
inquire into the suitability and cost of the
existing local administrative machinery in
connection with Local Government, Public
Health, Poor Law and the allied Services.
Steps have been taken to put into operation the Education Act which was passed
last session.
An Order in Council was
issued on the 27th of September last appointing the 1st October, 1923, as the date
on which the whole of the Act should take
effect so far as relates to the exercise of
powers and the performance of duties in
the general administration of education by
the Minister of Education or any other
Minister under the Act. The same date
was appointed for the commencement of
the local administration of education in
the County Boroughs, those sections of the
Act being brought into operation which
have enabled the Councils of the County
Boroughs to set up the necessary machinery. The administration of the Act, so
far as it is at present in operation, has
proceeded smoothly, and it is hoped to extend its scope in the County Boroughs at
an early date. The extension of the operation of the Act to the Counties in the
Autumn is under consideration. Certain
Rules and Regulations will be laid forthwith before Parliament under the provisions of the Act.
The absence of facilities in Northern
Ireland for higher education in agriculture
has for some time been a source of anxiety
to those concerned with agricultural development. The authorities of the Queen's
University of Belfast, acting in conjunction with the Ministry of Agriculture, have
King's Speech.
King's Speech.
11 MARCH 1924.
now decided to establish a Faculty of Agriculture within the University, so that
henceforth in the matter of agricultural
education in all its branches Northern Ireland will be an independent unit. At the
moment the development of the scheme is
handicapped by lack of suitable buildings
and equipment, but provision is being
made in the estimates of the Ministry of
Agriculture for a contribution towards the
cost of the requisite buildings and equipment.
In my speech on the prorogation of your
Parliament last November I stated that a
Bill dealing with the subject of Rent and
Mortgage Interest Restrictions would be
placed in the forefront of your legislation
in the ensuing session. A Bill is being prepared accordingly, but it has been thought
advisable to postpone its presentation until
consideration has been given to the measure now being introduced in the Imperial
Parliament on this subject.
If our agricultural produce is to hold its
own in face of increasing competition from
overseas it is essential that increased attention should be paid to the condition in
which commodities are placed on the market. The Minister of Agriculture is fully
cognizant of this matter and is taking steps
accordingly.
A measure will also be laid before you
which will provide for the disposal of the
sum granted by the Imperial Government
to the Government of Northern Ireland,
in final satisfaction of the claims made by
the Northern Government under the terms
of the Government of Ireland Act, 1920,
for the provision of buildings other than
Parliament Buildings or Departmental
Headquarter Offices.
This
will also contain proposals regarding the
measure
expenditure from the Exchequer of
capital moneys necessary for the construction and renovation of Constabulary Barracks in Northern Ireland, the dilapidated
condition of which has been a source of
constant anxiety to my Ministers, as well
as being detrimental to the health and
efficiency of the Police.
In accordance with Section 14 of the
Government of Ireland Act, 1920, the Parliament of Northern Ireland will have
power at the end of three years after the
date of its first meeting to alter the qualification and registration of the electors and
the distribution of members among the
constituencies. This period will expire on
the 5th of June next and the subject of
making such alterations is receiving the
careful attention of the Government.
Other important measures which will
engage your attention will include:-
An Illegitimate Children (Affiliation
Order) Bill, the object of which is to
make the putative father of an illegitimate child liable to contribute towards
the maintenance of the child.
A Pharmacy Bill to establish a Pharmaceutical Society for Northern Ireland,
and to carry out some necessary amendments in the existing Pharmacy laws.
A Bill to extend the principal sections
of the Imperial Dangerous Drugs Act of
last year to Northern Ireland, and thereby strengthen the existing safeguards
against illegal traffic in these drugs.
An Explosives Bill to extend the
provisions of the Imperial Explosives Act
of last year to Northern Ireland.
A Bill to simplify the procedure for
the appointment of persons to the Commission of the Peace.
A Bill to give security of tenure and
generally improve the position of the
staffs in the Offices of the Clerks of the
Crown and Peace throughout Northern
Ireland. The Bill will give effect to one
of the main recommendations of the
report of the Committee appointed by
the Lord Lieutenant in the year 1919 to
inquire into various matters connected
with the Crown and Peace Offices and
will bring about a long-needed reform.
A Char-a-banc Bill, the object of which
is to apply to char-a-bancs certain
provisions of the Town Improvement
(Ireland) Act, 1854, so as to give greater
powers of control to Town Authorities.
Debate on the Address. 8
King's Speech.
THE SENATE.
A Libraries Bill to transfer existing
powers of Rural District Councils under
the Libraries Acts to County Councils.
Members of the House of Commons.
The Special Committee, which by agreement with the British Government was
established under the Chairmanship of
Lord Colwyn has furnished a Report
dealing with the financial relations between
the Imperial Government and the Government of Northern Ireland up to the end of
the present financial year. Many matters
still await their decision in regard to the
future. Consequently, in the estimates of
expenditure for the ensuing financial year,
which will be laid before you in due course,
it will only be possible to present tentative
estimates.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I pray that Almighty God may bestow
His abundant blessing upon your deliberations.
Then His Grace was pleased to retire and
the Commons withdrew.
The House adjourned during pleasure.
The House resumed.

Ministry of Home Affairs:- (1) Return

Mr. SPEAKER acquainted the House that

Mr. SPEAKER having read the King's
Speech,
ADDRESS IN REPLY TO HIS MAJESTY'S
MOST GRACIOUS SPEECH.
Mr. LESLIE (in the uniform of a Deputy
Lieutenant of the County of Antrim): I beg
to move: That a humble Address be presented
to his Grace in the following words:-
Your Grace,
We, his Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave to
offer our humble thanks to your Grace for
the Speech which your Grace has addressed
to both Houses of Parliament.
a
full
Mr. Speaker, I am deeply conscious of
my own insufficiency to discharge the work
which lies before me, and I must pray of you,
my Lord, as of all the Right Hon. and Hon.
Senators who constitute the House to-day,
that of your clemency and accustomed
liberality you will extend to me
measure of indulgence and forbearance. 1
feel-I cannot but feel that I am very
highly honoured in being permitted to voice
this afternoon those heart-rooted sentiments
of loyalty to His Majesty the King, which
are entertained and cherished by every
member of this honourable House. And to
9 Debate on the Address.

11 MARCH 1924.
Debate on the Address.
usus hereas to all who think with
loyalty to our Sovereign implies also loyalty
to His Government of Northern Ireland, for
it is in the name and on behalf of His
Majesty that that Government acts here as
the Executive Authority.
We
The Gracious Speech records the fulfilment
of the hope expressed when the last Session
of this Parliament was opened, that the good
order then existing, and the respect for Law
then reviving might continue and prevail
throughout the Province. His Majesty's going
Judges of Assize are at present out on circuit,
and those who have studied the charges
which the Judges have delivered to the Grand
Juries of the Counties visited, and also the
charge delivered at the last Commission for
the City of Belfast will be able to appreciate
the solidity of the foundation on which the
record of fulfilment has been based.
thank Heaven that our own Governor has not
been disappointed in his own people, and we
are glad of the opportunity of recognising our
debt of gratitude to all those, from the
highest to the lowest, who are charged
with the protection of the lives and
properties of His Majesty's subjects, for
it is their exertions which have secured
the present general public peace. An
incident occurred in the streets of Belfast
within the last few days to which I cannot
refrain from drawing attention as showing the
present trend of public feeling. A man known
to be armed who was suspected of having
committed a serious crime had to be arrested
in the public street. The arrest was carried
out at some personal risk and with great
gallantry by a constable of the newly-formed
Royal Ulster Constabulary. That is in
accordance with the traditions of the ola
Royal Irish Constabulary, which
hope and believe the Royal Ulster Constabulary will carry on. But the point on which 1
desire to lay stress is that that arrest was,
in fact, facilitated by the action of the public
who happened to be present at the time. 1
feel before leaving that subject that we may
properly convey our most cordial congratulations to the right hon. Gentleman, the
Minister of Home Affairs, for he is the
Minister who is especially responsible for law
and order. His difficulties have been great,
and I think his triumph should be
acknowledged.
we
Reference is made in the Gracious Speech to
the Conference which, at the instigation of the
Imperial Government, has been convened to
discuss what is called the Boundary question,
and sundry other matters affecting the relations of the Irish Free State with Northern
Ireland. Those whom we represent will at
all times be willing to discuss points of
detail with their fellow-subjects in the Free
State, towards whom those in Northern
Ireland have no feeling other than that of
that
any
good will; but they are not prepared to
reopen those matters of principle which have
been settled by the Constitution under which
this Parliament assembles. The rectification
by mutual agreement of the actual frontiers
of recognised territorial entities for their
mutual advantage is one thing-the wholesale
surrender of populations, with the cession
of the districts they inhabit is another. The
distinction is appreciated and understood in
Northern Ireland, and it is not well that it
should be forgotten or obscured elsewhere.
I am convinced, as are, indeed, all of us
who live amongst the people of Northern
Ireland, that they will never tolerate
of their number should be
prejudicially affected by any agreement to
which they were not parties and as to which
they were not taken into council. Happily.
the interests of Northern Ireland are in safe
hands. It would not be possible to select more
vigilant wardens of our marches, more
redoubtable champions-should champions be
required of our Maiden City, than those
three distinguished Ulstermen who have
represented us at the initial stages of the
Conference. We have first the Prime Minister
himself, whose recent illness we so much
regret, and to whose complete recovery-with
that of Lady Craig-we so much look forward. And in addition we have the Noble
Marquess, the Leader of this House, and the
Attorney-General. I repeat that in those
hands we may safely leave our interests.
Passing to other matters, we welcome the
indications of expanding trade, and we join
in the regret expressed at the continuance of
unemployment. We shall very readily assent
to any proposals for the promotion of the one
or the alleviation of the other which are
brought before us with a certificate that the
liability and expenditure involved is within
the financial resources of the Province. We
are gratified to hear of the work of the important Commissions recently appointed, and
we shall not fail to give the most sympathetic
consideration to any suggested legislative
action which may be based on their several
reports. The members of this House will
take a particular interest in the proceedings
of the Commission which we understand it
is intended to appoint to inquire into the
practicability of reform as regards the administration of our Local Government, and
of the services thereto allied. Local Government is a matter of which many Members
of this House have a peculiar knowledge,
and on which they are specially qualified
to speak. There is, we believe, much work
to be done for the improvement of Local
administration, and it will be a great pleasure to all of us to give any assistance we
can in this direction.
We learn with pleasure that the recent
Education Act is coming into operation,
11 Debate on the Address.
Debate on the Address. 12
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie]
and that no difficulties in its application are
being encountered. I see no reason to depart
from the opinion which I ventured to express
when the Bill was before the House-that the
system it foreshadowed was admirably
framed and complete in every way and well
adapted for the purpose it was intended to
serve. I am aware that there is still some
misconception, even trepidation respecting
one
important matter, but I have confidence that experience and unbiassed consideration will show that the lines on which the
question I refer to has been dealt with are
not only right in themselves, but are also the
only lines on which it was possible for any
responsible Government to go. I trust that
examination of the Rules and Regulations
about to be laid before us will assist in allaying any honest doubt that may exist.
Numerous Bills are foreshadowed in the
Gracious Speech. One of them appears likely
to deal with a re-adjustment of our Parliamentary representation. If it should be
decided to make material alterations as
regards Parliamentary representation I
am
a
somewhat
sure that
radical
change resuscitating the
procedure of
a former day will not lack its advocates in
either House, or support outside. Of the
others, possibly those to which we shall
most cordially respond will be the Bills for
the further protection of the unfortunate,
but innocent, offspring of an unmarried
mother, and for the application to Northern
Ireland of the recent Imperial Statute extending the protection of the young as
regards employment in connection with the
manufacture and storage of explosives.
Such a provision is in reality a natural
complement of the Code regulating the employment of the youthful members of the community, of which no unimportant portion
is included in the Education Act to which
I have just now referred, and I congratulate
the Government on the proposition to enact
it-both on account of the intrinsic merits
of the proposal and because of the further
instance it affords of our determination to
work consistently in adopting all progressive
British Statutes.
We shall have many interesting problems
brought before us during the ensuing Session.
Each Bill will be taken on its own merite
when the time arrives, and will be considered
to the best of our ability, deliberately, without haste, for time is essential if a second
Chamber is to do the work for which it is
in being.
I have not attempted to exhaust
the topics mentioned in the Gracious Specch.
I know that my hon. Friend, the Chairman
of the Londonderry County Council, is about
to speak, and I believe that he intends to refer,
amongst other things, to agriculture, which
is our most important industry. I am sensible that it would be unbecoming to trespass
further on the patience of the House, but I
should like to express my thanks for the
consideration with which I have been heard.
I beg to move.
Mr. LONG (in morning dress): I beg to
second the Address in reply to the Gracious
Speech from the Throne, which has been
moved by Senator Leslie in such eloquent
and appropriate terms. It is a matter for
profound thankfulness, and one on which the
Government have to be congratulated, that
the Province has for the past year been in
the enjoyment of the state of peace mentioned
by his Grace the Governor. We have passed
through a trying time, but, thanks to a
strong and capable Government, security
of life and property have been assured and
our people are now enabled to pursue their
legitimate occupations without molestation
or fear.
In regard to the conference on the Boundary question it will be a relief to everyone
when this matter has been finally disposed
of. We have the fullest and most absolute
confidence in our representatives at the conference, and we exceedingly regret to note
the indisposition of our beloved and honoured
Prime Minister and trust that he may
speedily be restored to his wonted good
health.
Reference has been made in the Gracious
Speech to the revival in trade which has been
noted in some of our industries, and especially
linen. This is a most welcome change after
the period of depression through which we
have passed, and our earnest desire is that
this revival of trade may extend to other
industries as well as linen, as it will remove
the serious problem of unemployment which
has too long been in our midst, and thus
ameliorate the condition of our workers,
whose plight has been the cause of grave
concern to those in authority.
The decision to appoint a commission to
inquire into and report on Local Government, Public Health, and allied Services will
give the greatest satisfaction to those who
have been engaged in administering the
various Acts referred to. Several Commissions have inquired into this matter in
the past, but for some unknown reason their
recommendations have not been acted on. I
am of opinion that reforms can be suggested
which will be worthy of your consideration
and which will result in securing a more
efficient system while not greatly, if at all,
adding to the expense. I congratulate the
Government on their determination to deal
effectively with this matter in which reform
has long been overdue.
Debate on the Address. 14
Debate on the Address.
11 MARCH 1924.
It is with great pleasure we learn that the
local authorities have co-operated zealously
with the Minister of Labour in the promotion
of schemes for the relief of unemployment,
and on the other hand I can vouch for the
fact that the Minister of Labour has spared
no pains to inculcate in local authorities the
desperate position of the workless and the
necessity of proceeding with all possible
haste to put their schemes into operation. In
this connection I am pleased to be able to
say that the reconstruction of our classified
roads, on which about one million pounds is
proposed to be spent this year, should very
materially reduce unemployment.
It
The provisions for facilities for obtaining
higher education in agriculture will be
welcomed by the farming community.
must be remembered that at the present time
farming, to be successful, must be highly
scientific, and that farmers in the pursuit of
their precarious but interesting calling, and
in the marketing of the various commodities
they produce must meet and compete with
specialists in every branch of their business.
It is essential that we should have a place in
which to educate the youths of our Province
in the interests of themselves and of the
community. A decided want will be supplied
by the establishment of a Faculty of Agriculture in Queen's University.
We readily admit that in order to compete
successfully with the increasing competition from overseas our commodities should be
placed on the market in their most attractive
form, and we will willingly welcome any
steps the Minister of Agriculture may take
to ensure that we can meet the overseas
trader on equal terms. Agriculturists are
under an obligation to the Government for
the fatherly interest displayed in our most
important industry. I beg to second.
Mr. MONTGOMERY: I desire to support
the motion and to congratulate the mover
and seconder on their speeches. I do not
propose to move any amendment to this
Address, but I think it right to ask leave
to give expression to the great disappointment that will be felt by many industrious
and loyal subjects of his Majesty in
Northern Ireland that in this, in some respects, ample programme of legislation for
the coming session no room has been found
for an effort to redress a very serious
grievance, the redress of which is long overdue. I refer to the suffering of farmers from
the flooding of their lands from many rivers,
small and great, in the Six Counties.
has got worse and worse, owing to the increased drainage of the upper land farms
and the higher level of the rivers, and there
is no authority in existence to keep the outfalls open. There was legislation as long ago as
1842, and again in 1863, for the purpose of
It
enabling certain authorities to undertake
some drainage works, but by the operation of
the Land Acts and other things this legislation became unworkable and inoperative.
There was the Royal Commission on Irish
Public Works that went into this matter
under Sir James Allport, in 1887. That
Commission considered the need there was
for numerous and extensive drainage works,
and reported that new legislation was
necessary because the old legislation
was absolutely inoperative.
Nothing
was done, although some of their recommendations were adopted by Mr. Arthur Balfour
in some separate Drainage Bills brought in
by him, but which I think did not pass.
The failure of the Allport recommendations
was principally due to the fact that there
was no local body that could be brought
into the arrangement for these new drainage
districts. In 1905 and 1906
Viceregal
Commission sat under the chairmanship of
Sir Alexander Binnie and went to a great deal
of trouble in taking a large amount of
evidence. They agreed on a very excellent
report, but again nothing was done.
a
Now the farmers and those interested consider it is high time something was done on
the lines of that report. I do not think this
is the occasion to go into the details of the
grievance or into the details of the remedy
that is desired. These reports, and all the other
information necessary, are at the disposal
of the Government. The wet season has
brought the discontent in the affected districts to a head and there is an urgent
desire that something should be done.
I
venture to hope that it is not too late to
introduce some legislation in the present
session that will at any rate make a begining, so that these unfortunate people may
not suffer any longer. Of course any schemes
will have to have the sanction of the County
Councils and of some new drainage authority which, as far as I can see, ought to be
the Ministry of Home Affairs. At present
there is an absolute barrier against getting
anything started, and I do hope something may be done to remove this barrier
and an effort made to start some schemes
which will be considered on their merits,
and which will do something to relieve the
great suffering and loss now being borne.
What makes the matter all the more urgent
is the fact that part at any rate of the land
rendered useless by these floods is the very
best alluvial land in the whole of the Six
Counties. The top consists of five or six
feet of alluvial soil. With these few remarks I beg to support the motion.
THE MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): We find ourselves
at the opening of another session and in
Debate on the Address.
Debate on the Address.
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
I
justly deserves. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
We can only hope that in the time he is
absent from Northern Ireland he will not
be overwhelmed with the worries that are
inseparable from his office, and that he will
return to us in a comparatively short space
of time refreshed and renewed in health.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
process of considering, as we did last year,
the Gracious Speech which his Grace delivered from the Throne this morning.
hope I may be allowed to congratulate the
mover of the Address to his Grace and also
the seconder for the eloquent speeches with
which we have been favoured on this
occasion. The hon. Senator who moved this
Address is no stranger to this House, and,
I am glad to think, he is a distinguished
and a frequent contributor to our debates.
But he is not only a conspicuous Member of
this House; he is also one of those who
devote a great deal of time and attention
to Local Government, as he is a Member of
the Antrim County Council, also a Deputy
Lieutenant of the County, and a Justice of
the Peace. I am sure that we always listen
with great pleasure to his speeches, and I
hope that having moved an Address to his
Grace will not deter him from joining in
our debates in the future.
The hon. Senator Long, whom I wish also
to congratulate on the speech he has delivered in seconding the motion, is also, I
am glad to think, well known in this House.
Senator Long has enjoyed a lengthy and a
distinguished career as County Councillor,
and at the present moment he is, as we
know, Chairman of Londonderry County
Council. He has often made important contributions to our debates, and in every one
of them he has shown the instructed knowledge which he possesses, and has been able
to give us the benefit of his wisdom and
his experience. I need hardly say that I
hope he will go on giving us the benefit of
his advice. I should like to add that Mr.
Senator Long has attended this House at
great inconvenience to himself, and I think
that shows the public spirit that imbues
him and all the Members of our House in
the way they come up for debates and attend
our deliberations.
Since we met for the first time last year
there have been a great number of changes,
and I suppose the most important is the
change in the Government which has taken
place across the water. I do not feel that I
should be called upon to discuss that question on this occasion, but it naturally is
one of great interest to us. Our desire is,
as it always has been, to keep as close to
Great Britain as we can possibly do, and we
do most sincerely hope that we shall receive
the same friendly consideration from the
Government at present in office as we were
able to obtain from the late Government.
It is a matter of regret to all of us that the
Prime Minister (Sir James Craig) has been
overtaken by illness, and I am sure that we all
feel gratified to learn that he has been persuaded to take it is not a prolonged holiday,
but it certainly is a holiday that he most
There are many matters on which I will
venture to trespass on the indulgence of the
House, but I will make my remarks as
brief as I possibly can. My right hon.
Friend (Mr. Montgomery) has spoken of the
question of drainage. I should be the last
person to quarrel with him, or to find fault
with him, for having brought forward this
most important question. I think that he
is aware that drainage is a matter that
comes under, or rather is connected with,
the Ministry of Agriculture, the Ministry of
Home Affairs, and also Finance. All these
Ministries are both directly and deeply concerned. But my right hon. Friend the
Minister of Finance is at the present moment
investigating the whole of this important
question with a view to introducing legislation in connection with it in the future.
Now these are what I may call the unattractive answers which members of the Government have to give in response to questions.
I
My right hon. Friend told us that
there is a great deal of work foreshadowed
in the Speech which has just been delivered
from the Throne, and amongst the measures
which are to be introduced there is no
measure which deals with the question of
drainage. I am bound to say he is perfectly
right. He has told us that it is a question
which came up on many occasions in the
past. It is a question which exercises all
our minds and especially the minds of those
who are assailed as we are by this calamity
in different parts of Northern Ireland.
should like naturally to hold out hopes to
them that comprehensive legislation would
be introduced mitigating the hard conditions
under which many farmers exist, and also
for improving that portion of Northern
Ireland of which he speaks as having so
valuable an alluvial soil. I can only give
him the undertaking, which I have taken
steps to obtain within the last few moments
from the Minister of Finance, that this is a
question which is engaging the attention of
the Government, and the Minister of
Finance, at all events, hopes that it will be
possible for him to introduce legislation on
that particular subject.
The matter of the conference has also been
mentioned in the Speech in which his Grace
says that a suggestion was made by the new
Imperial Government that there should be
a conference to consider the Boundary question. As Members of the Senate are aware,
17 Debate on the Address.
Debate on the Address.
11 MARCH 1924.

this Conference has been sitting and considering one of those questions which,
aturally, are uppermost in our minds.
That conference has now been postponed
until it will be possible for the Prime
Minister to take his place in it. These Conferences, I know, generate a certain amount
I
of uneasiness and suspicion in the minds of
our friends in Northern Ireland, but I am
prepared to give them the assurance, which
I am glad to think both my hon. Friends in
their speeches indicated there is no need to
give-but I am glad to give the assurance
that they can rest contented that no steps
will be taken in their name without their
opinions having been ascertained as to the
wisdom and adequacy of these steps.
think I am justified in saying that the Prime
Minister enjoys the confidence of the great
majority of the people in the Six Counties. We
know quite well that he is imbued with the
Ulster spirit, and that he knows what is
good for the Province in a higher measure
perhaps than anyone else; and he also is
well aware that he represents the great
majority of the people of this Province and
it is not likely that either he or his colleagues will undertake anything in the name
of the people they represent without knowing
that they are carrying out their wishes and
carrying the people with them in any steps
that they take.
The House will have learned from the
Interim Report of the Colwyn Committe,
which has just been published, the details
of information which, from time to time,
have been given to the House. I do not propose to enter at any length into a discussion on
the detailed results of the provisional award,
in view of the fact that the future contribution of the Province to the Imperial Services
is still undetermined. But hon. Members
will naturally be anxious to know what
effect the award will have on the first full
year of the Government's activities, and on
the current year just drawing to a close.
With regard to both these years the Committee recommended that the revenue from
the Land Purchase annuities, amounting to
about six hundred thousand pounds, should
be made available to us in full to meet expenditure of abnormal character undertaken
of our own initiative and our own responsibility. In effect the Committee departed
from the estimates of revenue and expenditure which were framed during a period of
great trade prosperity and calculated
contribution to the Imperial Services for
the two years on the basis of the actual
surplus of revenue (including the annuities)
over normal expenditure. By these arrangements it has been possible to meet our
obligations up to the present, and the experience so gained will enable us to discuss
our
a basis upon which the contribution for the
future can be determined. This and other
important matters of principle will come
before the Committee so soon as the experience of this completed year enables the
discussion to be resumed.
The Colwyn Committee has awarded us
four hundred thousand pounds in satisfaction of certain claims which were laid before
the Committee in respect of all-Ireland
assets transferred to the sole possession of
the Free State. This was necessitated by the
establishment of two Governments
Ireland.
in
It will interest the House to know that
out of this award we propose to provide a
training college for teachers at Stranmillis,
a police depot, the science building for the
faculty of agriculture, and also agricultural
schools.
The Committee has awarded an additional
grant of five hundred thousand pounds as a
final contribution by the British Government to the settlement of malicious injury
claims in Northern Ireland. This, with the
fifteen hundred thousand pounds previously
granted, makes a total contribution from the
British Government of two million pounds,
which it is hoped will meet some sixty per
cent. of all the claims which came before us.
I think the House will expect me to give
them some information as to the progress of
the promised Land Purchase measure in the
Imperial Parliament.
Mr. DICKSON: Hear, hear.
The MARQUESS of LONDONDERRY :
I think I am justified in saying that our
Ulster Members have certainly not been
lacking in the manner in which they have
pressed this important question upon the
Imperial Authorities. I learn that the
only hope of progress in the Imperial Parliament is that our Land Purchase Bill should
be regarded and should go forward as a noncontentious measure, based on the recommendations of the Percy Committee. I know
that there has been agitation here for better
terms than those recommended in that Committee's report, but, so far as we can judge,
there is no prospect of obtaining better
terms. Accordingly we think that unbought
tenants will be well advised to accept the
terms offered under those recommendations
and to seize the opportunity, which may not
recur for many years, of securing a measure
of land purchase which, though it may not
give them all they hope for, will at least
give them that security of tenure which
they have called for for generations.
My hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie) has alluded in
his speech to the recent addresses of the
judges of Assize to the different Grand
Juries. I am sure that we have all read
19 Debate on the Address.

THE SENATE.
Debate on the Address.
[Marquess of Londonderry].
these addresses with considerable satisfaction, and I feel that the Government may
claim some measure of credit for the peaceable conditions which have been produced.
The political situation is by no means yet
settled, and this is mainly the cause for
the continuance of the precautions adopted
by this Government. There is no more ordinary crime in Northern Ireland than there
is in Great Britain, but the maintenance
of extra police forces in Northern Ireland
is essential, if for no other reason than to
secure the public in Northern Ireland from
the possibilities of armed agression for political purposes.
Those are considerations which we
have kept uppermost in our minds and we
feel that the duty is imposed upon the
Government of considering the safety of the
population of this Province and the maintenance of law and order in our midst.
Ireland, as a whole, has not yet gained that
security from crime which we should all
like to see. The Ulster public are entitled
to look to the Government to prevent a recurrence of the outrages which took place
during the spring and summer of 1922, and
which the Government had such difficulties
in repressing. Security is no less necessary
for the protection of trade, the revival of
which is so essential to deal with the problem
of unemployment.
In respect of the strength of the police
force a gradual reduction in the numbers
of the Special Constabulary may be looked
for if the situation improves. But the reports which we have lately had from the
South are hardly reassuring, and so long as
the danger of disaffected elements entering
our territory exists, so long do we feel that
it is incumbent upon us to maintain our
constabulary at its present strength, in
spite of the heavy burden upon our finances.
The recent Report on the Jury System is
under consideration, but as it is a somewhat
voluminous document and also as it contains
certain very important recommendations it
will take a little time to come to a definite
conclusion upon those and the other recommendations which have been made to the
Government.
The Government are about to appoint a
Committee to inquire into the administration of Local Government, Public Health,
and the Poor-law services. The Educational
services are to be excluded from it. The
personnel and terms of reference of that
committee will be announced shortly.
With respect to Home Office Bills my right
hon. Friend, the Home Secretary, is laying
before Parliament several measures of legislation, amongst which perhaps the most
important are the Rent and Mortgage Interest Restriction Bill, Pharmacy Bill, an
Illegitimate Children Bill, and
Public
Libraries Bill, which I intend to introduce
in this House to-day.
a
Turning to the Ministry of Labour, I am
happy to be able to state that while the
figures of unemployment are still far larger
than they ought to be, there has been an
enormous reduction of them since the establishment of the Northern Government in
June, 1921, and I feel sure that the House
will join with me in congratulating my
right hon, Friend, the Minister of Labour,
upon this fact, which is after all no small
tribute to the efficiency of his administration. I will give the figures to the House.
They are very remarkable:-
In June, 1921, there were over 63,000 insurable persons totally unemployed, while
another 45,000 were on short time.
By September, 1922, those numbers were
46,000 and 9,600 respectively.
The latest figures show 36,000 as totally
unemployed and 1,380 on short time.
This improvement is no doubt largely
due to the re-establishment of those settled
conditions of law and order throughout the
Province without which trade and industry
must languish and decline. At the same
time much must be attributed to the fact
that we have enjoyed remarkable industrial
peace during my right hon. Friend's tenure
of office, in spite of the fact that there have
been serious strikes in Great Britain and
Southern Ireland. I do feel that we should
value to its fullest extent the spirit of cooperation and compromise which exists
between our employers of labour and the
employed in Northern Ireland. It is a fact
of which we have every reason to be justly
proud. I do most sincerely hope that the
same spirit will be in operation across the
water in the very difficult times which seem
to be before us in the near future.
Much has also been done by means of
relief works. In Belfast alone relief work
covering an expenditure of over £2,200,000
has been sanctioned, and elsewhere in the
Province work to the value of upwards of
£300,000 has been put in hands. By these
means nearly five thousand men are directly
engaged on relief works, and their employment has its reflection in other trades which
produce the materials for these constructions.
Local authorities, not only in our capital
city but everywhere throughout the Province, have shown a splendid spirit of cooperation with the Government in tackling
the serious question of the relief of
unemployment.
The Loans Guarantee Acts have been of
Debate on the Address. 22
Debate on the Address.
11 MARCH 1924.
the utmost value, particularly in the shipbuilding industry, where nearly £3,400,000
of capital has been employed in productive
work under these Acts. It is hoped further
to extend the limit of safe guarantee during
the Session which is now before us.
The policy of the Government in regard to
unemployment insurance has been to keep
pace with legislation in Great Britain. We
have kept our workers in a precisely similar
condition to that of their brothers on the
other side of the water. The expenditure
has been very heavy. Since the first of
January, 1922, over three millions have been
paid in unemployment benefit.
Turning to the question of Agriculture,
which naturally is a matter of great interest
to the Members, the House will remember
that in October last the Prime Minister
announced that the Cabinet, with a view to
assisting the agricultural industry in its
present depression, had approved of certain
proposals submitted by my right hon. Friend,
the Minister of Agriculture for the expenditure of a sum of £54,000, for the development of agriculture under the following
heads:-
Agricultural Education.
Cow Testing.
Poultry.
Improvement of Pastures.
It will be of interest to the House if I
inform them that, in this connection of
progress made in regard to Agricultural
Education, Cow Testing and Improvement
of Pastures, agricultural overseers have
been appointed in each of the six counties.
Their duties will be primarily to advise and
instruct farmers in the more or less backward districts. In regard to Cow Testing,
farmers are rapidly becoming awake to the
value of milk recording. Whereas last year
there were only 17 Cow-testing Associations,
the number this year has increased to 34.
With a view to the improvement of pastures,
arrangements are being made for the establishment throughout the Province of
demonstration plots, where wild white clover
seed will be shown and the action of phosphatic manures in the direction of the improvement of pasture-land.
I am happy to state that prospects in
agriculture are good. The mildness and
dryness of the past month enabled the farmers to make considerable progress with
all classes of work in the field. Live stock,
though adversely affected by the poor quality
of the hay available for feeding, are expected
to improve with a continuance of the present
weather.
The continued and serious outbreaks in
Great Britain of Foot and Mouth Disease
have had their inevitable reaction upon
our cattle industry, but I am happy to be
able to state that my right hon. Friend (Mr.
Archdale) has been able to establish the
complete freedom of our herds from this
dreadful scourge and has succeeded again
and again in vindicating the health of Irish
cattle in the face of very determined and
repeated attempts in Parliament and the
public Press to impeach their immunity
from the disease and to attach to them
responsibility for the outbreaks. I think
we can congratulate my right hon. Friend
on what he has done in this direction.
Turning to Linen, which has already been
referred to in this debate, the increased
demand in American markets for linen
goods is reacting favourably on the demand
for the raw material, and the prospects for
flax-growers in the Province are at present
particularly bright. The potato trade, after
the bad season of 1922-23 is once again in a
healthy condition. It is a common complaint
of the tax-payer that when Commissions
and Committees of Inquiry are appointed
to investigate questions of public interest,
these Commissions and Committees regard
their task very seriously, spend much money
and time in collecting all the available
evidence, but that when the fruits of their
labours are presented to the Government
or to the Minister responsible, in the form of
a report, that report is too often pigeonholed, either indefinitely or for an unreasonably long time-
Mr. MONTGOMERY: Permanently.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:-
before it is transmuted into concrete results,
I sincerely hope the suggestion made by
my right hon. Friend (Mr. Montgomery) as
to drainage will not be dealt with in this
way. But, speaking as a taxpayer, I think I.
can call to mind occasions when there has
been ground for such complaints. Latterly
however, there has been conspicuously less
ground for such complaints. Within a few
weeks of the publication of the Linlithgow
Commission's Report a question in the Imperial House elicited a reply which indicated
that not only had the Imperial Ministry of
Agriculture already given careful consideration to the recommendations made by the
Commission, but had, in some important
instances, already taken action with a view
to giving effect to some of those
recommendations.
Our own Ministry of Agriculture in
Northern Ireland has evidently little to learn
in dealing with reports of Commissions. It
is only a matter of some weeks since the Com-
23 Debate on the Address.

THE SENATE.
Debate on the Address.
a
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
mittee under the Chairmanship of Lord
Charlemont, appointed to investigate the
natural and industrial resources of Northern
Ireland, issued as
special report on a
matter of urgency their report on the Egg
Industry of Northern Ireland. My right
hon. Friend, the Minister of Agriculture, so
far from shelving that report, has already
a Bill prepared for introduction to Parliament, a Bill which will be laid before
Parliament this Session. (Hon. Members :
Hear, hear.) This Bill is designed to give
effect to the more important recommendations of the Commission. In the Preamble
it is described as
“
a Bill for the Licensing of Wholesale
Dealers in Eggs, the Registration of Premises where Eggs are Preserved, the Better
Grading of Eggs by Wholesale and Retail
Dealers, the Better Prevention of the Sale
of Eggs Unfit for Food, and for other purposes connected therewith."
In the preparation of this Bill the Minister
of Agriculture has taken into consultation
not only the various trade organisations
affected, but also the best element in the
trade individually. He has also been helped
by the advice of an Advisory Committee, including some of the outstanding wholesale
dealers. I think hon. Members will therefore agree that this Bill has been drafted
under the happiest auspices, and my right
hon. Friend is looking with considerable hope
towards satisfactory results from this
measure.
The Minister of Commerce and also the
Permanent Secretary of the Ministry represented the Northern Government throughout the Imperial Economic Conference last
October. One of the features of the
Conference was the evolution by Ulster of a
scheme for the development of flax-growing
within the Empire, based on the commercial
bulking of bigger seed, which was performed
by the Linen Industry Research Association,
and such other seeds as might show a high
yield of fibre. This scheme has been approved by the Northern Government, and
is at present the subject of negotiation with
the Imperial Government. It is interesting
to note that whether this scheme as it stands
goes through or whether it does not, the
negotiations that have taken place over it
have undoubtedly aroused considerable interest in British Government circles and inside
the linen trade in Ulster, and authorities in
England have now informally suggested a
Treasury Committee on Flax Growing.
Until this matter has been officially dealt
with it is hardly possible to make any special
mention of it.
The same Ministry has been engaged for
many months in organising an Ulster
Pavilion at the British Empire Exhibition at
Wembley. An excellent site has been
acquired, the Pavilion is completely erected,
and the decoration is now in process of
being carried out. Space has been allotted
in such manner as to make the whole exhibition representative of every phase of Ulster
life. I should like to take this opportunity
of saying that in my capacity of Minister of
Education I am taking the necessary steps
to enable a certain number of children from
the schools to go from Ulster to view the
Exhibition, and thus gain the knowledge and
instruction which they must necessarily gain
an Exhibition which represents
much of Great Britain and the Empire.
from
SO
In December last the Ministry of Commerce held their annual Foreign Samples
Exhibition, which was well attended, and in
which much interest was taken by commercial circles. It is hoped in each year to show
a full range of samples and other features
which will be of use to business men. In regard to commerce there is the question of
electricity, and I need only say that the
Electricity Commissioners, who are officers of
the Ministry, have been paying considerable
attention to the development of electricity
in Ulster. It may be of interest to note that
since the inauguration of a separate Commission of Electricity in Ulster, six districts
have installed electric plant and many other
districts have taken or are taking the opportunity of obtaining statutory authority
from the Commissioners.
As regards trade conditions there has been
an undoubted improvement in trade since
Parliament last met.
I think the linen
trade in particular is experiencing what I
might call an extraordinary revival, and a
very happy feeling of optimism prevails in
the trade. This revival makes the steps that
have been taken in regard to flax of all the
more importance and urgency, because if the
trade remains at a high level for any length
of time the industry will begin to experience
a shortage in respect of its raw material, and
consequently prices will increase. As regards shipbuilding, there is also ground for
optimism. The general anticipation was that
a revival in shipbuilding would lag at least
twelve months behind the revival in general
trade, but from conversations with those
in the trade it would appear that conditions
in the shipyards in Belfast are at the present
day much better than was anticipated.
Hon. Members are aware that the Northern
Ireland Joint Stock Companies' records were
25 Debate on the Address.
Debate on the Address. 26
11 MARCH 1924.
destroyed in the fire at the Custom House in
Dublin, but the companies have now complied with the requirements of the Companies
(Reconstitution of Records) Act, 1923, and
considerable progress has been made and is
being made under this Act. I should mention also that an Industrial Insurance Bill
on the lines of the Act passed by the Imperial
Government last year is in course of preparation, and will be introduced in the House
at an early date. There is also a Bill in
course of preparation on the lines of the
Mining Facilities Act passed by the Imperial
Government last year. The object of this
Act is mainly to grant facilities for mining
in cases where there is danger of minerals
being left unworked, and also to give the
necessary surface rights to mineral workers.
I have left to the last the question in which
I am personally more particularly interested.
That is the question of education. We had
so much of education in the last Session
that I do not feel I should be entitled to
dilate on that subject at the moment, but I
will ask your indulgence to say a few words
in connection with what I may call the
controversy which has been going on in the
Press and in various other ways during
recent days. This education controversy
has raged in varying degrees ever since the
Act passed through both Houses last Session,
and a demand has been made for an Amending Act to alter the decision at which both
Houses of Parliament had arrived. The
main attack on myself made by my critics
is concentrated on the appointment of
teachers in provided and transferred schools.
I have taken every opportunity in public
of enunciating the policy of the Government.
The system I know all will agree is a
democratic system, and until it has been
tried and found wanting there is no case for
the amendment of an Act before it has come
into operation. I make no complaint of
criticism, but I regret the misunderstandings which have arisen for the simple reason
that many of those who have made attacks
on myself and the Ministry have not taken
the trouble to read the Act and to compare
the provisions made for religious instruction
in the Act with the religious instruction
which was given under the system which the
Act has superseded. The facilities, formerly
secured by practice but now secured by
Statute, are exactly the same, and it does
me that the Churches have
as great an opportunity as they had in the
past of taking their full share in the future
and of using the same instruments as in the
past in spreading the knowledge of religious
subjects under the facilities and opportunities which are accorded by the Act.
seem
to
I should like to draw the attention of the
leaders of the Protestant Churches to the
very remarkable words which were spoken
by the late Dr. Leitch, a most distinguished
and learned member of the Presbyterian
Church. My right hon. Friend (Mr. Montgomery) will remember him. I will quote
from his speech to the General Assembly on
the 7th of June, 1897, on the occassion of his
assumption of the duties of the high office
of the Moderatorship. He said:-
"The attitude of the Presbyterian Church
towards every proposal to denominationalise
State Education in Ireland in schools or
colleges is well known. It has been one of
continuous and consistent opposition.
That attitude we mean still to maintain.
Not that we commit ourselves to any principle that secular apart from religious
education is ideally the best. We are very
far from being secularists in education.
But we hold that in the circumstances of our
country, with its religious divisions and
its wide separations of creed and race, the
State ought to provide only united secular
education for all its subjects, and to leave
to the various churches to provide religious education for their own members."
Those are the words of the Moderator in
1897, That is exactly the position under the
Act. Have the Protestant Churches SO
entirely changed their position on this
important question in recent years?
I am laying on the Table in both Houses
regulations which deal with religious instruction. All these doubts that the local
authorities-which 1 am hoping will very
shortly be set up will deliberately flout religious opinion in the exercise of the power
of appointment of teachers, and also that
teachers will deliberately refuse to assist in
religious education, is something which I
cannot and will not believe. We have seen in
the past what public bodies have done not only
in this country but also across the water;
and I do not know that we have any reason
to complain of their work. I cannot believe
that teachers, who have hitherto given religious instruction in the schools will feel
that, under the Act, there is no moral obligation upon them to do so in the future. 1
am certainly sorry to feel that both in
speeches and also in letters anonymous and
otherwise in the Press, the occasion
has been taken by the Churches who, in
their desire to change the Bill according to
their own ideas, have gone far towards
creating a gulf between the Churches and
the teachers, which I hope both will do
their best to bridge at the earliest possible
opportunity.
We have made a start by bringing the Act
into operation as it affects two county boroughs, and I need hardly say that we are
very gratified by the results already
Debate on the Address.
Debate on the Address.
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
achieved. The Belfast Education Committee has begun its work in earnest, and
under the able and energetic guidance of
Alderman Duff has made great headway.
The machinery for the local administration
of education in Derry is also completed and
I have every hope of equally satisfactory
results. It is my intention to advise his
Grace to put the Act into operation as
regards the counties in October. The reason
for the slight delay is that the existing
county councils go out of office in the course
of the next few months and I have thought
it advisable to leave to their successors the
duty of putting the Act into operation.
I have ventured to deal with the questions
which will come before us in both Houses in
the present session. We have already had experience of nearly three years of self-government, and I do not know that we have any
reason to complain that we have not moved
forward in the arts of government and also
in the progress which we prophesied for this
Province. I certainly think that we owe a
great debt of gratitude to our Prime Minister,
Sir James Craig. I do not know of any individual who could have undertaken the
duties which he undertook so loyally and so
willingly. I do feel that for the manner in
which he has guided us through our difficulties
we owe him a debt of gratitude. We have
certainly overcome many difficulties, and we
have certainly not come to the end of them.
He would indeed be an optimist who would
feel that the years ahead of us are simple and
will be gone through without many of the
difficulties which we have sought to overcome
in the past. I most earnestly hope that the
people of the Six Counties will realise in the
future, as they have done to a very large
extent in the past, the duties and responsibilities which rest upon them as citizens of
this self-governing Province.
Sometimes I am inclined to feel that the
task of this Government would be easier if we
had a strenuous, militant Opposition sitting
opposite to us in both Houses of Parliament.
I am inclined to think that without that
Opposition many of our friends, in fact our
own stalwarts, are inclined to criticise the
Government, not only on the merits of the
measures, with which they may perhaps disagree, but also by reason of the fact that they
think there is no danger in attacking the
Government, because at the present moment
there appears to be no other Government to
take its place. While I am not one of those
to complain of any justifiable criticism, I
venture to suggest to those who are anxious
that we should pass through all the difficulties with which we are faced at the present
moment should not use every weapon
that they can possibly find to defeat
and undermine the Government, because that
is the way we shall be frustrated in carrying
this Province to the normal state of a selfgoverning community in which there are a
Government and an Opposition; and in the
controversies which exist between a normal
Government and
normal Opposition we
always hope that excellence of Government
will eventually be derived.
a
But I am not saying these few words with
any misgivings in my heart. I feel that
as we have triumphed over many difficulties
which have assailed us in the past so we shall
go forward and deal with many problems with
which we shall be faced in the future in the
same spirit which has brought us through to
the position we have reached at the present
moment.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the said Address be presented to his Grace by Privy Councillors of
Ireland.
BILL PRESENTED.
PUBLIC LIBRARIES BILL [S]
"to amend the Public Libraries (Ireland) Acts,
1855 to 1920, in their application to Northern
Ireland and for other
purposes incidental
thereto," presented by the Marquess of
Londonderry, read the First time and ordered
to be printed and to be read the Second time
on Thursday the 20th instant. [No. 1.]
Adjournment
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that this House do now adjourn.
As far as I am aware there will be no Government business before this House until March
20th, so I propose that this House do now
adjourn until March 20th.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Five
Minutes before Five of the
Clock until Thursday, 20th
March.
Papers Presented.
Public Libraries Bill.
20 MARCH, 1924.

Thursday, 20th March, 1924.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
1. Ministry of Commerce.-Report by the
Ministry of Commerce on Piers and Harbours
in Northern Ireland.
2. Ministry of Labour. - The National Health
Insurance (Insurance Committees) Amendment Regulations (Northern Ireland), 1924.
3. Ministry of Education.-Regulations for
Parliamentary grants in aid of expenses of
Education Authorities, for the years 1923-24
and 1924-25.
Laid before the House (pursuant to Act),
and ordered to lie on the Table.
