ORDERS OF THE DAY.
Public Libraries Bill [S].
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I beg to
move that this Bill be now read the Second
time.
The MINISTER OF HOME AFFAIRS (Sir
Dawson Bates): In the unavoidable absence
of my right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Education, and Leader of this House, it devolves upon myself as Minister of Home
Affairs to ask the indulgence of hon. Members
while I endeavour to explain the policy of
the Government in bringing forward this
measure, and to acquaint the House with the
provisions of the Bill itself. In asking the
House to consider this motion I would venture
to remind hon. Members of the discussion that
took place on the occasion of the Second
Reading of the Education Bill last session.
The hon. Member (Senator Greer) then expressed his disappointment that the Education
Bill included no provision enabling the County
Councils, the Education Authority, to strike
a rate for public libraries. The hon. Senator
was of the opinion that libraries are
not inconsiderable means of education." He
said:

a
"They take the place to some extent of
continuation schools, and if, when children are at school the teachers can only
get them to acquire what I might call the
reading habit, if they begin to read for
themselves and to enjoy reading, I think
they will derive very great benefit from
those county circulating libraries."
He then gave the House some most interesting figures showing the progress of the
circulating library which had been established in County Antrim under the Carnegie
Trust. In the first year there had been just
under 13,000 issues of books. He went on to
say that he thought it would be a very desirable
thing in the interests of education if the
County Council were empowered to contribute a rate, say, not exceeding a penny in
the pound for the purposes of supporting such
libraries. He quoted Mr. Clynes, the Labour
Minister, who attributed his success in life
to the opportunity he had of enjoying the
use of a circulating library when he had
Public Libraries Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Sir Dawson Bates]
been obliged to leave school at the age of
The hon. Member concluded his
twelve.
speech with the expression of the hope that
if the legislation he desired could be brought
under the Libraries Acts alone, and not
under the Education Bill, the Government
would see their way to introduce a measure
for this purpose.
In the course of his reply my noble Friend,
the Leader of the House, assured the hon.
Senator of the sympathy of the Government
with his proposals, but he pointed out that
he felt that his wishes could be realised more
appropriately under a Libraries Act than the
Education Act. Perhaps the House will forgive me if I repeat what the noble Leader
said on that occasion. I will quote his
words:-
a
"I am not sure really whether libraries
do not bear more of
recreational
character than an educational; or rather,
both educational and are
they
we,
reand
as the stern
creational,
administrators of education, I think, must
confine ourselves to the educational services
and leave the services rendered by the
public libraries to the Libraries Acts.
There is also the point, which I cannot
believe has escaped the hon. Member, and
that is that the County Councils would have
a very strong inducement to hand over their
libraries to themselves in their new capacity as Education Authority, and by doing
so would be able to claim relief of local expenditure on the libraries by calling upon
the State to pay two-thirds of the grant.
I do not know if that point has struck the
hon. Gentleman, but it is a very forcible
one which I think an overburdened taxpayer might raise in the interests of his
fellow sufferers."
The Bill before the House to-day is the redemption of the pledge that was then given
to my hon. Friend that his proposals should
It has been drafted
receive consideration.
as the result of consultation between the
Ministry of Education and the Ministry of
Home Affairs. I would like briefly to refer
to some of the Acts dealing with public
libraries in Ireland. The first Act of importance applying to Ireland in this respect
is the Public Libraries (Ireland) Act of 1855,
which empowered Municipal and Town
Authorities to raise a rate not exceeding 1d.
Certain
in the £ for Library purposes.
amendments of this Act were made from time
to time later, but the most important change
was that effected by the Libraries Act of
1902, which empowered Rural District Councils to adopt the Act of 1855 and to become
the Library Authorities in their respective
districts.
Consequently the situation in Northern Ireland at present is that we have some 35 or 40
Municipal Authorities and some 30 Rural
District Councils empowered to act as Library
Authorities. But I regret to say that few of
the Local Authorities have taken advantage
of their powers in this respect, and it would
not be an exaggeration to say that, in so far
as the Rural Districts are concerned, not
more than seven or eight of the Councils
have adopted the Act of 1855. The outstanding exception is County Antrim where, I
think, five out of the seven Rural
District Councils have
adopted the
Acts. I should mention that as the
Libraries Acts at present stand, Rural
District Councils and Urban Authorities
and towns have the power to combine
with other Authorities for the purposes
of carrying out
Library scheme,
but beyond
this somewhat remote
possibility of
combination of rural
districts within county, there is no
possibility of carrying out any large county
circulating scheme such as my hon. Friend
referred to in his speech. It does seem to the
Government that if we are to have a really
useful public library service throughout the
county districts we must proceed with a
scheme that has
considerably larger
financial basis than a single rural district, or
a combination of, say, two or three such
districts. I do not think it is necessary for
me to enlarge upon that; it will be obvious
to the House without my going into further
detail.
a
a
a
a
Before I go into the details of the measure
I hope the House will forgive me if I make
a short quotation from a most interesting
leading article which appears in the current
number of the "Times Educational Supplement." It gives in the most concise form the
present trend of instructed public opinion
on this important subject.
"No national system of education can
be efficient that is not co-ordinated to a
From the
national system of libraries.
moment when a student begins to think
for himself he must have access to an
appropriate library, since otherwise he will
waste his time in discovering what has
already been discovered, or will drift into
wrong or fruitless thinking. Half-educated
thinkers are often a danger to themselves
the community.
Ten years
ago Denmark suddenly realised the need
of the co-ordination of education and the
library system of the country. To-day the
library organisation in Denmark has succeeded in making every book in every
public library of the country accessible to
every citizen even in the remotest country
district. We in England have a great
population and the difficulties of communior
33 Public Libraries Bill.

20 MARCH, 1924.
Second Reading.
The
cation in rural areas are substantial. It
is a hard task to bring the books that
they need to the doors of the people. But
recent developments in all parts of the
country show that this difficulty can be
overcome if the demand is created, that
is to say if people are taught to know
what books are needful in the sphere of
life in which they move.
lack of co-ordination between the county
and the various other library authorities
makes the educational progress very slow.
Co-ordination within the
county controlled by the local education
authority is essential. At present a small
library authority can do very little despite strenuous efforts. The area is too small
to provide a sufficiency of books and skilled
advice. On the other hand, a very small
county rate can supply by careful distribution all the books and all the advice
that the same area needs.
"
"A real hunger for learning is as much
a note of our time as it was of the Renaissance, and librarianship as an educational
force is at last coming into its inheritance."
I do not know that any words of mine could
have put the case for the County Council as
a library authority as against the Rural
District Council more clearly: The proper
performance of the duties of public library
authorities is becoming more, and more
pressing every day. If these duties are not
carried out properly, if the question of the
provision of public libraries is approached
in any petty or parochial manner we run a
serious risk that the whole advantages of
our new system of education will be unduly
curtailed and the opportunities of progress
afforded by that system will be retarded in
their development. In the opinion of the
Government, it was never well advised to
lay these onerous duties upon the shoulders
of the Rural District Councils. It was an
extravagent system in the first place, but,
beyond all that, it was a system under which
it was extremely unlikely that a proper provision of public libraries could ever be
afforded to the people. Quite apart from any
other argument, the education authority is
the County Council by our recent Education
Act and the co-ordination in regard to
education and the library services will be
easy if the County Councils are also created
the library authorities as a result of the Bill
at present before the House.
The Bill itself requires but little in the
way of explanation.
Clause 1. Sub-section (1) empowers County
Councils to adopt the Public Libraries
for all or any of the Rural Districts in
their county and to be thereby the
Library authority in place of any Rural
District Council in the area. Sub-section
(2) withdraws the powers of Rural Disa
trict Councils from those bodies and
provides that on the lapse of their
powers the County Council shall carry
on any public library scheme in
Rural District which has already
adopted the Public Libraries Acts.
Sub-section (3) simplifies the arrangement
with regard to borrowing for Library purposes and excludes from the borrowing
limitations, prescribed in the Local
Government Act of 1898, loans raised for
Library purposes. Sub-section (4) is consequential upon the substitution of the
County Council as the Public Library
authority for the Rural District Council.
Clause 2, Sub-section (1) provides for an
agreement between the County Council
and any urban or town authority whereby,
with the approval of the Ministry of Home
Affairs, the County Council may take over
the powers and duties of library authority
for the urban district or the towns concerned. Sub-section (2) merely provides
an opportunity for the rescission of such
an agreement in the event of the parties
to the original agreement deciding that it
would be for the better enforcement of the
Public Libraries Act for the powers and
duties to revert to the urban or town
authorities.
Clause 3, Sub-section (1) provides for the
striking of a Libraries Rate as part of the
Poor Rate. Sub-section (2) limits the
amount to the produce of a penny rate
over the county districts and towns incorporated in the Library scheme, with the
proviso that where the area contains an
urban district or town the Ministry of
Home Affairs may consent to the limit
being raised to threepence in the pound
over the urban district or town. It will
be remembered that at present in urban
districts and towns the limit is threepence,
and it is therefore thought desirable to
retain the higher limit of the rate to be
levied over districts of this type in a joint
scheme.
Clause 4 provides for the transfer of property, rights and liabilities where the
County Councils take over schemes from
rural districts or from urban districts and
towns. Sub-section (1), paragraph (c),
provides for re-transfer in the event of a
rescission of an agreement which has
been made under sub-section (1), Clause 2.
Clause 5 enables the Library authorities in
general to take advantage of the existence
of voluntary agencies in carrying out their
powers and duties.
Clause 6 deals with the short title, the construction of existing Library Acts, and the
application of the present measure.
B
Public Libraries Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Sir Dawson Bates]
I think that covers the ground, and explains
the policy of the Government on this measure.
Mr. GREER: I am sure every Member of
the House is pleased to see the Minister of
Home Affairs here to-day, and I must congratulate him on the lucid explanation he
has given of the reasons for this Bill being
brought forward and also of the terms of the
Bill itself. As he has stated, this is a matter
in which I have taken a deep interest for
some time past. I moved an amendment to
the Education Bill, and on that occasion the
Leader of the House, the Minister of Education, promised that if possible a Bill would
be brought in to grant the powers I asked
for. He has now redeemed his promise by
bringing in this Bill, which I need hardly
say I heartily welcome. The Bill, as my right
hon. Friend has stated, is a sort of corollary
to the Education Act. It will give facilities
to young people who have acquired the reading habit and who wish to go on and improve
themselves intellectually to do so, and that
I consider is a very strong reason why this
measure should be passed.
I do not think I need go into the Bill in
detail as the Minister of Home Affairs has
already explained it Clause by Clause, but
there are a couple of points to which I would
like to call attention. The first point is that
it is by no means a mandatory Bill. It
merely gives the Council of the County
power, if it wishes, to undertake the duties
of maintaining, or assisting in maintaining,
a public library in the County. The second
point is that it does not necessarily undertake
to maintain a library for the whole county.
It may satisfy the area to which the resolution extends to adopt for all or any of the
Rural Districts situated in each County the
provisions of the Libraries Act, and therefore
I should think that if any Rural District has
good reason for not wishing to have a Library
in its district, if it has an excellent Library
that is being well endowed and well kept up,
it may not be desirable that a rate should be
struck for maintaining that Library. Therefore such a Rural District could, no doubt, be
left out, but I think that in most counties
in the Northern Province the Rural Councils
will be very glad to have the books brought
to their doors.
Mr. SPEAKER: Will the hon. Gentleman
speak a little louder? I do not think the
Reporters can hear him.
Mr. GREER: I will do my best. I shall be
very much surprised if there is any objection
on the part of any Rural District to the passing of this Bill, and I have reason to believe
that every County Council will welcome this
power that is now given to them for the first
time. I should like to say that I am specially
interested in this matter because I happen to
be a member of the Committee of Management of the Carnegie Library for County
Antrim, which was founded under the
Carnegie United Kingdom Trust. I hope the
House will allow me to take a few minutes
to explain what this Trust is. The late Mr.
Andrew Carnegie established and endowed a
Trust for the provision, amongst other things,
of Libraries throughout the United Kingdom.
He set aside a sum of money which produced
a very considerable income, and the idea is
that every year certain counties shou'd be
asked to come into the Carnegie scheme. The
majority of the counties in England have
already done so as well as a large proportion
in Scotland, and now Ireland is getting its
chance.
I think that already seven counties have
come into the scheme. Under the scheme the
Trustees provide books, a repository fitted up
with a bookcase, book shelves, and so on.
They supply it with whatever number of
books they think might be necessary for the
inhabitants in the county, and they supply
an efficient Librarian and the necessary staff
for working the Library. They provide the
boxes for sending these books from the central
repository to all parts of the county, and they
pay for the carriage of these books. In fact
they make themselves responsible for all the
expense of starting the library and running it
for two years. At the end of the two years,
if the county council is prepared to undertake the maintenance of the Library from that
time on they leave with the library all the
books and fittings and everything they have
collected during the two years they have
managed it.
There is some urgency in this matter so far
as County Antrim is concerned because the
two years will be up in June next, and unless
we can assure the Trustees by that date that
the County Council will be prepared to take
over the Library and run it they may take
away all the books and fittings and leave us
as we were before they came into the county
at all. I am pretty sure that if the Antrim
County Council is given the power by this
Bill to strike a rate it will be very glad to do
So. I am speaking in the presence of two
important members of that County Council
who will correct me if I am wrong in that
statement, but if they do correct me all I can
say is that it is not the County Council I used
to know.
Mr. LESLIE: The rates for the year have
been struck.
Mr. GREER: I think it would be possible
to agree to strike an additional rate. The
County Council of Londonderry has agreed
if it is possible, to strike a rate for the support of the library which they are getting in
37 Public Libraries Bill-

20 MARCH, 1924.
Second Reading.
피
T
that county from the Carnegie Trust. As
regards the total cost, it is limited to a penny
in the pound, but I have been looking at the
reports of the working of the libraries in England and I want to quote one statement from
the report of the present year.
"The most noticeable fact is that the cost
is working out exactly as was expected.
The great majority of the schemes are found
to cost the product of a 1d. rate or less.
In only one county-a county which has a
very low rateable assessment-has the cost
risen to the equivalent of a d. rate."
So although the limit is a penny I do not
believe the limit will ever be reached. In our
own county the Librarian assures me that
the rate will not exceed a d. In order to let
the House see the use that is being made of
the Carnegie Library by the inhabitants of
the county I would like to refer to the report
which has just been completed to cover the
period from 1st March, 1923, to 22nd March,
1924. The Report gives the following statistics:-

With regard to the class and character of
the books that have been issued, some people
have stated to me that they were not in
favour of these libraries because it would be
found that readers only wanted novels, and
some only wanted novels of a kind that it
would be better they should never read. In
regard to that I should like to say that there
is an influential Committee of Management
appointed, consisting of the leading people
in the county, and they go into the question
of the books that are to be issued from the
library. They are very careful, and if they
find that any book has been issued that had
better not have a general circulation it is
immediately withdrawn. The following
figures will show the class of books taken
out:-
Philosophy
Religion
Sociology

a total issue for that year of 60,182 as against
30,000 for the previous year. The number of
readers was:----
Adult

6,641

a
making a total of 9,059. I think that from
those figures it will be obvious to everyone
that the Library has been a great success and
is doing a very great educational work in the
county. I am afraid that education in Ireland has not progressed as far as it has in
other countries. I had an opportunity not
long ago of talking to
French boatman from whom I had hired boats for
fishing and sailing, and I found that
he had at home an English Shakespeare, an
English Milton, and several of Scott's novels
in English, and he frequently read them in
order to improve his knowledge of English
and also because he enjoyed what he
read. I think that very few men in his
position in Northern Ireland could say that
they made use of such books as foreign
classics. I do think
library of this
description will provide the opportunity for
people who wish to improve their education.
a
It is not necessary for me to dwell on the
broad general question of whether these
libraries are educational or not, but I
would like to refer to a speech that was made
by the right hon. J. R. Clynes, M.P., who is
now a member of the Government of the
Imperial Parliament, at the Carnegie Rural
Library Conference which was held in 1920.
I shall take the liberty to inform Mr.
Fisher (the then Minister of Education)
of what great good I am certain this
movement (the establishment of Libraries)
is going to do for the cause of education
which he has at heart and if he, as Minister of Education, can do anything by
co-operation, by encouragement, by any
sort of stimulus of the activities of the
Local Authorities to join with you in this
work, I shall inform him that I think
it is his duty to the cause of education to
do everything which he possibly can.
If I tell you the source of my special
interest in this work I hope you will not
suspect me of having any desire to talk
about myself. I had to leave school,
half time, at 10 years of age, and to leave
school altogether before I was 12, and to
work hard from that time on, say, until
I was some 16 or 17 years of age. That
gave me the opportunity of absolutely forgetting everything that I learned at school.
About that age, from one cause or the
other, I discovered my state of absolute
ignorance, and there grew up within me a
desire to use any educational opportunity
in the district in which I lived.
Only about 100 people between the ages
of 16 and 23, which might be called the
educational period, out of each 1,000 in
this Kingdom are getting any education
of any kind. That is a very serious thing
for the State, a very serious thing indeed.
Public Libraries Bill—
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Greer].
Then he explains how he went on to get
these books and to read them aloud at home
and how he was able to educate himself in
that way. Then he goes on to say:-
Government has been handed down
throughout the ages from those who were
privileged individually to wield the authority of Government. It has passed through
grades and groups of the community until
now we have a democracy possessed of an
electoral power never thought of by our
grandfathers.
This is the passage I am most anxious to
call attention to:-
And it is a most serious thing for the
future of any country to have a democracy
which is not educated, to have a democracy
which is dependent upon the penny newspapers or the penny pictorial, or whatever
it may be, so that although your efforts now
may not be able to reach these millions
the work has to be begun, and no matter
what discouragements you feel in the
earlier stages of an effort of this sort, the
greater the discouragement the greater the
energy in that purpose or the immense usefulness of such purpose, is bound to benefit
the State as a whole.
1 entirely agree with the observations made
by Mr. Clynes, who certainly knew what he
was talking about. I do not think anyone
will dispute the fact that these libraries are
really educational forces in the districts
where they exist. I hope, therefore, that
the House will see its way to pass the Second
Reading.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to join with the
hon. Senator who has just sat down in
welcoming to our House the right hon.
Gentleman the Minister of Home Affairs.
He is to-day paying us a visit which although
his first, we hope will not be his last. We
are grateful to the right hon. Gentleman for
coming here this afternoon in order to
explain to us the details of the Bill which
has been presented. As to the Bill itself,
the first thing that will strike us is
that any proposal to establish a Central
Library in a county necessarily implies a
central control, and it seems obvious that
the only possible body to exercise that central
control is the County Councils. If only on that
ground we should welcome this suggestion,-
that the County Councils of Northern Ireland should be clothed with a power which
they do not at present possess.
Mr. GREER: I do not think that is so. 1
think there is a Clause which would enable
the County Council to contribute to any
voluntary Association, and will enable the
County Council to contribute to the Carnegie
Library just-
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry, but I think my
hon. Friend misapprehends what I said. My
suggestion was that under this Bill, when it
becomes an Act, County Councils for the first
time will have power to strike a rate.
Mr. GREER: I thought you said they would
have control.
Mr. LESLIE: If you are to have a central
Library supported by a rate you must have a
central authority controlling that Library,
and that authority must be the County
Council.
Mr. GREER: No. That is not so. I do not
like to interrupt my hon. Friend-
Mr. SPEAKER: I think it would be better
to allow the hon. Member (Mr. Leslie) to go
on with his speech.
Mr. GREER: If I can have an opportunity
of dealing with the point afterwards.
Mr. SPEAKER: You can deal with it on the
Committee stage.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think the question of
the power of rating, given or not given, as the
case may be, is of very great importance. I was
really only using the argument as showing
that County Councils should be admitted to a
measure of control. The position at the
moment is that by the Act of 1902 the Rural
District Councils have been given specific
statutory powers, and of those statutory
powers it is now proposed to deprive them. I do not know that any
Rural District Council in Ireland made
use of those statutory powers until
a
very short time ago, when, I think,
some five out of eight Rural District Councils
in County Antrim adopted what was called
the Carnegie scheme. They did adopt the
Public Libraries Act, but the Carnegie scheme
was at the back of their mind. The position
at the moment is this. The Ballycastle Rural
District Council were equally divided on the
question as to whether they should or should
not adopt the Acts, and therefore they did
not adopt them.
The Ballymena Rural District Council
adopted the Act, limiting itself to d. rate.
The Antrim Rural District Councils, which
had adopted the Act, on hearing from the
Gracious Speech that this Bill was to be introduced, passed a resolution of protest.
They did not, I think, wait until the Bill was
in print, but they passed a resolution of
protest and said they intended to withdraw
from the Carnegie scheme if certain things
happened. I am therefore inclined to think
that on the whole there may be some little
feeling on the part of Rural District Councils
that they are to some extent being coerced,
and it is most important that if possible that
feeling should be allayed. I think we must
have unity of control if the work has to be
done at all, but we have to induce the Rural
41 Public Libraries Bill-
Second Reading.
20 MARCH, 1924.

F
i

District Councils to accept the substitution
for themselves of the County Councils as the
authority. Actually the wording of the Bill
gives the County Council power to limit their
scheme to such Rural Districts as they wish,
and there is no reason to doubt that any
County Council will act reasonably, and it
will, I hope, take the Rural Districts into
its counsel before passing a resolution.
I think it is a matter for consideration
whether either here or elsewhere a suggested
amendment should not be put down on the
Committee stage in order that that aspect of
the question may be discussed. It would be
quite easy to insert words in the Act limiting
the exercise of the County Councils power
to those Rural District Councils who assent to
its being exercised for their area. I do not
think it would seriously militate against the
chance of the Act, nor would it prevent us
from requiring that any Rural District
Council that had once come under the scope
of the Act should remain, because if a Rural
District Council once comes in it must stay
in, otherwise the proceedings would be
altogether unfair. I think that is a question
which ought to be discussed later on.
I was very glad to hear from the hon. Member (Senator Greer) that a Committee had
been set up which would be responsible for the
examination of books issued in the Circulating
Library, because there has been complaint
in my own part of the County as to the
quality of some of the literature which was
available for circulation. Complaints were
made not only that the stuff was trashy in
itself but that it was positively pernicious.
However, I feel sure that the Committee, in
which Senator Greer takes such an interest,
will be able to protect the youth of the county
from any further circulation of the books I
have in mind. Senator Greer is too assiduous
a
student of our classic dramatists not to
remember the words which one of our most
distinguished Irish playwrights puts into the
mouth of one of his best known characters.
The House will remember that Sir Anthony
Absolute remarks to Mrs. Malaprop,
Madam, a Circulating Library in a town
is as an evergreen tree of diabolical knowledge! It blossoms through the year; and
depend on it, Mrs. Malaprop, that they
who are so fond of handling the leaves
will long for the fruit at last.
My hon. Friend will also remember the
kind of books that had just been hidden
under the cushions. We do not want to
banish classics from our public libraries, but
we do want to exercise some discrimination.
1 should like particularly to congratulate
the Government on the repeal of Section 6
of the Act of 1902, because that was
Section which, if one may say so, might have
got rather in our way in dealing with the
a
Education Act. It is the Section which gave
the County Council power to contribute to
public Libraries from their Technical Education money. I join with Senator Greer in
hoping that the House will give the Bill a
Second reading, for I am sure it will prove a
useful addition to our educational work.
Mr. BARBOUR: I am in thorough sympathy with the Bill, but I cannot help calling
the attention of the House to a tendency
which I regret to see is developing in the
legislation we are passing, and which was
quite marked in the legislation coming from
Westminster. That is to pile on our Local
Authorities work which they were never
designed to carry out. We will be told they
are carrying out this work well in England.
I am glad of it, but I have yet to learn
that the County Councils will be able to
maintain a supply of members who will be
able to maintain these constant increases
of demand on their time. It was with some
misapprehension, I feel sure, that the people
who thought seriously about this matter
agreed that the County Councils were the
proper people to form the Education Authority.
We are told now that because the County
Councils accepted the responsibilities of administering education they are to be the
Library authority. I wish the Minister of
Home Affairs was here that we might ask
him what other duties the County
Councils will have to take on their
shoulders as a result of having assumed the
responsibilities of the Education Act. 1
think everyone who is anxious about Local
Government in our Northern area, and who
is anxious that the standard of results we
have attained in the past should not be impaired in any way, will look with alarm at
the tendency existing to-day of putting on
the backs of the County Councils work which
the members are finding more and more
onerous. I believe that if at the head of
affairs there was someone who could
constantly remind the Ministry of
Home Affairs of the personnel of our
County Councils they would see there is
something in this danger which I see in
increasing the duties we expect them to perform. Generally, I think everyone will agree
that in our community we should supply
everyone with a ladder which will enable the
poorest in the land to develop his faculties
to the highest possible pitch.
I would be the last one to agree to condemn
our people to be hewers of wood and drawers
of water, and the argument that the facilities
proposed to be given under this Bill are open
to other parts of the United Kingdom is an
argument which is good enough to win my
support for the Bill. There is one thing
which interests me very much, and I wish
the Minister of Home Affairs, when speaking
of Denmark, had been able to supplement the
Public Libraries Bill-
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barbour]
little information he gave us by informing
the House whether the Danes had been able
to say that the supply of these libraries had
in any way stifled the undue trend of our
rural population and the consequent extra
flow from the countryside to the town. That
is a danger some of us see. It is deplorable
in England and it is a danger which exists
in Ireland. It is a danger that seems to have
accompanied the whole of our Western civilisation, and if it can be proved from experience that the supply of libraries to dwellers
in the rural districts tends to make rural
people more satisfied with their lot, then I
think these libraries should be more and
more established. I suggest that the dweller
in the town is very much interested in this
subject. Anything that will maintain a
proper balance between town and country is
worthy of the support of every section of
our community.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I should
like to say that the Minister of Home Affairs
has been called to another place, and he asked
me to apologise to the House for his absence.
He would have liked to have replied to this
debate, and I would suggest that those hon.
Members who have raised points in connection
with this Bill should again raise them on the
Committee stage, when I am sure the Leader
of the House will be willing and anxious to
deal with them.
On Question, Motion, agreed to.
Bill accordingly read the Second time and
committed to a Committee of the whole
House for Tuesday next.
Motion for Adjournment.
THE LATE SENATOR ADAM DUFFIN.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: Before
moving the adjournment I think it would be
the will of the House that I should express
our sorrow at the loss of one of our Members.
I refer to the late Mr. Adam Duffin. For a
short time prior to his death the late Senator
had not been a regular attender at the House
on account of ill health, but he was always a
very useful and respected Member of the
Senate. He was one of the oldest and best
known citizens of Belfast and he had reached
the ripe age of over four score years. During
a great many of those years he had taken a
deep and lively interest in education, both
in the higher and lower branches, and as a
citizen of Belfast there was no movement for
the benefit of the people in which he did not
join. I think it will be the unanimous desire
of Members of this House that we should express our sympathy with the relatives of our
late colleague and that we should record the
loss we have sustained by his death. I beg
to move that this House do now adjourn until
Tuesday next.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at fifteen
minutes past Four of the Clock
until Tuesday next.
Public Libraries Bill-
Committee.
25 MARCH, 1924.
THE SENATE.

Public Libraries Bill (S).
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Resolved: "That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill" (The
Marquess of Londonderry).
In Committee accordingly.
The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.
CLAUSE 1 (Power of County Councils to
adopt the Public Libraries Acts).
(1) The council of a county shall have
power, by resolution specifying the area to
which the resolution extends, to adopt the
Public Libraries (Ireland) Acts, 1855 to 1920
(in this Act referred to as "the Public
Libraries Acts") for all or any of the rural
districts situate in their county, and thereupon the provisions of the Public Libraries
Acts, other than sections one and two of
the Public Libraries (Ireland) Act, 1894,
shall, subject to the provisions of this Act,
apply with the necessary modifications as
if the county council were the library
authority for the purpose of the exercise
and performance of powers and duties
under the Public Libraries Acts for the area
specified in the resolution, to the exclusion of the council of any rural district
therein.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 1,
page 1, line 18, at end add
"Provided that no council of a county shall
include in such area any rural district
without having first obtained the consent
of the council of that district."
I put down this amendment in order to
ensure further discussion on the subject
before we finally part with the Clause as it
stands. It will be remembered that an invi
tation to bring up matters on the Committee
stage was given us on behalf of the right
hon. Gentleman the Minister of Home
Affairs, in order that we might have a some
what fuller discussion than was practicable
when we were taking the Second Reading of
the Bill. I may say at once that I am not
particular about the actual wording of this
amendment. It is the principle rather than
the detail on which I wish to dwell. I do
not think the amendment can fairly be
described as being opposed to the general
principle of the Bill. The Bill itself is an
enabling Bill of benevolent intention. As I
say, I do not regard my amendment as being
opposed to the principle of the Bill.

think it is rather a reasonable extension of
the expression, "all or any of the Rural
Districts" which is to be found in this Clause,
because I think those words plainly intimate
that a County Council, may, if it sees fit,
exclude one or more of its Rural Districts
from the operation of the measure. I do not
think that we should have felt any difficulty certainly I should not have felt any
difficulty in adopting the Bill as it stands
if we were embarking on a consideration of
this matter entirely anew. In that case I do
not think the Senate would make any other
suggestion, but we are to some extent hampered by existing legislation.
In 1902 the Imperial Parliament extended
to Rural District Councils the power to
Public Libraries Billl-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
adopt the Public Libraries Acts which had
previously been given to Urban District
Councils by the Act of, I think, 1894.
Parliament gave to Rural District Councils
the power to say whether they would or
would not adopt these Acts. The proposal
now is to take away that power and to give
it to another Council the County Council.
I
am not sure that there is very much
precedent for a proceeding of that kind.
There have been cases where Parliament
itself has decided to exercise powers previously entrusted to a local authority and
not made ase of. There was such a case
in the Notification of Births Act, 1907, a
useful measure passed by Parliament which
gave Local Councils, including County
Councils, a power of adoption. Parliament
in that case undoubtedly intended the power
to be made use of, but, speaking generally,
it was not. Consequently Parliament, some
eight or nine years later passed an extending
measure and did for local Councils work
which the local Councils should have done
themselves but did not do. I do not think
cases of that sort are fair parallels because
in this case the County Council is given the
power of saying whether a Rural District
Council shall be liable for a new rate which
will have to be paid principally by the
farmers of the rural district. The farmer will
pay the full penny, because the agricultural
relief would not apply to a case of that sort.
The farmer would perhaps wish to go to
the Rural District Council as the natural
body for him to make representation to.
And while it is now proposed to give the
County Council power to decide as regards
the rural district a similar power as regards
the urban district is not conferred.
The
proportion of population as between the rural
district and its neighbouring urban district
may be almost ten to one. I have figures
here showing that the population of the
Ballymoney Rural District is 22,000, while the
Ballymoney Urban District is only 3,100. The
population of the Ballycastle Rural District is
11,200, and the population of the Ballycastle
Urban District is only 1,400.
I am inclined to think that we have every
reason to be confident that in applying the
Bill as it stands the county council will act
reasonably and will pay due regard to all
representations made to it. It is fair to
remember that on any county council the
rural districts are directly represented, because under the Act governing county councils
the chairman of any rural district council is
ex officio a member of the county council, and I am disposed to rely very much on
the fairness and generosity of the county
council. But from the rural district point of
view I think we must remember that it is in
the interests of the county council to enlarge
its contributory area, and therefore a rural
district may not unreasonably prefer taking
the matter into its own hands.
I think the question of leaving the power
with the rural district councils or taking it
from them may cut both ways. In considering the matter we ought to remember
that the County Antrim, important as it no
doubt is, is after all not the only gem in
the six-leaved diadem of Northern Ireland,
and it is possible to have a public library
without the assistance of the Carnegie bequest. We are not dealing entirely with
the Carnegie bequest nor with the county of
Antrim. We have to look at the thing as
a whole, and it seems to me quite possible
that if rural districts are in any way cut
out from their share in the decision, not
to mention the administration, there may
be a tendency in some counties which would
result in this Act not being adopted by the
county council. I think that perhaps the
tendency not to adopt the Act would be
greater where there was a possibility of
something like coercion being applied to
rural district councils. We all desire that
this Bill should be a success, and we want
to take all the rural district councils with
us, and I cannot get away from the feeling
that what we want to use is the handle of
persuasion rather than the weapon of compulsion.
I hope that whatever view may be taken
to-day the rural district councils will all
fall in and join to make the Act a success.
But I do think we ought to consider
whether it would not be worth while to give
them some little loophole of escape if their
desire be such as I have suggested. I put
down the amendment in order that the
matter should be discussed, but we all know
now that another amendment will be brought
before us later which was not in being, or
was not drafted, at the time I put down my
amendment. I must admit that it is very
possible that the discussion on the amendment I am referring to will help us very
much in dealing generally with the Bill. I
hope that other hon. Members of the Senate
who are interested in rural district councils
will give us the benefit of their views on
this matter. I do not wish in any way to
press the actual wording of my motion, but
for the moment I beg to move it.
Mr. GREER: As the Leader of the House
is present, I should like to take the opportunity of expressing my thanks to him for
bringing forward this Bill in redemption of
a promise he made to me during the discus
sion of the Education Bill. I am very glad
it has been brought forward so early in the
Session, as I think it will relieve County
Public Libraries Bill-
25 MARCH, 1924.
Committee.
Antrim of a serious difficulty that might
have arisen with regard to the Carnegie
Library. I should like to say that, subject
to a few remarks I wish to make later on,
I heartily approve of the Bill and the way
it has been brought forward, but I want to
say a word or two with reference to the
amendment which has just been moved by
my hon. Friend, Senator Leslie. I should
be the last person in the world to accuse him
of deliberately putting down
wrecking
amendment, and I do not accuse him of it,
because for one reason he is himself a member of the County Antrim Library Committee, and I am sure shares the feelings of the
other members of the Committee in hoping
that this Library may be a continued and a
great success.
a
But I would like to point out to him and
to the House that the effect of the amendment, if it is carried, will be to wreck the
Act, because it will prevent it from coming
satisfactorily into operation. I think
we should be
careful very
to
do
It
nothing which would interfere with
the strong desire on the part of the
public to have these public libraries.
would be a great pity if anything we did
here tended to prevent the growth of that
feeling. I gather that the hon. Member
wishes to give rural district councils power
to say whether the Act is to come into operation in their districts or not. At first sight
that seems reasonable enough. Parliament
has already given them that power, and this
Act proposes to deprive them of it. I would
like the House to consider what is the body
to which the hon. Member wishes to give this
power of vetoing any desire on the part of
a county council to take over a particular
district. It is the rural district council itself, the very body that since the year 1902
has had the power to set up libraries in its
own district if it chose. It is the very body
that has shown it has no desire to have
libraries in its district. The Act would be a
dead letter if things were allowed to go on
as at present, and I take it that this Bill is
now being brought forward to remedy a mistake made in giving these bodies the power
referred to in previous Acts.
It is within the province of Parliament to
pass a Bill as drafted, and I submit that,
having regard to what had happened, it
would be a very great mistake to continue to
give the rural district councils power to prevent that Act operating in the rural districts. Has anything happened in the meantime to make them more anxious now than
they were in 1902 to have libraries? So far
as I know the only thing that has happened
is that it is now proposed to take the power
from them. They are up in arms against it.
Is it likely that if a clause is inserted in
this Bill giving them power to veto the operation of the Act, they will be more inclined
to put the management in the hands of the
county council than they were to undertake
the mangement themselves? I think it is
almost certain that none of them will consent
to hand over the powers they now have to
the county councils, and for that reason, and
in order to ensure that that Act will be successful, I put it to the hon. Member, Senator
Leslie, that he should consider whether he
will not withdraw his amendment.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: So far as I
am concerned, I have an open mind on this
amendment, but I would like to know if
there is any particular desire on the part of
the rural district councils for this legislation. The last speaker stated that the rural
district councils refused to put their powers
into operation, and it is now proposed that
these powers should be transferred to the
county council. But the county councils are
composed of the rural district councils. Is
this legislation that is being forced on these
local bodies when they do not desire it? If
so, I do not think it is fair. If they do not
wish to have the advantages of these libraries
the scheme should not be forced upon them.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: So far as I know, the
rural district councils have not refused to adopt
this library scheme, nor do they object to
libraries; because I do not think the matter
has ever been brought to their notice. Antrim
seems to stand on a footing by itself. For
some reason or other, it was brought to their
notice, and they have exercised their powers to
a considerable extent; but, so far as I know,
none of the District Councils in my part of
the Province has ever heard the subject mentioned. Whether it is the fault of the officials
or whether it is due to lassitude or other
causes I do not know. but the matter has
never come before their notice. I think that
if they were aware of the extraordinary benefits conferred by the Carnegie Trust scheme,
and the fact that all the books, a paid
librarian, and all the fittings, etc., are provided free, they would be willing to undertake
the work. At present, if there is any objection on the part of the Rural District Councils
to the County Councils taking their powers
from them, it arises from a soreness of feeling
that began a long time ago. It resembles the
feeling that exists between the House of
Commons and the House of Lords.
The Rural District Councils consider that
the County Councils are inclined to encroach
on their powers, and they have always been a
little restive. Of course, it is a mistaken
view, but it exists, and it has been accentuated
by the fact that the control of the roads bas
now been taken from the Rural District
Councils. I feel sure that if the consent of
Public Libraries Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Armstrong]
the Rural District Councils were asked to
their powers being transferred to the County
Councils it would be very difficult to get it,
because a good deal of hot feeling exists, and
perhaps the merits of the measure might be
forgotten or obscured. For that reason I do
not think the Act would work satisfactorily if
the consent of the Rural District Councils was
required as a preliminary to the establishment
of a library.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The hon. Member has introduced an amendment which I think we can say has led to an
interesting discussion. I expect that before I
give him my own reply to his amendment he
will probably have made up his mind that his
amendment would not be a valuable addition
to the Bill. He certainly dissociated himself
from any desire to move an amendment which
would have a wrecking effect on the Bill, and
I know he has no intention of doing that. He
has moved an amendment which he believes
may have certain merits that might be
improved, but I think Senator Greer, in a
short but forcible speech, showed him that the
amendment would have the effect of wrecking
the Bill and making it more or less a dead
letter. Senator Greer made a very graceful
allusion to the fact that the introduction of
this Bill is a redemption of a pledge I made
to him during the discussion on the Education
Act. It certainly is a redemption of that
pledge, which I was very glad to make.
The hon. Member, Senator Woods, in his few
remarks, made what I might call a Second
Reading speech. I have taken the opportunity of reading Hansard, and I do not see
that his name appears in the discussion on the
Second Reading. It seems to me that if he
feels this is a measure which no one requires,
and about which there is no great enthusiasm,
he should have gone into the Lobby in opposition to the Bill and registered his opinion in
that way. It was my misfortune to be detained in London as a member of another
Legislative Assembly, and this prevented my
being present when the discussion took place
on the Second Reading; but I was very ably
represented by my right hon. Friend the
Minister of Home Affairs, and if the hon.
Member reads the speech he delivered on that
occasion he will note that this is a measure
which is consistent and in accordance with our
progressive idea of education, and, from the
quotations which he made in his able speech
it is very easy to see there are many great
authorities who feel that the progress of legislation should be accompanied by a national
system of libraries. I hope that this Bill
will stimulate the desire there is at the present
time for circulating libraries, and that it will
be consistent with the very agreeable expansion there is in the desire for education
It is
amongst all classes of the community.
certainly the duty of those who are members
of the Government to satisfy that desire to the
very best of their ability.
a
The hon. Member (Senator Leslie) spoke of
using persuasion rather than compulsion, and
I am sure he realises that it is mainly the
duty of the Government to resort to methods of
persuasion rather than to bring into operation the iron hand of compulsion. This
Bill will go out as
measure which
will have the maximum of inducement and the minimum of compulsion.
The Government is unable to accept this
amendment, because, as I said, it cuts across
the principle of the Bill. The object of this
measure is to secure that the Libraries Acts
should be administered by the County Councils, in place of the Rural District Councils,
which except in a single county-County Antrim-have found themselves unable, I will
not say unwilling, to adopt these Acts. We
quite appreciate the difficulties which have
prevented District Councils generally from
taking action under the Libraries Acts. My
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Home
Affairs, speaking on the Second Reading of the
Bill in this House, stated that in the opinion
of the Government the administration of the
Public Libraries Acts was properly a matter
for the County Councils, and not for the
smaller bodies, the Rural District Councils.
The Government, therefore, in bringing forward this measure, does not intend to attach
any discredit to those Rural District Councils
who have never adopted the Libraries Acts,
and still less to the Councils who, in spite
of all difficulties, have actually undertaken
the duties of public library authorities by
their adoption. I wish to lay particular
emphasis upon this. We attach no blame and
no reflection to the Rural District Councils.
It is our considered opinion that these duties
were never properly laid upon their
shoulders.
But, in the opinion of the Government,
when we make provision for the transfer of
the administration of the Libraries Acts to
the County Councils, there can be no such
fetter placed upon their discretion as the
hon. Senator proposes by this Amendment.
We feel certain, however, that the County
Councils, before adopting the Acts for any
rural district, will consult the views of the
inhabitants, and give full weight to any considerations reasonably urged against the
course which they propose to take.
I believe the hon. Senator (Senator Greer)
has an amendment to bring before the House
which will go some considerable way towards
meeting the hon. Senator's objections to this
Clause. The Government is inclined to adopt
some amendment framed to make a concession to the Rural District Councils whose
Public Libraries Bill-
25 MARCH, 1924.
Committee.
powers are virtually and practically being
transferred under the Bill. It is merely a
question of arriving at an agreed form.
I
I would, therefore, ask the hon. Member
not to press his amendment, and to allow the
Clause to go through in its present form.
will give him an undertaking that the Government will consider the whole situation,
and that, if necessary, I will move an amendment upon the Report Stage.
Mr. WOODS: I should like to inform the
noble Marquess that in dealing with the
speech made by Senator Joseph Cunningham
he referred to myself as having made it. I
am in perfect agreement with the Clause as it
stands, and I do not want to take the credit
for that speech.
Mr. LESLIE: I think you will all agree
that the object I had in view in putting
down this amendment that the matter
might be ventilated and discussed-has been
fully attained. I listened with great interest
to what was said by hon. Senator Armstrong, and I feel that there is really nothing more for me to say. I rather regret the
attack of my hon. Friend, Senator Greer,
on the rural district councils, but any
feeling of regret I have is entirely taken
away by what the Leader of the House said.
Nobody who heard, or who reads, what the
noble Marquess said to-day can feel that
there is any intention on the part of the
Government to slight in any way any of
the rural district councils. The last vestige
of suggestion against them for any alleged
neglect of duty has been taken away, and
I feel that the rural district councils will
be perfectly satisfied, as I am myself, to
leave the matter in the hands of the Leader
of the House to be dealt with if necessary
at a later stage. I beg leave to withdraw
the amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move an amendment which does not appear on the Paper,
but of which I have given the Leader of the
House notice. It is in this form:
Clause 1, page 1, after line 18 ("any
rural district therein"), insert:
"(2). In the management of any Public
Library Scheme undertaken by a County
Council under this Act, the County Council shall act through a Committee to be
called the County Library Committee,
such Committee to consist of three members of the County Council, to be appointed
by such Council, and of three members of
each Rural District Council to whose district the Act has been applied, to be
appointed by such Council, such Committee
when appointed to have power to co-opt.
twelve additional members, who need not
necessarily be members of any local
authority in the County."
I think it is necesstry to explain to the
House why I move this amendment, and 1
should like to apologise to the House for
not having brought it forward in the ordinary way. It was impossible for me to do so,
as the circumstances which caused me to
bring it forward only arose on Saturday last.
As I have already explained to this House
County Antrim is in a rather peculiar and
critical position in regard to its public
library. It got
fine library established
there and worked for two years free of cost
by the Carnegie Trust. The two years expired on the 1st of March, and the Committee arranged with the Trustees of the
Library to continue its management until
the 1st of June. At that time there was no
question of giving the County Council
power to become the library authority and
the only councils who could act in the
matter were the rural district councils.
a
a
The Library Committee undertook with
the Carnegie Trustees that they would go
round the rural district councils and endeavour to get them to take over the
management of this Carnegie library. They
did so with the result that out of seven
rural district councils, five were in favour
of forming a joint authority and taking
over the library. Two were not in favour.
One of these is the Belfast Rural District
Council, which controls merely
small
fringe around the city of Belfast. They
considered that the fine libraries which are
accessible to them in Belfast were quite
sufficient for their purposes. The other was
the Ballycastle Rural District Council, which
took a vote on the question with the result
that there was a tie, and by the casting vote
of the Chairman the Council decided not to
join the other five councils. The five councils
who were in favour of continuing the library
held
meeting and produced a report
which they brought forward at the meeting
in Ballymena on Saturday last. I have not
the slightest intention of delaying the House
by reading that report, but there are a
couple of sentences I would like to read
which show the view they took of the
importance of the library:-
a
From the returns made by the centres of
the Organising Librarian (Mr. A. Stewart
Roy) there is ample and conclusive evidence
that the library service is a necessity and
that its influence has added considerably
to the social and moral welfare of the
community.
Books on agriculture and books dealing
with the trades and industries of the county
have been specially provided in an en-
Public Libraries Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Greer]
deavour to serve as an actual link between
organised education and all post-school
social and educational movements.
It is pleasing to reflect that the efforts of
the Committee have been successful to the
utmost degree, and they have therefore no
hesitation in recommending to the county
authorities that the offer of the Irish
Advisory Committee of the Carnegie United
Kingdom Trust, to hand over the existing
organisation, should be accepted in trust for
the inhabitants of the county.
After the Second Reading of the Bill in this
Chamber a few days ago it was brought to my
knowledge that there was a good deal of
opposition on the part of Rural District
Councils in the counties to the taking away of
their powers by this Bill. I heard of this
opposition before a meeting in Ballymena
between the Carnegie Trustees, as represented
by the Irish Secretary, Mr. Lennox Robinson,
and the County Antrim Library Committee
and five Rural District Councils. I thought
I would attend the meeting, and see what it
was in the Bill they objected to, and whether
it would not be possible for us to act together
to support the library and continue to carry
it on without any friction. I found that the
Rural District Councils were afraid that after
they had taken the trouble to form themselves
into a united body to take over the management of the library they might lose it altogether, and the County Council might not
be prepared to take up and work the scheme.
I assured the meeting that five rural district
councils were anxious to join in the scheme,
and that one council was prevented from joining in only by the casting vote of the chairman. I told them I was almost certain they
could count on the Antrim County Council
adopting the Act, and that I could undertake
to speak on their behalf to that extent. They
did not object to the Rural District Councils
ceasing to be the Library authority, but they
did object to their having nothing to say in
the management of the library.
They said, and I think very fairly, that the
library could not be a success unless the
people in the neighbourhood were anxious to
make it a success; that it depended a great
deal on the voluntary work of school teachers
and other people who undertook to procure the
books from the central repository and distribute them in the various centres. A great
deal of that work, it was pointed out, was
done by national school teachers, who got the
scholars to distribute the books on their way
home from school. The representatives of the
Rural District Councils said that if they were
ousted from the management of the library
some people would be appointed who did not
know the divisions as they did, and they
thought that in the interests of the successful
working of the library they should be appointed as the managers. On behalf of the
Carnegie Trust, Mr. Lennox Robinson said
they were very anxious to have the County
Council appointed the library authority
for the county, because it would be easier
and more satisfactory so far as they were
concerned if they only had to deal with one
authority instead of a joint Board. Everyone
was anxious that the library should be continued, and I suggested that if they would
prepare a resolution showing what they
wanted altered in the Bill I would undertake
to move an amendment accordingly. I received this letter, dated 24th March, from
the secretary:
The Public Libraries Bill, at present
passing through the Senate of Northern Ireland, was under discussion at a conference
of the County Antrim Carnegie Library
Committee and representatives of the
Rural District Councils of Antrim, Ballymena, Ballymoney, Larne, and Lisburn, held
here on Saturday. As you know, the Public
Libraries (1902) Act has been adopted by
these rural district councils, and arrangements made for the establishment of
county library scheme.
a
It was felt, however, that circumstances
had been modified by the introduction of
the new Bill, and, while sympathising with
the conferring upon the county councils of
powers under the Bill, the general opinion
was expressed that the management of the
library should be retained, as far as possible, by the rural district councils as the
representatives of the public immediately
concerned.
After considerable discussion, it was resolved to approach you with a view to
obtaining the introduction of an amendment
to the Bill as it now stands, and I shall be
glad if you will be successful in doing so.
An extract from the minutes is appended
hereto which gives the proposed amendment. It was confidently believed that if
something of this nature can be inserted in
the Bill, all parties concerned will be
thoroughly satisfied.
The resolution referred to in the letter was
proposed by Mr. Joseph Maybin, J.P., chairman of the Ballymena Rural District Council,
seconded by Mr. James Graham, J.P., chairman of the Antrim Rural District Council,
and supported by Mr. Samuel M'Henry, J.P.,
chairman of the Ballymoney Rural District
Council. It is really the amendment I have
moved, so I need not read it again. I hope
that in the interests of the inhabitants of
County Antrim the Government will see its
way to accept this amendment. I have no
objection whatever to any alteration they
may think it right to make in the amend-
57 Public Libraries Bill-
25 MARCH, 1924.
Committee.
ment, but I would urge them as strongly as I
can to accept the principle of the amendment,
and at a later stage brimng forward something that will meet the views expressed.
Mr. BARBOUR: I sincerely trust the
Government are not anxious to rush this Bill
through. The local authorities are all dying
bodies. There will be a General Election in
June, and probably a good many of them
would feel shy of expressing any opinion on
this Bill. I think the two major pieces of
legislation that have been passed would have
found a more ready and general acceptance
if more time had been allowed before they
were finally passed, and if more time couid
be allowed for the consideration of this Bill
it might mean that a readier and a more
general acceptance of it would be reflected
in a more enthusiastic carrying out of the
aims and objects of the measure. Personally,
I think there is much to be urged in favour
of the principle of the amendment. I would
be the last person to deprive this scheme of
the support and assistance of the voluntary
workers. It would appear that County
Antrim is singularly fortunate in this
respect, but we must not forget there are
other counties of equal importance with
County Antrim, and we have got to consider
this Bill in its application to all of them.
co-
With regard to the proportion of representatives on the proposed committee-the
representatives of the county council, the
rural district councils and the twelve
opted members the body which has the task,
pleasant or unpleasant as it may be, of raising the cash has only three representatives,
whereas the total committee would be thirty
in a particular case. Those figures will not
apply all over, but they are indicative of the
whole. I should like a given time to consider
the matter further, and possibly a better
proportion of representatives of the different
interests can be suggested, because I am not
sure that the county councils would be fairly
treated if their representation was cut down
to three members, and they found themselves
in such a decided minority. I can think of
two other committees carrying on important
work in our county on which the county
council has a majority. There is a great deal
to be said for that, and it is a principle
which I believe has worked out very well in
practice. I have yet to learn of any committee carrying on good work in the county
where the representation of the county is
restricted to such a small number. Therefore, I hope the Government will give us
time to consider this carefully, and that the
Senate will not be too insistent in pushing the
Bill through at the moment.
Mr. LESLIE: I certainly agree with the
root principle of the Amendment moved by

Senator Greer, but like Senator Barbour 1
hope there will be ample time allowed before
we take the Report Stage of the Bill. I am
assuming in my mind that no County Council
will consider a library scheme at the moment,
because, as Senator Barbour pointed out, all
the County Councils will be out of office in
May, and it is scarcely reasonable to expect
that a County Council will start a new
scheme of this description immediately before
it goes out, and its successor comes in. I do
not want to deal in any way with the details
of the Amendment, because I think it will be
necessary for those to be left in the hands of
the Government to be dealt with on the Report Stage. There is only one point I would
like to direct attention to. The Amendment
is admittedly and properly brought forward
in the interests of the Rural District Councils. In the wording of the Amendment the
interests of Urban District Councils or of any
town with Commissioners are entirely
neglected.
powers
a
We see on reference to the Second Clause of
the Bill that there is a very useful power
given to small urban districts to relinquish
their
under the Library Acts
and join in
general scheme. If any
Urban Council or any town with Commissioners does take that decision it is only
reasonable that it should have representation
on the County Library Committee. Whether
the basis of that representation should necessarily be the same as that of the Rural District Council I do not say, but I hope the
Government will adopt the root principle of
the Amendment, and I hope, also, that the
actual wording will by agreement be considerably revised.
but
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :1
I feel rather at a loss to know exactly what
hon. Members who have discussed this
Amendment really mean. I have myself been
a member of an opposition, and I have had
to exercise a certain amount of ingenuity in
devising means for putting off the operation
of a Bill for various and particular reasons,
knowing that the hon. Members
who have spoken heartily support the
principle of the Bill I am not quite
aware of the length of the delay they desire
should take place before the Bill is brought
into operation. I should have thought this
was more or less an agreed Bill, and that any
difficulties we might encounter would be
difficulties of detail.
I do not know whether I am right in apprehending from hon. Members that this Bill
should be postponed until after the County
Council elections, or that it should come into
operation when we have discussed the points
which have cropped up on the Amendment
proposed by Senator Greer. It does seem to
me that in a measure of this description, in
which there is no highly controversial prin-
Public Libraries Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
ciple involved, there is no object in delaying
its passing into an Act until, for instance,
June. I had hoped we should have had the
Report Stage in the near future, and then
have gone on to the passage of the Bill into
law practically as an agreed Act, and one
which no County Council would have had very
much difficulty in putting into operation. The
suggestion is that the Report Stage should be
taken on 1st April, which would leave
certain amount of time for consultation with
my hon. Friends as to the form in which the
Amendment moved by Senator Greer should
appear in the Bill. It is an Amendment with
a
which I am in entire sympathy, but I should
like to have a little longer time in which to
consider the precise terms we can adopt.
I will, therefore, ask Senator Greer if he
will withdraw this Amendment, and I will
give him an undertaking that the Government will give full consideration to the matter
between now and the Report Stage, which we
hope to take in a week's time. I have no
doubt in my own mind that in consultation
with the hon. Member and his Friends we
shall be able to arrive at some correct form
of Amendment which I will move when the
Bill comes before the House on the Report.
Stage.
Senator Barbour has spoken of increased representation of the County
Council on the County Library Committee. That is merely
arrangement and adjustment.
A committee
of the County Council is a less unwieldly body
to work through than the whole County
Council, but I am sure between now and the
Report Stage we shall be able to arrive at a
satisfactory basis of representation. I would,
therefore, ask Senator Greer to withdraw his
Amendment on the undertaking I have given,
and I
sure he will be satisfied that
nothing will be moved that will not be in
accordance with his wishes.
am
a
matter for
Mr. GREER: I have to thank the Leader of
the House for the way he has met me in regard to this amendment. I would like to
point out that any delay in passing this Bill
into law may have a very serious effect on the
prospects of the library in County Antrim,
because the period has now expired for which
the Carnegie Trustees undertook the management of the library free of expense. It was
only after a good deal of persuasion that they
agreed to continue to manage the library free
of expense to us until 1st June, and so far as
I can see they do not propose to continue after
that date. If the public library authority
do not give the Carnegie Trustees an undertaking that they will take over the library
on 1st June they will have to hand back to
the Trustees all the books, fittings, etc.,
which will be a very serious thing indeed.
Therefore I hope my hon. Friends will not
urge any delay which will prevent this Bill
passing into law in time for the county
council to take over the duties of the library
authority.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Arrangements between certain
existing Library Authorities and County
Councils).
(1) The council or commissioners of any
urban district or town who have adopted
the Public Libraries Acts may, on such
terms as may be agreed upon between the
council or commissioners and the council
of the county in which the urban district
or town is situate, and approved by the
Ministry of Home Affairs for Northern
Ireland (in this Act referred to as "the
Ministry"), relinquish in favour of the
county council all or any of their powers
and duties under the Public Libraries
Acts; and in that case the powers and
duties so relinquished shall cease, and the
provisions of this Act shall have effect as if
the council of the county had passed
resolution under this Act adopting the
Public Libraries Acts as respects the urbau
district or town concerned.
a
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move in Clause 2, page 2, line 31, leave
out from "the" ("the provisions of this
Act") to end of line 34, and insert
"Public Libraries Acts shall, as respects
the urban district or town concerned, be
carried into execution by the county council, and the provisions of this Act shall
apply accordingly."
This is merely a drafting amendment. The
point has been raised that the words "pass a
resolution under this Act" are not appropriate to the case, because apart from this
sub-section itself a county council has no
power to become the library authority for an
urban district or town. The words proposed
to be substituted expressly confer this power
upon the county council.
Amendment agreed to
Clause as amended ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
Clauses 3 (Expenses of County Councils and
limit on rate), 4 (Transfer of property,
rights, and liabilities), 5 (Co-operation with
voluntary agencies), and 6 (Short Title, construction and saving) ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
Preamble and Title ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
