Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD : Hear,
hear.

library committees will be worked on party
House on the defence he has made of what
lines, the one party that of the county
he does not altogether believe in.
council and the other of the rural district
councils, and that all the county councillors
will go into one lobby and all the rural
district councillors into another upon questions of library administration. We believe
-and I think this House will agree with
me that members of the county library
committee will act quite independently of
party and give their vote according as they
think best for the general good of public
library administration.
The second consideration I would urge
upon the House is this There is a great
deal to be said in favour of a very full representation of the rural districts upon the
county library committee, because, after all,
the rate will be levied, not over the county
at large, but as the Bill says, equally
over so much of the county as comprises
the county districts and towns as respects
which the county council is for the time
being carrying the Public Libraries Acts
into execution." That, to my mind, is
really the most important point.
I do humbly submit to this House that
in a matter of this kind, which so closely
concerns matters of rating, this House
should bow to the wishes of the directly
representative assembly, as expressed in the
terms of the Amendment now before the
House.
success.
a
We are all of us anxious to devise
scheme whereby the Public Libraries Acts
should be put into operation generally and
administered with
We are all
agreed upon the principle embodied in this
Bill. The difference is in regard to a detail
of the form of administration. I do ask
this House, therefore, to allow the scheme
approved of in another place to be tried.
If that scheme should not turn out a
success-and we sincerely hope that it will
succeed-my right hon. Friend the Minister
of Home Affairs can always consider the
advisability of asking Parliament to amend
the Act, so that the county library committee may take another form-what form I
cannot say-perhaps that in which many hon.
Members of this House would at the present
moment like to see it first put into opera-
•tion. Therefore, I ask the House to agree
with this Amendment, and I trust the explantation 1 have given will in some
way allay the anxiety of the hon. and
gallant Member (Colonel Sharman-Crawford)
who sits opposite me.
Mr. DICKSON: I would like to congratulate the noble Viscount who is leading the
Mr. DICKSON: The point he has put
forward is this. The chairmen of Rural
Districts Councils become members of County
Councils as such and their whole interests
are
in the County Council. I disagree
entirely with that. They are Members of
the County Council simply because they are
Chairman of the Rural District Council and
they go there to represent the interests of
the district which they represent, not for
the whole county at all. They always. represent the Rural District Councils of which
they are Chairmen. However, I do not wish
to press the matter to a division.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
National Health Insurance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
a
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The principal object of this measure
is to provide for the payment for medical
certification in connection with the National
Health Insurance service in Northern
Ireland during the three years ending 31st
December, 1926. As, no doubt, hon. Members
are aware, the production of
medical
certificate is necessary before an insured
person can draw Health Insurance Benefit.
Such service should be free to the insured
person, and, at the same time, the medical
profession should be remunerated for their
services in giving them. It has been found
that on the average medical certification
costs some 2/- per annum per head of insured
persons. As near as possible, that is £30,000
per year.
surance
Under Section 3 of the National Insurance
Act, 1911, two-ninths of this expenditure is
payable out of moneys provided by Parliament, and under the National Health In-
Act (Northern Ireland) 1922, the
balance up to the end of the year 1924 was
provided as to one-third from the Central
Fund and two-thirds from the funds of the
Approved Societies. That has been the
position hitherto. The change proposed under
the Bill is in accordance with the proposals
of the Geddes Committee and has already been
brought into operation in Great Britain.
The method by which it is proposed that
the sum required shall be found involves
somewhat complicated details, which are
these. The Approved Society pays briefly
seven-ninths of 2d. per member, the Stamp
Sales Account of the Central Fund a sum
not exceeding seven-ninths of 1s. 4d. per
member, the Investment Account sevenninths of 6d. per member, and the State
179 Health Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
pays two-ninths of 2d, plus 1s. 4d, plus
1s. 6d. This contribution altogether amounts
to a sum of £29,950.
I would like to assure the House that
this measure does not seek to impose any
payment for medical certification upon the
insured person, or to reduce the fee which
is now paid to the medical profession for
giving these certificates. The arrangements
made under the First Section of the Bill
are to continue until the end of the year
1926. A Royal Commission is being established to inquire into Health Insurance
generally, and upon its recommendations
a Bill will be founded which it is expected
will establish the finance and organization
of Health Insurance upon a permanent
basis. The Commission is expected to report, and the resulting Bill to be brought
before the Imperial Parliament before the
end of 1926. During the intervening three
years it is proposed, after consultation and
agreement with the societies, to meet the
additional cost out of Health Insurance
Funds plus the Statutory State Grant.
Section 2 of the Bill provides for the recoupment to the societies who have undertaken to give free credits of contributions
to members who, owing to circumstances
beyond their control, are out of employment and have fallen into arrears with
their contributions.
The Third Section of the Bill brings the
procedure for recovery of arrears of contributions from an employer under the Health
Insurance statutes into line with that under
the Unemployment Insurance statutes.
The Fourth Section
repeals certain
Clauses in former Insurance Acts which
have proved inoperative or are no longer
of any value.
I beg to move.
Mr. WOODS: It is very gratifying to know
that the Government are passing a Bill to
enable medical certification to be supplied in
the future, as in the past, free of cost to the
insured person. Personally, I believe, it
would have been better if the Government
had gone the whole way and introduced in
Northern Ireland medical benefit similar to
that in force in Great Britain. I believe it
would be better for the three parties concerned-the insured persons, the Approved
Societies, and the medical profession. I am
confident that if medical benefits were introduced in Northern Ireland it would mean
some revision of the Poor Law administration. If the introduction of medical
benefits would reduce the rates I believe
the County Councils, of whom we have heard
SO
much to-day, would welcome it, and 1
believe the granting of medical benefit would
reduce the rates, because it would do away
with a great deal of Dispensary treatment,
I am satisfied with the Bill so far as it goes,
and I believe that in the near future we will
have medical benefit in Northern Ireland.
Mr. LESLIE: This Bills deals principally
with matters of administration and of
finance with which this House is not particularly accustomed to concern itself. If I may
say so, I think the Noble Viscount, in the
course of a very lucid statement, gave the
necessary explanation to allay some slight
anxiety which was felt on two points, the
freedom of expense of certification of the insured person, and the maintenance of the remuneration paid to the medical adviser. The
only point on which I would ask for further
enlightenment is that dealt with in Section 3
of the Bill. That is whether the passing of
this measure will in any way increase the
liability of an employer who has omitted to
stamp his card at the proper time. At present, as the House is aware, the provision of
the law is that if a card is not stamped at
the proper time the employer has no right
of recovery from the employee of his share of
the contribution. It always struck me as a
rather malevolent provision on the part of
Parliament. It may have been necessary in
1911, but having regard to the number of
years National Health Insurance has been in
operation, perhaps the time has come when it
might be admitted that no long delay can now
occur without the very active connivance of
the employee, and the employer who does
not pay up should not lose his chance of recovering the share he would have got if he
had paid at the proper time. I should like
to be assured that there is nothing in this
Bill which increases the employer's liability
in that way, and that he is not to be
rendered liable for the payment of arrears
for a longer back period than hitherto.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the specific point raised
by the hon. Senator (Mr. Leslie), this section
does not put any additional penalty on the
employer. It does not give additional power
but rather alternative power. Under Section
34 of the National Insurance Act of 1913, as
the hon. Senator knows, power is given to
the Ministry to recover as a result of criminal
proceedings contributions for the period of
twelve months preceding the date when the
information was laid. Where non-compliance.
goes back to a period prior to twelve months
it is necessary for the Ministry to proceed on
a separate summons in the case of each employee in each week of default. This may
mean a great number of summonses, perhaps
a hundred summonses. This is very expensive
and very cumbersome, and the cost may
mount up considerably. The civil process is
Private Business.
27 MAY 1924
Adjournment.
much simpler and less costly for all concerned. This is what the Bill does.
As regards the point of the hon. Member
(Mr. Woods) that is in the future, and I
may say, that while I sympathize with him,
I can give no definite answer now.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read a Second time accordingly and
committed to
Committee of the whole
House, the House to be in Committee tomorrow.

MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move.
That the Special Order made by the
Electricity Commissioners for Northern
Ireland under the Electricity (Supply)
Acts, 1882 to 1919, and confirmed by the
Ministry of Commerce under the Electricity
(Supply) Act, 1919, in respect to the Townland of Ballyfinaghy in the Rural District
of Lisburn and the Townland of Ballygammon in the Rural District of Belfast,
in the County of Antrim, and for the
amendment of the Belfast Electric Lighting Order, 1890, be approved.
The object of this Order is to enable
residents in the Balmoral and Finaghy
district outside the city boundary to obtain
a
supply of electricity from the Belfast
Corporation. The Corporation applied for a
Special Order to enable them to extend the
present area of supply. The Electricity
Commissioners have granted the Order and

Unemployment Insurance (Temporary Provisions) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Bill to which I have the honour
to ask the House to give a Second Reading
is an important Measure, and one which
materially affects our industrial classes. As
hon. Members are well aware, the Imperial
Government is attempting to secure the
enactment of a Bill which makes a number
of alterations in the Unemployment Insurance system. This Bill received
its
Second Reading in the Imperial House of
Commons on May 20th, after a not unfriendly discussion and with the approval of
all parties in that House. It is expected
that this Bill will be passed in some form
or other by the end of July this year, and
it will take effect immediately on receiving
the Royal Assent. As this Parliament will
not be sitting then to make any changes requiring legislation, the Bill now before the
House proposes to secure that the Governor
may, by Order in Council, make applicable
to Northern Ireland such provisions of the
Imperial Measure as they think fit pending
legislation in the Northern Parliament, the
object being that we may keep our Unemployment Insurance scheme on parallel lines
with that in Great Britain. I think I voice
the opinion of the House, or at any rate the
majority of the Members of the House, when
I say that we all desire in Measures of this
kind to proceed as far as possible on similar
lines to Great Britain.
I would like to remind the House that
since the beginning of 1922 we have passed
four Unemployment Insurance Measures. In
two of those Measures we gave my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Labour, anticipatory
authority. In October, 1922, a Bill was
passed to enable the Minister to adopt the
Imperial provisions in regard to the Third
and Fourth Special Periods, that is to say,
we removed the bar against the receipt of
benefit in excess of 26 weeks, and again in
May, 1923, we gave the Minister power to
abolish the gaps if they were abolished by
Great Britain. In this Bill we do not propose to give the Minister power, but to give
the Governor of Northern Ireland power to
make provisions on parallel lines with those
in the Imperial Bill which is now going
through the Imperial House of Commons.
I would also like to point out to the House
that as far as Northern Ireland is concerned
Parliament has invariably endorsed the decisions of Great Britain, and in these
matters has followed on parallel lines. I do
not think the House will expect me to go
into the details of the Imperial Bill, but I
think it only right that I should give the
House some of its main provisions. First
of all I would like to say that the Bill does
not affect the present contribution in any
way.
The Bill alters the rates of benefit to this
extent. It increases the benefit of men
from 15s. to 18s. a week, and single women
from 12s, to 15s. a week. Boys and girls
over 16 years of age are left unchanged, as
are also married women, but the dependency
allowance of a child is increased from 1s. to
25. The Bill also proposes to abolish the
refund at 60 years of age, and substitutes a
form of compensation. There are various
other matters which I do not think the
House will ask me to go into now, but if
any hon. Member desires information on any
particular point I shall be only too happy
to oblige him later on.
It must be remembered that this is a
purely temporary provision, and that the
utmost limit to which this Act can continue
in force is up to 31st December, 1924. I trust
that the House will pass the Bill so that the
men and women on this side of St. George's
Channel may receive benefit similar to that
which the men and women on the other side
will receive. We must remember that in the
Imperial House the Government is only
there on sufferance, and whatever Bill
passes must be passed with the consent of
the other two parties. With these few words
I beg to move the Second Reading of this Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: It would be impossible for
anybody to deny that the proposal which is
involved in the presentation of this Bill is
somewhat novel and altogether exceptional.
It would be seductively and dangerously easy
to attack the proposal of the Noble Viscount
on what I may call the pseudo-constitutional
ground that it was an invitation to Parliament to abrogate its powers and to transfer
elsewhere powers which can only be exercised
by Parliament itself. I am convinced that
the good sense of this House would revolt
against any suggestion of that nature. This
House invariably takes the broadest possible
view on any matter presented to it for consideration. It endeavours constantly to set
everything in its true perspective, and above
all this House never allows itself to lose its
sense of proportion. The situation
Northern Ireland, as we know, is perhaps
somewhat involved in various ways. But it
of
187 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
our resources
our
we
as
is neither difficult nor dangerous, although
it might become so-perhaps it might be induced to become so by ill-advised words or
thoughtless actions in one way or another.
One of the matters and it is a matter to
which the Noble Viscount referredwhich we have to keep constantly before
have again and
is that us
intention, so far
again asserted
permit us, to extend to
all the workpeople in Northern Ireland
terms not less favourable than those that
are enjoyed by their fellow citizens within
the United Kingdom who live in Great
Britain. We have to remember that and
to keep it constantly before us. And we must
remember, in that connection, that the
Government-our present Government is so
deeply committed to that policy that they
cannot possibly depart from it without the
direct sanction of Parliament. And again
the public funds of Northern Ireland
closely interwoven with those of
Great Britain, and still further it is
impossible for the
Members of this
Parliament to remain constantly in Session here in Belfast.
They have other
matters to attend to, and in some cases these
matters are of the greatest possible national
importance. Having regard to these
siderations it is obviously incumbent upon
our Government to devise a line of action,
and when the Government has devised its
line of action and has indicated what that
line is going to be, it appears to me that
support of it becomes equally incumbent on
all who desire to support the Government.
The question before the House-either House
-is simply one of confidence or want of confidence in the Government.
are
Mr. GREER: Hear, hear.
con-
The
Mr. LESLIE: It seems to me that there
are various courses which might perhaps
have been followed on this occasion.
first is, the Government might have decided
to do nothing at all. That would not keep
pace with their declared policy of extending
the benefits similarly to those across the
water. It would have been possible to
stretch and strain administrative powers,
and those powers to which the Noble
Viscount referred that were conferred on the
Ministry of Labour. That course would have
been impossible for the present Government
and would have been repugnant to this House
and to the Province. Again the Government might have decided to do without
previous Parliamentary sanction and have
decided to go on with the Measure when
passed by Great Britain, and then come before Parliament for an Act of Indemnity.
That is a course which is not generally
popular and it certainly ought never to
be taken except in the presence
the gravest possible emergency
of
Again
the Government might have legislated in
accordance with what they believed the
English Measure would be when it was
passed. That would be merely gambling,
and they might also have decided to call
Parliament together at some time in the
summer in order to specially consider the
matter. That course would have been highly
inconvenient (hon. Members: Hear, hear)
-and possibly dangerous, because even the
Government cannot state at what time it
might be necessary to call Parliament
together, or what the
circumstances
might be at that time. That is a possibility.
It must remain a possibility, but it is one
I am very glad myself the Government have
decided to avoid if they can. There remains
the course which the Government have
taken, that of asking the House to pass
this Bill. To my mind that course is alike
most prudent and most statesmanlike. The
mere fact of the Government coming before
this House and asking for a Bill of this
description is in itself the fullest possible
admission of the control and supremacy of
Parliament. It puts on one side altogether
any possible allegation of doing anything
in the dark that the Government are afraid
to do in the daytime; and coupled with that
application to Parliament we have the
double safeguard which is contained in the
second section of the Bill. The Bill is only
to become operative until the end of the
year or until such time as this Parliament
may make further provision in the matter.
There is really, so far as I can see, nothing
that can usefully be done to the Measure.
It would be possible, perhaps, by way of
emphasizing the control of Parliament to
make some trifling alteration; but I cannot
see that we would obtain any result by
that which we cannot equally obtain by
the mere recital of the possibility. On the
contrary, I may claim, I think, to have
endeavoured to consider this Bill and to
examine it in all its bearings, and I am
convinced that it is in the circumstances
of to-day the simple duty cast upon all of
us to support the Bill both loyally and
actively and exactly as it stands.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: As I
listened to the very interesting speech we
have just heard from Senator Leslie I closed
my eyes for a moment, and as I heard his
remarks I had a vision of the old days at
Westminister. I thought it was a speech
coming from the Leader of the House, and
not from one whom I may call a backbencher, because all those points which he
elaborated on the Measure are those which
would have been considered by the Government, and the Bill would not have been
189 Unemployment Insurance Bill.
28 MAY 1924

Health Insurance Bill.
before the House if they had not been
considered. I only wish to ask the noble
Viscount, who is leading the House, if he
purposes that this Bill should pass the
Committee and Third Reading Stages before
the House rises. Of course I suppose there
is no Member of this House who wishes to
delay this in any way at all. I would like
to put to him two questions as to what may
happen if it passes the Committee and
Third Reading Stages to-morrow. I am
told from London that considerable amendments may take place in the Imperial House
at Westminister before the Bill goes
through. These would have to be very carefully considered, and we would not have
an opportunity of seeing what they were.
Another question is this: I also heard
from London-it is hardly Parliamentary
language--that the betting is even money
whether the Imperial Government will be
in existence on Friday morning. I suppose
in that case, if the Government goes out,
this Bill would also be dropped, and our
Government would not initiate a Bill on
anything like the present lines, but a fresh
Bill would be brought in and considered.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am glad to think that the House
has approved, with some criticism, of this
Bill. I think I need not add anything to
what the right hon. and gallant Gentleman
(Colonel Sharman-Crawford) has said with
regard to the speech of Senator Leslie, to
which I think the whole House listened
with great attention. As regards the two
specific points raised by my hon. and gallant
Friend, we will take the Committee and
Third Reading Stages to-morrow. If any
drastic Amendment is introduced into the
Bill passing through the Imperial House of
Commons I can give him the assurance
which my right hon. Friend the Prime
Minister gave in another place when this
Bill was being passed through its Second
Reading, that if there is any serious matter
Parliament will be called together again to
consider it.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: Hear,
hear.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: As regards the other point raised
by my right hon. and gallant Friend, obvisously if the Bill does not pass through the
Imperial House of Parliament there will be
no need for the Bill here.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The Bill was accordingly read a Second
time, and committed to a Committee of the
Whole House, the House to be in Committee
to-morrow.

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-

ance Act, 1911, or in.”

Appropriation Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This Bill is merely a money Bill,
and I do not propose to take up the time
of the House by making a speech on it. It
is perfectly clear what it is for Accordingly I beg to move that the Bill be now
read a Second time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now committed.
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Exchequer and Financial Provisions
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that the Bill be now
read a Second time. This also is a money
Bill, but, at the same time, I think I ought
to explain some of its main objects to the
House. The Bill has two objects. The first
is the establishment of a special Capital
Fund, to be called "the Capital Fund No.
2" by the Ministry of Finance, into which
will be paid the sum of four hundred
thousand pounds received by the Northern
Government on the recommendation of the
Colwyn Committee in final satisfaction of
claims made under the Government of Ireland Act in respect of the Northern Ireland
share of certain buildings and equipment
employed before the transfer of services to
the new Governments of Northern Ireland
and the Free State for all-Ireland purposes.
The second main object is to provide for
the capital expenditure necessary for the
construction and adaptation of police barracks for the Royal Ulster Constabulary.
The first Sub-section of Clause 1 sets out
the purposes for which Capital Fund No. 2
is to be applied. Sub-section (2) provides
for complete Parliamentary control of this
Fund, as authority must be sought on the
annual votes for any expenditure from it.
The third Sub-section deals with the recoupment from this Capital Fund of moneys
which have already been spent in the two
previous financial years for the purposes
for which this Capital Fund is set up. Subsection (4) authorizes the Ministry of
Finance to borrow, by terminable annuities
or otherwise, a sum not exceeding seventyfive thousand pounds in the event of the
sum now placed in the Capital Fund No. 2
being insufficient for the purposes for which
it is established.
Clause 2 provides for capital expenditure
on police barracks and authorizes the
Ministry of Finance to borrow by terminable
annuities or other securities for a period not
exceeding twenty-five years a sum or sums
not exceeding an aggregate of three hundred
and seventy-five thousand pounds. The other
Clauses of the Bill are concerned with the
administrative details arising out of the
financial operations adumbrated in the first
two Clauses to which I have referred. I beg
to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That this Bill be now committed." (Viscount Messereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Constabulary (Acquisition of Land)
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This Bill, as its name implies, is
introduced in order to enable my right hon.
Friend the Minister of Home Affairs to
cope with certain difficulties which he has
experienced in obtaining sites suitable for
the erection of new barracks for the Royal
Ulster Constabulary He has unfortunately
failed to acquire these sites by agreement,
negotiations having proved abortive. It has
therefore become necessary to ask Parliament to endow the Ministry of Home Affairs
with compulsory powers, and that is the
object of the Bill before the House.
It is practically a one-clause Bill.
Subsection (1) of the first Clause gives the
Ministry power to acquire land compulsorily
by purchase for five years after the passing
of the Act. The remaining Sub-sections deal
with the procedure which the Ministry is to
follow in the exercise of these powers. A
provisional order must first be made in the
case of any specific site. or sites, to be
acquired. Notice of the making of this order
must be given to the owners, lessees or
occupiers, to whom
month is allowed
during which they may make objections and
the Ministry may consider them. After the
lapse of this period the Ministry may
modify or confirm the order in its original
form.
a
193 Constabulary (Land) Bill.

28 MAY 1924
Adjournment.

In fixing the price an arbitrator will be
appointed in accordance with the provisions
of the Second Schedule of the Housing of the
Working Classes Act of 1890 with certain
modifications whereby a dissatisfied party
has the right of appeal with respect to the
amount of the award. The House will be
aware that under the Housing of the Working
Classes Act referred to appeal is only allowed
where the amount of the award exceeds
£1,000. The appeal lies to the Judge of the
County Court within whose jurisdiction the
site in question is situated. This right of
appeal will extend to the Ministry as well
as to the other parties. Section 2 makes provision for the vesting in the Receiver of
the Force of any land compulsorily acquired
that officer being at present the person in
whom all land or premises held for the purpose of the Royal Ulster Constabulary are
at present vested. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read a Second time accordingly and
committed to a committee of the Whole
House; the House to be in committee tomorrow.
Adjournment.
Moved: "That this House do now adjourn."
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Ten Minutes before Four
of the Clock.

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Amendment to be moved which
stands in my name is as follows:-Clause 1,
page 3, line 5, to leave out from the first
"the" ("by the arbitrator") to "or" ("his
award (or") in line 6, and insert "Ministry
of the amount of the compensation ascertained by the award of the arbitrator." This
is a purely drafting Amendment, and is
rendered necesary by the fact that the certificate intended to be referred to in the lines
in question is the certificate given by the
Ministry and not the certificate of the
arbitrator. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.

of the Bill.

Illegitimate Children (Affiliation

LATE MR. H. de F. MONTGOMERY.
Mr. GREER: Before entering on to-day's
business I should like with the permission
of the House to refer to a matter which
affects us all deeply. Hon Members will have
seen in this morning's papers the announcement of the death of one of our most
distinguished colleagues, the Right Hon.
Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery, who fell
at the call of duty, having taken suddenly
ill when about to preside over the great
Omagh demonstration a few days ago. He
was one of the best known and most widery
esteemed public men in this Province. He
was a member of the Privy Council, High
Sheriff of the Counties of Tyrone and Fermanagh,
Deputy Lieutenant of both
a
203 The Late Senator Montgomery
[Mr. Greer]
Counties, Chairman of the Tyrone County
Council, and of many other boards and
councils. He has now gone to his rest after
a long life of public service to his country,
his county, and his church-of public service
well, faithfully and successfully done, and
his loss will be deeply regretted by the
whole community and by every Member of
this House.
Mr. PORTER: The duty devolves upon me
of seconding the remarks so feelingly made
by Senator Greer in reference to the late
Right Hon. Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery.
presume this duty has fallen upon me because I happen to be his nearest neighbour
and have been associated with him for sixty
years on many Boards and in many duties
in the county, and I can say without any
fear of contradiction that the late Mr.
Montgomery gave his whole life and his
whole great cleverness in discharging the
duties of a country gentleman. He put his
duty towards his county before his own
personal feelings and he did his duty to the
best of his ability for the good of his
country. I do not think it is necessary for
me to say any more, as the deceased gentleman was as well known in this House as he
was in his own county.
Colonel PERCEVAL-MAXWELL: I should
like to add a few words, as I have been very
closely associated with the late Mr. Montgomery during the last two years on the
Landlords' Convention, of which he was
chairman, and vice-chairman for the whole
of Ireland. Like everyone else associated
with him, I marvelled at the extraordinary
grasp he had of every subject that came up
for our consideration, and his wonderfully
clear intellect was always of great assistance
to us. But what, I think, will appeal to this
House most was this that before he came to
a decision on any question, he considered
how it would affect Ulster. He never gave
us any advice or took any decision himself
without considering very carefully how it
would affect this Province generally. He
was a great patriot, he had a great mind,
and he was a splendid type of Irish county
gentleman.
Mr. GREER: As the Bill which we are
expecting to arrive from another place has
not yet come to hand. I would suggest that
the House should adjourn for a short time
until the Bill is brought down.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That they have passed the Unemployment
Fund Bill, to which they desire the conourrence of this House.
THE SENATE
Business of the House. 204
Unemployment Fund Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the First
time. (Mr. Greer.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Business of the House.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move:
That Standing Order No. XXV be considered, in order to its being suspended for
this day's sitting.
Hon. Members will understand that three
of our leading Members-the Speaker, the
Leader of the House, and the Parliamentary
Secretary to the Prime Minister-are unable
to be here to-day, as they are engaged in upholding our interests in the important debate
which is now going on in the House of Lords.
I do not think it is out of place to say that
we congratulate the noble Marquess on his
cogent and powerful contribution to that debate a few days ago, and that we congratulate ourselves on having as a member of our
Government and as Leader of this House a
statesman of such distinction and influence.
I have unexpectedly and somewhat suddenly
been asked to take his place at this sitting,
and I hope to receive the indulgence of the
House in this novel position.
In moving the resolution which appears on
the Order Paper, I have to point out that it
is necessary to suspend the Standing Order
to make it possible to pass the Unemployment
Fund Bill, about which there is considerable
urgency, through all its stages to-day. We
are glad to see the Minister of Finance, the
Minister in charge of the Bill, present in the
House. It is, I believe, his first appearance
here, and I can assure him that we give him
a cordial welcome. The right hon. Member is
well known as one of the ablest financiers in
our Province, and it is no secret that he has
made his mark on the other side of the water
in defending our interests in the numerous
financial arrangements which have had to be
made from time to time with the British
Government. If this resolution is passed the
right hon. Member will explain the Bill and
the urgency for having it passed at once.
Copies of the Bill were sent to Members
yesterday, the earliest possible moment. It is
only with the goodwill of the House that it
can be passed through to-day. If the House
decide not to pass the resolution, as they are
entitled to do, the Senate could hold a special
Session on Tuesday next to complete the passing of the Bill.
The House will, however, probably think
that as this is a Money Bill, which this
205 Unemployment Fund Bill-
Second Reading.
9 OCTOBER 1924

House cannot alter in Committee, and as the
right hon. Gentleman is here in order to
explain the Bill, it would be only right to
pass the resolution, and give him an opportunity of being heard. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I think it would be entirely
out of accord with our feelings to-day that
we should offer any opposition to the motion
which has been proposed by the hon. Member
in charge of the House. In the first place
this is the first occasion on which the right
hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Finance, to
whom we owe so much, has appeared in this
House, and we wish to welcome him cordially
and, if I may say so, effectively. Moreover,
I do not think there is anything in this
particular Measure which calls for
delay, or which in any way requires that
we should go through our more formal procedure. I see no reason whatever for differing with the hon. Member in his proposal
that this House should suspend this Standing
Order, so that the Measure may be passed
through all its stages at this sitting.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Standing Order No. XXV accordingly
suspended for this day's sitting.
Unemployment Fund Bill-
Moved: That this Bill be now read a
Second time. (Mr. Greer.)
The MINISTER OF FINANCE (Mr.
Pollock): I cannot be otherwise than gratified
at the complimentary remarks which have
been made by the temporary Leader of the
House and by Senator Leslie in regard to
my first appearance here. It is indeed a
matter of pleasure that I should be permitted
to take the place of the noble Marquess, who
has requested me to undertake the duty
to-day of explaining this Bill in his own
temporary absence. The Measure which
brings me before your House is one of an
emergency character, and I think it is necessary that I should give hon. Members some
information regarding the circumstances
under which we find it necessary to proceed
with such expedition for the completion of
this short Bill.
The Unemployment Insurance Act of 1923
made provision for borrowing powers by the
Unemployment Insurance Fund for a sum of
£500,000. That act brought the liability of
the fund up to a sum of £2,000,000. It was
contemplated at that time that under the
circumstances then obtaining that power
would have been sufficient to carry the
Insurance Fund
until a period when
Parliament could, if such were necessary,
make further provision, but under the strain
of circumstances, which I will touch upon
shortly, at the end of the month of September
on
last the powers of the Insurance Fund came
to an end. That is to say, they had exhausted their borrowing powers as regards
the sum of £500,000 to which I have just
referred. Under these circumstances it was
necessary to take rather exceptional measures
for the purpose of financing the scheme
during the period between the expiration of
their powers and the necessary authority
which must emanate from Parliament for
borrowing sufficient to provide money for
the beneficiaries. It is perhaps desirable
that I should give the House some information in regard to the general character of
this fund and the great responsibility that
rests upon the Government, and indeed upon
the Ministry of Finance and upon the
Minister of Finance in respect of the condition in which it finds itself. When that
fund was handed over by the Imperial
authority on 1st January, 1922, no account
was taken of a possible liability or burden
which, as we have discovered, would be entailed upon the Northern Government. The
circumstances were undoubtedly new and
very exceptional, and no one, not even those
experienced men on the other side, had any
knowledge of what the requirements of the
new federal scheme of local devolution would
be. As a matter of fact, however, in the
financial arrangements that were provided
for in the Treasury White Paper which
formed the basis of the financial relations
between Great Britain and Northern Ireland,
no provision was made for any contingent
liability that would likely be imposed upon
Northern Ireland in respect of this fund. If
the conditions had been purely normal, and
if commercial and industrial matters had
continued on such a basis that the contributions from the several parties made up
the fund, that would have been sufficient to
provide for the liability for the benefits of
members who were unemployed.
The question that is agitating us to-day
would not then have arisen, but at that
period, and from that period onwards, the
regrettable and deplorable conditions of industry and commerce have, as all of our
people know who read the newspapers,
resulted in an acute condition of unemployment which has been unexampled probably
in our lifetime. The result has been that
as time went on and the number of unemployed increased the drain upon this insurance fund became so great that very large
sums of money had to be lent by the Government to the fund in order that the benefits
to the unemployed might be secured, and that
we should continue on a similar basis to that
obtaining in Great Britain. We have gone
on the assumption that in all matters of
social welfare in Northern Ireland we should
be entitled to the same benefits as those obtaining in Great Britain; that local autonomy
207 Unemployment Fund Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Mr. Pollock]
did not necessarily imply any lower social
status. Indeed, on the contrary, if we are
to measure our claims by the promises that
were held out to us at the time that the
Government of Ireland Bill was on the stocks
-that Bill that developed into the Act of
1920-if we were to rely on all those promises
we should not to-day have found many of our
services in the condition in which they are,
and I should not have been here to-day to
ask this House to pass a small measure of
urgency in order that we may make provision
for those who are out of work in the Province. But up to the present, although we
have been exercising some pressure on the
authorities at Westminster, we have been unable to secure the principle which we have
urged is necessary for the reasonable conduct
of our affairs in Northern Ireland. As I explained to our own House when moving the
Second Reading of this Bill, the disturbances
which have been caused by the frequent
changes of Government on the other side have
greatly added to our difficulties, because each
succeeding Ministry had difficulty in realising
the obligations that were entered into by its
predecessor. The result is that we have had
considerable trouble, when we approached
matters like this, in arriving at anything like
a decisive conclusion.
I should explain that while we have been
quite recently before a very important Committee in London, a Committee of Arbitration
which was mutually agreed upon, our application in regard to the general principle of
which I have just been speaking, namely,
equality of treatment to our people in regard
to all social matters with those in Great
Britain, and although I hope we shall obtain
a favourable decision in regard to the assertion of that general principle, it is probable
that the matter is outside the purview of that
Committee, and therefore has to be specially
dealt with by, shall I say, political pressure.
The only solution we can see to assist us in
dealing with this very heavy burden is that
the fund in Northern Ireland should be
merged into the general fund of the United
Kingdom, from which it never should have
been cut off, because it is quite obvious to
anyone with experience of insurance matters
that if you limit the area of contributions
you at the same time impose a contingent
liability on any insurance scheme. It
was because we were cut off from Great
Britain that we find that our contributions,
which come from the Government, the employer, and the employee, are insufficient to
maintain our fund under the unexampled
commercial depression through which we are
passing at the present time. Therefore, we
have found it necessary to help the fund by
making large loans, which have now actually
passed the £2,000,000 mark, and which, if
the present borrowing powers we are now asking for in this Bill are taken advantage of,
will be increased to £2,750,000.
I can give no idea when that money can be
repaid. We can only hope that industrial
conditions will before long resume their normal aspect, and that a measure of prosperity
will enable us at any rate to reach the point
when contributions and benefits will be on a
level scale. But I repeat that I see no hope
for a very long time of the fund being able
to repay its obligation to our Government,
and for that reason we are urging, and urging
strongly and I hope we shall succeed-that
the Imperial Government should agree to the
merging of our own fund into the general
fund of the United Kingdom such as prevailed before the transfer of the fund. If we
can induce the Imperial Government to agree
to that we shall have that assistance in bearing the burden thrown upon us. This in
effect has been the condition of the fund, and
I do not think it is necessary for me to oссиру
the time of the House by going into any details in regard to the measure of contributions,
the measure of benefits, and the question of
by what means any reduction may take place
in the claims on the fund, because after all
these are matters that are the outcome of
very great and close calculation, and I do not
think they form a subject for advocating the
Second Reading of such a small measure as
this. If any further information is desired
by any hon. Member, I shall be happy to
offer it.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now Committed.
-(Mr. Greer.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time.-(Mr. Greer.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now passed.-
(Mr. Greer.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House of
Commons to acquaint them that the Senate
have agreed to the said Bill without any
amendment.
Mr. GREER: It will be necessary now for
the House to adjourn for a short time in
the hope that after a few minutes we will
be able to announce that the Royal Assent
has been given to the Bill.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.
Royal Assent.
Adjournment.

to the House that His Grace the Govenor, in
the name of and on behalf of His Majesty
the King, has been pleased to give his assent
to the following Bill agreed upon by both
Houses:-
Unemployment Fund, 1924.
Adjournment.

do now adjourn. I may say it is proposed
to take up the Committee stage of the
Illegitimate Children Bill at the next meeting of the Senate, which will take place on
Tuesday, 21st inst.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Half-past Four of the Clock
till Tuesday, 21st October,
at 3-15 p.m.

Illegitimate Children (Affiliation
Orders) Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.-
(The Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
(The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair).
Clause 1. (Power to make Affiliation
Order upon man alleged to be father of illegitimate child.)
1.-(1) Where a man is alleged by the
mother to be the father of her illegitimate
child, and has been summoned in accordance
with the provisions of this Act to appear
before a court of summary jurisdiction, constituted as hereinafter provided acting for
the petty sessions district or place in which
the mother resides, or in which the mother
or her illegitimate child is in receipt of relief from the poor rates of any poor law
union, the court shall have power, after
hearing all the circumstances of the case,
to adjudge the man to be the putative father
of the child, and to make an order upon
him (in this Act referred to as an "Affiliation Order") for the payment of-
(a) a weekly sum of money not exceeding
twenty shillings a week in respect of the
maintenance and education of the child;
(b) the expenses incidental to the birth
of the child;
(c) if the child has died before the
making of the Affiliation Order, the funeral expenses of such child, not exceeding
five pounds; and
(d) the costs of obtaining the Affiliation
Order.
دو
(2) For the purposes of this Act the expression
the mother
means any single
woman who is with child or delivered of an
illegtimate child, and includes-
(a) a widow:
(b) a married woman
from her husband; and
living separate
(c) a married woman not living separate from her husband, but who at the time
of her marriage was with child of, or had
been delivered of, an illegitimate child.
(3) The court shall not make an Affiliation
Order unless-
(a) the birth of the child has taken
place;
(b) the man alleged to be the father of the
child appears before the court or it is
proved to the court that the summons was
duly served upon' him; and
(c) the evidence of the mother is corroborated in some material particular by
other evidence to the satisfaction of the
court.
(4) The court, in deciding whether or not
it shall make an order for the payment of
the expenses incidental to the birth of an
illegitimate child, shall not take into consideration the fact that the mother is entitled to receive maternity benefit under the
enactments relating to National Health Insurance.
(5) A. court of summary jurisdiction, when
hearing and determining any matter under
this Act, shall be constituted of a resident
magistrate sitting alone.
(6) The Bastardy (Ireland) Act, 1863, is
hereby repealed as respects Northern Ireland.
213 Illegitimate Children Bill- 21 OCTOBER 1924

Corimittee.
"
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 1.
page 1, line 13, to leave out from power"
("shall have power after hearing") to the
end of line 1 on page 2, and insert "to make
an Order (in this Act referred to as an
"Affiliation Order") adjudging the man to
be the putative father of the child. An
Affiliation Order may provide, under such
conditions as the Court sees fit, for the payment by such putative father of-
(a) the expenses incidental to the birth
of the child;
(b) if the child has died before the
making of the Affiliation Order the funeral expenses of such child, to an amount
not exceeding five pounds; and
(c) a weekly sum of money not exceeding
twenty shillings in any one week in respect of the maintenance and education of
the child. Any provision in an Affiliation
Order for any such payment shall continue
in force until the child, to whom it relates,
shall, if a male, attain the age of fifteen
years, or if a female, attain the age of
sixteen years or marry under that age,
and except for the recovery of arrears or
funeral expenses, for no longer period, but
no such provision for the payment of a
weekly sum shall continue in force after
the death of such child.
This first amendment which stands in my
name on the paper is in itself of such great
length that I feel bound to be as brief as
possible in laying it before the Committee,
and therefore I will endeavour to go at
once into what it is I desire to effect by this
amendment, and what changes I desire to
introduce into the wording of the Bill as
it is now before the Committee. First of
all I want the omission of a few unnecessary words which are not wanted because of
a provision towards the end of the Bill
applying the provisions of the Summary
Jurisdiction Acts. Then I wish to lay stress
on the distinction between an order and the
consequences flowing from that order, a
little more clearly perhaps than it is done
in the Bill. And I want to introduce
little more elasticity which, I think, will
be found to be of assistance to the court in
dealing with these
I think the
slightly altered wording I suggest will enable the court when it sees fit-only when
it sees fit-to get over certain troubles as
regards Boards of Guardians, which required special legislation in England some 50
years after the original legislation was
passed; also to give power for providing for
smaller payments during the first few
months of the child's life, the reason for
which I will deal with later on. These are
small matters which may be of use to the
court and which need not be used unless the
court sees fit.
cases.
a
Then I go on to re-arrange for convenience the various matters of expenses incidental to the birth of the child, where we
ought to begin, and funeral expenses. Then
I come to the sum of money. It is convenient, perhaps, to put that last, because
it is under paragraph (c) that the real
change that I want made in the Bill comes in.
I am asking the Committee in paragraph (c)
to standardise the age up to which relief is
given in accordance with the conditions of
the poor-law. The Irish poor-law requires
the parent to maintain the child until the
age of 15. In England as a matter of fact
the age is 16; but in Ireland it is 15, and it
seems to me most desirable that we should
select the age which we can support, and
we can, as it seems to me, certainly stand
over the adoption of the same age as that
defined by the poor-law. By doing that we
make the liability of the man and the
mother the same.
But although I am asking the Committee
to standardise the age of the boy at 15, I
want to ask the Committee that in the case
of the illegitimate girl we should, in
accordance with the trend of all English
legislation since 1872, go one stage further.
I want to make the age of the illegitimate
girl 16 instead of 15. I do not think I need
labour the point. It is very obvious that
we should give the utmost protection to a
girl who has the misfortune to be illegitimate. Having regard to such provisions as
we recently incorporated in the Education
Act, re-enacting the provisions of the
Employment of Children Act, laying down
definitely that the local authorities should
do everything they can to discourage the
employment of girls under 16 it seems to
me that there is a duty imposed upon us to
give special protection to them. I hope the
Committee will agree with me.
I am asking the Committee to standardise the age at
15 in the case of a boy, and at 16 in the case
of a girl. That would prevent the necessity
of applying to the court at a later date,
which has objections to which I wish to
draw the attention of the Committee in
another amendment.
Standardising the
age at 16 involves a few words of provision
in case
It is
the girl should marry.
obvious should the girl marry at 16 we
cannot leave a dual liability on the father
and on the husband.
Finally, I propose to leave out a few
words which you will find at the top of page
2 of the Bill as regards costs. I am asking
the Committee to do that in order to enable
me to introduce towards the end of the Bill
a new Clause which will enact what is the
latest law in England on the subject. It is
a little less one-sided perhaps than some of
the antediluvian wording taken from the
Act of 1872.
215 Illegitimate Children Bill-

THE SENATE.
Committee.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I have listened to
the speech which the hon. Member has
delivered in supporting the Amendment which
he has on the Paper and I need hardly say
I find myself in agreement with him in all
the points which he has put before the Committee. I would just accept the amendment
and will not go further into the details
which he has mentioned.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move on page 2,
line 3, after 'or" ("with child or delivered")
to insert "has been." This is a very trifling
matter. If you look at the words of subclause (2) "with child or delivered" it rather
reads as if it were dealing with the future
whereas if you look at the Bill as a whole
we see it reads with reference to the past
tense, and, therefore, I beg to move to insert the words "has been" so as to make the
Bill apply all the way to a woman who is
with child or has been delivered.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
This is a drafting amendment, and I am
grateful to the hon. Member for bringing it
forward. I accept the amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move in page 2,
line 10, to leave out "was with child of, or"
("marriage was with child of, or had been")
My objection to these words is twofoldpartly legal, and partly on the ground of
expediency.
I wish the House to note that these words
are not in any English Act, though the first
Act dealing with this subject was passed
some 80 years ago, and several amending
Acts have been passed since then.
as
These words were not in the present Bill
introduced into the other House
but were inserted in the Committee
discussion, and I think they must have been
inserted without due consideration of the
effect of them. If we keep these words
in the Bill we will be acting in absolute defiance of a rule of law which has been well
known in England and in this country. It
was well known in the year 1777 when Lord
Mansfield, one of the most distinguished
judges that our country has ever had, referred
to it. He said in the case Goodright v.
Moss the facts of the case are not important
-but this great judge laid down the law at
two separate stages of the hearing with
characteristic lucidity. The first passage is:
The law of England is clear that the
declarations of a father or mother cannot
be admitted to bastardize the issue born
after marriage.
i
The second passage is:
As to the time of the birth the father
and mother are the most proper witnesses
to prove it, but it is a rule founded in
decency, morality, and policy, that they
shall not be permitted to say after marriage
that they have had no connection, and,
therefore, that the offspring is spurious;
more especially the mother who is the
offending party.
I do not intend to discuss the legal matter
further than to say that there have been
various decisions on the same point when
this rule was recognised as binding. In the
latest case, the Russell case, it was decided
(since the Bill left the other House) in the
same way. I just want to refer to some
observations of Lord Birkenhead, the Lord
Chancellor, in the case.
He says:-
.1
Sir Douglas Hogg admitted with perfect
frankness that the evidence would be inadmissible in a legitimacy case
understand that admission as applying to
nothing except legitimacy suits in the
narrowest sense, by which I mean when
the person himself whose legitimacy is in
question is a party to the suit, or when
the question of Yea or Nay to legitimacy
necessarily settles the point of controversy.
He says:-
The dictum of Lord Mansfield is not
limited in expression.
There was an Act of Parliament passed in
the year 1869 to allow husband and wife to
give certain evidence in divorce suits. That
Act was discussed in the Russell case, and
Lord Birkenhead said:-
I am myself quite unable to understand
how this section can have the effect assigned
to it by the Court of Appeal. It makes
indeed the parties to any proceeding, instituted in consequence of adultery, and
the husbands and wives of such parties,
competent to give evidence in such proceeding. What in effect does it make such
persons competent to do? Plainly and
only, I should have thought, to give such
testimony, as the law of evidence allowed
at that date, or as afterwards modified
that law may allow.
That is the decision given on the 30th May
of the present year, and it is the latest exposition of the law on that matter. If these
words are allowed to stand it apparently
makes it legal for the wife, in fact it directs
the wife, before she can get a summons, to
make a declaration to the effect that the
child is illegitimate and stating who the
actual father of the child is. That is alter-
217 Illegitimate Children Bill-

21 OCTOBER 1924
Committee.
ing this rule of law in a very important particular and I say as strongly as I can that
an important alteration in the law ought.
not to be made, as is proposed in this case,
but it ought to be made by a Bill brought
forward for altering the law of evidence.
Sub-section 2 says that application may be
made by the mother or Board of Guardians,
and it goes on to provide that the mother
must make a declaration. In
further
section it says that the evidence of the
mother is to be received in evidence. I do
not propose to deal any further with the
legal point, but it is a very serious one and
one that we should consider very carefully
before we agree that these words should be
inserted.
a
On the ground of expediency, the proceedings must be taken by the mother and cannot be taken by the father, who is the
injured person in this matter. It is said
that if a woman finds herself in a certain
condition she may, look round and try to
get married so as to prevent the scandal
that might arise, and the husband is
surely the person who should complain.
The husband, however, cannot take the
proceedings. It must be the wife, the
offending person. She must come forward
and state to her shame that another man is
the father of the child. The person whose
interests we have to consider is the
child. This Act is passed, as the Preamble shows, for the benefit of the child,
and the child is certainly an innocent person. These proceedings must be taken by the
mother which will injure her child for life.
The effect of these proceedings will be that
this innocent child will have a stain upon
its character all through its life. Its school
days and its college days if it goes to
college will be rendered miserable, and its
whole life will be spoiled. In considering
how the child shall be benefited I do not
think we should allow a Clause of that
kind to get into the Act. It would
be a great pity also if a married couple
should be allowed to blast their own
married career by taking such
step
as this, the only result of which would be
to give the mother-not the father a few
shillings a week for a few years. On the
merits of the question I do not think we
should allow these words to go into the
Bill.
a
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am sure I am most grateful to the hon.
Member for having moved this amendment
to the Bill. I have listened with great
interest to his remarks, and on behalf of
the Government I may say that we are
prepared to accept the amendment.
certainly think it will be an improvement
in the Bill, and in view of the judgment in
We
the Russell case, to which the hon. Member has
referred, it is most important that the Bill
should be altered in this direction. Therefore I am glad to accept the amendment on
behalf of the Government.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: In Clause 1, page 2, line
14, I beg to move to leave out the words,
"the birth of the child has taken place,"
and to insert instead thereof the words,
"the child has been born alive." This does
not really effect any alteration in the Bill.
but it is to make the matter clear.
The
words in the Bill are, "the birth of the
child has taken place." I am rather afraid
there might be some difficulty about that,
and I therefore propose to insert instead
the words, "the child has been born alive,"
SO as to follow
well-known English
decision. I beg to move.
a
Mr. GREER: I am not quite sure what
the effect of this alteration may be, because
I understand that in connection with the
Affiliation Orders Bill the Court can make
an order that the expenses incidental to the
birth of the child shall be paid by the
father. There may be expenses incidental
to the birth, although the child is not born
alive, and I do not see why those expenses
should not be paid by the putative father
even though the child is not born alive.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The amendment which the hon. Member has
proposed I am prepared to accept on behalf
of the Government. The effect of the amendment will be to confine proceedings under the
Act to those cases where an illegitimate
child has been born alive. I do not anticipate
that there will be any difficulty in the matter.
I can assure the hon. Member that all the
amendments incorporated in the Bill at the
stage which we are now in process of going
through will be very carefully considered by
the Government, and by the Government
Draughtsman, between now and the Report
stage, and if the fear to which the hon.
Member has given expression is based on any
substantial ground I shall take steps to make
an alteration in the Bill.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 1,
page 2, line 25, to leave out the words, 'the
fact that the mother is entitled," and to insert instead thereof the words, "any right
of the mother." This is little more than a
drafting amendment. Sub-Clause 4 is taken
from one of the enactments relating to
National Health Insurance where it is perfectly
clear and is explained by the context. As it
appears here it rather suggests that the mere
fact of maternity is sufficient to confer a

Illegitimate Children Bill-

Committee.

[Mr. Leslie]
case.
right to insurance benefit. That is not the
Before you can get insurance benefit
you must be an insured person or insured by
marriage. Therefore, I suggest it is desirable
to change these few words lest any misunderstanding should arise. I beg to move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I accept the amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: Instead of moving the
amendment which stands in my name on the
Order Paper-to leave out Sub-Section 5 of
Section 1-I would ask the indulgence of the
House for one or two words of explanation.
I think it would be better if this question of
the constitution of the Court stood over until the Bill is further advanced, when we will
get matters more in their true perspective.
There is another amendment on the Paper the
consideration of which will bear on this
question. I therefore ask leave not to move
this amendment.
Amendment not moved.
Mr. GREER: In Clause 1 I beg to move
to insert at the end of Sub-Section 5 of
Section 1, the following:-
a
Provided that
summons under this
Section may be issued by any Justice of
the Peace having jurisdiction in the petty
sessions district or place where the summons
is returnable.
I am personally very strongly in favour of
having the Tribunal consist of the Resident
Magistrates Court. I think that very important questions will arise that will need
to be decided by a trained lawyer, a man who
has had experience in dealing with cases, and
in the interests of the child I certainly think
the Court should consist of a Resident Magistrate. At the present time no one can be
appointed a Resident Magistrate unless he
has had five years' experience at the Bar or
in the solicitors' profession, so we may assume
that they must have a certain amount of legal
knowledge. But there are certain cases where
it would be a very great hardship if a Resident Magistrate had to deal with the case in
every branch. For example, the mother of
the child may live in one county remote from
a railway and the Resident Magistrate may
be at a considerable distance, and this Resident
Magistrate may be a very busy man with a
large area to cover. It is not at all easy to make
an appointment with the Resident Magistrate
or for the mother to go and have the summons
issued by him. Issuing the summons is not
really a matter that affects the decision of
the case. Therefore, I think that that part
of the case might be left to the ordinary
justice of the peace, and I suggest that this
proviso should be inserted to make it clear
that any ordinary justice of the peace can
issue a summons on a declaration being made
by the woman.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The effect of the amendment which the hon.
Member has moved is to allow the ordinary
justice of the peace to issue a summons in
this class of case, instead of the power of
issuing a summons being confined to the
resident magistrate. This certainly will
relieve the resident magistrate of some of
his duties, and I feel will also facilitate the
taking of proceedings. Therefore I am willing to accept the amendment moved by the
hon. Member.
Amendment agreed to.
Ordered: That Clause as amended stand
part of the Bill.
