CLAUSE 2. (Provisions as to Summons.)
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
(2) An application for a summons under
this section may be made either by the
mother or, if the mother or her illegitimate
child is in receipt of relief from the poor
rates of any poor law union, by the board
of guardians of that union.
(3) An application for such summons may
be made-
(a) at any time before the birth of such
child; or
(b) within twelve months from the birth
of such child; or
(c) after the expiration of the said twelve
months, upon proof that the man alleged
to be the father of such child has within
the said twelve months paid money for its
maintenance; or
(d) within the twelve months next after
the return to Northern Ireland of the man
alleged to be the father of such child,
upon proof that he ceased to reside in
Northern Ireland within the twelve
months next after the birth of such child.
(4) No summons shall be issued under this
section, unless the mother has made an information in writing upon oath stating who
is the father of the child.
(5) It shall be lawful for the mother to
apply for a summons or take any other proceeding under this Act before the court,
notwithstanding that she is under the age
of twenty-one years.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 2,
page 3, line 8, to leave out "its maintenance" ("money for its maintenance; or")
and insert "the maintenance of such child."
This is a very small matter. It is only to
bring the words taken from an old Act
221 Illegitimate Children Bill- 21 OCTOBER 1924

Committee.
more into accordance with modern practice.
Since 1889 at all events it has never been
the practice to refer to a child as "it,"
consequently the more usual form is to use
the words which are applicable in this case.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
accept the amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
was
a
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 2.
page 3, line 11, after "he" ("proof that he
ceased to reside") to insert "was absent
from Northern Ireland at the time of the
birth of such child or that he." This is
another amendment which makes, I think,
no change in the meaning of the Bill, and
which is very desirable in itself. If Clause
(d) of sub-clause (3) is read carefully it
certainly does appear that
man could
escape his liability by fleeing the country
before the child was actually born. The
words of the sub-clause are taken from the
English Act of '72, and from '72 to 1896 it
held in England that a man might
escape his liability in that manner. However, in 1896
very determined woman,
whose name, I think, was Margaret Hughes,
brought a case up to the King's Bench
Court. The Court decided that the words
"ceasing to reside" was a continuing expression, and that therefore a man could
not escape liability by the simple process
of fleeing the country before the child was
born. It happened to be one of the cases
considered by a Select Committee set up in
1909, which recommended that the position
should be made clear. I therefore suggest
that we insert the words which stand in
my name on the Paper which, I may say, I
am assured will effect the object I have in
view.
a
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The amendment which the hon. Member
has moved is to meet an omission in the
Bill. I am prepared to accept it.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 2,
page 3, line 17 to leave out sub-section (5).
The sub-clause which I desire to omit for
the moment is in my humble judgment one
of the best clauses in the Bill. It is drawn
in order to enable the unfortunate woman
who is under 21 years of age and who has
an illegitimate child, to take proceedings on
her own behalf. I do not think that the
proposal that comes before us goes quite
far enough. The actual wording of subclause (5) seems to suggest that the powers
are limited to the court with which you have
been dealing hitherto-that is a Court of Summary Jurisdiction-and do not carry on to the
Court of Appeal. However, that suggestion will,
I think, be easily disposed of, because you
cannot commence an action in the Court of
Appeal. I have a stronger reason for asking
the Committee to remove the sub-clause for
the moment in order to allow me to introduce a somewhat expanded form at a later
stage of the Bill where it properly belongs.
When I come to it later on I shall explain
that it will be necessary to give to the
woman power to use the services of the
collecting officer.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
accept the amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Ordered: That Clause as amended stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3. (Recording of Evidence and
non-abatement of proceedings in certain
cases.)
(1) As soon as a summons has been issued
for the appearance of, and duly served upon,
the man alleged by the mother to be the
father of her illegitimate child, the court
may proceed to hear such evidence as may
be adduced on oath and shall record the
same in writing. Such evidence shall be
read over and signed respectively by each
witness who may have been examined, and
shall be signed by the resident magistrate
hearing the same.
The record of such evidence verified as
aforesaid shall be admissible as evidence in
any proceedings under this Act, where it is
proved that the witness who gave such
evidence is dead or so ill as to be unable to
attend or where his attendance cannot be
procured.
(2) Proceedings under this Act shall not
be determined by the death of either the
mother or the putative father of an illegitimate child.
In the case of the death of the mother,
the proceedings may be continued by the
person having the custody of the child, or,
if the child is in receipt of relief from the
poor rates of any poor law union, by the
board of guardians of that union. In the
case of the death of the putative father, his
personal representative may be made
party to the proceedings.
a
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 3,
page 3, line 21, to leave out sub-section (1).
I am approaching a more difficult matter,
and I have some diffidence in asking the
Committee to excise the whole of sub-clause
(1) of Clause 3 of the Bill. My difficulties
arise from the fact that I know that this
clause was introduced with the best possible
intentions. It was designed to meet a possible hardship and therefore it would naturally awaken our sympathies. It was this
Illegitimate Children Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie]
clause-or rather this was one of the clauses
I had in my mind when speaking on the
Second reading of the Bill when it came to
us, I said that the Bill breathed humanity
in every line. First of all we have to clear
up to what cases this sub-clause is intended
to apply. I assume-I would ask for correction if I am wrong-that it is intended
that this sub-clause would apply to all cases
where application is made before or after
the birth of the child. If it were limited to
cases of application after the birth, I should
still think the sub-clause unnecessary, but
the greater part of my objection would be
gone. I am afraid, however, that is not the
case.
an
of
us
or
Let
The proposal means that when the mother
has taken out her summons under Clause 2
(1) there is to be no time limit, but
immediate hearing for the purpose
recording
the evidence before the
birth of the child has taken place.
To that I cannot possibly agree.
look and see what happens, why the
thing has been introduced, and what it will
come to. It is to meet the case of a woman
who is going to die in child-birth. How
many cases of that sort are there actually
before us? We find from the 1922 report of
the Registrar-General that in the year 1922
the number of women throughout Northern
Ireland who died in child-birth,
from
causes connected with the state of pregnancy
amounted to 173, which is one less than the
That
average of the preceding ten years.
number of 173 stands in relation to the
number of births of living children in, I
think, the ratio of 5.83 per thousand, that
is to say, approximately one half of one per
cent. There is no evidence that I know of
that the number of deaths under such conditions amongst mothers of illegitimate
children is greater than amongst married
women, and the table to which I am about
to refer seems to me to suggest the probability that the mortality amongst mothers
of illegitimate children is rather below than
above the actual average.
I find from the Registrar-General's report
that of these 173 cases only 29 occurred under
the age of 25. The great bulk of these
cases occurred above the age of 30. I do not
think that the number of cases in which death
occurred leaving a living child behind can
possibly be great.
The point that the clause can only become
effective if the mother dies and leaves a
living child behind is one to which I must
draw the closest possible attention. If the
mother dies not leaving a living child the
clause is useless and I must remind the Committee at this stage that still-born births are
not registered in Northern Ireland. That
does not affect my figures.
But passing to the method by which this
sub-clause is put into operation I must confess that I have great difficulty. It starts by
saying "As soon as a summons has been issued
for the appearance of." What sort of a
summons is that? It can only be the summons referred to, I imagine, in Section 2 (1)
of the Bill, that is, a summons to appear
to show cause why an affiliation order should
not be made upon him in respect of such
child. It seems to me that a man presented
with that summons, if he paid any attention
to it at all, would not have great difficulty
in securing the dismissal of the case. All
he has to say is, "Where is the child?"
You have no power to summon him. What
we want in reference to this Clause is, of
course, the Clause passed in the English Act
of 1845, which provides that when a summons
is taken out before the birth of the child
the magistrate shall make the summons returnable at some time after the date when
the woman expects the child to be born. If
when that date arrives the child has not been
born, or if the mother has not sufficiently recovered from the effects of the birth then,
the hearing will be adjourned. That is the
usual practice, and that is the practice we
ought to follow.
secure.
come.
I have dealt with the difficulties about the
summons. Now let us look at it again on
other lines.
I say that if this sub-clause
stands we shall wreck our Bill. We shall
take away the assistance which we desire to
We want to make the unfortunate
woman an ally of ours in doing all we can
for the child. I say if this Clause stands,
if she has first of all to take out a summons, and then come before the court for
intermediate hearing and be subject to examination and cross-examination, she will not
You will lose her entirely. You
will deter the woman from taking advan-
And if the women does
tage of the Bill.
come, what will happen? You will have to
examine and cross-examine a woman probably in an advanced stage of pregnancy. It
would be indecent and it would be revolting,
and it is proposed to do all that to catch
the possible case, which, I am informed, is
one in a thousand of women who die in
child-birth leaving a living child. I venture
to assert that this House would not be doing
its duty as a Second Chamber if it allowed
this sub-clause to stand.
The CHAIRMAN: In putting this amendment I propose only to put the first few
words of Sub-section 1, otherwise Senator
Greer's amendments would be shut out
altogether. The Question is that the words,
as soon as the summons has been issued"
be left out.
Mr. GREER: Like my hon. Friend (Mr.
Leslie), I had some difficulty in understand-
225 Illegitimate Children Bill- 21 OCTOBER 1924

Committee.
ing what was meant to be carried out by
the Section as it appears in the Bill;
whether it was only to refer to cases which
were brought forward before the birth of
the child, or whether it was intended to perpetuate the evidence of the mother in all
cases. I came to the conclusion that it referred merely to cases occurring before the
birth of the child. I do not know whether
I was right in that or not, but I put down
some amendments having that view in my
mind, and referring to such cases. On the
merits of the case I think it is a good
Clause to have in the Bill-that the evidence
of the mother should be perpetuated. We
are here, not so much to consider what is
going to happen to the mother but what is
going to happen to the innocent child, and
I think it is in the interests of the child that
if the mother is in a position to give evidence, that evidence should be preserved
and used in the event of her death. My
view is that we should preserve this Clause
with my amendments. I would have liked
to say a word or two on my amendments
before we dispose of this amendment.
The CHAIRMAN: You cannot do that.
Colonel SINCLAIR: I should like to offer
a few observations in support of the deletion of this sub-section. It seems to me
that the machinery invoked here is perhaps
too cumbersome. Assuming it is the first
pregnancy there is the shame and mortification attaching to the application, and then
I think there is another consideration.
It
may jeopardise the marriage of the couple
which might otherwise ensue and thus legitimise the offspring if legal proceedings
were not instituted.
With regard to the difficulty of supplying
corroborative evidence, it would be extremely difficult to obtain such evidence in
any other form than by a medical examination and I should like to enter a protest
against a medical examination for this purpose of a woman in an advanced state of
pregnancy. It is almost of a semi-public
character and it may have an evil effect
upon her health and may influence a normal
delivery, with the result that the child may
be born prematurely. The difficulty of
ascertaining the fact of pregnancy should
also be remembered. It may appear to a
woman that she is pregnant, but there are
a great many examples of what is called
pseudo pregnancy, or false pregnancy. Either
in a nervous state or a state of expectancy
a woman may imagine that she is pregnant
and she may institute proceedings or issue
a summons against a man when the pregnancy is really of a false character. Therefore before birth it is very difficult to be
quite certain of the fact of pregnancy and
the necessary examination may not always
be unattended by some risk. I therefore think
that this clause is unnecessary. It introduces an amount of cumbersome machinery
that is scarcely workable. The hold you
would have on the putative father would
not be so effective if this Sub-section is included in the Bill. It seems to me that the
institution of proceedings is much more
likely to frighten the putative father.
support the deletion of this Clause.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The amendment which has been proposed
by Senator Leslie for the deletion of this
Sub-section I am prepared to accept on behalf of the Government. It is quite true
that this is a reversal of the Clause as it
was passed in another place, but in view
of the very powerful arguments adduced by
Senator Leslie and Senator Sinclair the
House will agree that the Bill will be improved by the deletion of this Sub-section.
There are three main reasons that I may
mention.
The first is in accordance with
what Senator Leslie has said that death in
child-birth is very rare, and if the mother
dies the child usually dies also. In the
second place this is a provision which in
England and Scotland it has not been
thought necessary to incorporate in the
Bills dealing with the same subject.
Thirdly, I feel that such a provision might
bring about all those difficulties which
Senator Sinclair has referred to. Therefore, on behalf of the Government, I find
myself in complete agreement with my hon.
Friend in his suggestion that this Subsection should be deleted from the Bill. I
have much pleasure in accepting the amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendments to Clause 3
stood on the Order Paper in the name of
Mr. Greer :-

the return of a summons."
line 22. Leave out "and duly served
upon."
line 24. After "may" insert "if the birth
of the child has not taken place."
line 28. At end insert "and the court may
then adjourn the hearing until the birth
has taken place."
line 30. After "any” insert "further."
Mr. GREER: I beg leave to withdraw
the amendments standing in my name.
Amendments not moved.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
On a point of order, I think I may perhaps
simplify the procedure if I tell the hon.
Member that I do not propose to accept the
amendment, because he will realise that
owing to the deletion of Sub-section 1, a
change will have to be made in the form of
Sub-section 2, and the matter he is dealing
with is the subject of one of his amendments later on.
Mr. LESLIE: I am deeply sensible of
what the noble Marquess has drawn attention to, and I am inclined to think it would
be better to withdraw this amendment.
But if I do I shall be in a difficulty with
regard to my later amendments, because the
second one of the group is really dependent on
the third. The real pith of this question
comes in an amendment later on. In any
case this particular Sub-section must be reexamined.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon. Member suggests that this Subsection should be deleted because of the
bearing which the deletion of Sub-section 1
has on Sub-section 2. I proposed that he
should defer his remarks because it will be
pocessary for the Government to re-examine
this Sub-section before the Report stage
and bring it in in such form as will be consistent with the Bill after the deletion of
Sub-section 1. Perhaps it would be as well,
therefore, if this Sub-section was withdrawn
for the purpose of allowing it to be introduced in another form on the Report stage.
I am not clear that by the Sub-section being
left in, as at present, it affects the amendment he proposes to move at a later stage.
Nevertheless, to simplify matters, I am
quite willing that the Sub-section should be
omitted on the understanding that on the
Report Stage I may bring in another Subsection consistent with the Bill as it stands
after the deletion of Sub-section 1.

Mr. LESLIE: I move the amendment and

Mr. GREER: I beg to move, to insert
after Clause 3 as a new Clause:
Where an agreement is entered into
between the mother of an illegitimate
child and the man alleged by her to be
the father of that child, for the payment
by that man of a weekly sum of money,
or of a lump sum, for the maintenance
and education of the child, and in discharge of any liability under this Act in
respect thereof, such an agreement shall be
a bar to proceedings to obtain an Affiliation Order under this Act in respect of
that child, or in case such agreement is
entered into after a summons has been
issued under this Act, but before the hearing of the case, a bar to further proceedings for that purpose, if a resident
magistrate is satisfied on an ex parte
application by either party (which may
be heard and determined otherwise than
in open court):-
(a) that the agreement will afford to the
child benefits at least equal to those which
might be expected to have been obtained
by the making of an Affiliation Order or
the commutation of the weekly sums payable under an Affiliation Order; and
(b) that the agreement provides for the
payment of the weekly sums, or the lump
sum, to some person or body of persons
approved by the resident magistrate; and
(c) where the agreement is for a lump
sum, that it provides for the investment
and application of that sum for the maintenance and education of the child in
such manner as the resident magistrate
may approve.
229 Illegitimate Children Bill- 21 OCTOBER 1924

Committee.

El
I would like to point out that this
Amendment is the most important one I have
put down on the Paper. It provides in
word for an amicable settlement between the
mother and the actual father of an illegitimate child without insisting on having any
proceedings in open court or any undue publicity. I think that on reflection hon.
Members will see that it is in the interests of the child that I have brought this
forward. We all know that the person who
is the father of an illegitimate child is very
anxious to prevent any scandal arising that
may involve him in the matter and would
be very glad to pay a larger sum than the
court would probably award in order to have
a quiet settlement of the case out of Court,
and I see no reason why, if he is prepared to
pay a considerable sum of money to make a
settlement which would provide better for
the child than the Act of Parliament, he
should not have the chance to settle the
case. I think my amendment makes it clear
that a settlement has to be approved by the
Resident Magistrate sitting in camera, and
he would have to decide on hearing the
evidence that the child would at any rate
get as much per week as any Court would be
likely to award to the child. This would
avoid bringing into Court
very large
number of cases. At present the only way
of having these settled is by summons and by
having the case heard in open Court. This
plan of mine would prevent the necessity for
such publicity and enable a settlement of the
case to be made out of Court. I need hardly
say that the probability is that if a man who
is in a position to pay money to settle the
case liberally, finds
summons issued
against him he would go to the best lawyer
and get him to defend the case and rake up
all the past history of the mother and bring
forward a great many facts that would not
be pleasant to listen to. If an order were
made against him he would appeal and have
the whole thing over again in Court, whereas
if it were possible to make a reaonable
settlement out of Court he would be glad to
do so. I hope the Committee will accept the
Amendment.
a
a
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
It would obviously be advantageous as far as
possible that these matters should be kept
out of public Courts, and I am prepared to
accept the Clause which the hon. Member has
moved. The Clause provides for agreement
between the parties to abandon proceedings
under the Act and substitute for such proceedings mutual agreement which must be
to the satisfaction of the Resident Magistrate,
and which agreement must certainly not be
prejudicial to the interests of the child-an
agreement under which the child receives
treatment not less advantageous than under
any Order which could be issuable under the
*
Act. Therefore, I am prepared to accept
the Clause which the hon. Member has
moved.
Amendment agreed to, and new Clause
added to the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Provisions as to duration, effect
and variation of Affiliation Orders).
(1) An Affiliation Order shall not (except.
for the recovery of arrears or unless extended under this section) continue in force
after the child, in respect of whom it was
made, has attained the age of fourteen
years, or after the death of such child.
(2) A liability under an Affiliation Order
shall not be determined by the death of
the mother or of the putative father of
the child in respect of whom the order has
been made. In the case of the death of the
putative father the court may make an
order for the payment out of his estate
of such lump sum as the court thinks just,
but in no case exceeding the aggregate of
the sums for the payment of which the
putative father would, if he had lived,
have been liable under the Affiliation
Order.
as
(3) Any weekly sum payable under an
Affiliation Order shall be calculated
from such date, not earlier than the birth
of the child, as the court thinks fit.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
(5) A court of summary jurisdiction may,
on the application of the mother or a board
of guardians or any person for the time
being having the custody of the child, or
upon the application of the collecting
officer or the man adjudged to be the putative father of the child, make an order
varying an Affiliation Order-
(a) by providing for the continuance thereof after the child has attained the age
of fourteen years; or
(b) by altering the amount of the sums
payable thereunder or directing that the
payment of those sums shall cease to be
revived; or
(c) by directing that the sums payable
thereunder shall be paid to some person
other than the person named in the
Affiliation Order.
Provided that no order SO made shall
continue in force after the child has
attained the age of sixteen years, and that
the sums payable under any such order
shall be weekly sums not exceeding twenty
shillings a week.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause 4,
page 4, line 3, to leave out sub-section 1.

THE SENATE.
Committee.
[Mr. Leslie]
This is purely a consequential Amendment
to the Amendment which the Committee did

Amendment agreed to.
or of
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause 4,
page 4, lines 9 and 10, to leave out
the putative father."
I am in another difficulty. The point is
whether I should discuss this and the next
Amendment together, or whether I should
leave this to the Report stage, because it
really is more or less consequent on the
other. Perhaps it would be better if I deal
with the whole matter together. The point
of the Clause is that, in the case of the death
of the putative father, the Court may make
an order for the payment out of his estate
of a lump sum, that is to say, as I say, now
for the first time you propose to introduce
into the law of Northern Ireland something
analogous to the old Roman law about willmaking, and also to give the illegitimate
child a distinct advantage which a legitimate
child would not possess. You are risking, as
it seems to me, doing a great injustice to
innocent parties, and I am not at all satisfied that the machinery it is proposed to set
up is altogether satisfactory. The proposal
is, as the Bill stands at the moment, that the
Court, that is, a Resident Magistrate, sitting
alone, may make an order for the payment
out of a man's estate of such lump sum as
the Court thinks just. It does not give the
Court any indication as to what considerations
the Court is to admit in deciding. It does
not either here or elsewhere in the Bill provide, so far as I can see, for any appeal from
that Resident Magistrate. I cannot find any
right of appeal as the Bill comes to us in
this particular case. It is a complete dislocation of the ordinary law and practice, and
I must endeavour to show in what way the
dislocation comes in. It is also inequitable,
because you would only pursue the estate
of the father. If the mother died and left
property the child would not succeed to that
in default of testament.
The ordinary rules of devolution of property on death are well known to lawyers.
In case of absolute intestacy, and where a
man does not leave any heirs at all, the
estate passes to the Crown. I do not know
whether the Government is satisfied that
this Parliament has power to deal with the
rights of the Crown in the manner suggested.
However, I will leave that. If the man dies
leaving a widow without children, by statute
that widow is entitled to the first £500 of
that man's estate. She has that right by
statute. If there are dependants, let us say
there are half-a-dozen legitimate relations,
these half-dozen have their rights by law. It
is proposed to put their rights at the disposal
of a tribunal which they did not establish,
and to which they did not consent, and in
whose decision they may have no confidence.
At all events, that is the proposal. Again,
before leaving this, what is to happen to
this lump sum which will be provided, if
it can be provided, in the event of the death
of the child first referred to? We are not
told. The mere production of the lump sum,
of course, gives someone an interest in the
death of the illegitimate child. Illegitimate
children die already at a rate about twice
the rate of ordinary children, and if we
introduce the lump sum I am not sure that
you are acting in the interests of life protection. I will leave that case.
I want to put to the Committee one or two
possible occurrences. So long as a putative
father is alive, he will have to make a weekly
payment, if he can, out of his income or
earnings. If he marries and has a family,
and so on, he will have perhaps to limit
himself, to go without tobacco perhaps, to
make that payment. When the man dies he
cannot any longer subject himself to selfdenial. The money has got to be paid by
somebody else. The man who has an illegitimate child pays the money out of earnings.
He marries, perhaps he has children, he takes
a small house and furnishes it. If he dies,
perhaps this little house and furniture are
all the property he leaves behind. There is
the widow, who, by statute, is entitled to the
first £500. Are you going to sell up that
house and that furniture, and smash up the
home of the widow and children, for the
sake of providing for the illegitimate child?
It has never been done in England, and it
should not be done here.
an
a
a
Take
rather more complicated case.
We are dealing with, ex hypothesi,
immoral man. I assume he becomes
the putative father-only putative father;
we never say real father. He is condemned to pay SO many shillings
week, and does it. He goes away and
marries, and he has, or has not, legitimate
children; it really does not matter. Being
still an immoral man, he deserts his wife
and children. His wife promptly goes to the
magistrate's court, and gets an order for
maintenance under the Act of 1886, with
allowances for the children. That does not
bring the husband back. He takes another
mistress, and by her has two or three children, and then dies. What is the position?
He leaves property of less than £500. It is
plain that the last mistress and two or three
illegitimate children, in respect of whom an
affiliation order was not obtained, will get
nothing. Are you not preferring the right
of the original illegitimate children to the
rights of the widow and legitimate children?
The matter was considered, like many other
matters of this kind, by the Select Committee
of the House of Commons in 1909. I hope
the House will pardon me if I read what
was said-
233 Illegitimate Children Bill- 21 OCTOBER 1924

Committee.
"
Mr. Blackwell, in his evidence on behalf
of the Home Office, pointed out that such
a process would be applicable to a very
small number of cases, since in the
majority of cases no estate is left worth
administering, and that it would promote
the interests of the illegitimates at the
expense of lawful children and widows."
That seems to be the position. If we allow
these two paragraphs to stand we are giving
the illegitimate child something that the
legitimate child does not possess.
There are two possible ways of doing
better. One of them is by life insurance
and the other would be by the introduction of
series of Clauses drawing
a
a
case of
a
distinction between the
putative father who dies leaving legitimate
heirs and a man who does not, and that
would protect the interest of the widow in
the first £500 and the interests of the
children. There does not seem to be any
reason why the illegitimate child should have
special privileges that the legitimate child
does not enjoy.
case
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: It is
very hard for the amateur mind to follow the
windings of Senator Leslie's speech, but he
makes a mistake when he says that there is
no provision in law for the illegitimate child.
There is one law which affects the illegitimate child just as much as it does the legitimate child. I refer to the
where a
father is killed and an award of, say, £600
compensation is made. Under the Act I
understand that the widow gets a certain
amount, say £200 or £300, and if there are
legitimate and illegitimate children the law
looks upon them as equal and the money is
divided amongst them. It is a very conflicting question and my object is to see that the
illegitimate child gets more protection than
the legitimate child because the illegitimate
child has no one to look after it, whereas the
ordinary law looks after the rights of the
legitimate child. The amendment is one that
I strongly object to.
Mr. GREER: I do not propose to follow
the hon. Member through his interesting
speech, because I look at the matter from an
entirely different standpoint. In my opinion,
when a mother gets an Affiliation Order
against a putative father that is an order
for the payment of a definite sum per week
for a definite number of years. That is a
debt due by the putative father to that
woman. Without going into the law on the
distribution of deceased's effects we all know
that before there is any distribution of
effects the deceased's debts must be paid.
Therefore, I consider it is not giving an
illegitimate child an advantage over the
legitimate children. The man must pay his
debts in the same way as he must pay a debt
due to his grocer or baker, or to any other
person before he begins to distribute his
assets to his wife and children. Therefore,
I do not think it is a good thing that the
putative father, who has assisted to bring
this child into the world, should, simply by
leaving the world, deprive the child of what
the Court decided it was entitled to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The hon. Member who has introduced this
amendment has supported his argument by
some ingenious methods, and I can assure
him that his flights of imagination have well
nigh bewildered me. I may say at the outset
that the Government are not prepared to
accept the hon. Member's amendment. In
fact, they are prepared to offer strong
opposition to it. It is quite true that this
provision is not contained in the Measure
which is effective on the other side of the
water, but the Government are of opinion
that the Bill we are introducing is an improvement on the Bill which is in operation
in England and Scotland.
The object of this amendment is to remove
from the Bill the provision which gives the
illegitimate child a claim upon his deceased
father's estate, the amount being within the
discretion of the Court, but limited to a sum
not exceeding the aggregate of the payments
to which the father would have been liable
under the Affiliation Order. This Amendment is based on a proposal which was considered by the Select Committee of 1909 on
Bastardy Orders. This proposal was opposed
by the Home Office, on the ground that it
would be applicable in a very small number
of cases and would promote the interests of
illegitimates, at the expense of lawful
children and widows. That was the substance of the case which Senator Leslie put
forward
In the House of Lords Lord Onslow, speaking on the Bill, said:
a
"In the House of Commons on
Report
Stago an undertaking was given to consider whether there would be introduced
into the Bill a new principle, allowing the
mother of a bastard to rank as a creditor
of the estate of the putative father. This
point has been most carefully considered,
but the Government are unable to accept
it. A similar proposal was examined by
Select Committee in 1909 and was unanimously rejected by them on the following ground:-They said that the great
majority of persons against whom
Affiliation Orders are made leave no
estate worth administering. If the man
were a bachelor he would probably leave
no estate at all, and the provision would
be a dead letter. But in the case of a
married man with a wife and two or
three children, his estate would probably
consist of his home and furniture. These
235 Illegitimate Children Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
would have to be sold up, the widow and
her children would be turned into the
street, and, after paying the costs of
administering the estate, there might
possibly be a small balance left-certainly
not enough to make any substantial provision for the illegitimate child. Therefore, the Select Committee was not in
favour of accepting this Amendment of
the law."
The
But notwithstanding this argument to the
contrary I feel sure the House will agree
with me that Lord Onslow's observations
upon the financial position of the father
would appear to be not very well founded.
If there is no estate, there would certainly
be no benefit, while no Court would turn
the widow and legitimate children out of
house and home to make an award to an
illegitimate child. The Bill gives the
power to the Court to order the payment
of such lump sum "as the Court thinks
just." That is a sufficient safeguard of
the rights, what I may call the prior
rights, of the legitimate connections.
effect of the Clause as it stands is to safeguard not only the illegitimate childwhich certainly is a public consideration,
and one that we should not lose sight of,
as I am sure the hon. Member (Mr. Leslie)
does not-but it is a protection for the ratepayer as well. There can surely be very
little to be said against a provision that
ensures that, where the Court, with all the
circumstances and necessary considerations
before it, judges that the deceased father's
estate can well bear the expense, without
injury to the legitimate offspring, the
illegitimate child shall be maintained out of
that estate, and not at the expense of the
ratepayer.
I think that if the hon. Member will consider the facts that I have ventured to
bring before him, and not allow his mind
to range over an imaginary region, which
I feel is very unlikely, he will agree with
the Government that it is best that the Bill
should be allowed to remain as it is at the
present moment
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Marquess has
attacked somewhat severely my imagination, but as regards the accusation of
imagination of my own I must refer the
Committee to the quotation from Lord
Onslow which the noble Marquess read to
us. As regards the observations of Senator
Sharman Crawford
the subject of
Workmen's Compensation, I venture to remind the Committee that I mentioned the
question of Workmen's Compensation in
my speech on the Second reading. With respect to the question of the payment to the
mother of an illegitimate child being a civil
debt, I think I must remind Senator Greer
on
that according to the law in England, when
a putative father dies leaving arrears of
payment, those arrears cannot be recovered
from his estate. I am still left in doubt
as to how the money is to be raised out of
this deceased man's estate, and what steps
you are going to take to prevent the unfortunate dependants from being attacked.
I notice that the Leader of the House did
not deal with my point as to no appeal
being provided for. If I could be assured
that leave to appeal would be provided for,
I would ask leave to withdraw the amendment. If there is no right of appeal, I do
not think we ought to pass the Bill as it
stands. I need scarcely take up the time of
the Committee by drawing attention to the
great necessity which exists in all cases of
summary jurisdiction of having an appeal,
which would be to the County Court Judge
or Recorder. If we had such an appeal in
this case you would take away a great deal
of the difficulty because it might be an
assurance that purely flimsy cases would
not be brought. It seems to me that the
question of appeal is of the greatest possible
importance. I would ask for a little further
enlightenment on that subject before we go
on.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In answer to the hon. Gentleman's remark
in further support of his amendment I
think that he rather underrates the jurisdiction and breadth of view of the court who
are likely to deal with these matters. I do
feel that all these considerations which he
has put very forcibly forward will be subject matter for the court to consider, and I
cannot feel that the court sitting with all
the knowledge of the facts before it is likely
to err in the direction which he fears. He
has suggested that he would be prepared to
withdraw his amendment if he was satisfied that there was a right of appeal. Perhaps the hon. Gentleman will leave that over
until the Report stage when we can go further into that matter. I am not prepared
to say at this moment whether there is an
appeal or whether there is no appeal. Between now and the Report stage I shall be
able to get some enlightenment on that
matter. At the present moment it is
impossible to accept the amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I think I may take it that
if there is not a right of appeal it will be
given?
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I am unable to give any undertaking, but
I will look into it.

Mr. LESLIE: I quite understand.
would ask leave to withdraw my amendment dealing with the putative father.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
237 Illegitimate Children Bill-

21 OCTOBER 1924
Committee.
The following amendment stood on the
Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie Clause
4, page 4, line 11, to leave out from 'made'
to the end of line 16."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not move.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 4,
page 4, line 18, to leave out "not" ("date,
not earlier than the") and insert "within
twelve months before or after the date of
the order, but in no case."
The difficulty I want to meet here is the
chance of there being too large a sum made
payable to and placed at the disposal of the
woman without any means of insuring that
it will be properly disposed of. Also when
we come to consider the date that this Bill
will come into operation you may find it
convenient to insert the phrase used, “within twelve months before or after the date
of the order."
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I accept the amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 4,
page 4, line 37, to leave out "the man
adjudged to be" and insert "of." This is
purely a drafting amendment.
The words
were put in in another place, and is obvious
now that they do not quite conform to the
wording used elsewhere. It makes
difference to the sense.
no
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
accept it.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
4, Sub-clause (5) to leave out paragraph (a).
This is purely consequential on the standardisation of the age.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
It is consequential. I accept.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Subsection (5) to leave out paragraph (b). I
now approach, I am glad to say, the last
of the amendments that are likely to raise
anything even faintly resembling controversy. I want to leave out paragraph (b)
because on the whole the balance of advantage is found in that direction. I have to
admit at once that it is a matter on which
opinions are very likely to differ, and it is
difficult to form
really useful
opinion as to which side is correct. Now,
first of all, we have to consider, I think,
the question whether this right of altering
the amount of the sums payable or directing that the payment of these sums shall
cease or be continued exists in England.
I say quite definitely that this right does
very
a
not exist. It is appropriate to examine and
inquire into what does exist. First of all I
may remind the Committee of a reference
the noble Marquess made, when speaking on
the Second reading, to a section of the
miscellaneous criminal Justice Administration Act of 1914. He quoted to us a Subclause which, taken by itself, would seem
to show that this power of varying existed
in England. However, if that Sub-clause
be put back into its proper place and read
in conjunction with its context, it is perfectly
plain that the whole Section relates to
an
entirely different matter, that is,
varying the payee, not the amount. But
there is something similar at the moment in
England and it is this: The Committee will
remember that the Act of 1872 originally
fixed the maximum payment which might
be required to be made at 5s. per week. That
stood until 1918 when the Affiliation Order
(Increase of Maximum Payments) Act was
brought in and that Act provided that the
maximum amount as well as for past as
for future applications should be raised to
10s. and it further provided that in the case
of existing orders opportunity should be
given to those entitled
pay the
come before the court
money
to
to
and show cause why their payment
of 5s. should not be increased. That was
done of course on account of the rise in the
cost of living. In 1923 the 10s. was raised
to 20s. and the same powers as in the Act of
1918 were given. But the Committee will
remember that we are not starting at 5s. or
even 10s.; we are going the whole road and
leaving the maximum at 20s. There is no
power in England when once this maximum
has been raised, let us say, from 5s. to 10s.,
or from 10s. to 12s. to review the matter
again, except, of course, by appeal in the
ordinary way. A possible advantage claimed
for this power of varying is usually that if
the circumstances of the putative father improved very much he ought to pay more for
maintenance of the illegitimate child. I
think perhaps he ought. There is nothing
to stop him doing it if he desires, but if you
allow the amount of the payment to vary
according to the increase in the man's wealth
so must you also when it decreases. If for
instance a man's rates and taxes go up or if
his wife increases the family he must go in
a decrease. You have against that
possible advantage as it seems to me certain
and extremely well marked disadvantages.
And I propose to trouble the Committee for
a moment in demonstrating what these disadvantages are. First of all if you allow an
application to be made for increase or
variation you are bringing up again the old
trouble. You are once again reminding
the world of the shame of the woman and
the folly or sin of the man. You are also
for
239 Illegitimate Children Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
[Mr. Leslie]
stamping the child anew as bastard. You
are doing under this sub-section exactly what
my hon. Friend objects to doing under
Clause 2 when we cut certain words out of
the definition of mother. You are losing all
sense of finality. You are imposing a continuance of applications going from court to
court as residence changes. I do not quite
know how the words as they stand will affect
the question of an application which has
been dealt with on appeal to vary the original order. The magistrate's court of summary jurisdiction would still have power to
interfere? I am not sure about that.
woman
Then there is also the terrible question
of blackmail. We have kept that out of
our discussions in this House hitherto, because we have always, 1 think, been satisfied that the provisions put into the Bill
were sufficient protection. Now look at the
terrible avenue to blackmail you would be
opening if you allow the order to be varied.
You may have a young
who has
fallen once and is about to be married. Up
comes her partner in the former transaction and wants to make some bargain with
her, otherwise he will bring her up before
the court. On the other side is a man also
who has not again sinned, or at all events
who has not been detected in doing it again.
He is about to embark upon some commercial undertaking, perhaps about to go into
the ministry and so forth. In this case an
evil woman or her advisers come to blackmail, because if he does not pay they will
come to the courts and bring the old story
up again.
I must confess that I do feel some diffidence in pressing this amendment on the
Committee because one cannot disregard the
fact that in these cases there are considerations on both sides, but to my own mind the
want of finality, the discouragement to
thrift, the risk of blackmail, and above all,
the re-branding of the child, lead me to
think that our Bill would be the better for
the omission of this paragraph. I beg to
move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The hon. Member desires to take away any
power of varying an Order-
Mr. LESLIE: No, of varying the amount.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
Of varying the amount, and on behalf of
the Government I am bound to oppose the
amendment which he is proposing now,
namely, the deletion of paragraph (b). He
will see in the Bill that the variation is
in more than one way—
Mr. LESLIE: Yes.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The variation can be made on the applicaa
tion of the mother or
Board of
Guardians or any person for the time being
having the custody of the child, and on
application to the collecting officer or the
man adjudged to be the putative father of
the child. Therefore, the Order may be
varied upwards or downwards. I think I
shall carry the Committee with me when I
say if we take one instance only if the
circumstances of the putative father increase in such a degree as to enable him to
make a larger contribution per week for
the maintenance of the child the court
should be able to order him to do so rather
than that the balance of the amount should
fall upon the rates. I think that is a consideration which ought to weigh with the
hon. Member. I am fully alive to the
suggestion he makes that there may be an
opening for possibilities of blackmail, that
it may be said if the putative father's
circumstances have been improved by a
small amount, he might be pressed to make
a larger contribution, and if his circumstances have not improved, he might have
the whole matter brought into court again.
I am not sure that I really attach very
much importance to that danger. Besides.
in the hon. Member's speech he talked
about finality, and rather suggested there
would be no finality. The finality comes at
the age of 15. I do not feel that the
Government can accept the Amendment,
because they believe that this principle of
variation should be incorporated in the
Bill. I am quite prepared to say that
between now and the Report stage, I will
look into the way the variation in the Bill
might be limited to those cases where there
is fresh evidence of changed circumstances
and fresh facts since the last Order was
made. If this sort of evidence can be produced, I might be able to put something
in the Bill on the Report stage, but on the
broad basis of the desire of the hon. Member to move to take away the power of
varying, I am bound on behalf of the
Government to oppose.
Mr. GREER: I understand that the noble
Marquess says he will be taking this Clause
into consideration before the Report stage,
and I would just like to impress upon him
the desirability, if possible, of arranging
that any application to alter the amount
should be heard in camera and not in open
court. There is no question of principle-
The CHAIRMAN: I cannot hear you at
all. Would you mind speaking up?
Mr. GREER: I was wishing to impress
upon the noble Marquess the desirability
of any application to vary an Order being
taken in camera.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY.
I will consider that.
241 Illegitimate Children Bill-

21 ОСТОВER 1924
Committee.
Mr. GREER: Thank vou
Mr. LESLIE: I am grateful to the noble
Marquess for his promise to look into this
matter. He will remember that I stated I
brought forward this particular amendment with a great deal of diffidence, owing
to various considerations which I knew were
involved. I have not observed that any
other Member of the Committee desired to
support me and, therefore, I would ask
leave to withdraw the Amendment.
In
doing so I would like to assure the noble
Marquess that I am not inclined to be
troublesome on the matter of the introduction of fresh evidence. If this paragraph
is to stand, I think we should probably be
satisfied to rely on the discretion of the
court, which is given by the word "may"
in the Clause. I do not want to put any
difficulty in the way of the working of the
Bill when you have decided that point.
think the course we are taking to-day is
very possibly correct, and I ask leave to
withdraw.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 4,
page 5, to leave out lines 7 to 10 inclusive
("provided that no order so made shall continue in force after the child has attained
the age of sixteen years, and that the sums
payable under any such order shall be weekly
sums not exceeding twenty shillings
week.")
a
I have once again to ask something from
the Committee, and I think that what I have
to ask is really of some importance. I have
two Amendments here. The first is to leave
out lines 7 to 10. The omission of these
words is purely consequential on the first
Amendment which the Committee accepted.
I move that Amendment separately and will
then go on to my next one.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
It is consequential..
Amendment agreed to
Mr. LESLIE: I now propose to insert:
"(b) By ordering the payment by the
putative father of the funeral expenses of
any child who may have died while any
Affiliation Order in respect of such child
was in force against such putative father:
provided that no amount so made payable
shall exceed five pounds."
As the Bill stands, the Committee will
remember, we only make the putative father
liable for the funeral expenses of his offspring if the child dies before the Affiliation
Order is made. That is really illogical because if the child dies after the Affiliation
Order is made somebody has to bury it and
it may very well be the Guardians. It is
not a very serious matter as we are only
if
dealing with £5, and the consideration
which really moves me in urging it on the
attention of the Committee is that it seems
to make for infant life protection
you put this Clause in, because if
you leave it out you have to contend
in this matter with ignorance as well as vice,
and perhaps ignorance is the more serious.
If we leave this out we leave it open to the
woman to postpone the application for payment in respect of her child until the child
dies in order that she may get the funeral
amount, whereas if we put this in, the
temptation is done away with. I think the
mere presence of these words in the Act will
produce the desired effect, which is really
that applications will have to be made to
the court and if such applications are made
it is not open to the objection I urged to
another paragraph, because you cannot rebrand a child or re-disgrace a child who is
dead. I earnestly hope the Committee will
accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Ordered, That the Clause, as amended,
stand part of the Bill
Ordered, That Clause 5 (commutation of
weekly sums), stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Provisions for cases of default and as to recovery of sums ordered).
*
(1) If default is made in the payment of
any sum payable by the putative father
of an illegitimate child under an Affiliation Order, or under any order varying
the
same, the mother or other person
entitled thereto may apply to the court
of summary jurisdiction by which the
order was made; and upon such application that court shall have and may exercise, for the purpose of enforcing the payment of any such sum, all the powers (including powers of imprisonment) exerciseable by a court of summary jurisdiction
in cases where default is made in the payment of a penal sum.
*
*
a
*
*
*
*
*
(4) A liability under an Affiliation Order
shall not be included among the liabilities
from which
bankrupt or arranging
debtor is released by a certificate of conformity granted by a court of bankruptcy,
except to such an extent and under such
conditions as the court expressly orders
in respect of such liability.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 6,
page 5, line 36, to leave out the first "the"
("apply to the court") and insert "a."
This Amendment appears simply to involve a change from the definite to the indefinite article. What I want to do if the
Government are prepared to accept the
243 Illegitimate Children Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
[Mr. Leslie]
Amendment, and I do not want to press
it if they are not is to give the power of
enforcement to any Court of Summary
Jurisdiction instead of restricting it, as it
is in the Bill, to the Court of Summary
Jurisdiction by which the Order was made.
There may be difficulties in the way, but on
the other hand it may help the woman to
get her money a little more quickly.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I cannot accept the hon. Member's Amendment. There really is no hardship. The
Province is not very large and the original
court will have all the records of the case.
Those records will be in the original court,
and I really feel it is best we should leave
the matter as it is.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 7,

I want, if I can, to make this Sub-clause

our Summary Jurisdiction Acts. It is
necessary to give the court a definite power
to confirm or quash an Order, otherwise
there is danger of one excluding the other.
As I say, I think all our Summary Jurisdiction Acts give the power to confirm or
quash as well as, of course, to vary. I need
do no more than simply move the Amendment
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This Amendment is of a drafting character.
It gives fuller powers of variation, and I
accept it.
Mr. GREER: I think that disposes of my
Amendment, which was to leave out this
line and give the Appeal Court the same
jurisdiction as the court of the first instance.
My way of putting it, I thought, was
short and would carry out the views of the
Committee and of the Government. I am
not very anxious, however, about it, and if
the Committee has already decided I will
withdraw it.

Lord accepted Mr. Leslie's Amendment, and

is, that those words be there inserted.

Collecting Officer in respect of Affiliation
Order).

(1) There shall be appointed from each
county borough and each petty sessions
district an officer of the court, who shall
carry out the duties of the collecting officer
under this section. The collecting officer
shall be-
(a) in a county borough, such person as
may be appointed by the justices with
the approval of the Ministry of Home
Affairs for Northern Ireland;
(b) in a place outside a county borough,
the petty sessions clerk.
(2) Where the court makes an Affiliation
Order it shall, unless upon representations
expressly made in that behalf by the
applicant for the order the court
satisfied that it is undesirable
to do, provide in the order
that all payments thereunder shall
be made to that officer and not otherwise.

Mr. GREER: I beg to move in Clause 8,
"
page 7, line 38. after clerk" ("the petty
sessions clerk") to insert "or such other
person as may be appointed by the justices
with the approval of the said Ministry."
The object of this Amendment is to provide that some person other than a petty
sessions clerk may be appointed a collecting
officer. There are certain cases-I know
some of them myself-where the petty
sessions clerk would not, as it happens, be
a suitable person to appoint as collecting
officer, and there may be a considerable
number of such cases over the whole area.
The Amendment merely gives power for the
appointment of some other person by the
justices, with the approval of the Ministry.
I think it would be quite safe.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move, in Clause 8,
page 8, line 5, at end ("and not otherwise ")
to add, "Provided that if the Court is satisfied that it is undesirable to appoint its
own collecting officer, it may appoint instead
the collecting officer appointed for any other
petty sessions district or place."
I look upon this Amendment as one of considerable importance. Proceedings are to be
taken at the place where the woman resides.
Proceedings may have to be taken here in
the courts in Belfast against a man who
may reside in Tyrone or Fermanagh. When
an order is obtained against the man, unless
my Amendment is inserted, the collecting
officer would be the collecting officer of the
court where the order is made, that is the
collecting officer of Belfast, and he would
have to collect the payments from the man
in Tyrone and Fermanagh. Of course it
would be entirely a matter of correspondence,
and it would be very difficult to get the
payments from the man. The object of my
Amendment is to give power to the Court
to make an order to appoint the collecting
officer of the place where the man resides,
if the Court should think, on hearing
evidence about it, that that would be more
desirable.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move, in Clause 8,
page 8, line 31, after the word "through"
("payment of money through") to insert
the words "a petty sessions clerk acting as."
This is to some extent a consequential
Amendment. I want to add these words, so
that the Clause will read "a petty sessions
clerk acting as the collecting officer." Under
the Clause as it stands in the Bill, it is
possible that some other person would collect,
and I think it should be only the person
who is actually the petty sessions clerk.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I am not able to accept this Amendment.
Even when the collecting officer is not a
petty sessions clerk he is entitled to some
remuneration. I am afraid I am unable to
accept the Amendment.
Mr. GREER: I do not press the Amendment, of course, if the Government thinks
it should not be inserted.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. GREER: The next Amendment is in
Clause 8, page 8, line 35, to leave out "a"

This is to some extent a drafting Amendment. I am afraid I will have to refer
to the next Amendment. With these two

such sum in respect of his remuneration
and expenses as may be fixed by those
rules.

on
The Government consider that it is
necessary that a definite date for the coming
into force of important legislation of this
nature should be laid down by statute. We
feel that it is very necessary that the public
should receive the earliest possible notice,
instead of the matter, being left vague and
undetermined in the statute, as would be
the case where an Order in Council would
be required for appointing a date for the
coming into operation of new legislation. I
find myself in agreement with my hon.
Friend (Mr. Leslie) in this matter.
Amendment agreed to.
Ordered, That Clause 12 as amended stand
part of the Bill.
