The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): The first amendment
which I desire to move is to Clause 1, page
2, line 39, after 'magistrate ("resident
magistrate sitting ") to insert appointed
under the Constabulary (Ireland) Act, 1836."
This amendment supplies a definition of the
term resident magistrate. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 1,
page 2, line 40, to leave out "section
("this section may ") and insert "Act.”
If, as I expect, the amendment
standing in my name will be accepted simply as a drafting amendment
it will not be necessary to detain the
House with any further observations on
the subject. I am quite satisfied myself
that it carries out what is in the mind of
the House.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This amendment simply provides that a
justice of the peace may issue a summons
issuable under any of the sections of the
Act, instead of under Section 1 alone, provided he has jurisdiction in the Petty
Sessions district where the summons is returnable. I am willing to accept the
amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 2 (Provisions as to Summons.)
The following amendment stood on the
Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Clause 2, page 3, line 31.- At end insert
the following new Sub-section :--
summons
"(5) When application for a
under this section is made before the
birth of the child, the justice of the
peace to whom such application is made
may issue a summons for the appearance
of the man alleged to be the father of
such child, returnable at some petty
session to be held on a day after the time
when the mother shall expect the child to
be born, provided that if on such day the
woman shall not have been delivered, or
the court shall be satisfied that she has
been delivered at so short a period before
such day that she cannot appear at the
said session, it shall be lawful for the
court to adjourn the hearing of the said
case until some other day, and so from
time to time, until the child shall have
been born and the woman shall be able
to attend at the said session."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move Clause 2, page 3, line 31, to
insert the following new Sub-section :-
"(5) Where the application for a summons
under this section is made before the birth
of the child, the justice of the peace issuing
the same shall make it returnable at some
petty sessions to be held upon a day after
the time when the child is expected to be
born, but the court of summary jurisdiction shall have power to adjourn the hearing
of the case from time to time to such later
day as may be required in order to secure
the attendance before the court of the
mother or any other necessary witness."
The amendment which I am proposing is
instead of the amendment put forward by
my hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie). I am proposing that a new sub-section should be inserted. This amendment provides a suffi-
275 Illegitimate Children Bill-

THE SENATE
Report Stage.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
ciently elastic procedure to meet every
contingency in a class of case where the
definite fixture of a date is naturally most
difficult. In view of this uncertainty, it will
be noted that this amendment extends to
every important witness in the case, as well
as to the mother, whereas the amendment in
Mr. Leslie's name did not apply to any witness beyond the mother.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the first thing to be
done is to congratulate the Government
upon having reduced a very lengthy amendment, which was based, as will be recognised,
strictly on the wording of the English Act of
1845. The Government has been able to
reduce it to more manageable proportions.
There are two remarks which I have to offer
on the proposals of the noble Marquess.
The first is a very small matter, we see in
the second line of the proposed section the
expression" issuing the same." I should
like to that altered to "issuing the
summons." I think it will be recognised
that that will read better and carry out the
intention.
see
I
The second point is more important.
want to be quite clear that the Government
is satisfied that the words as they are now
before us provide for the adjournment until
the court is satisfied that the child has been
born. What we want to aim at in this new
Clause is, as the noble Marquess has indicated, that when the case does come on
everything necessary to the hearing and disposing of it shall be there. The first thing
necessary is that the child shall be born.
I confess I am not quite satisfied that that
is being carried out in the wording of the
section. The House will remember that we
cannot rely on the use of the word mother
as implying that there is a child, because
the expression "mother" has already been
defined in the early part of the Bill as including a single woman with child. I should,
therefore, like to be quite sure that the
Government is advised that they have put in
all that is required. If anything more is
necessary I would suggest that it might be
done by adding in the new sub-clause the
words, "or in order that the court may be
satisfied that the child has been born alive."
The question is whether that is really necessary and if so whether we should do it now
or perhaps postpone it until the Third
reading or leave it until later on.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
What is the proposal?
Mr. LESLIE: Instead of "issuing the
same," "issuing the summons."
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon. Member suggests that the words
should be inserted as a second line-" justice
of the peace issuing the summons shall make
it returnable to some petty session." The
Government have no objection to moving an
amendment on these lines. I should be prepared to alter the section to read accordingly,
but I think the correct procedure would be
to insert the section and amend it afterwards, but I am open to correction from
you, my Lord, as to the procedure.
The SPEAKER: I think the better course
would be to amend it later.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
As regards the other portion of my hon.
Friend's remarks, I am advised that there is
no danger and that the section covers all
the difficulties which he has enumerated. 1
feel that he can be quite satisfied that the
wording of the section covers those difficulties which he thinks that he foresees at the
present moment. I will, therefore, put an
amendment in on the Third Reading.
Amendment (to insert new
agreed to.
Sub-clause)
CLAUSE 3 (agreement as to payment of
weekly lump sum).
Where an agreement is entered into between the mother of an illegitimate child
and the man alleged by her to be the father
of that child, for the payment by that man
of a weekly sum of money, or of a lump sum,
for the maintenance and education of the
child, and in discharge of any liability under
this Act in respect thereof, such an agree
ment shall be a bar to proceedings to obtain
an Affiliation Order under this Act in respect of that child, or in case such agreement is entered into after a summons has
been issued under this Act, but before the
hearing of the case, a bar to further proceedings for that purpose, if a resident
magistrate is satisfied on an ex parte application by either party (which may be heard
and determined otherwise than in open
court):-
(a) that the agreement will afford to the
child benefits at least equal to those which
might be expected to have been obtained
by the making of an Affiliation Order or
the commutation of the weekly sums
payable under an Affiliation Order; and
*
*
*
*

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I accept.
Amendment agreed to.

Mr. GREER: I beg to move in Clause 3,
page 3, line 37, to leave out "an ("such
an agreement"). This is also the same kind

of amendment. I think it will probably be

CLAUSE 4 (Provisions as to duration,
effect and variation of Affiliation Orders).
(1) A liability under an Affiliation Order
shall not be determined by the death of the
mother or of the putative father of the child
in respect of whom the order has been made.
In the case of the death of the putative
father the court may make an order for the
payment out of his estate of such lump sum
as the court thinks just, but in no case exceeding the aggregate of the sums for the
payment of which the putative father would,
if he had lived, have been liable under the
Affiliation Order.
*
*
*
*
**
*
*
*
(4) A court of summary jurisdiction may,
on the application of the mother or a board
of guardians or any person for the time
being having the custody of the child, or
upon the application of the collecting officer
or of the putative father of the child, make
an order varying an Affiliation Order-

*
**
*
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move in Clause 4, page 4, line 24,
after "made" ("has been made. In the") to
insert "In the case of the death of the
mother after the making of an Affiliation
Order proceedings for the enforcement of the
Order or for the commutation of weekly
or
may be taken
continued by the
person having the custody of the child, or,
if the child is in receipt of relief from the
rates of any poor law union, by the
board of guardians of that union."
sums
poor
This amendment becomes necessary in
view of the amendment which was passed
on the Committee Stage of the Bill which
removed Sub-section (2) of Clause 3. On that
occasion I think my hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie)
will remember that I said that the Government accepted his amendment provisionally,
and reserved the right to restore or reintroduce in another form the sub-section
removed by his amendment, when the Bill
came for consideration upon Report.
This amendment provides for proceedings
for the enforcement of an affiliation order or
for the commutation of weekly sums under
such order being taken or continued by the
guardian of the child, in the event of the
death of the mother. I beg to move.
are
Mr. LESLIE: I quite agree that the
introduction of some words of this nature is
desirable, if not absolutely necessary, but I
am in a difficulty over the reference to
commutation of weekly sums. I quite agree
if the mother has died we must provide for
somebody acting on behalf of the child coming
to the court to obtain the enforcement of
any payments that may be due. But when
we go on to say "or for the commutation of
weekly sums" the commutation of weekly
sums, as I understand it, can only be done
after the Affiliation Order is made. The
commutation can apparently only be done
under Section 5 of the Bill, when it is done
on the application of the putative father, and
if we leave those words in to-day we
giving those representing, so to speak, the
deceased mother, a power which she herself cannot have. I cannot see any necessity
for inserting the words, "or for the commutation of weekly sums," and I am afraid that
it is not desirable that we should insert
them. It is not necessary to give a power
to the successors of the woman in the case
of the variation of an Order because the
wording of the Clause dealing with the
variation of an Order is sufficient to provide
for the case. I can imagine that the intention
might be to allow proceedings for the continuation of the proceedings, but I do not
think it is altogether well expressed. Therefore, while supporting the amendment as a
whole, I suggest that the words, "or for the
commutation of weekly sums" be omitted.
I think it would be better to do that also
on the Third Reading of the Bill.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I hardly think the hon. Member is right in
his contention, because if the mother is dead
there will be representatives of the mother
for the purpose of dealing with the case.
Consequently, it is necessary that these
words, "or for the commutation of weekly
sums" should be in the Bill. I do not feel
that there is any substance in the contention
of the hon. Member. This Clause has been
very carefully considered, and it has satisfied
the advisers of the Government that it
carries out the intention of the Bill. Therefore, I am not prepared to alter it.
Amendment agreed to.

CLAUSE 4 (Provisions as to duration,
effect and variation of Affiliation Orders) (1)
A liability under an Affiliation Order shall
not be determined by the death of the
mother or of the putative father of the
child in respect of whom the order has been
made. In the case of the death of the
putative father the court may make an order
for the payment out of his estate of such
lump sum as the court thinks just, but in
no case exceeding the aggregate of the sums
for the payment of which the putative father
would, if he had lived, have been liable
under the Affiliation Order.

(b) if the child is in receipt of relief
from the poor rates, such board of guardians
or relieving officer as may be designated by
the court.
(4) A court of summary jurisdiction may,
on the application of the mother or a board
of guardians or any person for the time
being having the custody of the child, or
upon the application of the collecting officer,
or of the putative father of the child, make
an order varying an Affiliation Order-

ak

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move on Clause 4, page 4, line 25,
after the word "father" ("father the court")
to insert the words,
"after the making of the Affiliation
Order, his personal representative may be
made a party to any proceedings for the
enforcement of the Order, and"
This amendment is necessary in order to
secure the continuity of an Affiliation Order
after the death of the father. This provision
was originally in the Sub-section of Clause
3 which was deleted when the Bill was in
Committee, as I have already said.
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move, in Clause 4, page 4, line 29,
at the end of Section 1, to add,
"Such lump sum shall be paid to the
collecting officer or to such other person or
body of persons as may be designated by
the court and shall be invested and applied
for the maintenance and education of the
child in such manner as the court may
direct."
This amendment secures the proper appropriation of the lump sum for the benefit of
the child. This safeguard is identical with
that which will be found in Sub-section (1)
of Clause 5, which deals with the lump sum
awarded by way of commutation.
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I beg to move in Clause 4, page 5, to leave
out from the word "may" in line 7 to the
word "make" in line 11, and to insert instead thereof the words :-
"upon cause being shown upon fresh
evidence to the satisfaction of the court."
It will be remembered that the hon.
Senator (Senator Leslie) in Committee on the
Bill sought to introduce an amendment
abolishing the power of the court to vary
the amount payable weekly under an Affiliation Order. On behalf of the Government
I then said that while I could not accept
the hon. Member's amendment, the Government would consider the advisability of
introducing an amendment upon Report,
which would limit the power of variation by
making its exercise dependent upon the
production of some material fresh evidence.
I think this amendment satisfies that point.
Mr. LESLIE: I must thank the Government very sincerely for introducing this
amendment, which I admit goes very far to
remove the objections which I had to the
paragraph referred to by the noble Marquess. Certainly the introduction of these
words will have the effect of assimilating
our legislation on this point in cases where
the power of variation has been allowed.
Such a case is the Maintenance of Deserted
Wives, which is very much on the same
lines. I feel quite satisfied that the introduction of these words will act as a great
protection against many flimsy and undesirable cases, and that it will go very far to
remove any possible objection to the paragrapt which can only have been passed on
speculative grounds with no evidence in
support of it. I am cordially in favour of
the amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
NEW CLAUSE (Provision as to unexpended portion of lump sum).
If an illegitimate child in respect of
whom any lump sum has been paid under
the provisions of sections three, four, or
five of this Act, shall die before the lump
sum paid in respect of him has been fully
expended on his behalf, the unexpended
balance of such lump sum shall be paid
to the putative father of such child.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move that this new
Clause be inserted after Clause 5. The
object of the new clause, as I am sure will
be recognised, is to prevent any possibility
of a lump sum made payable in respect of
an illegitimate child falling into possession
of the mother of that child and so providing
a possible incentive to causing the death of
the child. We all know, unfortunately,
that illegitimate children die at the rate of
Illegitimate Children Bill-- 3 NOVEMBER 1924

Report Stage.
vision has been made in the Bill for plurality
of births, including twins and triplets. The
lump sum would be affected by the number
of children born, but as far as I can see the
Bill deals with a child in the singular. In
the event of a lump sum being payable one
child might die and another survive. Is it
not necessary to provide in the Bill for
plurality of births?
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I hardly think the hon. Member (Colonel
Sinclair) need have any fears on that point.
Plurality of births would follow on the
same lines as two illegitimate children. In
calculating the amount of money to be paid
it would be governed by that consideration.
The hon. Member (Mr. Leslie) has moved an
amendment for the purpose of returning the
unexpended balance of a lump sum to the
putative father. That is the substance of
the amendment he has moved. At the
beginning of his remarks he referred to the
danger of children being done away with
for the benefits which might accrue from
their death. That point does not actually
arise in this amendment, but I will venture
to deal with it as the hon. Member has put
it. This amendment is an attempt to provide for the disposal of the unexpended
portion of a lump sum in the event of the
death of the child.
about two to every one legitimate child, and
it is most desirable to prevent any possibility
of holding out any inducement to commit
what is practically murder. The whole
question of the lump sum has given me a
good deal of trouble in considering this Bill.
The principle of a lump sum is entirely unknown to the English law of Bastardy.
Lump sums have always been paid in respect
of illegitimate children, but it has always
been in consequence of an agreement made
outside the Court. The Courts would, of
course, enforce an agreement as an agreement, but as far as an illegitimate child is
concerned a lump sum has never been recognised. We are proposing now to admit a
lump sum under three conditions. The first
is that we introduce a new Clause which, I
am sure, with the safeguards provided, will
be found of use. Then again we have in
Clause 4 given a new power altogether to the
resident magistrate to take so much out of a
deceased man's estate and provide a lump
sum for the maintenance and education of
the child. Under Clause 5 we have provided
for the commutation of the weekly sums
payable under the Affiliation Order on the
application of the putative father, but in no
case have we said what is to happen to the
lump sum if the child should die before the
lump sum is entirely used up. In the first
case I know that what was in my hon.
Friend's mind was that the money should be
expended in purchasing a weekly payment
from an Insurance Society, in which case
no difficulty would arise on the death of the
child. In the second case we are taking
money which would otherwise go to the
man's heirs, if he had any, and holding
them up temporarily so that the illegitimate
child may be provided for if it is alive, but
if it does not survive no provision is made
for the allocation of what remains of the
lump sum. I do not see any reason why the
legitimate heirs of the putative father, having
already been fined for the protection of the
illegitimate child, should continue to be
fined after the child is dead. That money
ought surely to go back to the estate of the
putative father, and under Section 5 we
ought to define by statute that the lump sum
shall return to the man who paid it.
proposition is to prevent anybody having
any inducement to do away with the child,
and I may perhaps digress from that point
to say that I accept the explanation which
the noble Marquess gave us on the
Clause dealt with a minute ago that
no difficulty will arise in that connection.
But having regard, as I have said, to the
paramount necessity of infant life protection
I beg to move the amendment standing in
my name.
My
Colonel SINCLAIR: I should like to ask
the Leader of the House whether any pro-
I am not prepared on behalf of the
Government to accept this amendment, and
I would venture to urge against its acceptance, first, that in all probability the lump
sum will have been invested by order of the
Court under some insurance scheme whereby the periodical sums are calculated and
fixed at a figure which includes the risk of
the child dying before the lump sum runs
out.
In the second place, if the child survives
for nine or ten years after the father's
death, does the hon. Member suggest that
the administration of the father's estate
should be re-opened after such an interval?
But the main foundation of the argument
I would venture to put forward for not
accepting this amendment is that the child
being illegitimate, and the hon. Member
referred to this point himself, in the event
of its death, its property passes to the
Crown, and it is obvious the Crown would
then make a fair disposal of it, after a very
full consideration of all claims which must
necessarily be brought before them. I feel,
therefore in the case the hon. Member has
in his mind a sum of money, substantial or
otherwise, would be most meticulously dealt
with by the Crown, and that either the
putative father or the representatives of
his estate would be the first to bring their
before the Crown in the hope of
recovering such sum of money as they deemed
case

they would be entitled to. I hope I have

Mr. LESLIE: Remembering that the

CLAUSE 7 (Appeals to Court of Quarter
Sessions and Recorder).

this section. The collecting officer shall be-
(1) An appeal shall lie to the court of
quarter sessions, against an Affiliation
Order, or an Order varying the same, and also
against the refusal by a court of summary
jurisdiction to make an Affiliation Order, in

索
*
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move in Clause 7, page 7, line 8, to
leave out from against" ("sessions against
an ") to "in" in line 10 (" Affiliation Order
in like ") and insert any order (other than
an order for the adjournment of a hearing)
made by a court of summary jurisdiction
under this Act, and also against any refusal
by such court to make such an order.”
My hon. Friend (Senator Leslie) on the
Committee Stage called the attention of the
Government to the fact that no provision
was made for an appeal against the decision of the court which made an award of
a lump sum to an illegitimate child out of
the estate of the deceased father, or refused
to make such award.
I promised to look into this matter, and
the present amendment will remove all
doubt upon this head, making as it does
provision for an appeal against any and
every order of a court of summary jurisdiction under this Act, excepting an order
for the adjournment of a hearing, to the
court of quarter sessions. I think that this
amendment gives the appeal which the hon.
Senator thought would be advisable to have
in the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: Again I have nothing to do
but to thank the noble Marquess for inserting these words which will bring our
law into absolute accordance with the law
in England in this matter.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 8 (Appointment and duties of
collecting officer in respect of Affiliation
Order).
(1) There shall be appointed for each
county borough and each petty sessions district an officer of the court who shall carry
out the duties of the collecting officer under
(a) in a county borough, such person as
may be appointed by the justices with the
approval of the Ministry of Home Affairs
for Northern Ireland;
(b) in a place outside a county borough,
the petty sessions clerk or such other person as may be appointed by the justices
with the approval of the said Ministry.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move to leave out
paragraph (a) and (b) and insert "such
person as may be appointed by the Ministry
of Home Affairs for Northern Ireland."
My object in putting down this amendment
was to provide for the appointment of a
collecting officer who would be an important
officer of the court, being made not by the
justices but by the Ministry of Home Affairs.
It will be remembered by hon. Members
that the jurisdiction of dealing with these
cases is now taken from the ordinary justices
and transferred to the resident magistrate,
and I do not see why in these circumstances
the justices should have power to appoint
an important officer to deal with these cases.
I presume that there may possibly be some
difficulty in getting them to make any
appointment, I can conceive that the ordinary justices may think they have been overlooked in this Bill, and that they will not
lend themselves to any procedure that would
make the Bill work, and I do not see how
that difficulty can be overcome, unless some
words are inserted in the Clause. I think
it would be better to leave the justices out
altogether and let the appointment be made
by the Ministry of Home Affairs. But I
will not press the point, and if the Government consider that it is better that the Bill
should stand as it is I am prepared to
withdraw the amendment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I regret I am unable to accept the amendment which has been moved by my hon.
Friend. I think the House will recognise
that it has been the aim of the Government
throughout to make the petty sessions clerk,
who is elected by the justices of the peace in
the first instance, the collecting officer in
country districts. The Government accepted
in Committee an amendment from my hon.
Friend by which a departure was made from
our original intention, inasmuch as it provided that the justices of the peace might
appoint some other person than the petty
sessions clerk, with the approval of the
Ministry of Home Affairs. That is as far
as the Government can go in this direction.
To give the appointment of the collecting
officer to the Ministry of Home Affairs,
which this amendment is designed to carry
out would be to take away one of the few
rights of the ordinary bench of justices of
285 Illegitimate Children Bill— 3 NOVEMBER 1924
Report Stage.

Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time.-(The Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
the peace under the Bill, and I feel that in
view of the great and overwhelming vote
against Socialism that we have recently
seen-(Hon. Members: Hear, hear)-this
amendment would undoubtedly be felt and
resented as an unwarrantable intrusion of
the bureaucracy of the Government. I
would, therefore, suggest to the hon. Member
that we should leave this appointment to
the discretion and power of the magistrates.
Mr. GREER: I have to thank the Noble
Marquess for the explanation he has given
me of the reason why he cannot accept my
amendment. I am quite satisfied with it,
and ask the leave of the House to withdraw
the amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Mr. GREER: I beg to move in Clause 9,
page 10, line 22, after "costs" ("reasonable
costs of either") to insert "and expenses."
This amendment is to add the words "and
expenses" where it provides for the reasonable costs being given either of the parties.
It will be remembered that in this Act we
provide that the defendant is to be brought
from where he happens to reside to the
court where the mother of this child
resides, which may be a long distance away.
The class of persons who have these illegitimate children do shift about from place to
place, and therefore a woman may have an
illegitimate child here in Belfast or County
Antrim, whereas the putative father lives
in Donegal or Fermanagh or some distant
county. Considerable expense will be incurred by the defendant, and if the complainant should fail in making her case, I
think that the expenses coming that long
distance should be provided for.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am willing to accept the amendment of my
hon. Friend. In fact it certainly is the
Government's intention that the clause
should cover travelling expenses and other
such charges, as well as the costs of the
court. This amendment will explicitly
ensure this, as there may be some doubt
whether costs in County Court practice
would cover expenses.
Amendment agreed to.
"
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I have two amendments to move. The first
one is in Clause 2, in the new Sub-section

line to leave out "same ("issuing the
same shall") and insert "summons." This
amendment is in accordance with what my
hon. Friend (Senator Leslie) moved and I

I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.

Mr. LESLIE: May I have the reference?

(3) In every premium receipt book issued
after the commencement of this Act there
shall be printed a notice stating that in the
event of the forfeiture, after the expiration
of five years from the passing of this Act,
of any policy of industrial assurance by
reason of default in the payment of premiums
thereunder, the owner of the policy shall,

THE SENATE
period as provided by this section, be

to the surrender value of his policy, and

(5) This Section shall not apply in the case
of a forfeiture occurring before the expiration of five years after passing of this Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I have two small amendments which merely require formal moving.
The first one is, in Clause 24, page 23, line
30, to leave out the words "expiration of five
years from the passing of this Act," and to
insert instead thereof, the words "seventh
day of June, nineteen hundred and twentyeight." The object is to get rid of the
period of five years mentioned in the Bill,
and to substitute for that period the definite
date, seventh day of June, nineten hundred
and twenty-eight. It will have the effect of
bringing our Bill into accord with the
practice under the English Act, which has
been an Act for some eighteenth months.
They put in a period of five years simply to
allow a breathing space, and it seems desirable that we should bring our Bill into
operation on the same date as the English
Act will be in operation. Therefore, instead
of deferring action for the full five years we
shall defer it until the seventh day of June,
nineteen hundred and twenty-eight. I beg
to move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I accept this amendment on behalf of the
Government. The amendments will advance
the date at which holders of policies of industrial assurance which may be forfeited
will be entitled to receive a paid-up policy,
and they will bring the date into line with
the date under the Act passed by the Imperial
Parliament at Westminster, under which
policy holders will be able to claim the same
benefit of a free paid-up policy. The Imperial Act received the Royal Assent on 7th
June, 1923.
Amendment agreed to.

Assurance Companies Bill.
Order for the Third Reading read.

And a message was sent to the House of

Deceased Brother's Widow's
Marriage Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This Bill, as its title shows, has been drawn
to remove doubts as to whether the Act of
the British Parliament of 1921, which made
lawful marriage with the widow of a dead
brother, applies to Northern Ireland.
Clause 1 achieves this by specifically
applying that Act to Northern Ireland from
the date of its passage in 1921. The proviso
to this clause provides relief for persons
who, having contracted a marriage of this
nature, have had it annulled, or after such
annulment have contracted a further
marriage. This proviso is practically identical with the proviso to Clause 1 of the
Deceased Wife's Sister's Marriage Act of
1907, the provisions of which were extended
by the Deceased Brother's Widow's Marriage
Act of 1921, to include marriage between
persons of the degree of affinity with whom
the Bill now before the House is concerned.
It is necessary in the present measure, in
order to protect those individuals who had
contracted such marriages in the interval
that has elapsed between the passing of the
Act of the Imperial Parliament of 1921, and
the passing of the present Bill, inasmuch as
that interval is, of course, not covered by the
Act of the Imperial Parliament of 1921
which it is the purpose of the present Bill
to apply to Northern Ireland.
It may be asked how such marriages have
occurred in Northern Ireland if there was
289 Brother's Widow's Marriage Bill-

3 NOVEMBER 1924 Second Reading.
doubt as to their validity. I may say at
once that, so far as the general public is
concerned, there could have been no doubt
inasmuch as the Registrar-General for Ireland in August, 1921, issued a notice to all
Registrars in Ireland calling attention to
the passage of the Deceased Brother's
Widow's Marriage Act, and indicating that
such marriages were henceforth lawful in
Ireland. Such marriages, therefore, were
bona-fide marriages. However, it is very
doubtful whether this Act of the Imperial
Parliament did in fact and in law so apply
to Northern Ireland, and, as I have said
before, the effect of the passage of the
present Bill will be to remove all doubts as
to the validity of marriages of this nature
contracted since the passage of the Act of
the Imperial Parliament to which I have
referred.
I beg to move the Second Reading.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure the whole House
will be in sympathy with the proposals outlined by the noble Marquess, and certainly
I am myself only too ready to affirm the
general principles of the Bill. But I have
this difficulty. We are providing with care
for the man who has contracted a real
marriage since, and we are providing for
the man or woman who has gone through
the form of getting such a marriage
annulled, but we have left out the man
who, having gone through such a marriage
as indicated, has taken no steps whatever
to annul the marriage, nor has he married
anybody else. If we pass this Bill at once
we shall possibly be doing a very grave injustice to a man who has gone through a
form of marriage with his deceased
brother's widow, a marriage which was not
only voidable, but since 1835 was absolutely void for affinity. A man who has
gone through a marriage of that nature is
under no responsibility whatever to take
any steps to annul the marriage because
it is not there. You cannot annul it. The
only thing the man could do would be to go
to the appropriate Court and give evidence
of the facts, and the Court might enquire
whther the lady was in fact his deceased
brother's widow, and if they were satisfied
that she was they could say, "Very well, you
cannot marry your deceased brother's
widow. Your marriage is not a marriage,"
and leave it at that. This particular
Measure was introduced in another place on
8th October. If we pass it as it is it will
become law in November, and the people will
have had no notice of the new requirements
of annulling such a marriage if they want
to, or if they are proposing to get married
to somebody else they must expedite
matters and get it through before 8th
November. They have not got time. A man
may have gone through a marriage of that
nature, and may not wish to annul it from
all sorts of motives. It is quite possible
that his motives may be of the highest or
they may be of the lowest, but we are not
concerned with motives. We are concerned to avoid working a possible
injustice and we can avoid it if we give
reasonable notice of our intentions.
It is quite open to Parliament to introduce
a reform of this nature because it is a reform, but if we give notice that we intend to
pass this Bill that is all we are called upon
to do. I do think we are bound to protect
the possible interests I have indicated, and
there are two methods by which we may do
So. It might be possible for the Government
to introduce an amendment providing for
a suspensory Clause, or it might be better to
give the Bill a Second Reading in order to
affirm the principle, and then the Bill could
be dropped and brought in again next
Session. That would be sufficient notice to
the general public that it was the intention
of Parliament to bring this measure into
operation as speedily as possible, and that
if anybody desired to annul such a marriage
they must take steps to do so, and if anybody desired to contract a marriage they
must do so before the Bill becomes law. I
venture to urge this on the attention of the
Government, because I am afraid we have
no source of information to tell us how many
of these cases there actually are. I do hope
the matter will receive consideration and
that the Bill will not be hurried. It is not
a case of hurrying the Bill through this
House but it is a case of hurrying the Bill on
to the Six Counties without giving those
concerned sufficient time to regularise their
position.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The hon. Member has certainly made a consistent Second Reading speech. He would
like the Bill to have a Second Reading and
then be postponed. The point he has raised
is one which could have been raised equally
well on the Third Reading of the Bill. The
matter he has raised is one on which I regret to say I do not feel myself in sympathy
with him at all. I am not quite sure as to
whom he desires to protect by postponing the
Bill until some future date, by which time,
he says, sufficient notice will have been
given.
After all, I should have thought that the
passing of the Bill in 1921 was sufficient
notice to anyone concerned, as it was of
the highest probability that such legislation
passed in Great Britain would of necessity
оссиру શ page in the Statute Book of
Northern Ireland when that Government
came to legislate on their own behalf. I
should have thought that anybody would
have taken that as sufficient notice as to
K
Finance Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
what the intentions of the Northern
Government were likely to be, and the effect
of postponing this Bill would really make
no difference to any parties concerned.
The hon. Member says he does not know
to how many people this Act would apply,
and I find myself in exactly the same
position as the hon. Member. But this
point has certainly not been put before the
Government before as far as I am aware.
There are succeeding stages of this Bill,
and I can assure him that I will go more
deeply into the matter. It is only right
that I should do so being in charge of the
Bill in this House, and I can assure him if
I find some reason to believe that there is
real substance in the arguments he has
put forward, I will take whatever steps
may be possible, or suggest to the Government that the Bill should be postponed. With
the knowledge I have in my possession I
can hardly feel that there is justification
for the postponing of this measure.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read a Second time accordingly, and
committed to a Committee of the Whole
House for to-morrow.
Finance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RAD: I do not intend to detain the House
any length of time over a Bill of this nature
which, in its essence, is a Money Bill, a
Bill on which this House, as hon. Members
know, has very limited powers.
I will,
therefore, confine myself to a brief statement on this measure.
This Bill deals entirely with those taxes,
the powers as to which have been transferred to the Government of Northern
Ireland. It is divided into three parts.
The first part deals with the entertainments
duty and certain licence duties. It provides
a small relief in the entertainments tax.
At present when an individual takes a seat
at a low price and exchanges his place for
one at a higher price he has to pay on the
lower and then on the higher price.
will now, under this Bill, pay the tax leviable on the aggregate amount he has spent
on the entertainment. This means a small
reduction.
He
Also relief for shows organised by agricultural and other societies which have an
educational value is made more readily
obtainable by the re-drafting of part of
Section 1 of the Finance Act of last year
which deals with entertainments of this
kind. That is to say, Clause 2, section (2),
is really a re-drafting of the clause of last
year.
As regards motor duties, a certain measure
of relief is also provided. A surrender value
is now attached to motor licences, while the
surcharge on licences taken out for a
smaller period than a year is reduced.
Hon. Members will, I think, be glad to
note that a reciprocal arrangement has
been made with the British Government,
whereby gun licences taken out in Northern
Ireland are valid in Great Britain and vice
versa.
Now I may for a moment turn to Part II;
it deals entirely with stamp duties. Section
7 relieves from stamp duty, wages, salaries,
superannuation, and other allowances, and
provides relief especially to those persons who
are in receipt of weekly wages. It is felt unjust that those whose wages are payable
weekly should have to pay stamp duty each
week, whereas others who, being in receipt
of larger salaries, are paid by the month or
the quarter only pay the same tax once for
the longer period for which their wages or
salaries are payable.
As regards Clause 8, I think I ought to
inform the House that the reference to the
Turkish Treaty is necessitated in order to
enable the Imperial Government to fulfil, so
far as Northern Ireland is concerned, an
undertaking given whereby the reapportionment of the Ottoman public debt may be
carried out without involving Bond-holders
in charges for extra duties on new Bonds
created as a result of the Treaty.
With regard to Part III-Miscellaneous
and General Provisions-I may particularly
mention a concession which is being made
to sailors, soldiers and airmen in His
Majesty's Forces. Exemption from Death
Duty in certain cases has been granted by
the British Government, and the Government
of Northern Ireland welcomes this concession and gladly extends it to cover property
in Northern Ireland. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now committed.
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Bill to be read the Third time to-morrow.

That they have passed the Unemployment
Insurance No. 2 Bill, to which they desire
the concurrence of this House.

Bill.

Read the First time accordingly, to be

Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that this House do now
adjourn. Perhaps the House will allow me
to say that it is suggested that on Wednesday this House should meet at half-past
four. I am not aware whether this is quite
in accordance with the wishes of hon.
Members. If it is not in accordance with
their wishes I hope they will make such
representations as they think fit.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty-five Minutes after
Four of the Clock
Game Licences-
Game Licences.
THE SENATE.
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 4th November, 1924.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair
Issue of Game Licences.
Colonel MAXWELL: I would like to ask
the noble Marquess, the Leader of the House,
how many game licences have been taken
out in Belfast since the 1st August, 1921.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The right hon. Gentleman's question is somewhat vague, as he does not specify the
particular type of game licence to which he
refers. There are licences to deal in game.
There are gamekeepers' licences. There are
certificates to kill game. These last may be
obtained for one year, for nine months, or
for three months, as desirable.
In all, since August 1st, in Belfast alone,
127 game
licences have been taken out-
Licences to deal in game, 28; Gamekeepers'
licences, 3; and Certificates to kill game, 96.
Colonel MAXWELL: By leave of the
House I should like to thank the noble
Marquess for the information he has given
us. Of course I need scarcely say that the
game licences that I referred to were the
certificates to kill game, and I do not think
that 96 out of a population of nearly 400,000
is excessive. I cannot help feeling that
there are a considerable number of peopleand it is worth the while of the Government
inquiring if there are not a considerable
number of people in Northern Ireland killing
game without the necessary game licences. I
know the difficulties of finding out who are
carrying on the practice, which is a fraud
really on the taxpayer, and also perhaps a
greater fraud on those who, perhaps for a
few days' shooting, carry out the law and
take out a game licence. A friend of mine
drew my attention to this matter, because
he took out a licence in the end of December
last year, a £2 licence, which lasted for two
months, I think, and he found that very
few people had taken out a licence. He
thought that perhaps this was just a chance
thing that happened in the particular post
office where he went. But he found when
he took out his licence on the 27th August
this year, at that particular post office, that
very few people had taken out a licence
before him, and he is a man who only gets
a few days' shooting. Naturally he felt
rather aggrieved; and he had a feeling
which, I think, all of us have that a good
many people were shooting without a
licence.
I would like if the Government could see
their way to make inquiries as to the powers
of the police. I believe the police in Northern
Ireland do not have the power to search a
man or to ask a man for his game licence.
Undoubtedly they have the power in the
case of a motor driver's licence. I do not
see why they should not exercise the same
power in the case of a man with a game
licence. I would also suggest that if a man
has a gun and game on him, or in his
pocket, he should have a game licence. I do
not know if that is the law, but if it is not
I would suggest that that ought to be the
law, because otherwise it is almost impossible
for the police or the excise to catch a man
actually in the act of shooting game bird.
I drew the attention of the House to this
matter because I think it really is a matter
that affects the ordinary taxpayer, and also
is particularly hard on the man who gets
a few days' shooting and goes to the expense
of taking out a licence.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY.
I am sure the Government will be grateful
to the right hon. Gentleman for raising this
matter. It is a matter of considerable importance and as he says it is a fraud on the
taxpayer if the contribution to the Treasury
is not made by those who should necessarily
take out a game licence But the numbers
which I read out are only for Belfast. One
would imagine, as this affects the rural
population more than it does the urban
population, that many licences are taken out
throughout the Six Counties in those
localities for which authority is required.
The further question which he puts to me
as to whether the police are entitled to ask
for a game licence in the same way as they
now ask for a motor licence is one into
which I will make inquiry. I am not prepared to say off-hand whether he is right or
whether he is wrong. My own impression is
that the law is entitled to ask any man for
the production of his game licence, but as
I say, I am not giving any instructed view
at the present moment. I will be very glad
to make inquiry. I will also go further into
the matter and will see what the Government can do in the direction of ensuring
that people who are killing game should
take out the necessary licence to give them
the right to do so. I am much obliged to
the right hon. and gallant Gentleman for
raising the question.
Colonel MAXWELL: I wish to thank the
noble Marquess.
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
297 Brother's Widow's Marriage Bill -- 4 NOVEMBER 1924

Committee.
Moved: That the House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.
(The Marquess of Londonderry.)

Deceased Brother's Widow's

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I did not quite anticipate the procedure
which you would adopt on this question. I
wish at present to answer a question which
my hon. Friend put to me yesterday which
dealt with whether this Bill should go further
than the Second Reading. I will take your
ruling as to what would be the best time
to make the statement which the hon.
Gentleman requires.
The CHAIRMAN: I do not quite understand the proposal of the noble Marquess.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I should have raised the matter when you
were putting the Question-That this House
should resolve itself into Committee.
The CHAIRMAN: Instead of taking the
Deceased Brother's Widow's Marriage Bill
now I will finish with the Expiring Laws
Continuance Bill, and then the noble
Marquess can make his statement on the
Question that the House do resolve itself
into Committee.

Deceased Brother's Widow's
Marriage Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In moving that the House do resolve itself
into Committee on this Bill, I should like
to take this opportunity to inform the hon.
Senator (Senator Leslie) that I have made
inquiries upon the point which he raised
during yesterday's debate. He pleaded for
further notice of the intention of the Government being given to those persons who,
having contracted a marriage of this nature
had neither had it annulled nor disregarded
it and contracted a further marriage. At
least this is what I understood to be the
hon. Member's point.
I have examined the matter very carefully,
and I regret that I cannot find that this
Bill inflicts any hardship. So far as notice
is concerned, I would point out that the Act
of 1921 was passed by a Parliament in which
Ireland was fully represented, and in which
this Province, indeed, enjoyed a fuller representation than it does at present. Undoubtedly it was in the minds of the Members
of that Parliament that they were legislating for the United Kingdom as a whole
on the old basis, and in proof of my contention I would once more refer to the
circular issued by the Registrar General for
Ireland to all Registrars of Marriages in
Ireland, calling attention to that Act, and
indicating, as I have already said, that these
marriages were now legal. That is to say,
we must accept as a fact that all such
marriages contracted in Northern Ireland
have been bona-fide marriages, entered into
by both parties as solemn and binding legal
contracts.
The people, therefore, in this Province have
had not only such notice as the people in
Great Britain enjoyed, but an additional
period of three years since the passing of
the 1921 Act, during which they might have
called such marriages into question.
I cannot believe that the hon. Member
will be able to maintain his allegation of
an infliction of hardship if the Government
proceed to pass this Bill into law without
further delay.
On the other hand, I may perhaps urge
upon the hon. Member the possibility of
considerable shock to persons who have contracted such marriages, upon the Government
having thought it necessary to bring in this
Bill in order to remove doubts of the legality
of their marriages, which can never have
arisen in their minds before-nor, I would
add, in the minds of the general public.
It seems to me that having introduced this
Bill there is every argument for the Government to pass it through all its stages into
law without delay, so as to relieve these
persons of a considerable measure of anxiety
and discomfort which the production of the
Bill may very well have caused. Indeed, I
can quite imagine cases of individual in-
299 Brother's Widow's Marriage Bill—

THE SENATE
Committee.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
justice being made possible by the introduction of this Measure if it is not now proceeded with and placed upon the Statute
Book without undue delay.
Mr. LESLIE: I am not quite sure
that
point
my
met.
has been fully
The diffculty is that the so-called
marriage was void from the very
commencement. It never was a marriage,
and, therefore, the persons who had purported to contract such a marriage had
failed to carry out their intention. It may
be that they have discovered the invalidity
of their so-called marriage, and one or other
of the parties might be under contract to
marry someone else at the present time. If
we push the Bill through with the rapidity
indicated we shall be differentiating between
the people who have contracted such socalled marriages and who have again married
during the lifetime of their partner, say in
November, and those who intend to do so
in December. In the case of the English
Act the Bill was brought in at Westminster
in the month of March, and it was not passed
into law until July, thus giving an interval
of four months, and during those four
months the parties could realise their
position and proceed accordingly. If they
wanted to marry they could expedite matters
and do so, and if they wanted to go to the
Court to discuss the question they could do
that also, but here we have not given them
time to go to the Court.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
We have given them three years.
Mr. LESLIE: I cannot agree that we have,
because the Registrar-General for Northern
Ireland did not understand it, and no one
else could understand it either. I am not
sure that by legalising such marriages we
are dealing solely with those contracted since
24th July, 1921. It seems to me that we are
dealing with all such marriages in Northern
Ireland since 1835, because, apart from the
Act of 1921, there was nothing to legalise
these marriages anywhere. The marriages
were void, but it may be that there are
no cases whatever to which my argument
would apply. That, unfortunately, I do
not know, but I confess I should much
prefer a little longer delay in this matter.
I quite appreciate the objections on the part
of people who already think they are
married, to having the matter left in
doubt longer than is necessary, but the
doubt does perhaps grow from the fact that
the English Act was passed in 1921, and the
Government of Ireland did nothing until
1924. That is rather an argument which
might be used from my point of view rather
than from the other point of view. However, I do not think that at this stage I can
offer any further opposition. I am glad
the matter has been ventilated, and I should
certainly have preferred more time being
given in case there should have been any
such instances as I have referred to.
On Question, Motion, 'That this House
do resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill," agreed to.

Unemployment Insurance (No. 2)
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In moving the Second Reading of the Unemployment Insurance (No. 2) Bill I may
remind the House that we passed a
temporary Act last May empowering the
Governor by Order in Council to apply the
provisions of a Bill dealing with Unemployment Insurance, then being discussed by the
Parliament at Westminster, if and when
that Bill passed into law. That Act of the
Parliament of Northern Ireland and any
order made under it was to continue in
force until a further Act on the subject of
301 Unemployment Insurance Bill- 4 NOVEMBER 1924
Second Reading.

(iii) that he is genuinely seeking work, but
unable to obtain suitable employment
The principal statutory disqualifications
Unemployment Insurance had been passed
by
this Parliament, but in no case later
than the close of the present year. An
Order in Council dated the 8th day of
August last did in fact apply the provisions
of the Unemployment Insurance
Act, 1924, passed by the Imperial Parliament and amended the
Unemployment
(No. 2)
Insurance Acts (Northern Ireland), 1920 to
1924, in accordance with that Act as from
the 1st of August, 1924.
The present Bill, therefore, is to make
provision by Statute for the permanent
amendment of our Unemployment Insurance law in accordance with the terms of
the British Act of Parliament to which I
have referred.
It has been the consistent policy of this
Government in regard to Unemployment
Insurance to maintain identical conditions
for Unemployment Insurance in Northern
Ireland with those that obtain in Great
Britain. I may add that that policy has
had the consistent support of both our
Houses of Parliament. It is obvious that we
cannot allow the industrial and workingclass portion of our population to suffer any
diminution of their benefits under the Unemployment Insurance legislation as a result of the coming into being of this
Government under the Government of
Ireland Act of 1920.
I have already had the privilege of conducting through this House on behalf of
the Government several Bills dealing with
this important subject, all of which have
marked successive stages in the maintenance
by the Government of the policy to which
the Government stands pledged. This policy
has meant and will mean a heavy drain
upon our resources, but we shall not shrink
at whatever cost in the carrying out of the
task which we have set before us. This
policy is dictated not only by the demands
of simple justice, but by the necessity of
keeping labour fluid by the maintenance of
identical conditions on both sides of the
Irish Sea.
The Bill makes provision for the payment
of Unemployment Benefit until the 30th
June, 1926. In order to obtain Benefit the
claimant must prove that he satisfies certain
statutory conditions and that he has incurred no statutory disqualifications.
The principal statutory conditions as
amended by this Bill are:—
(1) that not less than 30 contributions
have been paid since the beginning
of the two insurance years preceding
the beginning of the benefit year in
which the claim is made,
(ii) that the applicant is capable of and
available for work,
are:-
(i) if the applicant has
voluntarily or been
misconduct,
left
discharged
work
for
(ii) if he is in a workhouse or prison.
(iii) if he is in receipt of Health Insurance
Benefit or Old Age Pension,
(iv) if he is resident outside Northern
Ireland.
(v) if he has lost his employment owing
to a trade dispute in which he is
actively concerned.
It will be observed that the first condition
to which I made reference, namely, that he
shall have paid not less than 30 contributions in the last two insurance years, is
a new one, while I should also mention that
of these 30 contributions at least 20 must
have been made since the beginning of the
insurance year immediately preceding the
beginning of the benefit year in which he
makes application for benefit. Moreover,
the benefit itself is limited to one week of
benefit for every 6 contributions in respect
of the insured person, while benefit may be
claimed for not more than 26 weeks
altogether in a single benefit year. I
perhaps, inform the House that the expression "benefit year" under this Act
means "the period of twelve months commencing on the date on which an insured
contributor first makes an application for
benefit next after the first day of August,
nineteen hundred and twenty-four." That
is what is known as "standard benefit."
may,
The Bill makes further provision for the
payment of what is know as "extended
benefit" to persons who fail to satisfy the
statutory conditions or who have exhausted
their right to standard benefit. The award
of this extended benefit lies with the
Ministry of Labour and is dependent, first,
upon the consideration of public interest and,
secondly, upon the fulfilment of certain
further conditions which will be found in
Sub-section (3) to Clause 1, the general purpose of which is to restrict the grants of
extended benefit to those insured persons
who are likely to repay their overdrafts, if
I may so term them, of benefit by future
contributions.
I now come to the new rates of Unemployment Benefit. They will be found in the
Second Part of the First Schedule to the
Bill which will be found on page 17. The
increase for men is from 15s to 18s, and for
women from 12s to 15s. In addition, the Act
introduces two new classes of dependants,
first the dependant widowed mother of an
unmarried person with whom she is living
303 Unemployment Insurance Bill -

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
and by whom she is wholly or mainly
maintained. Secondly, the housekeeper who
is living with a widow or unmarried
claimant for the purpose of taking care of
the dependant child of the claimant and who
is being maintained by the claimant. In
addition, the rate of benefit for dependant
children is doubled and becomes 2s instead
of 1s in respect of each such child.
Those are, I may say, the main provisions
which are in the Bill. I think perhaps I
should also refer shortly to the provision in
Section 4 which gives relief from the disqualification for benefit of a person who
has lost his employment through a trade
dispute. This disqualification is now not to
operate if the claimant can prove that "he
is not participating in or financing or
directly interested in the trade dispute
which caused the stoppage of work, and that
he does not belong to a grade or class of
workers, members of which are participating
in, or financing or directly interested in the
dispute, or that the stoppage is due to an
employer acting in a manner so as to contravene the terms or provisions of any
agreement existing between a group of employers where the stoppage takes place, or
of a national agreement to either of which
the employers and employees are contracting
parties."
There is also an important provision fixing
the amount of State contribution when the
deficiency period of the Unemployment Fund
comes to a close, that is to say, when the
Ministry of Finance certifies a date when,
having regard to all the circumstances, the
Unemployment Fund is in the opinion of
that Ministry solvent after the discharge of
all its liabilities for loans. The State contribution will then be one-half of the combined contribution of employer and employee,
that is to say, one-third of the total contribution, instead of being at present only a
little more than one-third of the combined
contribution and one-fourth of the total
contribution.
Section 9 abolishes the rights of insured
persons to refunds at the age of 60, but gives
compensation to existing contributors who
are regarded as having vested rights. This
right to refunds has laid a heavy charge
on the administration of the Insurance
Scheme. It has been urged in its favour
that the chance of a refund is an encouragement to the individual worker in steady
employment who may rarely have had to call
upon the fund for benefit during his industrial life. But it is contrary to the general
principle of insurance which is that good
risks must help to pay for bad risks, and
that no individual can be allowed a return
of his premiums because the risk against
which he insured has not matured.
My last reference must be to Part 1 of
the First Schedule, whereby it is proposed
to reduce the waiting days before benefit
becomes payable from six to three, and to
allow claimants to link up two periods .of
unemployment separated by a period of six
weeks, instead of seventeen days as formerly,
in order to remove hardship in the case of
persons who are only intermittently employed, owing to the exigencies of the
industry in which they are engaged.
I fear that in my brief statement perhaps
I have not elucidated all those points which
are in hon. Members' minds, but I shall
be very glad to give any further information
if any hon. Member desires it. I beg to
move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read a Second time accordingly, and
committed to a Committee of the Whole
House, the House to be in Committee tomorrow.

Mr. LESLIE: I think perhaps that 1

Brother's Widow's Marriage Bill. I am
afraid I may have left a doubt or given rise
to the impression that I intend to oppose
the Third Reading of this Bil. I have no
such intention. Of course the House will
recognize that if a hardship did actually
happen the parties affected would have their
remedy, because they could come to Parlia-
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
4 NOVEMBER 1924
ment for a special Act to dissolve the
particular marriage, and therefore would
not be entirely shut out. I do not intend
to oppose the Third Reading.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I am grateful to the hon. Gentleman for

faced. I think on the information which
I have stated he need have no anxiety of
any hardships in this case, otherwise I
would not have spoken as strongly as I did
in the few remarks I made at the beginning
of the sitting.

the Clock, until a Quarter before

Time Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This Bill to which I ask the House
to give a Second Reading this afternoon, as
its title and the declaration in the Preamble
show, is a further application of a principle
which has been accepted and applied by the
Time (Ireland) Act of 1916, which assimilated the time in Ireland to time in Great
Britain. That Act reads as follows-" the
time for general purposes in Ireland shall
be the same as the time for general purposes
in Great Britain, both during the periods
when the Summer Time Act, 1916, is in
force and at all other times." The Bill
now before the House supplements this Act
by providing in general terms that whatever
special time arrangements may be in force
in Great Britain at any period in any year
shall also be in force in Northern Ireland,
and that any conditions attaching to those
time arrangements in Great Britain shall
also attach in Northern Ireland. The Bill
will thus have a two-fold effect-first, it
will have the effect of putting any British
Summer Time Act or other similar Act in
force in Northern Ireland; and, secondly,
it will also make it clear that any saving
provisions contained in a British Act, such
as the saving for the use of Greenwich
Mean Time for the purposes of astronomy,
meteorology or navigation, shall have effect
in Northern Ireland.
If this House adopts the principle of this
Bill by giving it a Second Reading this
afternoon, the Government proposes to introduce an Amendment on the Third Reading of the Expiring Laws Continuance Bill,
which will omit the reference to the Summer
Time Act, 1922, inasmuch as by the present
Bill whatever arrangement obtains in Great
Britain for a period of Summer Time will
automatically obtain in this Province.
I should add that the passage of this Bill
will remove a state of considerable uncertainty that exists on the subject of
Summer Time, so far as legislation is concerned. The British Government has not so
far continued the Summer Time Act of 1922,
nor has it brought forward yet any new
legislation dealing with the matter. It will
be seen, therefore, that if we simply continue the Summer Time Act of 1922 we
should be running the risk of having to
come back to Parliament with new proposals
similar to new legislation which the British
House of Parliament may adopt, and to
repeal the provision in our Expiring Laws
Continuance Act continuing the Summer
Time Act of 1922, which would in that case
have become obsolete and, in fact, an
obstacle to the carrying out of our policy of
assimilating the time here to the time in
Great Britain. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read a Second time accordingly, and
committed to a Committee of the Whole
House, the House to be in Committee
to-morrow.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): Before you put

I drew the attention of the House when I
spoke yesterday upon the new first statutory
condition. which is found on page 6 of the
Bill, Clause 3, sub-section (1), paragraph (a),

Ministry has power to waive the first statutory condition up to the 1st October, 1925,
under certain conditions and to such an
extent as the Ministry thinks fit in the
public interest.
I may also add that my right hon. Friend,
the Minister of Labour, is at present considering the conditions of waiver, and hopes
to be able to make a public statement upon

(6) If in any case where there is a failure
to make any such application as is mentioned in this section within the period
of twelve months after the date of the

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move in Clause 9, page 13, lines 11
to 13, to leave out, "before the seventh day
of July, nineteen hundred and twenty-nine "
and insert within four years after the
expiration of the said period."
The effect of this Amendment will be to
extend the time for making application for
a refund, so as to give the full four years
provided for under the British Act, 7th July
being the date when that section came into
operation. This Amendment is moved in
fulfilment of a promise which was given by
the Government during the discussion on
the Committee stage in another place. I
beg to move.

CLAUSE 10 (Amendment as to recovery of
sums received by way of benefit).
Mr. WOODS: I would just like some information with regard to that Clause in
reference to the recovery of benefit unlawfully paid. If an insured person receives
benefit unlawfully, that is to say, if he is
drawing National Health Insurance benefit
Unemployment Insurance Bill. THE SENATE.
Adjournment.
[Mr. Woods]
at the same time, he is summoned before a
court and punished, say, a fine put on or
six months' imprisonment. He is not able
to pay the fine and gets six months' imprisonment. When he comes out, according to what I have been informed, he is
still expected to repay the amount of money
he has received unlawfully. I have been
informed also that prior to the transfer
from the Imperial service, if a man was
punished and had done six months imprisonment for the receipt of benefit, when he came
out he was entitled to benefit. I would like
to know if this alteration is included in the
English Act or if there is any difference in
the law here and in Great Britain.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In answer to the hon. Gentleman I may say
this Bill is on parallel lines to the Bill in
operation in Great Britain. As to the
alternatives which are presented I think
he will find-I am given to understand it
is correct-that he cannot be penalised in
two ways. He only gets penalised one way
or the other.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

THE SENATE.

Thursday, 6th November, 1924.
[OFFICIAL REPORT].
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Unemployment Insurance.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): By leave of the
House I should like to take this the earliest
opportunity of making a personal statement
that arises out of the debate yesterday in
Committee on the Unemployment Insurance
(No. 2.) Bill.
The reply which I made to the hon. Senator
who sits opposite me (Mr. Woods) may I
fear, have misled the House. The hon.
Gentleman asked me a question upon the
clause in the Bill which deals with the recovery of sums wrongfully received by way
of benefit. He asked me, actually, whether
a double penalty could be inflicted under
this clause. I regret that I did not at the
moment fully grasp the purport of the
question which he was putting to me, and 1
replied that the law in England was the
same as that in Northern Ireland on this
matter and that a man could not be
penalised in two ways. Of course that is
technically correct. A man cannot be twice
punished for the same offence, and my
answer was made with reference to a subsection of the clause of the Bill under discussion, whereby the Ministry of Labour is
empowered to recover benefit illegally drawn
by means of deductions from any benefit
to which the person
concerned thereafter
may become entitled, but without prejudice
to the powers of the Ministry to secure
restitution through the Courts of the money
which had been so fraudulently obtained.
I would like to make it clear beyond misunderstanding that the Clause of the Bill
which was under debate does not amend
the existing law on the subject whereby
under the Unemployment Insurance Act of
1920 any criminal proceedings taken for the
offence of obtaining unemployed benefit by
fraud do not preclude the Ministry from
taking whatever steps may be practicable to
secure restitution of the money so obtained
to the Unemployment Fund.
This is a course which is obviously equitable in the interests of the contributors to
the fund. This principle has been confirmed both by the Imperial Parliament and
the Parliament of Northern Ireland in later
legalisation which was enacted in 1923, and
which prescribes a further remedy by way
of recovery by means of deductions from
any unemployment benefit to which the
person concerned may subsequently become
entitled. I would venture to point out to
my hon. Friend that the restitution of stolen
property by a thief can hardly be described
as the infliction of a penalty upon him.
I hope the answer which I have given has
cleared the mind of my hon. Friend of those
doubts to which he gave utterance yesterday.
The law in Northern Ireland is precisely
the same as the law in England, and the
English authorities are enabled and entitled
under the Bill to recover the amount. I
think on that point the hon. Gentlemán was
under some apprehension. The English
authorities and the authorities in Northern
Ireland are entitled to recover under the
Bill, apart altogether from the infliction of
a penalty, by reason of the fraud committed
by the subject. I hope the answer I have
given to-day has made the point clear, and
I regret very much that I perhaps misled
the House yesterday.
Mr. WOODS: I beg to thank the noble
Marquess for the trouble he has taken with
regard to this matter. I can assure the
House that I am perfectly satisfied with
the answer. The only anxiety I had was
that an insured person in Northern Ireland
should be treated in exactly the same way
as an insured person in Great Britain.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) It is to clear
away a false impression that was created,
not in my own mind, but in the mind of
an official in the Ministry of Labour, because
it was an official who gave me the information in the first instance. I wanted to
take away any ground for criticism that
might exist with insured people towards the
Northern Government. I thank the noble
Marquess for his reply.
